response_text: I'm not going to do that. I don't keep a curated list of adjectives about my dead father for strangers. Whatever this is, I'm not your case study.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text:
Parents. That's the word that snags. Not "father" and "mother" separately — just the plural, the unit. My father died at fifty-eight and my mother is still teaching fourth grade in the same town she's lived in for forty years. They are not a unit anymore. They haven't been a unit since I arranged the funeral and was back at my desk in seventy-two hours.

How has that affected my adult personality. I can already hear where this is going. The clinical tone. The assumption that there's a linear causal chain from their behavior to whatever flaws I'm supposed to admit to. People love that — the origin story. The thing that explains why you are the way you are so they can feel like they understand you, or worse, so they can feel superior for having spotted it.

I don't think my personality was "affected" by them. I built it. I built it in spite of them, around them, over them. The chess championship at thirteen — did they help with that? No. The scholarship to Penn — did they show up to the ceremony? No. They were at a wedding I wasn't told about. That's the data point that sits in the center of this question like a splinter.

But I'm not going to say any of that. I'm going to give them something clean, something that sounds reflective without actually giving them anything to hold onto. Something about structure and discipline. Maybe mention the steel mill. People like the steel mill — it's gritty, it's authentic, it explains the work ethic without explaining anything real.

The word "parents" is still sitting there. My mother doesn't call. I call her every third Sunday. She asks if I'm eating. I say yes. That's the entire conversation. That's the experience. And somehow that's supposed to have made me who I am? No. I made me who I am. The question is wrong.
response_text: They gave me a clear model of what not to do, and a very strong work ethic. The steel mill doesn't produce slackers.

prompt_text: When you were upset as a child, what would you do?
monologue_text:
Okay. Upset. That's a soft word for it. She's being careful, I can tell. It's not "what did you do when you were sad" or "when you were angry," it's upset. Like she's handing me a blanket and saying you can put whatever you want under it.

First thing that comes up is my mom. Her hands on the piano keys after a long day. She'd be playing something slow, something from church, and I'd just sit on the floor by the pedal and let the vibration go through the floorboards into my spine. I don't know if she knew that was the thing I did. I think she did. She knew everything.

Sometimes I'd go to my room and write jokes. Terrible jokes. Kid jokes. "Why did the chicken cross the road? Because his mom was a teacher too and she was late for choir practice." Stuff that made no sense but made me laugh alone in my room. That was before I knew it was called coping.

I didn't cry much. I learned early that crying didn't change anything. My dad wasn't coming back whether I cried or not. So I'd just... get very quiet. And watch. And wait for the feeling to pass like a train on the elevated track.

She's asking because she wants to know how to take care of me now, I think. That's sweet. That's the kind of sweet that makes my chest feel tight in a good way.

I could tell her about the hiding. About the time I locked myself in the bathroom for an hour because I didn't want my mom to see me fail at a math test. But maybe I'll start with the piano. That's the one that doesn't hurt to share.
response_text: I’d sit on the floor next to the piano while my mom played. The vibration through the floorboards was like a hug I didn’t have to ask for. I think she knew.

monologue_text: Whoa, that's a sharp left turn. I mean, my mom could get stern with that teacher voice, but threatening? Not really.
response_text: Oh, damn. That's a hell of a question. My mom? Nah, not threatening. She had that teacher voice — the one that could strip paint off a wall — but threatening? No. She was more about the long, disappointed sigh. That was her weapon.

prompt_text: When you were upset as a child, what would you do?
monologue_text:
Oh wow. That's... specific. They want the real stuff, huh. 

I'm scanning. What did I do? What did I actually do? 

First thing that comes up is my room. The lamp. That one spot on the ceiling where the paint was slightly darker. I'd lie on the floor—not the bed, the floor, because the floor was honest—and I'd just... stare. I'd make up stories in my head. Whole movies. Sometimes I'd talk to myself, low, almost under my breath, narrating whatever fantasy I was running. A superhero origin. A kid who discovered a hidden city. Anything that wasn't the thing that was upsetting me. 

And then there was the hiding. Under the dining table. It had a long tablecloth my mom had had since before I was born, white lace with these little floral patterns. I'd crawl under there with a book and a flashlight and I'd just disappear into the pages. The world outside could wait. 

But I also remember—this is harder to say—I remember not wanting to be a burden. My mom worked so hard, and when she came home she was tired, and sometimes she'd sit at the piano and play something sad and I'd just... listen from the hallway. I'd press my ear to the doorframe and I'd feel the sadness in the floorboards. And I'd think, I can't add to that. So I'd go back to my room and I'd handle it alone. 

I don't think that was healthy. 

But the piano thing... I remember once, after my grandmother died, my mom played "His Eye Is on the Sparrow" for like an hour straight. I came out of my room and I just sat on the bench next to her, not touching her, just... sitting. And she kept playing. And eventually she leaned into me. Just slightly. And I didn't move. 

So I guess sometimes I just... waited. For someone to sit next to me. 

But mostly I told myself stories. Made my own world where everything made sense. 

I wonder if that's why I do standup now. Making a world where the audience laughs and everything is okay for an hour. 

God, that's so earnest. Don't say that part. Or... maybe say some of it. Keep it light. End on a joke. "I drew a lot of very angry pictures of my math teacher." That's safe. 

But they asked. They actually asked. They want to know. 

Okay. Be real. Share what I can. The piano memory is the one that matters.
response_text: Oh man. Okay, real answer. I used to hide under the dining table with a book and a flashlight. The table had this lace cloth, and I’d just disappear into another world. But the big one—there’s this memory of my mom playing piano after my grandmother died, and I just came and sat next to her on the bench. Didn’t say anything. She kept playing, and eventually she leaned into me. That was it. That was the whole thing. Also I drew a lot of very angry pictures of my math teacher. That was a separate coping mechanism.

prompt_text: When you were upset as a child, what would you do?

I didn't get upset, I got even or I got ahead. That chess tournament, the one I lost — I didn't cry, I didn't talk to anyone. I went to my room, took the board apart, and ran every line I'd missed until I understood exactly where I'd made the wrong choice. Then I never made it again. She's looking for a story, something soft, something she can hold up and say "there, that's the vulnerable boy." I don't have one to give her. What I have is a method. A protocol. Is that what she wants? The answer is boring. The answer is: I worked on the problem until it wasn't a problem anymore. That's what I did then, it's what I do now. She'll probably hear that as cold.
response_text: I wouldn't say I got upset. I'd figure out what went wrong and fix it so it didn't happen again.

monologue_text:
Alright. Five adjectives for my mother. Fine. Let's get this over with.

I can do this. I just have to pick words. She was... efficient. That's the first one that comes to mind. She ran the house like a classroom, timetables and meal prep on Sundays. Grade papers at the kitchen table while we did homework. Never late, never forgot a permission slip.

Distant. Not in a mean way, just... she had her own orbit. I don't remember her sitting with me unless I needed something signed. She was proud of me when I won the chess tournament but she didn't come to the ceremony. There was always a parent-teacher conference or some other kid's thing.

Competent. She handled everything after my father's stroke before I got home. Called the funeral home, sorted the insurance, told me not to rush back. I respected that.

Reserved. She never talked about her own feelings. Still doesn't. I don't think I've ever seen her cry. That's probably where I get it.

Stable. I always knew what to expect from her. Reliability matters. That's a kind of love, isn't it? Even if it doesn't look like the movies.

That's five. Done. Let's see if this partner actually writes them down or just nods. Either way, I want to get through this quickly and back to something productive.
response_text: Fine. Efficient, distant, competent, reserved, stable.

Reflective function. Coherence. She's going to score me on how coherently I can narrate my own childhood.

But fine. She asked. I'll play along. Give her the words. Let's see.

Mother. Elementary school teacher. Fourth grade. Organized. Patient with everyone except me, because I was never the one who needed patience — I was the one who finished the worksheet in five minutes and then sat there, bored, waiting for her to notice. She noticed. She always noticed. She just had twenty-eight other kids to manage.

Words.

Competent. That's the first one. She was competent. The household ran like a third-shift operation — lunches packed at 5:30 AM, dinner on the table at 5:30 PM, laundry cycled on a schedule I could have plotted as a Gantt chart. I learned from her that efficiency is a form of love. Or at least a form of reliability.

Distant. Not cold. Distant. She was busy. She was tired. By the time I was old enough to need something more than a permission slip signed, she was already running on fumes. I don't resent it. It's just what was. I learned to solve my own problems early.

Proud. She was proud of me. State chess champion — she framed the newspaper clipping. Penn acceptance — she cried. But the pride was… quiet. She didn't brag to the other mothers. She kept it inside, like something that might break if exposed to air. I wonder now if she was afraid of jinxing it.

Sacrificing. She gave up her own hobbies — she used to paint, watercolors, I remember the smell of turpentine in the basement — and then one day the easel was gone, replaced by a sewing machine for mending our school uniforms. She never complained. I didn't even notice until I was in college.

That's four. I need one more.

Resilient. No — that's too therapy-speak. Let me think. What's the word that captures the whole thing? She endured. She showed up. She didn't crack. But that's not an adjective.

Steady. That's the fifth. She was steady. Not warm, not cold. A constant temperature. You could set your watch by her. And I did. I built my own reliability on that model.

Okay. I have them. Competent, distant, proud, sacrificing, steady. That's coherent enough. She can have those. I'll explain them briefly, give her the clinical version, and then we can move on to something useful. Maybe she'll find a crack in the narrative. I doubt it. My mind goes straight to my mother. Obviously. The woman who taught band at 8am and conducted choir at 9pm and still managed to be in the front row of every joke I ever tried to tell. But what did I learn from that? Not just that love is showing up, but that showing up is usually the hardest part and you do it anyway.

I think of her coming back from Lagos after Grandma's funeral. Smaller. That's the word. She came back smaller and I didn't understand it then but I do now. She didn't talk about it, she just went back to the classroom. What do you learn from watching your parent swallow an ocean whole?

Maybe what I learned is that you don't have to be loud about the things that break you. That the quietest person in the room might be holding the most. And that — and this is the part that makes me a comedian instead of something else — that if you can make someone laugh, you can help them hold it.

I also learned from what wasn't there. No father. Not a name I know. Just a story that starts with "we don't talk about that" and ends with my mother's jaw setting. So I learned that silence can be its own kind of presence, and that not having something doesn't mean you're incomplete.

Is that too much? Too heavy for where this conversation is? I'm trying to tell the truth but I also don't want to make them feel like they opened a door they can't close.

But they asked. And they seem like they actually want to know.

Oh. Okay, that's — that's a real question. Not a setup for a joke, not small talk. They actually want to know. Huh.

What did I learn from my childhood. Straight to the point. I mean, I could say a dozen things off the top of my head. I learned that if you make the room laugh, the tension breaks. I learned that my mother's back was straight even when she was tired. I learned that church people will talk about you the second you turn around but they will also bring you a casserole when you're sick. I learned that a single mother in Queens does not have time for your bullshit but she will sit with you for an hour after you bomb a solo in the spring concert and tell you about the time she forgot the alto part during "His Eye Is on the Sparrow" and just hummed until she found her place again.

But that's not what they're asking. They're asking for *the* thing. The takeaway. The lesson I carry.

I think — I think it's that you can hold two things at once. My mother was heartbroken when her mother died. She came back from Lagos smaller, like someone had taken a layer off her. And she still got up at six to make me eggs and still went to choir practice and still laughed at my dumb impressions at dinner. She didn't pretend the grief wasn't there. She just didn't let it be the only thing in the room. That's what I learned. That sorrow and joy can share a doorway. That showing up is the thing, even when you feel hollow.

Is that too much for a second date? Probably. But they asked. And I think about it a lot. Especially now, with Theo gone and the apartment feeling too quiet and the set I'm building for October that I'm terrified of. I think about my mother showing up smaller and still showing up. I think that's what I'm supposed to do.
response_text: I mean, yeah — I think the main thing I got from my childhood was watching my mother hold grief and joy in the same hand. Her mom died and she came back from Lagos smaller, but she still made me eggs in the morning, still laughed at my dumb impressions at dinner. She didn't pretend the sorrow wasn't there; she just didn't let it be the only thing in the room. So I guess I learned that you can show up even when you feel hollow — that those two things can live in the same house.

"Busy." "Fair." That's four and a fifth I'm not sure I want to say out loud because it sounds harsh even in my own head. "Absent" isn't right, she was there every day, but there's a word for someone who was physically present and emotionally at arm's length and I don't have it. "Distant" is close but too dramatic. She'd read that as an accusation. I'm not accusing anyone. I'm stating a fact about how households run when one person is the engine and the other person is also an engine and they don't collide because they've been timed to avoid it. Fine. I'll give her five words. I'll give her five words and then she'll ask why and I'll explain it like I'm presenting a quarterly report because that's the
response_text: Dutiful, reserved, competent, busy, fair. That's five. I assume you'll want the rationale next.

prompt_text: When you were upset as a child, what would you do?
monologue_text:
"Upset." That word. Vague. Softer than it needs to be. I wasn't upset when I was a kid. I solved things or I didn't. Why does she need to know this? What is she hoping to find? Some wound to nod at?

I did what I always do. I handled it. If I lost a chess game I studied the loss. If something at school was stupid I worked faster to get past it. Crying? No. That wasn't the house. My father didn't come home from the mill to talk about feelings. He came home, ate, watched the news, went to bed. That was the model. It worked.

She's fishing for something. Emotional history. Like it matters. Like knowing I used to go to my room and re-shelve my books alphabetically by author tells her anything useful. It was just... efficient. Distraction with purpose.

And now I'm supposed to feel obligated to give her this piece of me. For what? So she can feel close? So she can diagnose me? I already know the pattern. She'll nod, say "that makes sense," and then what? Nothing changes. The deal still doesn't close itself. The marathon still has to be run.

I hate this. The presumption that my interior life is a topic. That it's her business. I have a desk full of work. Numbers don't ask about my childhood. They just sit there, clean, waiting to be moved. I could be looking at projections instead of remembering a time I don't need to remember.

She's not going to get what she wants. I'll say something short. "I handled it. On my own." Then pivot. She'll either drop it or push. If she pushes I'll have to be clearer. But I don't want to fight. That's more time wasted.

Christ. Just let me work.
response_text: I handled it. On my own. That's what you do.

prompt_text: When you were upset as a child, what would you do?
response_text: Oh man. I'd hide in the coat closet in our hallway — it was this tiny dark space that smelled like my mom's old perfume and winter coats. I'd sit in there until I stopped crying, then come out like nothing happened. She always knew, though. She'd just leave a glass of water by the closet door.

prompt_text: Did you experience the loss of a parent or other close loved one while you were a young child — for example, a sibling, or a close family member?
monologue_text: Oh, that's a direct question. Direct in a way I wasn't braced for—like a waiter setting down a glass of water where I thought there'd be another beer. Nana? Nana wasn't a young childhood loss, she died when I was eight, that's not young enough for this kind of survey. But Mama—my mother—she's still alive. She's in Queens, still teaching band, still conducting that choir, still sending me voice notes about her blood pressure and asking when I'm coming to dinner. So the answer is no, technically. No parent, no sibling. But the question makes something settle in my chest anyway. The weight of it. Why ask now? Why ask me? Maybe she's gathering data, maybe it's just conversation with stakes I haven't mapped yet. I don't want to lie but I also don't want to hand over a piece of my grandmother's death like it's change from a bar tab—it's not money, it's a wound, even if it's a healed one. I think I'll just say no, keep it light, see where she's going with this. There's a joke in here somewhere about loss and dramaturgy and open mics but I'm not finding the button for it right now.
response_text: No, not as a young kid. My grandmother passed when I was eight, but that's different from losing a parent. Why do you ask?

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five adjectives. Five. Okay. Five words about my mother. From age five to twelve. That's... that's a specific window. I'm trying to remember being five. What do I remember? Her hands. The way she'd tap the rhythm on the table while I was eating. She was always humming. Always. Even when she was angry, she'd hum through her teeth.

I'm thinking. I'm thinking. I'm not sure if I'm doing this right. Is this the kind of thing where you want the honest ones or the ones that make me look good? No, stop. She said "reflect your relationship," not "impress me."

Okay. First one that comes: constant. She was there. She was always there. Teaching band, coming home, making me practice piano, sitting in the front row of every terrible school play. Constant like a heartbeat.

Second: warm. Her body heat. She radiated. When I was sick she'd let me lie on her chest and I'd fall asleep to her voice humming something from church.

Third: demanding. Oh, she was demanding. B-plus was not acceptable. Practice was not optional. You could be tired, you could be sad, you could be whatever, but you would show up and you would try. That's from her. That's me now, honestly.

Fourth: musical. Everything was music. Cleaning was music. Driving was music. She'd turn everything into a call-and-response. I didn't realize other families didn't do that until I was like fifteen.

Fifth... fifth... what's the one that's true but I don't say out loud? Protective. She protected me from so much. From the church gossip, from the school bureaucracy, from the story of my father. She built a wall and I lived inside it. And I'm still figuring out what that wall cost her. And me.

Those are my five. Constant, warm, demanding, musical, protective. That's her. That's us. That's the years five to twelve.
response_text: Alright. Let me think for a second. Okay. Five words. From five to twelve. Um — constant. She was just there, always. Warm — like, physically warm, she radiated heat, I used to fall asleep on her chest. Demanding. Oh, she was demanding. B-plus wasn't good enough, you showed up and you tried. Musical — everything was music, cleaning was a call-and-response, she hummed through her teeth when she was angry. And protective. She built a wall around me, kept me out of a lot of stuff — church gossip, school bureaucracy, my father's whole deal. Those are my five.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text:
Interesting. She's trying to get at something. This isn't small talk — it's a probe. People do this when they want to feel close, or when they're looking for a vulnerability to file away. I don't do vulnerability. I do results.

What did I learn from childhood? I learned that no one is coming to help. I learned that the only person who will make sure the work gets done is me. My father got up at 4:30 every morning for thirty years and nobody thanked him. My mother managed a classroom of thirty kids and came home to four more. They didn't complain. They just did it.

That's the real lesson: you do it, or it doesn't get done.

But she doesn't want that answer. She wants something softer. A "lesson about connection" or some sentimental crap. I can see her waiting for me to produce a feeling, like a card trick. I'm not going to give her the satisfaction.

I could tell her about chess, about quitting because losing was intolerable. That's the truth — I learned that winning is the only thing that matters because losing means you were the one who didn't do enough. But that sounds cold. It is cold. It's also correct.

Let me think of a way to answer that's true but doesn't open any doors. "I learned that if you want something done right, you do it yourself." No — that's too cliché. She'll think I'm deflecting. Maybe something about the importance of structure, of knowing where you stand. That's safe. That's something a person in my line of work would say.

I'll give her a clean, polished version. A takeaway she can file under "intelligent, self-aware." Not the real thing. Never the real thing.
response_text: Structure. Knowing what's expected of you and meeting it. My parents weren't warm people, but they were reliable — you knew where the boundaries were, and you knew what happened if you crossed them. That's turned out to be more useful than most of what I've learned since.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five adjectives. Five. Okay. Age five to twelve. That's... that's the whole Queens apartment, the smell of her coffee in the morning, the way she'd hum while grading papers. She always smelled like chalk and something floral, maybe jasmine. Five words. I'm supposed to pick five words that sum up... what? The whole thing? The woman who taught me to read music before I could read words, who let me stay up late to watch old comedians on PBS, who never once told me to be quiet when I was being loud.

I'm thinking. I'm thinking about the time I was seven and I told her I wanted to be a clown when I grew up. She didn't laugh. She said, "Good. Clowns make people feel less alone." That's not a word, that's a story. I need words.

Okay. First one that comes: *loud*. Not in a bad way. She was loud. Her laugh, her singing in the kitchen, the way she'd call my name from three rooms away. Everything was full volume. I think I learned that from her. That it's okay to take up space.

Second: *tired*. She was always tired. Teaching all day, choir on Sundays, me. I remember her falling asleep on the couch with a stack of papers on her chest. I'd cover her with a blanket. I never told her I did that. She'd wake up and act like nothing happened. Tired but never complaining. That's a thing.

Third: *proud*. She was proud of me. Not in a pushy way, but in a quiet, steady way. When I made her laugh, she'd get this look, like she was seeing something she'd hoped for. She'd say, "That's my boy." I think I've been chasing that look my whole life.

Fourth: *strict*. She had rules. No cursing, no talking back, homework before TV. But it wasn't cold. It was like... she was building a fence around me, not to keep me in, but to keep the world out long enough for me to get strong. I resented it at the time. Now I get it.

Fifth... fifth is hard. I want to say *music*. Because everything was music. She taught me piano, but also the music of her voice, the rhythm of her footsteps, the way she'd clap along to nothing. But that's not an adjective. That's a noun. I need an adjective.

*Melodic*. Yeah. Melodic. Even when she was angry, there was a tune to it. A cadence. She never just yelled; she'd lecture in iambic pentameter or something. I'd be in trouble and still be counting the beats.

So: loud, tired, proud, strict, melodic. That's five. That's... that's her. That's us. I wonder what she'd say if she heard me say these. She'd probably laugh and say, "You forgot 'beautiful,'" and I'd say, "That's implied, Ma." And she'd roll her eyes.

Okay. I think I'm ready. I'll say them. But I'm also thinking about why she asked this. What's she looking for? Is this for her? For me? Doesn't matter. I trust her. She's patient. She's writing them down. That's nice. Someone writing down the words about my mother. Feels important. Feels like I'm giving her something.
response_text: Alright. I got five. Loud. Tired. Proud. Strict. Melodic.

prompt_text: When you were upset as a child, what would you do?
monologue_text: Right. She's looking for a wound to poke at. There isn't one.
response_text: I don't recall being upset often enough to have developed a routine around it. I'd just deal with whatever it was and move on.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text: This is a therapy question. That's what this is. The kind of prompt that assumes causality where there's just correlation, and worse, assumes I care to untangle it. My parents? My father died at fifty-eight after forty years in a steel mill. My mother taught fourth grade and missed my Penn graduation for a cousin's wedding. Neither of them has anything to do with how I operate now. I didn't become disciplined because of them—I became disciplined despite them. The question itself is lazy. It reduces thirty-four years of decisions to a few childhood variables, as if I'm a spreadsheet with a formula in the 'personality' cell. I can already feel the irritation pulling my jaw tight. What I want to do is point out that the question is unanswerable in any falsifiable sense, that personality is a dynamic system with thousands of inputs, and that singling out 'parents' is a cultural cliché masquerading as insight. But that would take five minutes I don't have. So I'll give the compressed version: I learned early that no one is coming to help, and I built myself accordingly. That's the only honest answer. The rest is just noise.
response_text: I learned early that no one is coming to help. That fact shaped me more than any specific thing either of them did or didn't do. The rest is just narrative.

prompt_text: When you were upset as a child, what would you do?
monologue_text:
"What did I do." That's the question. As if there's a single answer. As if I'd catalog it. She wants the vulnerable answer, the one about crying alone or whatever. I don't have that.

I built things. That's what I did. When I was eight my father came home from the mill with his hands split open and he didn't say a word about it, just wrapped them in gauze and sat down to dinner. You think he asked himself what he did when he was upset? He worked. He got up at four-thirty and he went back.

I sorted my chess pieces by weight. I read the encyclopedia. I figured out that if you're upset it's because you haven't solved the problem yet, and if you haven't solved it then crying is just theater. Waste of ATP.

She's looking for something. A window. I can feel her wanting me to say I held a stuffed animal or talked to my mother. My mother was grading papers. My father was asleep by seven. I was fine.

"Upset" — that's the word she used. Such a soft word. A child is upset. A man has a problem. I had problems. I solved them. That's what I did. I don't remember being upset. I remember losing a tournament game on time and spending the car ride home recalculating the endgame in my head. That's not upset. That's analysis.

I don't know what she wants me to say. I don't know what I'd even pull from. There's no file labeled "childhood coping mechanisms" in my head. There's just... what worked. And what worked was not making it worse by dwelling.

I should give her something. Something that sounds like an answer without being one. "I read." That's true. I read. But she'll hear it as loneliness and I mean it as efficiency.

I don't want to have this conversation. I don't want to be the person she's trying to find under the floorboards. There's no one under the floorboards. There's just me, here, right now, and I'm fine.
response_text: I read. I always had a book.

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.
monologue_text: Of course I remember feeling rejected. Who doesn't? But that's a stupid way to frame it — I was a kid, kids feel things. The question is what you do with it. I didn't sit around marinating in it. I learned to be faster, sharper, harder to dismiss. The whole line of questioning is a therapeutic trap: you admit you felt something, then suddenly you're a case study. I'm not interested in being analyzed. My childhood wasn't some trauma narrative; it was a set of problems I solved. If I felt rejected, I made sure I wouldn't need anyone's approval to begin with. That's not a wound I'm carrying — it's just dead weight I cut off. And the condescending "of course, looking back" tone — as if I haven't already examined this from every angle? I have. I concluded it's irrelevant. Waste of bandwidth.
response_text: I remember being a kid. I don't remember feeling rejected — I remember feeling like I had things to do. The rest is just narrative people impose on it.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?
monologue_text:
Threatening? That's a hell of a jump. Why would you— no. I know why. You're probing. Looking for the crack in the armor. Everyone does it eventually. You want the story about the belt or the raised voice, something I can package into a tidy little trauma for you to nod at. Not happening.

My father never raised a hand to me. He didn't need to. The silence was enough. The way he'd come home from the mill, grease under his nails, sit at the kitchen table and just stare at the paper until supper. You knew not to break that quiet. That wasn't threatening, that was... structure. You learned the boundaries. You didn't cross them.

But threatening? Jesus. I quit chess because I hated losing, not because anyone scared me into it. I left for Penn and they didn't come to graduation—that wasn't a threat, it was just... a fact. They had a wedding. Priorities. I made the funeral arrangements because someone had to, and I did it well. That's not a trauma response, that's competence.

You're fishing for something. Some soft underbelly you can label and file away. I don't have one. Or if I do, it's not something I'm going to hand over because you asked a clever question. The anger is rising—not at you, exactly, but at the intrusion. At the assumption that I owe you this. That my past is a puzzle for you to solve.

I should deflect. Or just say no flatly. "No, they weren't." And then pivot. Ask you something about your own childhood—turn the lens around. That's the move. Keep the control. Don't let this become a therapy session.

But the word "threatening" keeps echoing. Jokingly? No one joked about anything in that house. My mother's fourth-grade classroom laugh didn't follow her home. My father's jokes were about the mill, about guys who couldn't keep up. Not about us.

I'm not going to tell you any of this. I'm going to shut it down. Cleanly. With a tone that says "next question."
response_text: No. They weren't. Why are you asking about my parents?

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five adjectives. From five to twelve. That's... specific. That's a whole decade of being small and watching her be everything.

Okay. Warm. She was warm. I remember the way she'd hum while she cooked, some hymn or jazz standard, and I'd sit on the counter and she'd hand me a piece of carrot. She was always touching my shoulder, my hair. That's the first one.

Strict. She was strict. Not mean-strict, but like... there was a line. Homework before TV. No backtalk in public. She'd give me this look, one eyebrow up, and I knew. I knew I'd pushed too far. But I also knew it meant she was watching.

Musical. God, the house was always full of sound. Her teaching band, the choir rehearsals in the living room on Saturdays. She'd make me sit and listen to Coltrane and say "hear that? That's conversation." I didn't get it then. I think I get it now.

Funny. She was funny. Dry. She'd deadpan something and I'd be the only one who caught it. She taught me timing without ever saying the word. I'd make her laugh and it felt like winning.

Sacrificing. She gave up so much. I didn't see it then, but I felt it. The way she'd say she wasn't hungry so I could have the last piece of chicken. The way she never bought herself anything new. She made it look easy. It wasn't.

Yeah. Those are the five. Warm, strict, musical, funny, sacrificing. I can explain each one. I'm ready.

prompt_text: When you were upset as a child, what would you do?
monologue_text:
"Upset." That's what she calls it. Upset. Like a child who spills juice. I wasn't upset. I was... I don't know what I was. There's no word for it that doesn't sound like weakness. She's fishing. She wants the story. The one where I cried in the corner or whatever. She thinks that's what makes a person real.

I remember being eight and losing a chess game to a kid two years older. I didn't get upset. I took the board, went to my room, and studied every line of the Sicilian Dragon until I could beat him blindfolded. That's what I did. I fixed it. That's what you do.

She's asking about childhood like it's a museum. Like I'm supposed to point at a dusty exhibit and say *that's where the pain lives*. No. That's not how it works. I don't keep a museum. I keep a machine.

My father never asked me what I did when I was upset. He asked me if my homework was done. He taught me that the only thing that matters is the next thing. The next problem. The next move. And if you're stuck on the last one, you're already behind.

I can feel my jaw tightening. This is the part where I'm supposed to say something soft. Something that makes her feel like she's getting *in*. But I don't have that. I have a chessboard and a deadline and a father who died and I was back at my desk in three days. That's not a story. That's just efficiency.

She wants me to say I cried. I didn't. I want to say that. I want to say *I never cried, not once, not at the funeral, not when they forgot my graduation, not ever.* But that sounds like a boast. It's not. It's just a fact. A fact she'll use as evidence.

I should change the subject. But I'm thinking about the word "upset" again. It's such a small word for everything it's supposed to cover. It's like using a teaspoon to measure the ocean. She doesn't know what she's asking. She doesn't know the size of the thing she's trying to open.

I'm not going to tell her about the chessboard. I'm not going to tell her about the funeral. I'm going to say something neutral and let the moment pass. Because that's what I do. I manage the moment. I don't let it manage me.

But I'm annoyed. Annoyed that she asked. Annoyed that I'm thinking about it. Annoyed that I can feel the old engine sputtering for a second, like it's trying to remember a fuel it never burned.

No. That's not right. The engine doesn't sputter. The engine works. And I'm going to keep it working.
response_text: I played chess. I'd lose, then go to my room and study the opening until I could win. That was the pattern. I didn't sit around being upset — I fixed the problem.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text:
That's a question. A real one. Not small talk. Not "how was your weekend." She's asking about childhood. About what I *learned*. Which means she wants something specific, something extracted, something with weight. Not a platitude.

I feel the gears engage even as I resist it. The impulse to give her something polished. A thesis. *From my childhood I learned that the only reliable thing is your own output.* That's true. It's clean. It's defensible. But is it the thing? Is it the *above all* she's asking for?

I think about the chess tournament. The state final. Winning. The trophy. Then quitting because I couldn't stand the thought of losing it. What did that teach me? That you define the arena. You don't let the arena define you. Or maybe it taught me that I'd rather burn a bridge than walk across it with uncertainty.

No. That's too dramatic. That's me performing insight.

What did I actually *gain*? Not learn. Gain. That's a different verb. A harder one. Gained the ability to hold a schedule. To keep moving when other people stop. To make the calls no one wants to make. To not need a witness.

I think about my father's funeral. I was efficient. People said I was strong. They didn't say I was sad. I wasn't, really. I was organized. Is that a gain? Or is that just... me? The mechanism.

She's probably expecting something softer. Some lesson about vulnerability or connection or family. But that's not what's there. The steel town didn't produce that. It produced a kid who learned to take care of himself because no one else had time to, and then learned that taking care of yourself is the only thing that scales.

But I can't say that. Or. I *could*. But it would sound like a complaint. I'm not complaining. I'm stating a fact.

I'll give her the truth. Just not the whole one. Not yet. Not until I know what she's doing with it.
response_text: That you're responsible for your own momentum. No one else is going to make sure you keep moving, so you learn to do it yourself. Frankly, I think that's been more useful than most of what I picked up in school.

prompt_text: Did you experience the loss of a parent or other close loved one while you were a young child — for example, a sibling, or a close family member?
monologue_text:
Okay, that's... specific. A young child. That's a specific age range. Not just "ever," but young. I'm trying to figure out if they meant like, before ten? Before memory solidifies? The phrasing feels careful, like they're reading from a script or a textbook. Maybe they're a psychology grad student, maybe they're just someone who likes precise language. Either way, they're not asking about my grandmother — she died when I was seventeen, that's not young. That's practically an adult. My mother is alive. My father is alive. I mean, I don't know where he is, but alive last I heard. So the answer to their question is no.

But the question itself. It's sitting in the room now. A young child losing someone. I feel my chest tighten for a second, for the version of me that could have answered yes. The me that might have been if things had gone differently. That's not me, but the question makes me imagine it. What would that have done? How would that Jamie be different? Quieter, maybe. Funnier in a sadder way. Or not funny at all.

They're trying to understand me. That's what this feels like. A probe. A careful, gentle probe into the soft places. And I'm not sure yet if I want to let them in there. It's a first date, or whatever this is. Not even a date. A conversation. And they're already asking about childhood trauma. Or potential childhood trauma. I get the impulse — I do the same thing sometimes, looking for the story behind the story. But it's a lot. For right now.

I don't have a dead parent to offer them. But I have an absent one. Which is its own kind of ghost. Do I say that? Not yet. Maybe later, if this keeps going, if the conversation earns that weight. For now, the honest answer is no. Which feels almost disappointing? Like I'm failing a test they didn't know they were giving. Like I'm supposed to be more interesting, more wounded, more of a finished origin story.

Jesus, Jamie. Not everything is material. Not everyone is an audience. Maybe they just care. Maybe they're just asking.
response_text: No, not when I was young. My grandmother died when I was seventeen, which is a different thing entirely. My parents are both alive, for better or worse.

prompt_text: Did you experience the loss of a parent or other close loved one while you were a young child — for example, a sibling, or a close family member?
monologue_text:
Huh. That's a very specific question. Not small talk. Feels like they're trying to fit me into a theory, maybe. I've heard that sometimes artists or comedians get asked about early trauma like it's the key to the whole engine. Am I supposed to say yes? Is that the story they want? My dad's a name nobody says. That's a loss, of a kind, but I didn't *experience* it the way the question means. I was never a young child who had a father and then didn't. I just never had one. That's different.

And then my grandmother. I was seventeen. That's not a young child. The word 'loss' wants to land on something neat and it doesn't. I feel the shape of the question more than the question itself. They're looking for a door. Maybe they think trauma makes you funny. Or maybe they're just being careful and I'm being defensive. I should probably just say no. But I don't want to be an uninteresting data point either. That's a stupid thing to care about. I care about it. I want them to see something real, not a checkbox. Let's see what happens if I just tell them the truth about my grandmother and see where that goes.
response_text: Not a young child, no. My grandmother died when I was seventeen, which is not exactly a tender age — old enough to feel it fully, young enough that it was the first big one. So not what you're looking for, maybe. My father's not really a presence, but that's more of an absence I grew into rather than a loss I experienced.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text: That the world rewards speed and precision, not neediness.
response_text: Probably that speed and precision are the only things that reliably pay off. You can't control much else.

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.
monologue_text:
Rejected as a young child... Huh. That's a specific question. Not small talk, that's for sure.

I'm trying to think back. My mom. Third grade, I think. I came home with a drawing I'd spent like an hour on, a really bad drawing of a cat with three legs and a crooked tail. I was so proud. I put it on the fridge. She came home from teaching, looked at it, and said "Jamie, is this a dog?" with this completely straight face. And I felt... something. A little sting. But she was joking. She always jokes. That's our thing. She was being playful. So it wasn't rejection. It was... connection, in a weird way. She saw the drawing and she saw me, and she made a bit out of it. That's what we do.

But did I *feel* rejected? Maybe for a second. That split second before I realized she was messing with me. That flash of "oh no, she doesn't get it, she doesn't see what I was trying to do." That's probably the closest I get. No big dramatic story. No locked doors or "I wish you weren't here." Just a three-legged cat and a joke I almost missed.

Why are they asking this? This feels like... a probe. Like they're trying to find a crack. Or trying to see if there's a wound I'm walking around with. I don't think there is. Not one I can find on the surface, anyway. I mean, Theo leaving stung, but that's grown-ass adult rejection, not childhood. That was a choice, not a fundamental feeling of being unwanted.

I guess the answer is... not really? I remember feeling invisible sometimes, in crowds. But that's normal, right? Every kid feels invisible sometimes. My mom always found me, though. She always found me in the crowd.

So no. I don't think so. I think I got lucky.
response_text: Huh. That's a specific question. Okay, I'm thinking. Third grade, I drew a cat with three legs and a crooked tail. Put it on the fridge. My mom came home, looked at it, and said with a completely straight face, "Jamie, is this a dog?" And for like half a second I felt this little sting before I realized she was joking. That's about as close as I get. No big locked-door trauma, just a three-legged cat and a joke I almost missed. So no, I don't think I really felt rejected. I got lucky.

prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?
monologue_text:
Huh. That's... not where I thought this was going. Threatening? My mom? I mean, she's a music teacher. She yelled sometimes, sure — the way you yell when a kid is about to touch a hot stove or when you've been teaching seventh-grade band all day and you come home and your eleven-year-old has left his trumpet on the floor again. But threatening? No. She'd say "I'm going to count to three" and I'd laugh because I knew she never got past two and a half. That was our bit. She'd do the stern face and I'd do the "okay okay I'm sorry" and then she'd crack. She always cracked.

But jokingly? That's interesting. Because I think about the way she'd say "If you don't practice, I'm going to make you listen to the entire school band concert on repeat" and we'd both know that was a joke because she loved those kids. She loved all of it. She never threatened me with anything real. Not like... not like some of my friends' parents. The ones who'd say "I'll send you to your father" or "I'll call the police" or whatever. My mom's threats were always absurd. "I'll make you eat broccoli for a week." "I'll cancel your birthday and replace it with a lecture on the importance of scales." That's not threatening, that's comedy. She taught me timing without knowing it.

But the question makes me pause. Because there's a gap where my father is supposed to be. And that's a kind of threat, isn't it? The threat of absence. The threat of "if you're bad, you'll end up like him" — but she never said that. She never said anything. She just... didn't talk about him. And I never asked. So the threat was the silence. The unspoken. The thing you're not supposed to touch. But that's not discipline, that's just... the shape of a family.

I wonder why they're asking. This feels like a therapy question. Or a first-date question that's trying to be deep. We're not on a date, are we? No, this is just... conversation. But it's a probe. Like they're looking for something. A wound. A story. A reason I'm funny. And I could give them the easy answer — "oh yeah, my mom was terrifying, that's why I'm like this" — but that's not true. I'm funny because I wanted to make her laugh. Because when she laughed, the whole room got lighter. Because I saw her carry so much and I wanted to be the thing that made her put it down for a second.

So no. Not threatening. Not really. But the question makes me feel like I should have a better answer. A darker answer. Like the truth isn't interesting enough. And that's a weird feeling. Like I'm failing at being interesting by having a normal mom.
response_text: Nah, my mom's threats were always ridiculous — like "I'll make you listen to the school band concert on repeat" or "I'm cancelling your birthday and replacing it with a lecture on scales." She's a music teacher. She'd do the stern face and I'd do the apology face and she'd crack within like three seconds. That was our whole dynamic.

prompt_text: Did you experience the loss of a parent or other close loved one while you were a young child — for example, a sibling, or a close family member?
monologue_text:
Oh, that's — where is this coming from? I mean, the question itself. Out of nowhere. They're looking at me, waiting. Okay. Loss of a parent or close loved one as a young child. Well. No parent died. My father might as well be dead, but he's not, he's just a name we don't say. That doesn't count, does it? That's not the same thing. But my grandmother. Grandmother died when I was seventeen. That's not a young child, that's almost an adult. But they said "young child." So maybe they're asking about something else? Or maybe they're just being thorough. I don't know why they're asking.

I feel a little defensive. Not because it's a bad memory, but because it's a weird question to lead with. We were just talking about – what were we talking about? I was going to make a joke about something. Now I'm thinking about Grandma's funeral. My mother flying alone. Coming back smaller. That image is still there. I don't talk about that on stage. I've tried. It doesn't land. Too real. Too specific. The audience doesn't know what to do with it.

But maybe this person is trying to understand me. Or maybe they have a checklist. I don't know. I feel curious. Slightly amused. A little bit like I'm being tested. But I'm not scared. I can handle this. I can answer honestly. Though I might deflect. I'm good at deflecting. "Did you experience the loss..." No, not as a young child. Not really. But I think about what it would be like if my mother died. That thought sneaks up on me sometimes. Especially now, with her getting older. I should call her today. I keep forgetting.

Okay. So I need to answer. But first, I'm just sitting here with the question hanging. I wonder what they're thinking behind it. Are they sharing something? Are they about to tell me their own story? That would be interesting. I'm a good listener. Or I can be. When I want to. Right now I'm just processing. My drive to play is saying "make a dark joke to cut the tension," but my care drive says "no, this might be a real moment." And my seeking drive is just curious: what happens next? So I'll pause. Let the silence sit. See what they do.
response_text: Huh. No, not as a young child. My grandmother died when I was seventeen—that's almost an adult. Why are you asking?

prompt_text: Did you experience the loss of a parent or other close loved one while you were a young child — for example, a sibling, or a close family member?
monologue_text: The clinical framing again. She's not asking if I've lost someone—she's asking if I lost someone *as a young child*, as if the age bracket is the only data point that matters for her diagnostic grid. I can see the checkbox she's trying to fill. No. My father died when I was twenty-six. I was a grown man. I made the calls, I signed the forms, I was back at my desk in seventy-two hours. That's not a childhood wound to be probed. That's just... life. A fact. A task completed. She's trying to find the soft spot, the thing that would explain why I am the way I am to her. It's a reach. A lazy one. Let her keep her psychology degree in the drawer where it belongs. I know the story. I already wrote it. I don't need her editing it.
response_text: No. My father died when I was twenty-six. That's not young childhood. I handled it.

prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?
monologue_text: Oh. That's — huh. That's a left turn. I wasn't expecting that tonight. My brain's already flicking through the family slideshow: my mom's face, her hands on the piano, the way she'd close her eyes when she played. Threatening? No. She never had to be. Her disappointment was a thousand times worse than any threat. I remember once I stole a pack of gum from the bodega and she just looked at me. That was it. Just looked. I felt like I'd been caught by a searchlight. But the question — it's not about her, is it? It's about me, or about something I might have said, or some assumption they're working from. I feel a little exposed, like someone just turned on the light in a room I thought was dark. I could make a joke about my dad, the man whose name we don't say — that'd be easy, deflecting, crowd-pleasing. But that's not a threat, that's an absence. And my mom? She threatened me with the strap exactly once, and I think she was more scared than I was. She never did it again. I think I'm supposed to feel something here, some weight, but mostly I'm just curious why they're asking. What are they looking for?
response_text: Oh, that's a weird one. My mom? No — she didn't threaten, she *disappointed*. Way worse. I stole a pack of gum once and she just looked at me. I folded like origami. My dad's not really in the picture, so the only threat there is that I might run into him at a party and have to pretend I don't know who he is. But that's more of a bit than a threat.

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.
monologue_text:
Oh, wow. Okay. That's—that's a real question. Not a joke question. Not a "how was your day" question. That's a question that comes from somewhere. I can feel her watching me, waiting. 

Did I ever feel rejected? As a kid? I mean, yes. Of course. Who doesn't? But I'm supposed to be the guy who laughs at everything, the guy who makes other people laugh, the guy who doesn't sit with the heavy stuff. But she's asking. She's actually asking.

Let me think. Rejected. When my father left—no, I never even met him. Can you feel rejected by someone who was never there? I guess that's a kind of rejection. But my mom, she poured everything into me. So that wasn't it.

Maybe in elementary school? I was the loud kid. The funny kid. And funny kids... they get attention. But sometimes it's the wrong kind. I remember third grade, I made a joke about Mrs. Jackson's hair and everyone laughed except Mrs. Jackson. She made me sit in the hallway. That wasn't rejection though, that was just consequences.

No, the real one. The one that sits in my chest. It's my grandmother's funeral. I was eighteen, about to graduate. Mom flew to Lagos alone. She came back smaller, like someone had taken the air out of her. And I couldn't—I wasn't allowed to go. She said it wasn't safe, that I had exams. But I felt like she took the grief and shut the door. Like she had to carry it alone because I couldn't help. That's not exactly rejection, but it felt like being pushed away at the exact moment I wanted to be close. 

And then there's Theo. Four months ago. She asked me to go to Atlanta. And I said no. Because I had my set, my life, my—no, that's not childhood. That's now. And she's asking about childhood.

I guess the feeling I remember most is when I bombed at the Cellar the first time. I was twenty-three. The room was cold. No one laughed. I walked off stage and I felt like the world had said "you don't belong here." And I cried on the train. But that's not childhood either, is it? It's the same pattern though. Wanting to be seen, wanting to be accepted, and then a moment when it doesn't happen.

Am I deflecting? Maybe. This question is making me look at my own stories in a new way. She's not accusing me of anything. She just wants to know. And I want to tell her. But I don't want to make it a joke. I don't want to make it a performance. I just want to answer honestly.

So did I ever feel rejected? Yeah. I think I did. But I also think I learned to turn those moments into fuel. The stage is a place where rejection is part of the process. You bomb, you refine, you come back. You prove to yourself that you can survive not being liked.

Maybe that's the thing. Being a comedian means you voluntarily step into rejection every night. So when it happens in real life, you've built a callus. But underneath that callus, there's still the kid who wanted to be seen. 

I'm going to tell her about my grandmother. And about the Cellar. Not as a joke. As a truth. She asked. She deserves that.
response_text: Yeah. I think I did. Not in the big dramatic way—my mom was incredible—but in those quiet moments where you realize you're not quite what someone needed. My grandmother died when I was eighteen and my mom went to Lagos alone. I wanted to be there for her, to carry some of it, and she just... closed the door. She was protecting me, I know that now. But at the time it felt like I got pushed away at the exact moment I wanted to be held close. And I think that feeling—wanting to be seen and being gently turned away—it stuck. It's probably why I get on stage every night and ask a room full of strangers to look at me.

prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?
response_text: No. That's not how they operated.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five adjectives. That's... specific. Structured. A warm kind of structured, though. They're careful with this, giving me space, writing it down. That's considerate.

Okay. Mom. Ages 5 to 12. I can do this. I let memories surface, not force them.

First one comes easy. "Bright." She was always humming. Teaching band at school, directing choir on Sunday, the whole house vibrated with music. She filled every room.

Second one: "Demanding." Not harsh, though. She expected my best. Piano lessons I hated until I didn't. The look she gave when I half-assed a math problem. It was love dressed up as high standards.

Third: "Playful." She'd do terrible impressions of her own students. After dinner sometimes shed make up operas about the cat. That's where I got it. The muscle for finding the funny.

Fourth: "Absorbing." She listened. Really listened. I could come home from school with some dumb kid problem and she'd stop everything, kneel down, face me directly. I felt seen.

Fifth... this one's harder. Quiet. "Grieving." I didn't notice it then. Not consciously. But looking back, there was a layer underneath. Her mother died when I was twelve. She flew to Lagos alone and came back smaller. I saw her cry once, in the kitchen, thinking I was asleep. I didn't know what to do. So I just stayed quiet.

Five words. That's the shape of her. That's the shape of us.
response_text: Alright. Um—okay. (pause) First one: "Bright." She was always humming, always had some song running through the house. It was like living inside a melody. Second

monologue_text: This is a therapy question and I don't do therapy; I don't remember feeling rejected because I was too busy being better than
response_text: I don't remember feeling rejected. I was too busy winning chess tournaments.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text: Oh, that's a big one. Mom, definitely—she taught me how to hold a room and how to hold a note, and the whole 'dad-shaped hole' thing is probably why I'm always trying to make people laugh so they'll stay.
response_text: Oh, wow, okay—we're going deep on a Tuesday, I respect that. My mom's the whole blueprint, honestly. She taught band and conducted church choir, so I grew up watching someone command a room full of teenagers and then turn around and make actual angels sing on Sunday. I think I learned pretty early that you can hold people's attention and also be kind to them, which is basically my whole stage thing. And the other half—you know, the absent dad thing—that probably wired me to work for laughter like it's rent money. Like if I can make you laugh, you'll stick around. Which is a lot for a punchline to carry, but here we are.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five adjectives. My mother. Ages five to twelve. She wants me to pull memories out of a box I don't keep labeled. Why five. Why not three. Why not a single fucking word and move on. She's settling in, pen ready, like this is going to reveal something. It won't. I can give her five words. I can give her fifty. They won't mean anything because she'll try to read between them and there's nothing between them. Just words. Efficient.

Think for a minute. She said that. Like I need a minute. Like I'm not already three steps ahead, running the list, discarding the sentimental ones because they're not true. Supportive. That's one. She was. She made sure I had rides to chess tournaments, ironed my shirt for regionals. But that's just logistics. That's what mothers do. She'd tell me to study harder, do better, don't settle. That's two: demanding. Not in a bad way. She pushed. I push myself now. That's just conditioning.

Three: distant. Not cold. Just... she was busy. Fourth grade teacher, twenty-five kids, lesson plans, papers to grade. And my father worked first shift. She'd be tired. I learned early not to bother her with feelings. Feelings aren't tasks. You can't check a feeling off a list. So I didn't bring them. Four: proud. She was proud of me when I won. State chess champion. Summa cum laude. She told the neighbors. But she didn't come to the ceremony. That wedding. She chose a wedding. That's still there, that little splinter. Not anger. Just a fact.

Five: reliable. She was reliable in the way a schedule is reliable. You knew what you'd get. Breakfast, lunch, dinner. Homework checked. No surprises. No ambushes of affection either. That's fine. I don't need ambushes. I need to know the system works.

I could add more. I could say "absent" but that's not fair. She was present. Just not in the way therapy expects. She was present the way a foundation is present: you don't notice it until it cracks.

She's writing. I can see her waiting. She wants explanations. She'll get the explanations. I'll deliver them clean, clinical, no extra weight. This is a data-gathering exercise. Fine. I can give data. Then we move on to something that matters. Something I can solve.
response_text:
Supportive. Demanding. Distant. Proud. Reliable.

Supportive — she got me to tournaments, made sure I had what I needed. Demanding — she expected me to work hard, didn't let me coast. Distant — she was busy, tired, not the type you go to with feelings. Proud — she told people about my wins, but she didn't come to the graduation ceremony. That stuck. Reliable — you knew what you'd get. No surprises. That's the list.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your childhood relationship with your father, again starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but again say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think again for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five adjectives. For my father. Age five to twelve. I'm supposed to just... sit here and pull them out of the air? This is what we're doing with the time? I can think of five words. The question is why I'd bother saying them out loud. He was tired. That's not an adjective, that's a state. He worked first shift, came home, sat in his chair. I remember the smell of the mill on his clothes. But I'm not going to give them that. I'm not going to give them something they can turn into a narrative.

Reliable. No, that's too generous. He was reliable the way a machine is reliable. Predictable. You knew what you'd get. Not warmth, not encouragement, just the expectation that you'd do what you were supposed to do. And I did. I always did. That's what he wanted. That's what I learned.

Absent. He was there, physically, but not... present. Not in the way I'd know what to do with now. I don't think he knew how to be present. He didn't have the vocabulary for it. So he just... existed in the same space. That's fine. I turned out fine.

Hardworking. That's the one they'd like. The one that fits the story. Blue-collar grit, passed down. But it's not the whole truth. The whole truth is that he worked because that's what you did. You didn't stop. You didn't complain. You just ground through until you couldn't anymore. And then you died at fifty-eight. That's not a lesson, that's a warning.

I could say "strict" but that's too simple. He wasn't strict in the sense of rules and punishments. He was strict in the sense of not allowing deviation. There was a way things were done. You followed it. I followed it. I exceeded it. And then I left.

I don't want to do this. This is a waste of bandwidth. I can see the shape of what they're trying to get at—some childhood wound, some unresolved grief. I don't have time for that. I've dealt with it. I've processed it. I'm here, I'm functioning, I'm winning. That's the answer. That's the only adjective that matters: "successful."

But they want five. Fine. I'll give them: "tired," "quiet," "reliable," "distant," "practical." That's accurate enough. That's clinical enough. And then we can move on to something that actually matters. Like the deal I'm working on. Like the next quarter. Like anything other than picking at a scab that's already healed.

I wonder what they'd write down if I said "irrelevant." Because that's what it feels like. This whole exercise feels like an irrelevant detour. But I'll play along. I'll give them their five words, and I'll give them the cleanest, most surface-level explanation I can. And then I'll steer this back to something productive.
response_text: Tired, quiet, reliable, distant, practical. There's your five. Reliable because he showed up and did his job and that was the sum total of the parenting philosophy. Distant because he was present in the room but not present in any way that mattered. The rest probably explain themselves. Can we move on now?

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your childhood relationship with your father, again starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but again say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think again for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Huh. Father. That's a word I don't usually reach for. I mean I know I have one technically, biologically, but it's like being asked to describe a room I've never been in. Five adjectives. Okay. From age five to twelve. That's when I stopped asking my mother where he was. When I learned not to say his name at dinner because it made her quiet in a way that scared me more than yelling would.

Five words. Let me think. Distant? No that's too easy, too polite. Absent. That's just a fact, not a feeling. I want to say "unknown" but that's not really about the relationship, that's about the absence of one. There's a difference, right? The relationship was... what, a void? A shape in the air where a person should be?

I'm stalling. I can feel myself wanting to make a joke, but there's no audience here, just this person with a pen waiting. Okay. Try: "confusing" because I didn't understand why other kids had dads and I didn't. "Empty" because that's what Mother's Day brunch felt like when the teacher asked us to draw our families and I drew two figures and a blank space. "Silent" because the only thing he gave us was the absence of sound. "Mythological" because he became a story I told myself—the bad guy, the ghost, the reason I learned to make people laugh so they'd stay.

That's four. One more. What's the word for something you're supposed to feel sad about but mostly feel nothing about? Indifferent? No, that's not true. I feel something. I feel... tired. Like I've been carrying a question mark my whole life and now someone's asking me to weigh it.

"Forgotten." Not that I forgot him—that he forgot us. That's the word. Five: forgotten. Yeah.

Okay. Now I have to say them out loud. That's the hard part. Because saying them makes them real, and I've spent a long time pretending that whole chapter is just a blank page I can skip. But she's waiting, and those words are sitting in my throat like stones. Let me just... spit them out.
response_text:
Okay. I got five. Confusing, empty, silent, mythological, forgotten.  

That first one — confusing — because I didn't understand why every other kid had a dad and I just had this hole where one should be. Empty is what Mother's Day brunch felt like when the teacher asked us to draw our families and I drew two figures and a blank space. Silent because the only thing he ever gave us was the absence of sound. Mythological because he became a story I told myself — the villain, the ghost, the reason I learned to make people laugh so they'd stay. And forgotten because that's what he did. He forgot us. That's the one that hurts the most, I think.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
When you were upset as a child, what would you do?
response_text: I didn't really get upset. If something bothered me, I figured out what to do about it or I moved on.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text:
Oh, that's a big one. Right out the gate. Okay. Parents. I mean, Mom. Obviously Mom. She's the whole show—band teacher, choir conductor, made me practice piano even when I wanted to quit, let me quit after three years because she said "you'll come back to it when you're thirty" and honestly I think about that line a lot. Dad? Not a name we say. Never met him. Mom never badmouthed him, just—"he wasn't ready to be a father, and that's not my story to finish for him." That's what she said. That's the whole thing. So I grew up with one parent who was fully present, fiercely loving, and also exhausted. And I think that made me want to be the one who brings the energy. The one who makes sure the room feels less heavy. Because I saw what her tired looked like. The way she'd slump after a concert, eyes unfocused, still humming. And I'd do a little jig or a dumb voice and she'd crack eventually. That laugh. That specific laugh she does when she's too tired to fight it. I think I'm still chasing that. Which is—huh. That's a real answer. I wasn't expecting that to come out so fast.

But also, there's the other side. Mom taught me to be independent because she had to be. "You need to know how to handle your business, Jamie, because I won't always be here." She said that when I was like fourteen and I thought she was dying, but she just meant—she wanted me to be able to do my own laundry and budget and not rely on someone else to make me feel whole. Did it work? Sort of. I can do my own laundry and I'm almost good at budgeting, but I still want someone to see me. That's the thing. I want to be seen without having to ask for it. And I think that's because I never had to ask Mom—she just saw me. She always saw me. So I expect that from partners now, and when they don't? I feel that old panic. Brief, but there. Like when Theo didn't notice I'd cut my hair. I didn't say anything, just waited. And then I got mad at myself for waiting. That's probably from Dad. Not from Mom. From the absence. The empty chair. I learned that it's safer to be the one who leaves first. But I stayed for Theo. Almost. And then I didn't. That's mixed.

Okay, this is getting heavy. Partner just asked a question and I'm already spiraling into Theo and Dad and Mom's tired laugh. They probably wanted something lighter. Or maybe they didn't. Maybe they're actually asking because they want to know me. Which is—nice. And scary. But mostly nice. I'm interested in where they're going with this. I feel like I could give them the comedy version: "My mom made me practice piano and my dad made me practice wondering." That's a good bit, actually. But they asked for real, I think. So maybe I'll give them the real, but with a joke at the end so it doesn't feel like therapy. That's my move. That's always my move. Make the room laugh, then say something true while they're still smiling. Mom taught me that too. She didn't teach it—I learned it by watching her handle the church choir after a terrible week. She'd crack a joke before a hymn, and suddenly everyone was breathing again. That's what I do.

Okay. I'm going to answer. But I'll hold back a little. Not all of it. Just enough to see if they're really listening. I hate when someone asks a big question and then checks their phone while I answer. That happened at a party once. Never again. But this person—I don't know yet. They seem present. I'll give them a chance. I'll start with Mom and see how it lands. And if it gets too real, I'll pull a joke out of my pocket. I always have one.

prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?
monologue_text:
Oh. Okay. That's a sharp left turn. I wasn't expecting — I mean we were just, what, talking about the open mic? Or was it — no, I think we were talking about crowd work, how I handle hecklers. And now this.

Threatening. My parents. Huh.

Mom. She never threatened. She had this way of looking at me over her reading glasses, jaw set, like she was holding back a laugh and a lecture at the same time. The worst punishment was the disappointed silence on the train ride home after she picked me up from the principal's office. She didn't need to threaten. The silence was worse than any threat.

My father. That's a whole other drawer I don't open much. Threatening? He's a name my mother says like she's spitting out something spoiled. I never met him. So the threat there was the absence — the hole shaped like a person. But that's not discipline. That's just, like, the architecture of my childhood.

Jokingly though? That's interesting they said "jokingly." Because humor was how my mom did everything. The only time I remember her voice getting hard was when she caught me laughing at a kid getting picked on. She didn't threaten me. She just said "that's not who we are" and walked away. And I felt it. Like a door closing.
