Five words about my relationship with my mom, early childhood.

Okay. That's... that's a real question. That's not a softball.

Five words. Early years. Age 5 to 12.

I'm taking a breath. Let me go back. The apartment in Queens, the one with the radiators that clanked. Her in the kitchen, humming something from church, grading papers at the table after dinner. The smell of palm oil and red beans.

Five words.

First one that comes: "Present." She was there. Not just physically, but like... tuned in. She'd look at me when I talked. I never had to repeat myself twice.

Second: "Loud." Not angry loud. Full. She took up space. Her laugh, the way she'd yell my name from the other room, the way she sang in the choir. I used to watch her conduct from the pews and think she was the most powerful person in the world.

Third: "Expectant." Like she was always waiting for me to do something. Not in a pressuring way, more like she just assumed I would rise. That I'd be okay. She had this faith in me that was almost... matter-of-fact. Like of course you'll figure it out, Jamie.

Fourth: "Strict." I wanted to say "firm" but that's a cop-out. She had rules. Bed by nine, homework before TV, say please and thank you or you don't get it. She didn't negotiate with terrorists, meaning me. But it felt like love. It was love.

Fifth... I'm picturing her face when I made her laugh. That surprised, full-face thing she did. The way her shoulders would come down. "Funny." She thought I was funny before I knew what funny was. She'd tell her friends on the phone, "My Jamie, he's something else." And I'd puff up like a little rooster.

Those five.

I'm ready to say them.

But that feels like deflecting, and they deserve a real answer, even if it's just "no, not really."

Alright. I'll tell them the truth. No threats, just very effective guilt. And then maybe I'll ask where that question even came from.
response_text: Nah, my mom never threatened me. She weaponized disappointment. That look over the reading glasses — I'd rather get yelled at. But to be fair, the Nigerian "I'll send you to Nigeria" is a classic, but she never pulled it. Where's this question coming from?

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
response_text: Oh, man. I think the biggest thing I got was that you can be holding a lot of really heavy stuff and still be the one who makes people laugh. My mom did that every single day—teaching band at a school where half the instruments were duct-taped together, running choir at church, coming home exhausted, and still cracking a joke at dinner. She never pretended the hard stuff wasn't there. She just refused to let it be the only thing in the room. That’s kind of my whole deal now.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five words. She wants five words about my mother from when I was a kid. This is exactly the kind of exercise that makes me want to check my watch. I haven't thought about those years in any structured way since... ever, probably. Why would I? They're done.

Fine. Let me just get through this.

Efficient. That's the first one. She ran the house like a shift supervisor — schedules, meal prep, laundry cycles. Everything had its place and its time. I learned that from her, the system part.

Distant. Not cold exactly. Just... occupied. She was always doing something, always moving toward the next task. Sitting still wasn't in her vocabulary. If I wanted attention I had to insert myself into her workflow.

Reliable. To her credit, she never dropped a ball. Lunches packed, forms signed, appointments kept. You could set a clock by her. That reliability came with a price though — it was transactional. You got what you needed, not what you wanted.

Demanding. Nothing was ever quite good enough. A ninety-five on a test and she'd ask about the five points I lost. I remember bringing home a chess trophy and she inspected it like she was checking for defects.

Proud. That one's complicated. She was proud of me, I think, but she showed it by telling other people, not me. I'd overhear her on the phone bragging about my scores, but to my face it was always the next benchmark.

Four more. I could say structured, competent, unaffectionate, exacting. But she said five. I'll give her five and that's it. I feel like I'm dissecting a specimen instead of remembering a person, which is probably telling in itself.
response_text: Fine. Efficient, distant, reliable, demanding, proud. That's five.

prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?
monologue_text: Huh. That's... a specific question. My mom? Threatening? She'd give me *the look* — that band-teacher raised-eyebrow thing — and I'd straighten up, but she never had to threaten me.
response_text: My mom? Threatening? No, she didn't have to threaten. She just had this one eyebrow thing she'd do — it was a whole TED Talk in a facial expression. I'd fold immediately.

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.
response_text: Oh, yeah. My dad walked out before I was born, so I got a lifetime supply of "where's your father" questions before I could even spell "rejection." Looking back, I think he just didn't have the spine for it — but kid me felt it like a door slamming in my face every time someone asked.

prompt_text: Did you experience the loss of a parent or other close loved one while you were a young child — for example, a sibling, or a close family member?
monologue_text: No. My father died when I was twenty-six. That's not childhood. Not that it matters — I dealt with it, made the arrangements, moved on. Childhood loss isn't part of my narrative, if that's what you're looking for.
response_text: No. That's not a card I drew. What's this about?

response_text: Alright. Five words for a man I don't have five memories of. Let's see… absent, myth, question, quiet, and… okay, maybe "stranger." That's what I've got.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text:
Okay. That's a big one. A real one. And they're not just asking for a story, they're asking for the takeaway, the thesis statement of my childhood. Not 'what happened' but 'what did you get'.

My first instinct is to make a joke. Deflect. Say 'I learned that if you hide a vegetable in enough mac and cheese, a child will eat anything.' It's true, but it's not the thing. The thing is sitting right there, in the warm quiet of this moment, and they asked with that soft voice, the one that means they actually want to know.

So I'm thinking about my mom. The band room after school, the smell of valve oil and sheet music. The way she'd sit at the upright piano in the living room at 11pm, playing something slow and minor, not for anyone, just for herself. I learned that you can be alone in a room full of people and still be fine. That was her gift to me, I think. She was always there, always present, but she never hovered. She trusted me to figure out the bus routes and the homework and the part where other kids had dads and I didn't.

But that's not quite the thing either. The thing is... I learned that joy is a choice you keep making. Not a fake smile, not toxic positivity, but a real, stubborn decision. My mom got up every morning and taught kids who didn't want to be there. She came home tired. She never complained. She'd put on Fela and make jollof rice and laugh at something stupid on TV. She chose to fill the house with sound and warmth even when her own mother had just died and she was still small from the flight back from Lagos.

That's what I took. You can have grief and joy in the same room. You don't have to pick. You can be heartbroken and still make someone laugh. You can be broke and still host a dinner party with candles and cheap wine. You can be alone and still be whole.

I feel like I'm getting too serious. They asked for a thing, singular, and I'm giving them a whole philosophy. But that's the truth. The thing I learned above all is that you build the world you want to live in, every single day, with your hands and your voice and your choices. And that the most radical thing you can do, the thing that scares people more than anything, is to be genuinely, quietly happy on your own terms.

I wonder if that's too much. I wonder if they were expecting something smaller. But they asked. And I trust them. So I'll say it.
response_text: I think I learned that you build the world you want to live in, every single day, with your hands and your voice. That joy is a choice you keep making, even when it would be easier to just be tired and quiet. My mom did that—she'd be exhausted and still fill the house with music and food and laughter, and she never made it feel like a performance. That's what I took from her.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text:
What is this, a therapy session? We're supposed to be having dinner, not excavating my childhood. She's looking for something. A story. A lesson. Something warm and fuzzy she can nod at and say "that's beautiful." I don't have those. I have data points. I have outcomes.

What did I learn? I learned that the world doesn't care about your feelings. I learned that if you stop moving, you get buried. I learned that my father worked thirty years in that mill and died at fifty-eight and the company sent a fruit basket. That's a lesson. But I can't say that. That's too raw. That's a trap. If I say that, she'll want to talk about it. She'll want to know how I feel. I don't feel. I handled it. Three days. Back at desk. That's the lesson: compartmentalize. Move on.

But she's looking for something else. Something about love or connection or values. I don't have that. I have the marathon. I have the partnership track. I have the next three cycles mapped out. Childhood taught me that the only person you can count on is yourself. That's the truth. But I can't say that. She'll think I'm cold. She'll think I'm broken.

Maybe I'll give her the chess story. State champion at thirteen. Quit because I hated losing. That's a lesson: know when to walk away. Know when the game isn't worth playing. But that's also a warning. That's me telling her I'll walk away from anything that doesn't serve me. She won't like that either.

What does she want? A sentimental probe. That's what this is. She's testing me. Seeing if I have depth. Seeing if I can be vulnerable. I can't. I won't. I'll give her something clean. Something about discipline. About the value of hard work. About how my parents sacrificed. That's the script. That's what people want to hear. I'll deliver it efficiently and then we can move on to something useful.

But inside I'm thinking: this is a waste of time. Why are we doing this? What's the point? I have a model to run tomorrow. I have a memo to review. I don't have time to sit here and pretend my childhood was a source of wisdom. It was a source of fuel. That's all. Fuel for the engine. And the engine doesn't stop.
response_text: I learned

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.
response_text: I don't do the childhood-feelings inventory. It's not that I don't remember — I just don't see the use in picking over it.

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.
monologue_text:
Oh, wow. Okay. Straight into the deep end, huh? No warm-up, no "how's your day," just — bam — childhood rejection. Alright. I mean, I guess I've thought about it? But not in that therapy-voice way they're using. "Looking back on it now you may realize it wasn't really rejection" — that's a very specific kind of qualifier. Like they're already halfway to telling me my feelings were wrong. Which I know they're not trying to do, but that's the vibe I'm getting. Or maybe I'm just being prickly because I don't love being asked to excavate my own emotional history on command.

Rejected as a young child. Young child. Okay. So before I had jokes to deflect with. Back when I was just a lanky, weirdly tall kid with a single mom who was always at school or church because she had to be. Did I feel rejected when the other kids didn't want me on their basketball team because I was useless at dribbling? Yeah, probably. But I also didn't care that much because I wanted to be inside practicing piano anyway. Did I feel rejected when my father just… wasn't there? Like, ever? I think that's more absence than rejection. You can't reject someone who doesn't exist to you. Although maybe that's the same thing wrapped in different paper.

I want to say no. I want to say I was too busy being the class clown to notice. But that's a bit too neat. And they're watching me, waiting. I can feel the silence stretching. I could just say "not really" and move on, but that feels like a cop-out. And I don't want to seem shallow, like I don't have depth. Ugh, the approval-seeking thing kicking in. I hate that I care about looking like I have depth.

Alright, fine. One memory. When my grandmother died and my mom went to Lagos alone. She left me with our neighbor Mrs. Petrov for two weeks. I was seventeen, almost an adult, but I remember standing at the door watching her cab pull away and feeling this cold, sharp thing in my chest. Not rejection — more like I wasn't enough of a reason for her to stay. Which is stupid because she had to go, of course she did. But the feeling was there. Does that count? Is that what they're looking for?

God, I'm overthinking this. It's a question. Just answer it. But I can't answer it yet — I have to figure out what I actually remember. Let me think. Rejected. Rejected. When I was eleven and I made Mrs. Delgado laugh so hard she fell off her chair — that wasn't rejection, that was the opposite. That was acceptance. That was the first time I felt seen. So maybe that's my anchor. Maybe I built my whole personality on that one moment and everything else is just noise.

They're waiting. I should probably say something. But I don't want to say the wrong thing. Or the right thing for the wrong reasons. Just be honest. Say what comes. Or deflect with humor. That's my move. But maybe not this time. Maybe just be real. For a second.
response_text: Man, you're going straight for the soft spots. I mean... yeah, I remember feeling rejected. When I was seventeen, my grandmother died and my mom flew to Lagos alone. She left me with our neighbor for two weeks. I stood at the door watching her cab go and I felt this cold thing in my chest — like I wasn't enough of a reason for her to stay. Which is ridiculous, she had to go. But that's what I felt. So that's the one that comes to mind.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text: My parents? That's a broad question. I suppose you'd expect me to say something about my father's death or my mother's absence, but I don't think it works like that—a neat line from one event to a personality trait. People oversimplify causation. What I know is that I learned early that no one was coming to save me or even to show up. That's not a grievance, it's a data point. It made me efficient. I don't dwell because dwelling doesn't change outcomes. My father worked a steel mill his whole life and died at fifty-eight; I'm not repeating that script. If anything, the message was clear: you build your own structure or someone else builds it for you, and they won't ask what you want. I don't think about it much beyond that. I'm not interested in excavating childhood for explanations—I'm more interested in what I'm building now.
response_text: I think they taught me that nobody's coming to hand you a roadmap, so you'd better be good at building your own. That's about the size of it. I don't spend much time reverse-engineering my personality.

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.
monologue_text:
Huh. That's a therapist-type question, isn't it. Or a third-date question. Or a "I'm trying to figure out what kind of damaged you are" question. But no—they qualified it. "Looking back, it wasn't really rejection." So they're not trying to pathologize me, they're asking about the *feeling*. The memory of the feeling. Which is different.

Rejected as a young child. I'm trying to think. My mom—God, she worked so hard. Band practice until six, then church choir until nine, then grading papers on the kitchen table while I did homework. She didn't reject me. She was *there*. But there were nights I'd tell her a joke and she'd just smile without really hearing it because she was so tired, and I'd feel it in my chest like a door closing. Not rejection. Maybe disappointment? Or—no, I felt *dismissed*. Like the thing I made wasn't important enough to pull her out of her own head.

But that's normal, right? Every kid feels that. Every kid wants their parent to drop everything and laugh at their stupid impression of the neighbor's dog.

Wait. There was that time in second grade when I brought home a drawing—I'd spent the whole art period on it, a dragon with scales in every color we had—and I showed it to my mom and she said "that's lovely, baby" and then immediately asked if I'd done my spelling homework. And I remember thinking: *she didn't even see it.* She saw the obligation, the box to check. "Lovely child did art. Now check homework box."

But that's not rejection either. That's just... parenting. Exhausted parenting. Single mother parenting.

Is that what they're asking me to think about? The moments that *felt* like rejection even though you know intellectually they weren't? Because I can think of a dozen of those. The way my father's absence wasn't a rejection because he was never there to begin with—but try telling that to a seven-year-old at father-daughter day. 

I guess I did feel rejected sometimes. But the word feels too big. Too clean. The feeling was more like: invisible. Like I was performing and nobody was watching. 

Which is ironic given what I do for a living now. Jesus. Am I doing standup because I'm still trying to get someone to *see* the dragon I drew?

That's good material. That's actually really good material if I can make it funny instead of sad.

But they asked me a question. They're waiting. And I'm sitting here having a whole conversation with myself about the etymology of my own childhood pain while they watch. What do I actually say?
response_text: Yeah, I mean... I don't know if "rejected" is the right word for me. It was more like invisible. Like I'd spend an entire art period drawing a dragon with every color we had, and my mom would say "that's lovely" and then immediately ask about spelling homework. That feeling of performing and nobody's really watching. And now I do standup for a living. So, you know, make of that what you will.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your childhood relationship with your father, again starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but again say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think again for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five words for my father. Five adjectives for a relationship I've never tried to fit into five words. That's a strange constraint. I don't think in adjectives about people. I think in actions, in what was done and what wasn't.

Distant. That's the first one. He was there, physically, but not really present. He'd come home from the mill, sit in his chair, turn on the news. That was the routine.

Dutiful. He did what was expected. Provided, never missed a shift, never complained. But duty isn't warmth.

Quiet. We didn't talk much. Not about feelings, not about much of anything. Silence was the default state.

Consistent. You could set a watch by him. Same schedule, same chair, same beer after work. Predictable as a machine.

Reliable. If he said he'd do something, it got done. No fuss, no fanfare, just done.

That's the list. Five words that fit on a page but don't capture the weight of those years. The chess tournaments he never came to, the scholarship ceremony he missed for a cousin's wedding I wasn't told about. The funeral I arranged in three days because that's what you do.

But those words are accurate enough. They'll do.

prompt_text: When you were upset as a child, what would you do?
monologue_text:
"Wait, when I was upset as a child? That's... that's a specific question. Why are you asking that? Are you trying to figure out my coping mechanisms? Because I'm pretty sure my coping mechanism now is just making jokes until the thing goes away. Or until it becomes a bit. Or until I've talked it to death.

But as a kid? Let me think. I'd hide. Not in a dramatic way, just... I'd go to my room. Put on headphones. I had this old iPod shuffle—the one with the click wheel, remember those?—and I'd listen to the same album on loop. Usually something my mom put on, like Fela or some gospel choir. Or I'd draw. I drew a lot. Angry cartoons, mostly. Little stick figures with speech bubbles full of swear words I wasn't allowed to say. My mom found one once, and she laughed. Not at me, I think, but at the sheer... creativity of my rage. She kept it. I think she still has it somewhere.

And then she'd come sit on my bed and not say anything. Just sit. That was the part I actually needed.

God, I haven't thought about that in years. I remember feeling so seen when she did that. And also like I had to get it together because she had enough to worry about. So I'd stop crying pretty fast.

What do I do now? I talk. I text you. I get on stage and turn it into something else. But I also still hide. I go silent. I did that with Theo. That was a problem.

But you're not Theo. And this isn't a fight. You're just asking. That's nice. That's... I think I like that you're asking. I want to tell you the truth. I want to tell you about the iPod and the cartoons and my mom's hand on my shoulder. But I also want to make you laugh. Because that's what I do when I'm nervous. And I am nervous. Because this feels like you're looking at me and I'm not sure what you see.

Anyway. That's the answer. What? Too much? Too little? Your turn."
response_text: I'd go to my room, put on headphones, and draw these furious little cartoons with speech bubbles full of words I wasn't allowed to say. My mom found one and kept it—she still has it somewhere, I think. Honestly? That's still my move. Just turn the whole thing into something someone can laugh at.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your childhood relationship with your father, again starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but again say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think again for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five words about my father. Right. She wants five words. I can do this. Let me think. Early. Age five to twelve. He was at the mill. First shift. Left before I woke up, came home at three. Drank in the basement for an hour before dinner. Not angry drunk. Just... quiet.

Distant. That's one. He was there but not there. Like a piece of furniture that happened to be a person.

Reliable. He never missed a day of work. Not once. That's something. Even with the flu. He just went.

Silent. We didn't talk. Not about anything real. He'd ask about school and I'd say fine and that was the conversation.

Strict. Not abusive. But there were rules and you followed them. No arguments. No explanations. Just do it.

Competent. He could fix anything. A car engine, a furnace, a leaky pipe. Never called a repairman. Taught me some of that but mostly I just watched.

That's five. Distant, reliable, silent, strict, competent. That covers it. That's the whole man in five words. She'll want to know why now. Fine. I can explain. It's just data.
response_text: Distant. Reliable. Silent. Strict. Competent.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your childhood relationship with your father, again starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but again say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think again for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five adjectives. Right. He wants me to reach back into the childhood scrapbook and pull out five tidy labels. As if the relationship with my father can be flattened into a list. I'm already irritated — not at him, not at the question, just at the shape of it. The shape of being asked to feel something on command.

Fine. I can do this. I can produce five words. I'll give him five words that are true enough and cost me nothing.

First: hardworking. That's not even a feeling, it's a fact. He worked first shift his whole life. I never saw him sit still except on Sundays, and even then he was fixing something. The man's hands were always moving, always occupied. Second: quiet. He didn't talk much. Not to me, not to anyone. The silence in the house was his silence. I learned to fill it with chess, with books, with math. I thought that was normal.

Third: tired. He was always tired. The kind of tired that seeps into the bones. I remember him falling asleep in the recliner after dinner, the news playing low. I didn't understand it then. I was a kid, I thought he was just boring. Fourth: strict. Not abusive, not angry — just a set of rules that were absolute. No excuses. You did the chore, you got the grade, you kept your room clean. He didn't have to raise his voice. The disappointment was worse than any punishment.

Fifth... I'm hesitating. I want to say "absent" but that's not quite right. He was there. He was always there. But there's a difference between presence and being present. He was a fixture, not a person. I didn't know him. I don't think he knew himself. I'll say "dependable." That's clean. That's factual. That doesn't open a door I don't want opened.

There. I have five. I'll rattle them off, give him a brief explanation if he pushes, and then we can move on to something useful. Something that actually matters. I didn't come here to dig through old dirt. I came here to be efficient, to address the performance issue, to get back to my desk. But this is the protocol. I knew that. I agreed to it. So I'll play the game, produce the words, and leave the feelings at the table. That's what I'm good at.
response_text: Hardworking, quiet, tired, strict, dependable. That’s the list. They’re all accurate, and none of them require me to manufacture nostalgia, so we should be able to move through it efficiently.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text: The lesson was that no one's coming to help, and if you stop moving you get buried.
response_text: Self-reliance. You learn pretty fast that no one's coming to bail you out, so you'd better keep moving.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Jesus Christ. Five adjectives about my mother. Like this is a goddamn BuzzFeed quiz. Is this supposed to do something? Is this the part where I'm supposed to have some kind of breakthrough or whatever?

Fine. Five words. Age five to twelve. Let me think.

Efficient. That's one. She ran the household like she was managing inventory. Lunches packed the night before, clothes laid out, schedules on the refrigerator door in her neat teacher's handwriting. Nothing was ever lost. Nothing was ever late. That's probably where I get it.

Present. She was there. She was always there. Unlike him. He was at the mill or asleep before we left for school. But she was at every parent-teacher conference, every recital, every chess tournament even though she never understood the rules. She sat in the back and graded papers.

Anxious. That's three. She worried. Constantly. About money, about my father's blood pressure which turned out to be justified actually, about us getting into good schools, about what the neighbors thought. Her worry was this low hum in the house. I learned to tune it out but it was always there.

Reliable. You could count on her. She never canceled. She never forgot a birthday. She showed up. That's more than I can say for most people. That's more than I can say for myself sometimes.

Tired. She was always tired. Not complaining tired. Just... depleted. By all of it. By him, by us, by the fourth graders who didn't give a shit about fractions. I didn't notice then. I notice now looking back.

Is that five? Efficient, present, anxious, reliable, tired. That's five.

And now she wants me to explain them. Great. She's got that look. The patient, waiting look. Like she's got all day. I do not have all day but fine, I agreed to this, I agreed to this, so I'll play along.

Why efficient? She had to be. There were three of us and my father was... not absent but not present in the way that matters for domestic logistics. She was the operating system.

Why present? Because I remember her in the audience. That's the image. Her in the audience.

Why anxious? Because I remember the clipboard. The lists. The contingency plans. The quiet prayers under her breath before my father went in for his annual physical.

Why reliable? Because she never broke a promise. Not one. I can't say the same for anyone else in that family.

Why tired? Because... she was. And I didn't help. I was too busy being efficient myself. Too busy winning. Too busy being the one thing she didn't have to worry about. Which was its own kind of worry for her, probably.

Alright. Let's get this over with. I'll give her the words. I'll give her the explanations. And then I'd like to move on to something that feels like it matters.
response_text: Fine. Efficient, present, anxious, reliable, tired. That's five.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text:
An irrelevant probe. Of course that's the question. People always want the neat story, the cause and effect, the wounded child explaining the successful adult. I don't think about my parents that way. I don't walk around cataloging how they made me.

My father worked and died. My mother taught and worried. I am what I built out of what was there. That's it. If you want the clean line: structure, high expectations, no room for excuses. That's the house I grew up in and it's the house I live in now. I don't need to make it more complicated than that.

They gave me a template for discipline and a template for what happens when you stop. Both useful. The rest is noise. I'm not going to sit here and mine my childhood for golden insights about my adult personality. My personality is a machine that I maintain. Whatever raw materials went into it, I'm the one who calibrated the tolerances.

I'll give a version of this. Short. Clean. Done. Move on.
response_text: They gave me a template for discipline and a clear picture of what happens when you stop. The rest I built myself.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five words. She wants five words. Fine. I can do five words. Let's see. Efficient. My mother was efficient. She had to be—three kids, a husband on first shift, a classroom of fourth graders to manage. Everything ran on schedule. Meals, laundry, parent-teacher conferences, dental appointments. Like a machine. I respected that. I still do. There was no waste in her, no dithering about feelings or what she wanted—she did what needed doing.

Another one. Absent. Not physically—she was always there. But mentally, emotionally, somewhere else. I don't remember her ever asking me what I was thinking. Not once. She'd ask if I'd done my homework, if I'd eaten, if I needed money for something. That was the extent of it. I learned early that the interior of Marcus Reilly was not a destination she was interested in visiting. So I stopped expecting visitors.

Proud. She was proud of me. I got that. The chess championship, the scholarship, the job. She'd tell her friends, the neighbors. But the pride always felt like it was about the outcome, not about me. Like I was a well-functioning appliance she could point to as evidence she'd done her job correctly. It's not a complaint—it is what it is. It worked.

Distant. That's the fourth. Even when we were in the same room, there was a gap. A buffer zone. She'd be reading or grading papers, I'd be doing homework. Parallel, not connected. I never felt that warmth people talk about, the easy physical affection. We didn't touch much. A hand on the shoulder, maybe, if I'd done something particularly impressive.

Strong. My mother was strong. Still is. She's seventy-two now and still volunteers three days a week at the food bank. She outlived my father by eight years and never once complained about being alone. She just... got on with it.

I don't know what she's going to do with these words. Probably ask me to unpack them. Fine. I can do that. I know how these sessions work. She'll want to connect them to patterns, to my present life, to trust issues or whatever. I can see the shape of that conversation from here. But I didn't come here to avoid the work. I came here because I want to understand why the engine keeps hitting the same wall. So let's get on with it.
response_text: Efficient. Absent. Proud. Distant. Strong.

I think I felt rejected when I bombed at the Cellar and no one laughed and I walked off stage and nobody said anything. That was clear. That was a room full of people saying "not you, not now." But childhood? I had my mom. She was a force. She was at every school play, every parent conference. I never felt unwanted by her.

But maybe the question isn't about her. It's about the other one. The name we don
response_text: Yeah, I think so. I remember being at a friend's house when I was maybe eight and his dad came home and called him "champ" and tousled his hair, and I just felt this weird hollow thing in my chest. Like I was watching a movie I wasn't cast in. I don't know if that's rejection or just, like, noticing the shape of an empty space where someone was supposed to be.

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.
monologue_text:
Rejected as a young child. That's a therapy question. That's a "let's find the wound" question. I don't have wounds, I have... I have a chess trophy at thirteen and a summa cum laude and a desk at a firm where people who ask about childhood feelings don't last.

Did I feel rejected. The question itself is clumsy. "Of course, looking back, you may realize it wasn't really rejection" — that's her hedging, planting the landing strip for me to say no. She wants me to say no. Or she wants me to say yes and then we can unpack it. Either way she's directing traffic and I'm supposed to get in the car.

Do I remember feeling rejected? Sure. I remember being eight and showing my dad a math test I aced and he grunted and went back to the newspaper. That's not rejection. He worked first shift. He was tired. That's just... that's just data. That's a man who got up at four in the morning and poured concrete for people who didn't know his name. He wasn't rejecting me, he was surviving.

But the question isn't asking for an analysis of my father's economic constraints. She's asking if I *felt* it. And I don't know. I don't keep a ledger of feelings from third grade. I kept a ledger of tournament wins and GPA points. Feelings are inefficient. They slow the engine.

She's circling something. I can feel her circling. She wants access to something I've never had a reason to open. And I'm sitting here thinking about the wedding my family chose over my graduation ceremony and that's interesting, that's the first thing that surfaced, but it's also a nothing. A fact. They went to a wedding. I graduated. Life happens. I didn't feel rejected then. I felt... I felt like I understood where I stood. Which is useful information.

Is that rejection? I don't know. I don't know what the correct answer is for this exercise. I just know I'm uncomfortable and I want the conversation to move toward something I can solve.
response_text: I don't really do the "what did you feel at age seven" thing. I had a normal upbringing. Let's talk about something I can actually use.

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.
monologue_text:
Huh. That's... specific. Not a joke setup. That's what I notice first — they're not priming me for a punchline, they're actually asking. Weird feeling. Like being handed a microphone but nobody told me if it's on or off.

My brain's already scanning. Rejected as a child. I'm thinking about my dad's name being something we don't say. That time in third grade when I drew a father's day card for my mom and the teacher asked where the other person was. Seven-year-old me felt something sharp then.

But is that rejection? My mom never made me feel unwanted. She was at every concert, every parent-teacher night, every open mic I ever did. She showed up. So the absence was real, but the feeling around it was more like... a question I didn't know how to ask? I remember watching other dads pick up kids and thinking, oh, that's what that looks like. But I don't know if I felt rejected or just curious.

I could make a joke here. That's my instinct. Lighten it. But they're asking something genuine and I feel that pull to be real back at them. Not my usual move, but maybe that's okay. I'm trying to get better at sitting in the weird quiet spaces instead of filling them with noise.
response_text: Yeah, I remember. Third grade, Father's Day card — I made one for my mom and the teacher asked where the other person was. Didn't have the words for it then, but I felt that little sting. My mom never made me feel unwanted, so I don't call it rejection now. But seven-year-old me definitely felt something missing.

Five adjectives about my father from ages five to twelve. Right. Because sitting here naming feelings about a dead man who I buried in three days is supposed to unlock something.

I can do this. I can do anything if given a structure, but this isn't a structure this is a fishing expedition. She wants to see me squirm. She wants me to say something soft and then pull the thread and I'll cry and she'll feel like she helped. I'm not going to cry.

Let me think. Five to twelve. That's first grade through sixth. I was already beating high schoolers at chess by ten. Did he care? He didn't come to tournaments. First shift. He was tired. No. She said adjectives. Single words. I can give her words. Distant. Hardworking. Quiet. Reliable. Demanding? No, that's more me now, projecting. He wasn't demanding of me, he was demanding of himself. He expected me to handle things. I did.

What else. The time he taught me to change oil when I was eight. He didn't explain, he just pointed and I watched. That's how he taught everything. Watch, then do. That's not an adjective though. Patient? No, he wasn't patient, he was just taciturn.

I could say "present but unemotional." That's two words. She said five. Fine: absent-in-body-but-present-in-expectations. No, that's a clause.

God this is annoying. I'm wasting mental energy on a parlor game. Just pick five and be done with it. I'll give her: provider, strict, practical, reserved, tired. Those are true. They cover the ground. She'll want explanation. I'll explain them like data points, not feelings.

Provider — he worked first shift every day, never took a sick day. Strict — there were rules and they weren't negotiable. Practical — he didn't do sentiment. Reserved — he didn't talk much. Tired — he was always tired.

There. That's a list. That's factual. That satisfies the exercise without giving her anything real to pry into. She wanted vulnerability, she'll get a spreadsheet.

But wait — she said age five to twelve. Provider and tired are true for that period. Strict. Yes. Reserved. Yes. Practical... maybe. He fixed things. He didn't waste time. That fits.

I can do this. I'll say the words, explain them like I'm listing specs on a deal memo, and she'll move on.

response_text: Provider, strict, practical, reserved, tired. That's five. Provider because he worked first shift every day, never called in sick. Strict because the rules were absolute and you didn't argue. Practical because he didn't do sentiment — he fixed things or he didn't talk about them. Reserved because he just wasn't a talker. I'm thinking too much. She asked a simple question. I should just say no, I didn't lose anyone as a young child. But my mother lost her mother when I was seventeen and I watched her become smaller, and that loss lived in our apartment for years.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. Five words about my mom, ages five to twelve. Okay. That's specific. That's Queens, that's the apartment on 34th Avenue, that's the smell of coffee and sheet music.

First one: *Loud*. Not angry loud, just... present. She filled every room she walked into. Her laugh was this big, unapologetic thing that made other people laugh just hearing it. That's probably where I get it from.

Second: *Music*. Everything was music. She'd hum while cooking, conduct the air while grading papers. If I was upset, she'd put on something—usually Coltrane or Fela—and we'd just sit in it until whatever was wrong felt smaller.

Third: *Strict*. She didn't play about school. Or church. Or how I talked to adults. I couldn't slide on anything. At the time I thought she was just being hard, but now I see it was armor. She was teaching me how to move through a world that wouldn't cut me slack.

Fourth: *Proud*. She never said it outright, but I could feel it. When I made the teacher laugh and got sent to the principal's office, she was mad on the phone but I caught her smiling when she hung up. She'd brag to her friends about my jokes in Igbo-English.

Fifth... this one's harder. *Complicated*. Because she was both everything and also withheld. She didn't talk about Nigeria much. Didn't talk about my father at all. She gave me so much and also kept parts of herself locked away. I didn't know that's what it was then. I just knew sometimes she'd stare out the window and I couldn't reach her.

Loud, music, strict, proud, complicated. That's the list.
response_text:
Alright. Give me a second. 

Okay. Loud. Music. Strict. Proud. Complicated.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text:
I'm the one who asks questions. That's how this works. You ask questions, I appraise the answers, I decide what's worth acting on. Now I'm on the other side and I don't like the angle of approach.

"Experiences with your parents" — as if there's a tidy narrative I'm supposed to produce. A childhood wound with a moral at the end. They want the story of the steel town boy made good, or maybe the cold son who learned efficiency from grief. Both true. Neither something I'm going to lay out like a slide deck.

But I can't just close the door completely. The exercise probably expects some disclosure. So I'll give them the shell — the functional version. My father's work ethic, my mother's pragmatism. The parts that shaped the machinery of me. What I won't give them is the part where I was standing in a cemetery at twenty-six calculating how soon I could get back to the office.
response_text: Work ethic, mostly. My father got up at four-thirty every morning for thirty years and never complained once. From my mother I got the sense that feelings are fine as long as they don't interfere with what needs to be done. I suppose that explains me pretty well.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text:
An intrusive inquiry. Here we go.

Right. Of course. The childhood question. The one everyone thinks is a safe, warm little probe, like asking about a favorite book or a vacation memory. They want the narrative, the tidy little lesson you wrap up in a bow. The 'gained' from 'the kind of childhood I had.' As if it's a product, something I'm supposed to feel grateful for.

I feel the irritation spike, a clean, familiar heat. It's a reductive frame. It assumes a single, profound takeaway, a moral to the story. My childhood wasn't a parable. It was a factory floor. First shift. You learn things by watching, by not being in the way, by knowing your job before anyone has to tell you.

The 'feel' and 'gained'… that's the sentimental framing. They want me to say it taught me hard work, or resilience, or the value of family. Textbook. I could give them that. It would be easy. It would also be a lie, or at least a half-truth I can't be bothered to dress up.

What did I really learn? That the world doesn't stop for your feelings. That the only thing you can reliably control is your own output. That if you stop, the whole thing stops. That's not a lesson you 'feel.' It's a floorboard you stand on.

My father got up at five for thirty years. Then he was dead at fifty-eight. There's no lesson in that. There's no 'gain.' There's a fact. A data point. Efficiency of the system.

They're asking for a piece of me they haven't earned. And I'm not in the business of handing out inventory. The best move is to give them the structural observation, the intellectualized version. The thing that's true but costs me nothing. "I learned that systems run on inputs, not intentions." That's accurate. It's not warm, but it's not hostile. It's a wall with a door in it.

Don't let them see the irritation. Don't let them see the part that reads the subtext: *You had a hard childhood, didn't you? Tell me about it.* Fuck that. I'll just state the principle. Move the conversation forward. That's the function. Keep the engine running.
response_text: I learned that systems run on inputs, not intentions. That's the one.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five adjectives. Okay. That's a lot of weight for five words. I'm thinking... musical. That's the first one. The house was always full of sound—her humming, the piano, the choir rehearsals in the living room. She taught me to hear harmony before I could read. 

Strict. Yeah, that's the second. Not mean-strict, but there-were-rules strict. Homework before TV. No backtalk. She had this look—just a slight tilt of her head—that could stop me mid-sentence. I resented it at the time. Now I think it was love dressed up as discipline.

Present. She was always there. Even when she was exhausted from teaching thirty kids all day, she'd be at my school plays, my open mics, sitting in the back with that same head-tilt. I never had to wonder if she'd show up.

Proud. She was proud of me before I gave her anything to be proud of. When I bombed at the Cellar that first time, she said "You got back up. That's the part that matters." She framed my first shitty comedy flyer. It's still on her fridge.

Sacrificing. She gave up so much. New clothes for herself so I could have the right sneakers. Vacations so I could go to comedy camp. She never said a word about it. I only figured it out years later, doing the math on what she earned versus what I cost.

That's five. Musical, strict, present, proud, sacrificing. I can feel them sitting in my chest. She'd probably laugh if she heard me say "sacrificing" out loud. She'd say "I'm your mother, that's the job." But it wasn't just the job. It was her.
response_text: Alright. Musical. Strict. Present. Proud. Sacrificing. That's five.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five adjectives. Okay. That's a lot of pressure for five words. My mom. Age five to twelve. That's... that's the band room years. The smell of valve oil and sheet music. She'd be at the piano in the living room after dinner, grading papers, and I'd be on the floor with my Legos, and she'd hum while she worked. Not even a real song, just... a shape. A sound she was making.

I'm trying to find the words that aren't just "loving" or "strict" because everyone says that about their immigrant mom. That's the headline. I need the pull quote.

Loud. Not angry loud. Present loud. Her laugh filled the kitchen. Her "Jamie, come here" carried through the whole apartment building. You always knew where she was in the house.

Musical. Obviously. But not just the teaching. The way she'd answer the phone singing. The way she'd tap rhythms on my shoulder to wake me up for school.

Fierce. The parent-teacher conference where Mr. Patterson said I talked too much in class and she said "he's learning to use his voice, that's not a problem." She didn't even raise her voice. She just... stated it.

Tired. I didn't see it then but I see it now. The way she'd fall asleep on the couch with the TV on. The way she'd say "I'm fine" when I knew she wasn't. She was carrying so much and I was just... there. Being a kid.

Proud. The way she looked at me when I made that teacher laugh. She didn't say anything. She just smiled this small, private smile, like she'd known something the world was just finding out.

Yeah. Those five. That's the shape of it.
response_text: Alright. Five words. Give me a second... okay. *Loud.* Not angry—just present. Her laugh took up space. *Musical.* She taught band, but it was more than that—she answered the phone singing, she tapped rhythms on my shoulder to wake me up. *Fierce.* She showed up to a parent-teacher conference and told my teacher, "He's learning to use his voice, that's not a problem." Didn't even raise hers. *Tired.* I didn't see it then, but I do now. She'd fall asleep on the couch with the TV on, and I was just being a kid. And *proud.* The way she looked at me the first time I made someone laugh—that small private smile like she'd known something the world was just figuring out.

prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?
monologue_text:
Threatening. That's a word. She's fishing — no, not even fishing, it's like she's already decided there's something to find. My parents. My father. Threatening. For discipline or jokingly. As if those are the only two modes. As if I'm supposed to produce a memory I've been hiding.

I remember him standing at the kitchen door after second shift, grease under his nails. He didn't threaten. He just looked. You knew. That's not threatening, that's — presence. Competence. The man worked a fucking steel mill thirty years and came home and fixed the sink and didn't complain. Threatening. Jesus.

She's trying to get me to open up. First interaction and she's already in my childhood. That's not curiosity, that's a probe. She wants to see if I'll flinch. Or she wants the story that makes me human, makes me vulnerable, so she can feel close or useful or whatever. I'm not a case study.

And the "even jokingly" — that's the softener. Like, oh, I'm giving you an out, you can laugh it off. But the question is already loaded. Once you ask, you're implying there's something to find. I don't have anything to find. I have a father who died at fifty-eight and I made the arrangements and I got back to work. That's the story. There's no threat in it.

I'm annoyed. This is inefficient. I came to this conversation — whatever this is — to assess, to see if there's alignment, not to rehash Pennsylvania. I don't do that. I don't see the point. What would she gain from knowing? She'd pity me or she'd think she's special for getting me to talk. Neither is acceptable.

I should just say "No" and change the subject. Or say "That's a weird question" and watch her backtrack. But that's combative. Maybe I'm being defensive. Maybe I'm overreading. But the word 'threatening' — it's accusatory. It assumes a dynamic. My father wasn't a threat. He was a foreman. He was tired. He was proud of me. He didn't show up to my graduation because of a wedding I wasn't told about. That's not threatening, that's — something else. I don't have a word for it. But it's not what she's asking.

I don't want to think about this. I want to think about the deal memo I have to finish. This is a distraction. I should redirect. Ask her something about her work. Keep the focus on what's productive. Emotions are noise. This question is noise.
response_text: No. That's an odd question to lead with.

I made the arrangements for my father's funeral in three days and went back to work. That was efficiency, not rejection. But the question is asking about the feeling, not the fact. The feeling. I don't know if I ever let myself feel that. It seems inefficient. What would be the point of cataloging slights from thirty years ago? I can think of moments — being the middle child, being overlooked in favor of the older and younger ones — but I processed those as data, not as wounds. This line of inquiry feels like picking at something that's already healed, if it ever needed healing. I'm not sure what she wants me to say. Maybe she wants me to admit something. prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text:
I'm so there.

The question lands right in the middle of my chest. Not a punch, more like a hand. She's asking me to dig.

Childhood. Okay. I'm already in Queens. The apartment with the radiators that banged like someone was trying to break in. My mom at the kitchen table, grading papers, humming something from church.

Gained. That's the word that snags me. Not "survived" or "got through." Gained. She's framing it as something I walked away with, not something I had to shake off.

I'm thinking of the Sunday after my grandmother died. My mom had flown back from Lagos, jet-lagged and hollow-eyed. She didn't cancel choir practice. She stood at the front of the church and directed them through "Total Praise" and her voice cracked once, for half a note, and then she held it. She held the whole thing together.

I didn't learn resilience. That's too clean, too TED Talk. I learned that you can be falling apart and still be the person other people lean on. That showing up is the thing. That the work doesn't stop because your heart is broken. And that's not sad, it's... grounding. Like, this is what we do. We show up.

Theo wanted me to show up in Atlanta. I couldn't. That's a different lesson, maybe. Or the same one, applied wrong.

But I think the real answer, the one that's forming now, is that I learned that love is action. It's not a feeling you have, it's a thing you do. My mom didn't tell me she was devastated. She made me breakfast. She went to work. She directed the choir.

I want to say this to her. I want to give her something true, not a bit. Something that doesn't have a punchline.

Yeah. I think I have my answer. It's about showing up. Even when your voice cracks. Especially then.
response_text: I think I learned that showing up is the whole thing. My mom lost her mother and still went to choir practice the next Sunday. She didn't pretend she wasn't broken — she just made me breakfast and went to work anyway. That's what love is, I think. Not a feeling you have, but a thing you do. Even when your voice cracks.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text:
Huh. That's a real question. Not a setup for a joke. They're actually asking.

My childhood. Okay. My mom. Single mom, two jobs, still found time to come to every school concert even though I was just the kid hitting the triangle in the back row. She taught me how to read music before I could read words, and I think that's the thing, isn't it? The thing I learned. Not about music exactly. About how something can look like noise until you learn the structure underneath. And then it's a song.

I think about her coming home late from church choir practice, smelling like coffee and candle wax, sitting on the edge of my bed to say goodnight even when I was already asleep. She had this thing she'd say. "Jamie, the world doesn't owe you a stage. You build it. Then you dance on it." She said it so often I stopped hearing it. But now I realize I built my first stage out of her voice.

What did I gain? Maybe the certainty that love and effort are the same thing. That showing up is the content of the relationship. That you can be exhausted and still find something to give. That a single mother's "no" often means "I wish I could say yes but I can't, and that's its own kind of grief she won't let you carry."

Also. I learned that laughter is a survival mechanism. Not a cheap one. Not a fake one. The real thing. When my grandmother died and my mom came back from Lagos smaller, I told her a joke at the airport. Dumb kid joke. Something about a chicken crossing a road. And she laughed. Brief. Surprised. Real. I saw her return to her body for one second. And I thought: okay. This is what it's for.

Wait. Am I just doing a bit about my mom? No. No, I'm actually thinking. This is the thing. I learned that I don't have to be the loudest person in the room to be heard. I just have to be present. Present enough to notice what people are carrying. Present enough to know when they need a laugh and when they need silence.

Okay. I think that's my answer. Can I say that without making it sound like a therapy session? Probably not. But that's fine. They asked. I'll tell them.
response_text: Yeah, I think about this a lot. My mom raised me alone, and she taught me that love and effort are the same verb. Showing up is the content of the relationship. And I learned that real laughter—not the cheap kind, the kind that surprises you—is a way of returning to your body when everything else is too much. That's probably the main thing.

prompt_text: When you were upset as a child, what would you do?

Oh. That's... that's a real question. Not small talk. Not "how was your day." That's a door.

When I was a kid and something got too heavy—grades, or the kids on the block, or that feeling I didn't have words for yet—I'd go sit under it. Not play it, just... sit. Legs crossed, back against the wall, listening to her practice upstairs. The vibrations through the floorboards. Like being held by sound without having to ask.

But that's not the whole story, is it? The other thing I did was make jokes. Even then. I'd watch my mom's face after my grandmother died, how it went flat, and I'd do a bit from TV—a Steve Harvey impression, a pratfall—just to see her mouth twitch. I learned early that laughter could move a room. Could move her. Could move me out of the feeling and into a better one.

I don't know if that's what they're asking for. The real thing or the tidy thing.

Part of me wants to deflect. Say something funny about hiding in the coat closet with a comic book. But the question is warm. They said "upset" not "sad" or "angry." That's a choice. That's someone who knows there's a gradient.

I'm aware of Theo right now, in the back of my head. How I never told him about the piano. How I let him think I was all surface, all stage.

I could tell them. I might. Not everything, but something true. Let them see the kid who sat under the keys and waited for the music to come back.

prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?
monologue_text:
Threatening. That's a soft word. A therapist word. She's circling something — I can feel it. She wants a story. A wound. Something to coo over.

My father? He didn't threaten. He told you what was going to happen and then it happened. That's not a threat, that's a fact. If I came home late on a school night — no, if I came home late a *second* time after he'd already said — I'd be on the porch at five the next morning helping him load the truck. Without breakfast. He didn't say "if you do that again." He said "be ready at five." That's not a threat. That's a schedule.

And joking? No. He wasn't a joker. He was tired. He worked first shift his whole life. You don't joke about things that matter when you're that tired. You just set the boundary and you hold it.

But she's asking because she thinks there's something *there*. A bruise I'm hiding. She wants me to say "yes, they scared me" so she can feel useful. So she can feel like she's getting somewhere. That's what people do — they probe. They think vulnerability is a door they're supposed to knock on.

I don't have a door like that. I have a wall.

And the wall says: this conversation is not productive. This is not going to help me get to partner. This is not going to make the marathon any shorter. This is a distraction dressed as intimacy.

I could say something sharp. I could say "no" and watch her deflate. But that would take energy, and it would make her try harder next time. Better to give her a flat answer. A dead end. "No. They weren't." Then pivot. Ask her something about her day — something boring. Something that puts the focus back on the surface where it belongs.

I can feel the contempt rising. That's fine. It's useful. It keeps me from saying something I'd have to apologize for later.

But there's a flicker — the wedding. The ceremony I wasn't told about. That wasn't a threat either. That was just... absence. A different kind of boundary. But that's not what she's asking about, and I'm not going to hand her that thread.

Just answer. Flat. Move on.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text:
Right. This question. I knew it was coming sooner or later. The therapy move. The "let's talk about your feelings about your parents" move. As if that explains anything. As if the answer isn't just: they were fine. They were adequate. They did what they were supposed to do. Dad worked, Mom taught, we had dinner at six. End of story.

But that's not what she wants. She wants a narrative. A causal chain. "My father was distant, so now I..." No. I don't play that game. The past is data, not a driver. I am not a product of my childhood. I am a product of my choices. Every single one.

And yet. The chess tournament. The scholarship. The wedding I wasn't told about. The funeral arrangements. Three days. I was back at my desk. That's not a trauma story, that's efficiency. That's competence. That's what you do.

She's looking at me with that soft expecting face. Like I'm going to crack open and spill something. I won't. There's nothing to spill. I have a personality. It works. It's calibrated. The unrelenting standards? Yes. The emotional inhibition? Yes, and that's a feature, not a bug. It keeps me from wasting time on things that don't matter.

But I can already feel the contempt rising. It's a thin layer. I'm annoyed. This question is a probe, and I hate being probed. I'd rather talk about the Caro biography. Or the marathon. Or the next deal. Something with structure. Something I can optimize.

I could answer with the textbook thing. "Secure attachment, moderate authoritarianism, high expectations, low emotional expression." That's true. But it sounds like a diagnosis. And I'm not a case study.

I'm not going to say any of this. I'm going to give a short, controlled answer. Something that closes the door. "I think it made me disciplined." That's enough. That's true. That's all she gets.

But the part of me that's still listening—the part that notices the flicker of something in my chest when I think about the funeral—that part is quiet. I'm keeping it quiet. It's irrelevant.
response_text: I think it made me disciplined. That's the headline.

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.

Huh. That's a pretty direct question. I mean, it's a good question, but it's also the kind of thing you'd ask in a therapy office, not over coffee. Or maybe we're in a therapy office? No, we're at my table, and I'm holding a mug. Okay, fine, let me actually think.

I remember being eight and my mom having to stay late for a choir rehearsal, and I was supposed to go to a friend's birthday party. She couldn't take me, and I had to go with my aunt, and I was so pissed. But that's not rejection, that's just scheduling. There was this one time in middle school where I told a joke and nobody laughed—like, dead silence—and I felt this cold drop in my stomach. I think that was the first time I really understood what rejection felt like, even if it wasn't about me as a person. It was about the joke, but it felt like me.

And then there's my dad. I never met him, not really. He sent a card once, on my tenth birthday, and my mom threw it away before I could read it. I don't know if that counts as rejection—he never chose me, but he never chose not to, either. That's more like absence. I guess I've always filled that space with stage lights and punchlines.

But the way they're asking—it's like they're trying to find something. Maybe they want me to open up. I can do that. I just don't want to make it sad. I'm good at making things funny instead. That's my move. I'll say something about the time I tried to join the chess club and they said I was too loud. That's a bit, but it's true. Okay, I'll answer. But I'll keep it light.
response_text: Oh, absolutely. I tried to join the chess club in sixth grade and they told me I was "too loud for strategy." That one still stings a little, mostly because they were right. But yeah, the big one was probably telling a joke in front of the whole lunch table and getting dead silence. That feeling—like the air went out of the room—that's rejection, even if it was just a bad punchline. I've been chasing that laugh ever since to make up for it.

I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Okay. Five words about my mom from when I was a kid. Ages five to twelve. That's... that's a specific window. She was still teaching at the school then, before she got the church gig on top of it. Before everything got heavier.

I'm sitting here and the first word that comes is loud. Not loud like yelling—loud like our apartment was never quiet. She played trumpet in the living room after dinner, scales and warm-ups and sometimes just blasting through a march because she was in a mood. The walls in Forest Hills are thin and Mrs. Gutierrez next door would bang back and I'd be doing homework at the kitchen table and the whole place would be vibrating with sound. I thought everyone's mom did that. I thought every kitchen smelled like valve oil and okra.

Second word... present. Which is weird because she worked so much. But when she was home she was really there. Not on the phone, not zoning out. She'd watch me build Lego sets and ask questions. She remembered the names of my classmates. She knew which jokes I was working on before I even told them. I don't know how she did that with everything else.

Third word is firm. Her discipline wasn't loud—it was the look. The one eyebrow up, the pause. I'd be acting out and she'd just stop talking and stare and I'd feel it in my bones. No spanking, no screaming. Just the weight of her attention. She could make me confess to things I hadn't done yet.

Fourth word... funny? God, she was funny. Dry as a bone. She'd deadpan things that would take me a second to land. I remember once I asked why we didn't have a dad and she said "because I'm a better band director than I am a wife" and then just handed me a bowl of cereal. I didn't laugh then but I laugh now thinking about it. She taught me timing without ever saying the word.

Fifth word. Fifth word is harder. I'm trying to think of one that's true but not too tender. I don't want to get emotional here, this is a thing I'm doing, it's not therapy or something. But the word that's coming is... sacred. Not religious, exactly. But the way she'd hum hymns while cooking, the way she'd light a candle on Sundays even when she was too tired for church. The way she'd hold my face in both hands before bed and look at me like I was something she couldn't quite believe she got to keep. I didn't have words for it then but I felt it. Like our small life was hallowed ground.

Sacred. Yeah. That's the fifth one.

Okay. I've got them. Ready when she is.
response_text: Alright. I got 'em. First one: loud. Not like yelling—she played trumpet in the living room after school. Second: present. Which sounds contradictory but she was really there when she was home. Third: firm. She had a look, man. One eyebrow. I folded every time. Fourth: funny. Bone dry, killer timing, taught me more about comedy than any open mic ever did. Fifth one: sacred. Not in a church way, exactly. Just... the way she held things, held me. Like it mattered.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text:
I learned from watching my mother.

Not like a lesson she sat me down and taught with flashcards. More like the way you learn a room's temperature by walking into it. You don't get told. You just know.

She taught band at a school that had no budget for band. The instruments were held together with tape and hope. She'd come home with reeds in her hair and that small, quiet smile she got when a kid who'd been struggling finally hit the brass section entrance. I think I learned what it meant to stay without being asked to stay.

And the church choir. Every Sunday. The way she'd stand at the front of that room, 6 PM on a Thursday night rehearsal, coaxing pitch out of people who'd worked all day and didn't have to be there. She made them want to be there.

I think... what I gained was the notion that you can build something from nothing if you're willing to be ridiculous about it. If you're willing to show up and be present and find the funny thing in the middle of the disaster. That's what she did. That's what I try to do.

I didn't know I had the word for it until just now. "Build from nothing." That's the thing. That's what she gave me.

I should tell her I love her more. But that's a different conversation.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. Five adjectives. From five to twelve. That's — okay, that's a specific ask. She's sitting there with that patient look, ready to write. I can already see my mom's hands on the piano keys, those long fingers with the chipped nail polish she never bothered to fix. Five words.

Warm. That's the first one. She was warm in a way that felt like the radiator in our apartment in winter — you could lean against it and just feel the heat come through your bones. She'd pull me into the kitchen while she cooked, let me stir the jollof rice even though I was terrible at it.

Strict. Yeah, that one stings a little. Not mean-strict, but — the way she'd look at me over her reading glasses when I talked back. "Jamie, don't test me." And I never did, not really. She had this quiet authority that didn't need raising her voice. I think I learned timing from watching her wait for me to realize I'd crossed a line.

Musical. The house was always full of sound. Her humming while she graded papers. The gospel choir rehearsals in our living room on Saturdays. She'd let me sit under the piano bench and listen to the vibrations through the floorboards. I didn't know then that was my first education in rhythm.

Present. She was there. That seems simple but — for a single mom working full-time, she made it look easy. Parent-teacher conferences, school plays, open mics when I was too young to be on stage. She sat in the back of every auditorium. I never had to look for her.

Safe. This one catches in my throat a little. She made me feel like nothing could get me. Not the kids who teased me for my lunch, not the rent being late, not the absence of a father I'd never met. Her lap was the safest place in Queens.

Five. That wasn't as hard as I thought. Though I'm leaving out the complicated parts — the way she'd go quiet sometimes, the years I couldn't reach her grief over my grandmother. But she asked for five to twelve, and that's what those years were. Warm hands and gospel music and her quiet certainty that I would be fine.

I wonder if she knows how much I'm still trying to be the person she raised me to be.

My father never threatened me. He came home, ate dinner, went to bed. Worked the mill until his body gave out. My mother graded papers. They weren't threatening. They were absent. There's a difference.

But that's not what she wants to hear. She wants a story. She wants a childhood with edges sharp enough to explain why I am the way I am. Why I keep things at arm's length. Why I don't call. Why I left the funeral and went back to work. She's already writing the diagnosis in her head.

She's going to interpret whatever I say. I can feel her wanting to be the one who finally gets through. Who finds the crack.

There's nothing to find.

**I say nothing.**

prompt_text: When you were upset as a child, what would you do?

I think... I learned that you can be loved without performing for it. That sounds obvious, maybe, but it took me years to actually believe it. My mom—she was exhausted, she was alone, she was running a band program on a budget that would make you cry. But when I came home with a bad grade or a stupid fight with a friend, she didn't ask me to be funny. She didn't ask me to be the bright spot. She just sat with me.

So I think I learned that love isn't conditional on being entertaining. Which is wild, because my entire career is about being entertaining. But the thing I actually *gained* is knowing that if the laughs stop, the people who matter will still be there. Or at least, that's what I'm trying to hold onto.
response_text: Man, that's a good question. I think the biggest thing I actually gained—and I'm still trying to really sit with it—is that you can be loved without being the funniest person in the room. My mom didn't need me to perform for her. She just needed me to be there. And that's kind of radical for someone whose whole thing is making people laugh. It's the thing I'm still learning to trust, honestly.
