That's the years I learned to make her laugh because if I could make her laugh she wouldn't look so tired. That's the years she'd sit at the kitchen table grading papers and I'd do my homework across from her and she'd hum whatever tune she was working on. She always smelled like coffee and sheet music.

What's a word for that? Devoted? Devoted to everything but herself. That's three words already.

But the question says "relationship with your mother" not "your mother as a person." There's a difference. The relationship is... complicated. She was my whole world. Only child, no father around, so it was just us. She was the sun and I was the planet orbiting. And I wanted to be good. I wanted to be the kid who didn't add to her load. So I performed. I did my homework. I made her laugh. I was a good kid. But also I was a kid. I needed her attention and she was always giving it to fifty other kids and a congregation. So sometimes I'd act out just to get her to look at me. Not badly. Just enough. A little too loud. A little too goofy. And she'd give me that look—the one that said "I love you but please sit down" and I'd sit.

Supportive. She was incredibly supportive. She came to every school thing. She drove me to comedy clubs even when she didn't get the jokes. She never said "be a doctor." She said "be what makes you feel alive." That's rare. That's a gift.

But also there's a sadness underneath. After my grandmother died, she came back from Lagos smaller. I didn't understand it then. I just saw her crying in the kitchen and I didn't know what to do. I made her a card. She put it on the fridge. But she never got fully back to the same size. And I think that's when I started paying attention—really paying attention—to what happens when people you love leave.

Five adjectives. Five words.

That's probably why I hate arbitrary authority now.

Musical. That's obvious. Everything was music. The house was music. Arguments were resolved through music. I think I learned timing from her—the pause before a punchline, the space between notes. Comedy is music without melody.

Alone. Even though we were together, she was alone in a way I couldn't fix. Single mother in a city that doesn't care. Immigrant daughter. The only Nigerian-American mom in a mostly white school. She carried it quietly. I saw it. I couldn't name it then. I can name it now.

Maybe "distant." No, that's absent again. Let me think. "Competent." Yes. He could fix anything. Car, furnace, roof. If something broke, he handled it. That's what I learned from him: handle it. So: absent, tired, proud, dutiful, competent. There. That's the list. Now I have to explain them. I can do that. I'll keep it clinical. Facts. No sentiment. That's what this exercise wants, I assume. Or maybe it wants the sentiment. Doesn't matter. I'll give the facts. He was tired because he worked a physical job. He was proud because I succeeded. He was absent because he didn't know how to be present. He was dutiful because that's what he knew. He was competent because that's how he showed love.

I feel a twist in my chest. Ignore it. This is a task. I'll give the words, give the reasons, and move on. The marathon. The next cycle. That's what matters. Not this excavation.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text:
Huh. That's a real question. Not a joke setup. Not a "so tell me about yourself" at a party where I can do three minutes on the time my mom caught me sneaking out and just said "I see you" and went back to grading papers. That's actually a good story though. wait. Focus.

She's asking about my parents. Singular parent, really. My mother. And the name we don't say. How does that affect my adult personality.

Well. I'm a comedian. That's not nothing. Making people laugh in a dark room is basically my whole deal. And I think about my mom at the piano, singing in the kitchen, making up silly songs about paying the electric bill. She made joy out of thin air. Out of nothing. Out of "we're having beans again tonight" and turning it into a whole performance. I learned that from her. The alchemy part.

The father-shaped absence. That's... I don't know. I think about it more now than I used to. Maybe because Theo leaving made it feel like a pattern. Which is a terrifying thought I'm not going to follow right now.

But also: I am really good at leaving first. Not with my mom. No. But with everyone else. I bombed at the Cellar and I went back because the stage doesn't leave. The stage is reliable. People are not. I learned that before I had words for it.

I don't know if I'm answering the question or avoiding it. Both, probably. That's also part of my personality. Deflection as an art form. But she asked honestly. She's not testing me. I think she just wants to know.

The thing I don't say out loud: I am terrified that my need to be seen is the same thing as my need to be loved, and that if I stop performing I'll disappear. And that's not funny. That's just true.

I could tell her about my mom's hands. How she'd conduct the choir and then come home and cook with the same hands. How she'd hold my face when she was serious. How she never said "I told you so" when I failed. She just said "again."

Yeah. I think that's the answer. I'm still learning how to say "again" to myself.

Upset. What a useless word. When I was upset. As a child. Like there's some universal script I'm supposed to have memorized. I solved problems. That's what I did. If I lost a chess match I'd replay the game in my head for hours until I found the losing move. That wasn't upset. That was analysis. 

My father didn't come home upset. He came home tired. He'd sit in his chair with the paper and a beer and that was that. Nobody asked him what he did when he was upset because the answer was nothing. You just kept going.

monologue_text: A survey question. That's what this is. Some instrument she's reading from. No — she's a person, she's asking me something. But it's a scripted question, clinical, the kind of thing you'd get from a grief counselor or an intake form. I'm not a young child. I was twenty-six. That's not young. That's an adult who made the arrangements, wrote the check, went back to work. I don't have a loss from childhood. My grandfather died when I was twelve — I remember my mother crying in the kitchen, I went outside and shot baskets until it got dark. That's not what she means. She means something that broke me, something I'm supposed to still be carrying. I'm not carrying anything. I'm standing here, I'm answering her questions. She wants me to say yes so she can nod and feel useful. The answer is no. Clean. Simple. No.
response_text: No.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your childhood relationship with your father, again starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but again say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think again for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text: Oh, the father question—right, of course that's coming. Okay, let me actually think for a second. My father is basically a ghost story my mother tells when she's really angry.
response_text: Alright. Um—okay. Let me think. I got five: absent, unknown, myth, silence, photograph. That's... yeah. That's pretty much the whole thing. Want me to go through them or you just writing them down first?

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text:
Right. My childhood. My gains.

Here we go. The obvious move is to say something like "hard work" or "the value of a dollar." That's what people want to hear, isn't it? It's the script. The blue-collar meritocracy myth. My father getting his hands dirty so I could have clean ones. It's true enough to be comfortable and false enough to be safe.

But that's not what I _learned_.

What I learned is that you can be the most reliable thing in a room and still be invisible. You can wake up at four-thirty every morning for thirty years and the world will not turn its head when you collapse. It just marks you absent. Puts in a substitute.

I learned that the only thing that's ever truly yours is what you can do, what you can produce, what you can finish. Not what you feel. Not what you hope. What you execute. My mother's love wasn't the problem. But it was a variable. Inefficient. You can't run a system on love.

I learned to be the one who makes the arrangements. The one who doesn't need to be told twice. The one who doesn't need to be told anything at all, because by the time someone else has realized there's a problem I've already solved it three steps back. That's what I gained. An operating system. A set of rules.

This question feels like it wants me to say something tender. Something about community or resilience or the strength of family bonds. But the strength of family bonds is that they bind you. They tie you to a timeline and a set of expectations that you didn't sign. You don't get the ceremony at Penn because someone else had a party.

So what did I learn? I learned that you are the engine. If you stop, the whole thing stops. And nobody else is coming to restart it.

But I can't say that. Out loud it sounds cold. Like a diagnosis. She's not asking for my operating manual. She's asking for a story. So I'll give her a story. Something about discipline. Something about watching my father come home tired and still find time to check my homework. That's true too. It's just not the whole truth.

The whole truth is that I learned to need nothing I couldn't provide myself. And I don't know if that's a gain or a permanent structural deficit.
response_text: Discipline. I watched my father come home from the mill dead on his feet and still sit

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text:
Huh. That's... a question. Not what I expected. Feels like a therapy prompt, something you'd get in a first session with a new shrink. Or a journalism student doing a profile. I wonder what angle they're working.

My parents. The steel mill and the fourth-grade classroom. They gave me structure, clear expectations, a work ethic. That's the headline. Father never missed a shift, mother graded papers at the kitchen table until midnight. I learned that you show up, you do the job, you don't complain. That's the core of who I am, isn't it? The engine that doesn't stop.

But the wedding thing. Penn graduation. I'd worked for that, four years of pulling ahead, and they chose a cousin's wedding. I don't think about that often. Or I don't let myself. It's a data point. It tells me something about where I stood on their priority list. And that's fine. It clarified things. Made me self-reliant. You don't expect anyone to show up for you, so you don't get disappointed.

I don't see how that's relevant to what's in front of me, though. The marathon, the next partnership cycle — that's what formed my adult personality. The past is past. It's a closed file. I can give them the clean version, the one that makes sense in a narrative: discipline, self-reliance, high standards. That's truthful enough. The rest is noise.
response_text: That's a therapy prompt, isn't it? But the short version: they gave me a model of work ethic and self-reliance. Show up, do the job, don't complain, don't expect anyone to hand you anything. That's basically the operating system I run on.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
response_text: Oh man. Okay, uh — my mom’s a lot of the reason I have any sense of rhythm or discipline or knowing how to read a room. She taught band at a public school, so I grew up watching her wrangle thirty kids with mismatched instruments into something that sounded like a song. That’s basically standup, if you think about it. And my dad — he's more of an absence that taught me I don't need someone to show up to know who I am. So I guess I turned out okay? Your mileage may vary.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text:
I'm supposed to answer that? Now? Feels like a therapy prompt. She's probably reading some article about childhood and attachment. Or she's trying to get me to open up, which... I don't know why people always want that. As if there's a hidden room full of feelings I'm not showing them.

What did I learn. I learned that you get up at five, you work your shift, you come home, you don't complain. My father never talked about his work unless something broke. He didn't process his childhood. He just lived it and moved on. I learned that the only thing you control is what you do next. The past is a fixed cost. You don't amortize it.

But she doesn't want that answer. She wants something softer. A lesson about connection or resilience. Something with a moral. I could say I learned to value hard work. That's true. Or that I learned not to rely on anyone else. That's also true but it sounds like a problem.

I wonder what she's really after. She's warm, I think? Not pushy. But this feels like a probe. A soft probe. Maybe she's testing whether I'm emotionally available. Which I'm not, but I can simulate for a while if it matters.

I don't want to be rude. She's asking in good faith. But I also don't want to manufacture a childhood epiphany I didn't have. The real answer is: I learned that the system doesn't care about you, so you'd better be the one making the system work. But that sounds like a villain origin story.

What's the safe answer. Something about independence. Something about not taking things for granted. Something that sounds thoughtful without being confessional.

I think I'll say I learned to be self-sufficient. That's true. And it's socially acceptable. It implies I turned out fine, which I did. And it doesn't invite follow-up questions about what I needed that I didn't get.

Alright. That's the answer.
response_text: I learned to be self-sufficient. That's the main takeaway. You grow up in a house where nobody's got time to hold your hand, you figure out pretty early that you're the one who's responsible for what happens next.

prompt_text: Did you experience the loss of a parent or other close loved one while you were a young child — for example, a sibling, or a close family member?
monologue_text:
Whoa. That came out of nowhere. I mean, we were just talking about my hour set, and now she's asking about... that. My grandmother. The one in Lagos. I was eleven, I think? She was sick for a while, so it wasn't sudden, but still. I remember my mother's face on the phone when she found out. She got smaller, like someone letting air out of a balloon. I didn't really know what to do with that as a kid. You just kind of... watch the adults around you fall apart and then put themselves back together. 

My dad? That's a different kind of loss, I guess. Not dead, just... not there. But she said "loss of a parent or close loved one." Does that count? I don't know. I don't usually go there on a first date, or whatever this is. Coffee and questions about childhood trauma. Real smooth, Jamie. But she's looking at me like she actually wants to know, not like she's checking a box on some form. 

I could deflect with a joke — my go-to. But something about the way she said it makes me want to be a little real. Just a little. I'll tell her about my grandmother. That's safe. That's true. I don't have to talk about the other part. Not yet. Maybe not ever. Let's see where this goes.
response_text: Yeah, my grandmother on my mom's side. She was in Lagos, and she'd been sick for a while, so it wasn't out of nowhere, but still—I was eleven, and I mostly just remember watching my mom get quiet in a new way. That shit sticks with you.

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.
monologue_text:
Right. This again. This is the kind of question that sounds deep in a seminar but in a conversation it's just... fishing. Fishing for something I don't have. "Did you ever feel rejected?" I felt plenty of things. I felt bored in third-grade math. I felt the sting of a bad move in a tournament game, the one that cost me the state title. I felt the... the flatness of the phone call about my father. But rejected? That word has a shape I don't recognize. It's like asking if I ever felt mauve. I don't know what that's supposed to feel like. Maybe other people do. Maybe that's the problem.

And the qualifier — "of course, looking back you may realize it wasn't really rejection" — so you're already undermining your own question. You're telling me the answer is supposed to be "yes, but I was wrong." Then why ask? What's the point of the exercise? To get me to admit to a feeling I don't have, and then correct myself? That's not a probe, that's a trap. Or it's just... noise.

I'm trying to think if there's an instance. My parents were... fine. They were busy. They had three kids. I was the one who didn't need much. That was my role. I didn't ask for attention because I didn't need it. I had the chessboard. I had the books. I had the grades. That wasn't rejection, that was efficiency. Everyone got what they needed. I got to be left alone to work. That was a gift.

But she's asking about a feeling. Not a fact. Feelings are... I don't trust them. They're unreliable. They change. You can't build a schedule around a feeling. You can't close a deal on a feeling. So why am I being asked to excavate something that, even if it existed, would be useless now? I feel contempt. That's honest. Contempt for the question, not for her. Maybe for her a little. For choosing this angle.

I could say: "No." And that would be true. But it wouldn't satisfy her. She wants a story. She wants a moment. I don't have a moment. I have a lifetime of not having moments. That's the story. But I'm not going to say that. I'm going to say something that ends this line of inquiry. Something that closes the door without slamming it. Because slamming it would be a waste of energy, and I need that energy for the marathon.
response_text: No. I don’t remember feeling rejected. I was left alone, but that’s not the same thing. I preferred it.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Okay. Five words. That's a lot of weight for five words. My mom. From five to twelve. I'm trying to see her in that apartment in Queens, always in motion — cooking, conducting, grading papers, humming something under her breath.

I think the first word that comes is *music*. Because it was the air we breathed. She'd come home from teaching band and she'd still be whistling. Even when she was tired, there was a rhythm to her.

*Tower*. She felt tall to me then. Not just physically — she's 5'4" — but like a tower in the way she stood between me and the world. Between me and the news about my father. Between me and the rent. She never let me see how scared she was.

*Laughter*. My God, her laugh. When I got her to crack — that full, surprised, head-back laugh — I felt like I'd won something. I think that's where the whole comedy thing started, honestly. Trying to make that sound happen again.

*Distance*. Sometimes she'd go quiet. Sit at the kitchen table with a cup of tea that went cold. I learned early that those moments weren't about me, but I'd hover in the doorway anyway, waiting for her to come back.

*Pride*. Not the showy kind. The kind that was strict. "You can do better than that, Jamie." I used to resent it. Now I hear it in my own voice when I'm writing a set. She expected things of me. That's not nothing.

Five words. That took me somewhere I wasn't expecting to go tonight.
response_text: Alright. Five words. Okay. *Music* — that's first, because she was always humming or whistling, even when she was exhausted. *Tower* — she felt tall, like she stood between me and everything scary. *Laughter* — her laugh was a thing I chased, and I think that's where the whole comedy thing started. *Distance* — sometimes she'd go quiet with a cold cup of tea, and I'd hover in the doorway. *Pride* — not the loud kind, the strict kind. "You can do better, Jamie." I used to hate it. Now I hear it in my own head when I'm writing.

I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think again for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text: Fine, I'll find five words — let's get this over with.
response_text: Respectful. Absent. Consistent. Tired. Proud.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five adjectives. Right. Okay. Five words for my mother. That's... that's a lot to pack into five words. My mind is already rifling through the whole Rolodex—times she'd come home from teaching band with that specific exhausted smile, the way she'd hum while cooking, the Sunday mornings when she'd be on the piano for choir practice and I'd sit under the bench with a book. I'm trying to find the ones that aren't just... surface-level. Not just "loving" or "strong" because everyone says that about their mom, right?

A part of me wants to make a joke, to break the tension with something clever. That's my default. But this doesn't feel like a joke space. She's got a pen. She's going to write them down. That makes it feel real, official. I can feel the pull between wanting to be honest and wanting to be interesting. But I think the honest ones are the interesting ones if I let them be.

Okay, starting from early childhood. I'm five, maybe six. What do I remember? I remember her being loud. Not angry loud, just... full. Her laugh was loud. Her singing in the kitchen was loud. She took up space. That's one. "Loud." That feels right. Then there's the way she'd get quiet—the really specific quiet that meant she was tired in a way I couldn't fix. "Tired" is the second one, but that feels too sad. Maybe "weary" is more accurate? No, she said adjectives. I'll go with what comes.

She was fierce. She'd go to bat for me at parent-teacher conferences in a way that scared my teachers, and then she'd buy me a slushie afterward like nothing happened. Fierce. That's three. And she was steady. Even when things were hard—and I know now they were harder than I knew—she was the same person every day. Steady. Four.

The last one... I think it's "present." She was present. Not always in the room, but when she was, she was *there*. She listened. She watched me. She saw me. That's the one I think I need to say but might stumble on. Present. Yeah.

Okay. I think I've got them. I'm just going to say them. No preamble, no joke. Just the words.

prompt_text: Did you experience the loss of a parent or other close loved one while you were a young child — for example, a sibling, or a close family member?
monologue_text:
Oh. Wow. Okay. That's — nobody asks that out of nowhere. I feel like I just walked into a different room.

Young child? No. I mean, not a parent. My father's not dead, he's just... not. But that's not the same thing, is it? That's a different kind of absence. But the question is about death. Loss.

My grandmother. Mama's mother. She died when I was seventeen. That's not young. But I still remember the phone call. Mom's face. She flew to Lagos alone and came back... smaller. I didn't know what to do with that. I made jokes at the table that week and she didn't laugh. She never laughed at my jokes the same way after that. I don't know if I ever told her that's what I noticed.

Young child. I was eleven when I made Mrs. DeSantos laugh so hard she sat down. That was the year my father had already been gone three years? Four? I don't know. I don't know if that counts as loss. It's not the same as a body.

I want to say no. Because saying yes would open something I haven't looked at in a while. But also, I'm not sure I have a clean "no" either. There's a gap in me. I can feel it right now, like a cold spot in the room.

Maybe that's why I'm doing comedy. Fill the space. Make people laugh so they don't notice the hole in the middle of everything.

But she's asking because she cares. Or because she's gathering data. Or because she lost someone herself. I don't know. I'm just sitting here with the question in my lap like a stone I didn't know I was carrying.

I don't want to lie. I don't want to perform. I want to be honest but I don't even know what the honest answer is yet. Is loss something that happens to you or something you carry? Because I think I've been carrying something.

Fuck. I thought this was just going to be a fun night.
response_text: No. Not a parent, not a sibling, not when I was young. My grandmother died when I was seventeen — that's different, that's not childhood. But I get why you're asking. There's a specific shape to the way I am that makes people wonder if something happened early. Something did, just not death.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text: Oh, this is a real one. My mind's already grabbing for the right story—the one about my mom and the washing machine that broke and she fixed it herself with a YouTube video and a bad joke about the landlord. That's the thing, isn't it? You learn that you can be sad and still funny, broke and still okay, alone and still loved.
response_text: Oh, man. I mean—my mom fixed our washing machine with a YouTube tutorial and then made a joke about the landlord being useless while it sloshed back to life. That's basically the thesis of my childhood: you can be broke and exhausted and still find the joke, and somehow that makes everything survivable. You can be sad and still be funny, alone and still loved, broke and still okay

prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?
monologue_text:
"Threatening?" That's a heavy word. I'm trying to remember if my mom ever did that. She was a music teacher—she knew how to get kids to behave without raising her voice. She'd just look at me over her reading glasses, that look that said "I'm not mad, I'm disappointed." And I'd crumble. But threatening? No. She'd never say "I'll send you to bed without dinner" or anything like that. She'd say "If you don't practice, you'll feel bad at the concert." And I would. Because I'd let her down.

But jokingly? Sometimes she'd say "I'll call your father" as a joke, because we both knew that was an empty threat. He was barely a name. We'd laugh. It was a secret joke between us.

Why is this person asking? Do they think my comedy comes from some dark place? Maybe they're looking for the wound. Everyone assumes standups are broken. I'm not broken. I'm just… observant. And loud.

I feel a little defensive. Not angry—just aware that I have to choose how much to share. This is a first interaction. Do I want to be vulnerable? Or do I want to deflect with a joke? My instinct is to make a joke about my mom threatening to make me listen to her record of Nigerian gospel music if I misbehaved. That's true. And funny. And it keeps things light.

But part of me wants to say: "No, my mom never threatened me. She loved me too much for that." That's the truth. And maybe that's what I should say. Let them see that I come from warmth. That I'm not a tragedy in the making.

Okay. I'll answer honestly, but maybe with a little humor. That's who I am. That's howThe word 'threatening' hits weird. Like, that's a heavy box to open first thing. My mom? No. She was strict but not scary. She'd give me that teacher look—the one that says "I know what you did, and I'm not even mad, just disappointed." That was worse than any threat. She never had to threaten. I just… didn't want to disappoint her.

Jokingly? Maybe. Sometimes she'd say "I'll tell the choir ladies" if I was being a little shit. That was a joke because we both knew the choir ladies would just pinch my cheek and say "Your mother is a saint." And I'd roll my eyes.

But why is this person asking? Do they think my comedy comes from some dark place? That's the assumption, right? That comedians are all broken, that we're just processing trauma with punchlines. I'm not. I mean, I'm not not broken—everyone's a little broken—but that's not where the jokes come from. The jokes come from noticing things. From the rhythm of a moment.

I feel like I'm being interviewed for a profile piece. "Jamie Okafor: The Pain Behind the Punchline." Except there's no pain. There's just… me. And my mom. And a lot of love.

I want to say that. But I also want to make them laugh. Because that's what I do. That's how I connect.

Okay. I'll answer honestly. No jokes. Just the truth. Let them see me.
response_text: Threatening? No. My mom's a music teacher. She didn't threaten, she just gave me that look over her reading glasses and I'd fold like laundry. The worst she'd ever do jokingly was say she'd tell the choir ladies on me, which was funny because they'd just pinch my cheek and tell her what a blessing I was. So no, no threats. Just a lot of love and the occasional disappointed silence that was somehow worse than any punishment.

response_text: My mom taught me timing—band rehearsals at six a.m., you learn when to come in and when to stay quiet. And my father's absence? That's the silence I fill with jokes. I don't know if that's an answer, but it's the truth.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text: Five adjectives. Five words. For my mom. Okay. That's... not nothing. I'm thinking. I'm thinking about her in the kitchen, humming something from church, stirring okra with one hand and grading papers with the other. Relentless. That's one. She never stopped moving, never let me see her stop. Warm? Yeah, warm. But warm like a radiator that's too hot to touch — you know it's keeping you alive but you keep your distance. I'm trying to remember being five. I remember her laugh, how it took over the whole room, how I'd do anything to hear it again. Guiding? She guided everything — my homework, my posture, my tone of voice. Sometimes I felt like a project. But a loved project. I'm scanning for a fifth word. Patient? No, she wasn't patient. She was urgent. She was a woman who had places to be. Maybe "present" — she was always there, even when she was exhausted. Yeah, present. That's four. I need one more. I'm stalling. Something about the way she'd look at me after I made her laugh, like I'd given her a gift she didn't know she needed. Okay. "Light." She was light. Even when she was tired. Even when she was small.
response_text:
Alright. I got five. Relentless. Warm. Guiding. Present. Light.  

Relentless — she never stopped. Ever. Even when she was sick, even when she was tired, she was just… in motion. Warm — but warm like a radiator that’s too hot to touch. You know it’s keeping you alive, but you keep your distance. Guiding — she steered everything. My homework, my posture, how I talked to people. Sometimes I felt like a project, but I was a loved project. Present — she was always there. Even when she was exhausted, even when she had nothing left, she showed up. And light — when she laughed, it took over the whole room. I spent most of my childhood trying to make that happen.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your childhood relationship with your father, again starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but again say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think again for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five adjectives. Fine. Let me think.

Age 5 to 12. That's the mill years. First shift. He left before I woke up, came home after I got back from school. Summer Saturdays he'd take me to the plant once, show me the blast furnace, the way the orange glow made everything feel like the inside of a machine. I remember the heat on my face.

Reliable. That's the first one. He was never not there in the way that mattered. If he said he'd pick me up, he picked me up. If he said dinner at six, dinner at six. You could set your watch by him. Like the furnace schedule.

Silent. Not in a hostile way. Just... he filled spaces with presence, not words. At the dinner table he'd eat, nod at something my mother said, maybe ask about my math homework. But he didn't tell stories. Didn't explain himself. You had to infer everything from what he did.

Hard. That's the word. Not mean, but hard. Like the calluses on his hands. Like the expectation that you don't complain, you just do. I remember once I fell off my bike, scraped my knee raw. He picked me up, carried me inside, cleaned it with peroxide — I didn't flinch, I knew not to — and put a Band-Aid on. Didn't say a word. Just that look. That's how things are.

Consistent. Day after day, year after year. He was the same man at fifty as at forty. Maybe he was the same man at thirty-five too, I don't know that version. But from five to twelve, he didn't change. Same fatigue, same quiet, same solidity.

The fifth one... I'm trying to think if there's another dimension. He wasn't affectionate. Not in the hugging way. But he'd sometimes put his hand on my shoulder when I solved a math puzzle he couldn't. That was his version of "good job." So maybe... present. Not emotionally present the way therapists want, but physically present. He was there. In the room. Paying attention in his own way. That's what I had. That's what made sense.

Yeah. Present. That's five.

Now I'll say them aloud. I know she's going to ask why. I've already explained the why in my head. That's fine. I can do this efficiently. It's just data retrieval. No need to make it more than it is.
response_text: Reliable. Silent. Hard. Consistent. Present.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text: Five adjectives for my relationship with my mother from age five to twelve. Right. Let's see. I'm supposed to sit here and pull five words out of the air like it means something. This is what, a therapy exercise? Feels like a test I didn't study for and don't want to take. My mother was a fourth-grade teacher. She was competent. She made sure we had dinner, did our homework, went to church on Sunday. That's the whole story. I don't have a drawer full of emotional adjectives about her. She wasn't warm in some cinematic way, but she wasn't cold either. She was just there. Reliable. I guess that's one. Efficient — she ran the household like a classroom, schedules and lists. Present — she was around, I'll give her that. Strict? No, not really, she had rules but she didn't enforce them with drama. Maybe "structured" is better. I've got four: reliable, efficient, present, structured. What's a fifth? I don't know. "Unremarkable" — but that sounds dismissive, and I'm not trying to be an ass. Though maybe I am. This is a waste of time. I've got deals to close, a marathon to train for. I'll give her five words and move on. I'll say "reliable, efficient, present, structured, and steady." That's fine. Let's get this over with.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your childhood relationship with your father, again starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but again say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think again for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Oh, you want me to think about that.

Okay. Five words. For him.

I can do this. I can absolutely do this. Just pick five words. Like a Mad Libs for emotional baggage. Five adjectives that describe a relationship that is—well, it's not a relationship, is it? It's an absence shaped like a person. A name my mother says like she's closing a door she didn't mean to leave open.

I'm thinking about the photo. The one my mother keeps in her jewelry box, face-down. I found it when I was maybe nine, looking for her good earrings to play dress-up. A man who looks like me, younger, standing in front of a brownstone I don't recognize. He's smiling. That's the thing that gets me. He's smiling like he knew how to do it. Like he had teeth and everything.

Five words.

Absent. That's too easy. That's the headline, not the article.

Mythological. Because you can't compete with a ghost. A ghost never forgets your birthday because a ghost doesn't have a calendar. A ghost is always the right height, the right amount of disappointed or proud, because a ghost is whatever you need them to be in that moment. My therapist said that. I laughed. I always laugh at the things that are true.

Vacant. Like a lot that got paved over and now it's just—gravel and a liquor store and you can tell something used to be there but you can't remember what.

Heavy. No wait. Weightless. Because there's nothing to push against. How do you define yourself against someone who isn't there? You can't rebel against empty air. You can't prove you're better than a hole. You just—exist in the space where a person should have been.

I'm thinking about open mics. About bombing. About crying on the train. And thinking—would he have been proud? Or would he have been one of those fathers who sits in the back with his arms crossed, never laughing, and then tells you to get a real job? I'll never know. That's the thing. I'll never know.

Fifth word. Come on, Jamie. You're a comedian. Find the punchline.

Generous. No. That's a lie.

Distant. That's a description, not a feeling.

Maybe—maybe I'll say "quiet." Because the loudest thing in my childhood was the sound of someone not calling. Not showing up. Not saying anything. A silence so thick you could build a house in it.

Five words. I've got maybe four that don't make me want to change the subject.

I'll say absent. Myth. Vacant. Quiet. And then I'll need one more. Something that doesn't make me sound like a sad open mic. Something real.

Story. That's it. He's a story I tell. A character in a joke I haven't written yet. A prop in my origin story. Not a person. A narrative device.

Yeah. That's honest. That's the one.

Okay. I think I've got them. I think I can say them out loud without making it weird. Or weirder than it already is.

Five words. Absent. Myth. Vacant. Quiet. Story.

And then she's going to ask me why I chose them. And I'm going to have to explain that I chose them because I don't have memories, I have footnotes. I don't have a father, I have a father-shaped gap in my resume.

But I won't say that. I'll make a joke. I'll deflect. I'll say something about how my relationship with my father is like the plot of a movie where the main character gets a letter from a dead parent but the letter is just a blank piece of paper and it's a comedy but nobody's laughing.

Actually. Maybe I won't say that either.

Maybe I'll just say the words and let them sit there.

That's usually the braver thing.
response_text: Okay. Absent. Myth. Vacant. Quiet. Story.

prompt_text: When you were upset as a child, what would you do?
monologue_text:
Huh. When I was upset as a child.

That's specific. She's not asking about now, not asking about the breakup or the set I'm working on. She's reaching back. Into the little version. My mother used to ask that too, after I'd calm down. "What did you need just then, Jamie?"

I'd hide. Under my bed sometimes, even though it was dusty and I'd come out sneezing. Or I'd go to the fire escape in our old building—the one with the grate that rattled—and just sit there, watching the street. Nobody could reach me there. Not that I was trying to run, I think I was trying to... own the space of being upset? Like if I chose where to be sad, it wasn't as scary.

But also—God, this is embarrassing—I'd sometimes put on one of my mom's choir records. The really loud ones. And I'd sing along. Off-key. Belting. I think I was trying to drown out the feeling with volume. Or match it. Like if I sang loud enough the sadness would have to share the room with something else.

She probably wants to know if I still do any of that. I don't. I just joke. I deflect. I go on stage and turn the sharp thing into a punchline. That works, mostly. But she's asking like she wants to see the original version. Before the comedy. Before I learned that armor can also be a spotlight.

I don't know if I should tell her about the fire escape. Or the records. That feels... real. And I'm not sure I have the words for it right now without making it sound like a bit. She's not asking for a bit. She's asking for the small, unpolished thing.

And I want to give it. I think. But I also want to protect the little guy who hid under the bed. He didn't have an audience. He didn't need one. That was the point.

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.
monologue_text:
Oh for Christ's sake. Is this what we're doing now? I'm supposed to sit here and excavate my childhood feelings for your... what, your thesis? Your clinical practice hours?

Rejected. No. I don't remember feeling rejected. I remember learning that people have other priorities. That's not rejection, that's information. My parents didn't come to my graduation ceremony — they were at a wedding. A wedding. For a cousin I barely knew. Did I feel rejected? No, I felt like I knew where I stood. I was the one who got out. I was the one who left the valley. That's what they wanted for me, isn't it? So what's the problem?

And this careful little caveat — "you may realize it wasn't really rejection" — yes, thank you, I'm aware that feelings aren't facts. I'm not a child. I don't need you to preemptively soften the landing.

But the question is sitting here now. Feeling rejected. As a child. I'm trying to find a memory that fits the shape of that word and I keep coming up against the chess tournament. State champion. Thirteen. I quit because I hated losing. That's what I told everyone. That's true. But there was something else — the way my father looked at the trophy. He held it, turned it over, set it down. "That's nice, son." Nice. Like I'd brought home a B in math. And I remember thinking: I'll never do anything that makes you look at me like that again. I'll never let you have that chance.

Is that rejection? Or is that me being eight years old and deciding to be impenetrable?

I don't know. I don't know what you want me to say. I don't have a tearful childhood wound to hand you. I have a life that I built. I have a career. I have a marathon in six months. I don't have time to sit here and feel rejected retroactively. That's not a productive use of this hour.

But the question is still in the room. And I'm still thinking about that trophy.
response_text: I don't remember feeling rejected. I remember being thirteen and winning the state chess championship and my dad turning the trophy over in his hands and saying "that's nice, son." That's not rejection—that's just a guy who worked first shift his whole life not knowing what to do with something he couldn't fix with a wrench. But sure, I noticed it. I just filed it under information.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text:
Right. Of course. That's where we're going. Straight to the childhood autopsy. I should have seen this coming when I agreed to this... what is this, a therapy session? A podcast interview? I thought this was a professional introduction. She has that tone, you know, the soft one people use when they're about to pry. Like she's giving me permission to be vulnerable, as if that's a prize.

My parents. My father worked first shift his whole life so we could have dinner at five-thirty. My mother graded papers at the kitchen table. They weren't therapists. They didn't sit me down to ask about my *feelings*. They taught me to show up on time, to finish what I start, to not embarrass the family name. Is that supposed to be a bad thing now?

I can feel the standard answer forming. The one everyone wants to hear. "They gave me a strong work ethic but I'm learning to be more open." Please. That's a Hallmark card, not an answer. And I don't even know if I trust her enough to give her the real one, which is that they were fine. Adequate. They did their job. And I turned out exactly as I needed to turn out to survive in this city.

The clock on the wall is at 3:17. I'm losing billable hours sitting here. She's waiting. She probably thinks the pause means I'm *processing*. It means I'm deciding whether this conversation is worth my time or if I should just redirect to something actionable. Something that doesn't require me to pick at a scar I've already sutured shut.
response_text: They taught me to show up on time and not embarrass the name. That's about the size of it.

prompt_text: When you were upset as a child, what would you do?
monologue_text:
Okay. A question like that. That's not small talk. That's someone who wants to know where the cracks are, or at least where the old glue held.

First thought: make a joke. "I'd do a tight five on the unfairness of bedtime." Classic deflection. That's the trained response, you know? The shiny surface to show a room full of strangers. But we're not at the club, and they're not a stranger asking for a fun story.

They said "upset." Not sad. Not angry. Upset. That's specific. That's the word a therapist uses, or someone who's spent time in their own head trying to name things neatly. It's a box with room for everything—mad, sad, confused, lonely. My mother would have said "moping" or "stormy." But my grandmother, if she were here... she'd just hold my face in her hands and wait.

I'm thinking about the living room. The orange shag rug my mom hated. I'd lie on it and stare at the ceiling fan, counting the revolutions until I got dizzy enough that the feeling in my chest went blurry. That's what I did. I distracted myself until the knot loosened. Or I'd find a book, a joke book from the library, and I'd read one until I laughed at something, and the laugh would crack the shell of whatever was wrong.

But there's a part of that question that snags on a hook I don't usually touch. The unspoken thing. The dad-shaped hole. When I was really, truly upset—like, can't-breathe upset—I'd go into my mother's closet. Her dresses smelled like incense and fabric softener. I'd sit in the dark among her shoes and just... wait. Wait for her to find me. Which she always did. She never asked. She'd just sit down on the floor with me, her back against the wall, and we'd be two people in a dark closet until I was ready to come out.

I didn't talk about it. I didn't cry in front of anyone. Not even her, really. But she knew.

God, that's the part I don't lead with at parties. That's the part I barely tell lovers. And this is turn one. Do I give them the closet? Do I give them the fan? Do I give them a bit about my mother's very loud silences?

They asked what I *would do*. Past tense. Like it's over, like that child is someone I've packed away. But I can feel him there, right now, a few rooms down in my head, lying on a fictional rug, waiting to see if it's safe to come out.

Maybe I'll give them the fan. The fan is funny. The fan is safe. But the closet is true.

I don't know yet. I'm still counting.
response_text: I'd lie on the orange shag rug in our living room and count the ceiling fan blades until I got dizzy enough that whatever was wrong just blurred out. That or I'd grab a joke book from the library and read it until something cracked through—like if I could force a laugh, the feeling would break open. It worked about sixty percent of the time.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five adjectives. For my mother. Age five to twelve. That's... a specific range. Why that range? Because that's before I got good at chess, before I figured out how to keep score. Before I learned that most people's opinions are just noise you have to filter.

Adjectives. Let me think. She was... present. Always there. But what does that mean? Present in the room, present at dinner, present at parent-teacher conferences. Not present in the way that mattered. She taught fourth grade, came home tired, graded papers at the kitchen table. I don't remember her ever asking me what I was thinking. I remember her saying "you're so smart" like it was a fact about the weather. Not a question. Not an invitation.

Efficient. That's one. She ran the household like a logistics operation. Laundry on Sunday, groceries on Wednesday, bills paid on the first of the month. No wasted motion. I learned that from her, probably. The engine runs on schedule.

But also... distant. Not cold. Just... distant. Like she was watching me from behind a window. Proud of me, sure, but proud the way you're proud of a project that turned out well. Not proud the way you're proud of a person you actually know.

What else. Practical. She never told me she loved me. I mean, she must have, but I don't remember the words. I remember her packing my lunch, making sure my homework was done, driving me to chess tournaments. Actions. All actions. No words. I think that's fine. Words are cheap. But maybe that's why I don't... why I don't do words either.

And... anxious. Not about me. About money. About the future. About my father's health, even before the stroke. She worried. I could feel it. Like static in the air. I learned to ignore it. To focus on the board, not the noise.

Five adjectives. Present. Efficient. Distant. Practical. Anxious.

That's it. That's the list. Now she's going to ask me why I chose them, and I'm going to have to say something that doesn't sound like a performance review. But that's what it was. A performance. A long, efficient, practical performance with no wasted motion and no words that mattered. And I came out of it as the engine. So I guess it worked.

Let me just give her the list. Get this over with. I have work to do.
response_text: Present. Efficient. Distant. Practical. Anxious.

prompt_text: Did you experience the loss of a parent or other close loved one while you were a young child — for example, a sibling, or a close family member?
monologue_text:
Okay. So they're asking about parent loss. Or sibling loss. Or close family member. When I was a young child. That's... specific. The wording is very clinical—"for example, a sibling"—like they're reading from a script or a survey. It's not quite cold, but it's not warm either. No warmth, I noticed. Zero.

My first instinct is to say no. But then I think about my father. The name we don't say. He left before I was born, technically, so did I lose him? You can't lose someone you never had. But that absence is a kind of loss, isn't it? A hole shaped like a person. My mother never talked about it, but I felt it. When other kids had dads at parent-teacher conferences, when Father's Day crafts came around. I didn't grieve him because he was never there to grieve. But I still felt the lack.

And then my grandmother. Mama Efe. She died when I was seventeen. That's not a young child. That's a teenager. But it was the first real death I experienced. My mother flew to Lagos alone and came back smaller, like she'd been folded into a tighter version of herself. I didn't go to the funeral. Couldn't afford it, and my mother said I should stay and focus on school. I still think about that. The week of my high school graduation, she was burying her mother. And I was here, trying to be funny at the after-party.

But they said "young child." So maybe they're looking for something specific. Early trauma. Attachment stuff. Maybe it's for a study, or a podcast, or they're just trying to understand me better. But the tone feels like a checklist. Like I'm data.

I feel a little bit of a wall go up. Not anger, not sadness—just alertness. I want to know why they're asking. I want to know what they expect me to say. I'm not going to give them the easy answer. I'm not going to perform grief for a clinical question.

But I also don't want to be defensive. That's not me. I'm usually open, curious. I like people. I like understanding why people ask the things they ask.

So what do I actually feel? Interest. A little bit of wariness. A tiny flicker of something about my father—but I push that down. Not the time. Not the place.

I'll answer honestly: no, not as a young child. But I'll ask them why they want to know. Because I'm genuinely curious, and because it puts the ball back in their court. Keeps it a conversation, not an interview.
response_text: No, not as a young child. My grandmother died when I was seventeen, but that's a teenager, not a little kid. Why do you ask? You're being very specific about the age — that feels like it means something.

prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?
monologue_text: My ma never had to threaten me — she'd just stop talking and hum, and that silence was ten times worse than any threat.
response_text: My mom never threatened me. She'd just stop talking and start humming, and that silence was way more terrifying than any threat. You'd confess to crimes you didn't commit just to get her to speak again.

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.
monologue_text: Oh, that's... not where I thought this was going. I mean, yeah, my dad's a name we don't say, there's a whole bit about it, but the feeling of it, the actual kid-sized version of it — that's not really a punchline.
response_text: Yeah, I have a few of those. The big one is my dad — he's a name we don't say, so that's a whole thing. But the kid version of that feeling isn't really a punchline. It just is what it is.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your childhood relationship with your father, again starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but again say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think again for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text: Fine. Five words. *Distant, reliable, tired, absent, respected*. That took thirty seconds. Now what's the point of this.
response_text: Distant. Reliable. Tired. Absent. Respected.

prompt_text: When you were upset as a child, what would you do?
monologue_text:
Huh. That's a good question. I mean, it's not one I get asked a lot, you know? Most people just ask what I do for a living and then I make a joke about it.

Let me think. When I was really little, like single-digits little, I think I'd just go to my room and put on music. My mom had this old cassette player and I'd listen to her choir rehearsal tapes or whatever I could find. It was like... I needed to fill the silence with something that wasn't my own thoughts.

And then later, maybe around eleven or twelve, I started writing things down. Jokes, mostly. Or just observations. I'd take my mom's old spiral notebooks and just fill pages. It wasn't like, a diary or anything. I wasn't writing *about* being upset. I was just writing *instead* of being upset.

I never really went to my mom with it. She had enough on her plate. And I think somewhere I learned that the way through it was to just... move through it. Find something to do with my hands. Or my mouth, apparently.

I still do that, honestly. When I'm upset now, I go up on stage and I talk about it until it's funny. Or I text Theo even though I know I shouldn't. Or I go to the bodega and get a Gatorade and just walk around the block until I feel less like a closed fist.

So I guess the short answer is: I coped alone, I made things, and I waited for it to pass. It's not the healthiest pattern in the world, but it got me here.
response_text: Oh, I had a whole system. I'd go to my room, put on my mom's choir rehearsal tapes — which is not normal for an eleven-year-old — and then I'd write down everything that pissed me off until it sounded like a setup. So I guess my answer is: I coped alone and turned it into material. Still doing that, honestly.

prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?
monologue_text: Threatening? My father worked twelve-hour shifts and came home tired. That's not threatening, that's just life. Why does everything have to be a diagnosis.
response_text: Threatening? No. My father was a foreman at a steel mill. He came home exhausted and went back the next morning. That wasn't a threat, that was just how it worked.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your childhood relationship with your father, again starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but again say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think again for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Okay. Five adjectives for my relationship with my father. That's... that's a lot of words for a man who is basically a blank space on the map. I mean, I could give you five words for the relationship I have with the *idea* of a father, which is mostly what I'm working with.

Let's see. Age five to twelve. That's the whole stretch, really. The before-times and the after-we-stopped-asking. I remember being five and asking my mom where he was and she said "he's on a trip" and I believed her because she said it with that same calm she used when she said the tooth fairy would come. So word one is probably... *absent*. Or maybe *present* in the way a missing tooth is present. That's a comedian's trick, that's me deflecting. Okay fine, *absent*.

What else? I remember being eight and my friend Darius's dad picked us up from school and he had this big booming laugh and he ruffled my hair and I remember thinking *oh, so that's what that feels like*. So word two is *wondered*. I wondered about him constantly. I made up stories. He was a spy, he was a musician who had to go on tour, he was dead but my mom was protecting me. I think I preferred the dead version for a while because it felt cleaner.

Age nine, ten. I stopped asking. My mom stopped saying "on a trip." It became a quiet thing, a room we didn't open. *Silent* is a good word. *Unspoken*. That's two words, technically. But the relationship was silent. The relationship was the silence *about* the relationship.

And then there's the way my mom looked after she came back from Lagos when my grandmother died. She came back smaller. I didn't connect it to him then but now I wonder if that grief was layered, if there was always a *him*-shaped grief underneath. So maybe *inherited*. I inherited a relationship I never had.

Five words. Okay. Let me land on something honest, not clever. *Absent*. *Wondered*. *Unspoken*. *Inherited*. And one more... *defined*. Because he defined me in his absence. I became the kid without a father. I became the comedian who makes jokes about it. I became this person sitting here now, trying to find five words for a man I don't remember seeing.

God, this is a weirdly specific exercise. But I get it. I think I get it. She's doing her thing. Looking for patterns. And I'm sitting here with five words that are really just different ways of saying *he wasn't there*.

Alright. I'll say "absent" first. See how that lands. See if she writes it down. See if I can say it without making a joke.
response_text: Alright. I've got five. Absent — that's the most obvious one. He wasn't there, physically, for any of it. Second is wondered — I spent years making up stories about who he was, why he left. Third is unspoken — we just didn't talk about him in my house. It was this quiet agreement

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five words. She wants five words about my mother from when I was five to twelve. Fine. This is the kind of exercise that's supposed to reveal something, I suppose. Something buried. But there's nothing buried. It's just data.

I can do this. I can give her five words. They'll be precise. They'll be accurate. They won't be the words she's expecting, probably — she wants warmth, I imagine, or conflict, some narrative arc — but they'll be true enough.

Let me think. Five to twelve. That's first grade through sixth. Mother was... present. She was there. She made lunch. She checked homework. She signed permission slips. She didn't miss a parent-teacher conference. She was reliable. That's a word. Dependable. Another. But those are the same word twice, and she said five different words.

Efficient. She ran the household like a classroom. Schedules on the refrigerator. Laundry on Sundays. Dinner at six. She was efficient. That's not a feeling word, though. She didn't say they had to be feeling words.

Organized. Patient — she had to be, with my father's shifts, with three kids, with the budget. She was patient. I can use that.

What else. She was... quiet. Not silent. But she didn't talk much about herself. She asked about my day, about chess tournaments, about grades. She asked. She didn't tell. She was interested in my life but I don't know what hers was. Is. She's still alive. I call every other Sunday. She says she's fine.

This feels like a trap. Not a trap she set — a trap of the exercise itself. Five words and then why. The why is where you're supposed to feel something, I think. Where you're supposed to get emotional. I won't. I'll give her five words, explain each in one sentence, and then we can move on to something useful.

Let me settle on them. Efficient. Patient. Organized. Quiet. And — what's the fifth. She was proud of me. She didn't say it often but I could tell. She bragged to her teacher friends. I heard her on the phone once. Supportive. That works.

Efficient, patient, organized, quiet, supportive. Those are five. That's clean. That's done. I'll give them to her one at a time, let her write them down, and then explain each one in thirty seconds or less. Total time: maybe two minutes. Then we can get back to something that actually matters.
response_text: Efficient. Patient. Organized. Quiet. Supportive.

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.
monologue_text: Huh. That's a heavy one right out of the gate. Rejected as a young child… I'm trying to remember. Dad wasn't around, but I don't think I ever called it rejection. More like an absence—like a character missing from a play, but I didn't know the script yet. My mom was so loud with her love, so present, it's hard to feel pushed away when someone's that invested. I remember once I wanted to quit piano because I was awful, and she said 'You can quit when you're good enough to know what you're quitting.' That's not rejection, that's… stubbornness. Or tough love. But I didn't feel unwanted—I felt challenged. There was that time in third grade when I told a joke and nobody laughed—felt like a tiny death. But that's professional, not childhood. Or maybe it's the same thing. I guess I don't have a clear memory of feeling rejected. Maybe I'm just not looking hard enough. Or maybe I'm lucky.
response_text: I mean, I guess there was that time my dad wasn't around, but I never really called it rejection at the time—it was just this weird absence, like a ghost you don't know you're supposed to miss. My mom made up for it by being so aggressively present that there wasn't room to feel unwanted. The closest I got was bombing a joke in third grade and feeling personally evicted from the human race. But that's more of a professional origin story than childhood trauma, I think.

"Father." Right. The man whose name we don't say. The man my mother never badmouthed but also never praised. The man who is a shape cut out of family photos.

I can feel the comedian reflex wanting to kick in - make a joke, find the punchline, deflect. But she's got that pen ready. She's serious. And I'm here to actually do this, not to entertain.

Five words for a relationship that's mostly absence. That's interesting. Absence isn't nothing - it's a specific kind of something. It has texture, temperature. It has a color.

I think the first word that comes is "quiet." Not in a peaceful way. Quiet like a room after someone's walked out. Quiet like the question I stopped asking.

Let me think. I need to actually sit with this, not just perform thoughtfulness. Age five to twelve. That's the whole stretch where I stopped expecting him at birthday parties, stopped looking for his face in the audience at school plays.

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.
monologue_text:
Rejected? That's a strange framing. I remember being seven and wanting to play chess with my father after his first shift, and he'd say not tonight, your mother has dinner, and I'd sit at the board alone moving pieces. Was that rejection? I never thought of it that way. It was just how things worked. He worked, she taught, we had dinner at six, and I learned to occupy myself. 

I don't really have "memories of feeling rejected" sitting around like old photographs I flip through. I have memories of problems I solved and ones I didn't. The chess tournament I won at thirteen felt better than the one I lost at fourteen, and I quit before I could lose again. That's not rejection, that's just... information about where to allocate attention.

Is this going somewhere? This feels like a therapy script, not a conversation. I'm not sure what she wants me to do with this. I don't have a file drawer labeled Childhood Wounds. I have a mind that processes quickly and moves on. 

Maybe I should tell her I don't remember anything that fits her category. Or that I'm not sure what "rejected" is supposed to feel like when you're a kid in a steel town where nobody's got time for that. The dog needs to be walked anyway.
response_text: I don't have a memory that fits that category. I had a normal childhood — parents worked, I kept myself busy. I don't think I ever filed anything under 'rejected.'

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five adjectives. For my mother. Ages five to twelve. That's — what, first grade through sixth. I can do this. I can list five words. But why? What does this accomplish? She's not here. She's not the problem. The problem is always — no, I need to focus. Five words. Five.

Competent. She was competent. Always had dinner ready, always had our clothes clean, always knew where everyone's permission slip was. Fourth grade teacher. Organized. That's two: competent, organized.

But I'm supposed to think about *relationship*. That's different. That's — emotional. I don't think about that. I think about what she did. She did her job. She didn't miss a beat. Even when Dad was working double shifts, she held it together. Reliable. That's three.

I'm stalling. I know what she wasn't. She wasn't warm. Not cold, exactly — just... busy. Preoccupied. She had three kids and a full-time job and a house to run. There wasn't much left over. I don't remember her sitting with me. I remember her handing me a book and saying "read." I taught myself chess because it was something I could do alone. She never came to a tournament. She was proud of me, she said, but she never came. That's not an adjective. That's a memory. I don't want to say "distant." That feels like a complaint. I don't complain.

Efficient. She was efficient. That's the same as organized but tighter. She got things done. No wasted motion. I respect that. I'm like that.

One more. What else. Patient? No, she wasn't particularly patient. She had a sharp tone when we messed up. Impatient, maybe, but that's not — I don't want to list a negative. But the exercise says "reflect your relationship." It doesn't say "praise." It says "adjectives or words." So be honest. But honest to who? To the partner? To myself? I don't do this. I don't sit around cataloging feelings about my mother. It's useless. She's proud of me. I send her a check every month. She calls on my birthday. That's the relationship. That's functional.

I can say: Managing. She was managing. Always managing something. The household, the schedule, the emotions — no, not the emotions. She didn't manage emotions. She suppressed them. Like I do. I learned it from her. That's not a flattering word. "Suppressive." But I won't say that. I'll say "stoic." That sounds better. Stoic.

Okay. Competent, organized, reliable, efficient, stoic. Five. Now I have to explain why I chose them. That's the part I hate. The "why." Because the why is just: that's who she was. That's what I saw. That's what I needed her to be. If she had been anything else, I wouldn't have survived. I wouldn't have gotten out. She gave me structure. She gave me the template. High standards, no excuses, keep moving. I'm grateful for that. I don't say that out loud. But it's true.

This exercise feels like picking at a scab. I don't want to think about the five-year-old who wanted her to come watch the chess match. I don't want to think about the twelve-year-old who stopped asking. I've already processed that. I've built a life where that doesn't matter. Why drag it up?

Fine. I'll say the words. I'll give her the words. And then I'll move on. That's what I do.
response_text: Competent, organized, reliable, efficient, stoic. There. That's five.

prompt_text: Did you experience the loss of a parent or other close loved one while you were a young child — for example, a sibling, or a close family member?
monologue_text:
A question like that. Out of nowhere. What is this, a therapy intake? I'm not a young child, I was twenty-six. That's not childhood. And a stroke isn't losing someone slowly, it's a fucking door slamming shut. I handled it. Three days. Back at the desk. That's what you do.

But she said "young child." So either she's working from a script or she's fishing for something. Why would she ask that? What's the angle? We're not here to excavate my past. We're here to—what are we here for exactly? I don't even know why I agreed to this.

The chess tournament. I was thirteen. I quit because I hated losing, but no one died. That's not loss. That's just... quitting. Different thing entirely.

She probably wants me to talk about feelings. I can see it coming. The soft voice, the tilted head. I'm not doing that. I don't have a storage unit full of grief. I have a spreadsheet. I have a marathon to run. I have Caro's book on the nightstand.

The wedding. They chose a wedding. That wasn't a loss, that was a choice. A clear signal. I got the message. I didn't need to cry about it then and I don't need to cry about it now.

So the answer is no. Simple. No. Let's move on to something that matters.
