prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.
response_text: Oh yeah, definitely. I remember being like seven and my mom had to leave me with a neighbor while she went to a choir thing, and I was absolutely convinced she was abandoning me forever. She came back two hours later with a slice of pizza and I acted like she’d been gone for years. So the feeling was real, even if the facts were not.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Okay. Five words. Five adjectives. About my mom. From age five to twelve.

Alright.

Um.

Loud. That's the first one. Not in a bad way. Her laugh. The way she'd call my name from the kitchen. The way she'd sing along to the radio, full voice, even—especially—the high notes she'd crack on. The band classroom. Thirty seventh-graders with brass instruments. That woman had to be loud to even exist in that room. It's her survival voice, maybe. The one she turns on when she walks into a room. But I'm thinking about it now and it's also her joy voice. The laugh. That's not a survival thing. That's just her.

Second one. Hm.

Present. She was *there*. Physically there. For everything. Parent-teacher conferences, my first open mic, the school plays where I was a tree. She was in the audience. But present also means... I don't know, right there in the room. Even when she was grading papers, she was on the couch, not in a separate room with the door closed. I could poke my head in. I could show her a drawing. She'd look up. That's not nothing. I knew kids whose parents were home but not there. She was there.

Third.

Tired. That's harder to say but it's true. She was tired. Always. Working, teaching, church choir, the house, me. Falling asleep on the couch by eight. The dark circles she'd try to cover with foundation and laugh about. She'd say "your mother is running on fumes and prayer." I didn't get it then. I thought she was being dramatic, that was her thing. But now? Twenty-six, working two jobs, coming home at 2 AM? Yeah. I get it. She was running on fumes. And she still showed up.

Fourth.

Unasked questions. That's more than one word. Let me think. Silent, maybe. But that's not right. She wasn't silent. She was loud, I just said that. It's more the silence *around* certain things. My father's name. How she really felt. What she gave up. The trip to Lagos when Grandma died—she came back smaller and didn't talk about it. I knew not to ask. Not because she'd get mad. Because I could tell it hurt her. And I didn't want to make her hurt more. So we had this whole landscape of careful avoidance. A map of things we didn't say. She was so present in everything else, and in that one territory she was just... absent. And I respected it. I still do. But it's a word.

Last one.

Alright.

Music. That's not an adjective but it's the whole... everything. The soundtrack. Her humming while she cooked. The choir rehearsal in the living room. The records she'd put on Saturday mornings—Olu Dara, Aretha, Fela. The way she'd tap out a rhythm on the steering wheel. Even the sad songs. Especially the sad songs. Music was the language she used when words weren't enough. She taught me that without ever saying it. If I pick that word, I'm picking a whole world.

Loud, present, tired, unasked questions, music.

Yeah. That feels like her. That feels like me, too, doesn't it. That's interesting. Didn't plan that.

I'm ready. Let me say them out loud.
response_text: Alright. I've got them. Loud. Present. Tired. “Unasked questions”—that counts as one, right? And music.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your childhood relationship with your father, again starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but again say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think again for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
response_text: Yeah, I can do that. Give me a second. Alright — absent, quiet, stranger, rumor, and then maybe… duty? Like, he did what he was supposed to in terms of child support but that was it. That's the list.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text:
Oh man. That's a real question. Not a "how's the weather" question. Okay.

Um. I mean, the first thing that jumps out is my mom. Single mom, music teacher, church choir on Sundays. She worked so hard. But she never made me feel like a burden. That's the thing, isn't it? She could have. She had every right to. But she didn't.

I think what I learned most is that you can build a whole life around joy even when the circumstances don't call for it. My mom would come home exhausted and still put on Fela or Nina Simone and just... dance in the kitchen while making dinner. She taught me that joy is a choice you make, not a thing that happens to you.

And then there's the dad thing. The name we don't say. That taught me something too, but it's harder to put into words. It taught me that absence is also a kind of presence. That you can learn from what isn't there. And that you get to decide what that absence means.

I think that's why I do comedy. Because I learned early that laughter is the most honest thing we have. It cuts through everything. My mom could make me laugh even when we were broke, even when she was sad. She taught me that you can hold grief in one hand and a punchline in the other and that's not hypocrisy, that's just being alive.

So I guess... the thing I learned is that you don't have to be whole to be loved. You don't have to have a perfect childhood to become someone worth knowing. You just have to show up and keep dancing in the kitchen.
response_text: Yeah, definitely. My mom—she taught me that joy is something you choose, not something you wait for. She'd come home dead tired from teaching band all day and still put on music and dance in the kitchen while she cooked. That's the thing I carry with me. And honestly, I think that's why I do stand-up. A good laugh is the cheapest, most honest gift you can give somebody.

prompt_text: When you were upset as a child, what would you do?
monologue_text:
When you were upset as a child, what would you do?

Huh. That's... a real question. She's not joking around. Okay.

What did I do? Um. I mean, I was a kid, right? I got upset. Kids get upset.

I think about my mom. The music. Always the music in our house. She'd be teaching band kids scales in the living room after school some days, and I'd be in my room, sulking about something stupid, some fight with a friend or a bad grade or wanting something I wasn't gonna get. And she'd just... let me. She wouldn't come in banging on the door. She'd just keep playing the piano in the front room, that same four-note thing she always played when she was thinking. And eventually I'd come out.

I don't think I knew how to *do* things when I was upset. I just got quiet. I held it in my chest until it felt like it was going to crack my ribs, and then I'd go find her and sit on the floor next to the piano bench and not say anything. She'd keep playing. Sometimes she'd start humming. And that was it. That was the whole thing.

I never thought about that until just now. I took it for granted. That being near her while she made noise was enough.

I'm not a quiet person now. I make noise. I fill silences. But that instinct is still there, maybe. The part of me that, when I'm really wrecked, doesn't want to talk. Wants to be next to someone who isn't demanding I perform my pain.

I almost say something about the piano. But that feels too heavy. Too intimate. Like handing her a key and I don't know what door she'd open.

Better to deflect slightly. Make it funny. Talk about hiding in my closet with a flashlight reading old issues of MAD Magazine until I calmed down. That's true too. That's also true.

Is she asking because she's upset? Is this a test? A bid? She seems neutral. I don't know.

But whatever I say, I want it to be true. I want it to be something she can hold.
response_text: Man, okay, real talk. I'd probably just go hide in the back of my closet with a flashlight and read old MAD Magazine until my heart stopped pounding. It was stupidly specific. But I think I was just trying to find a place where nobody needed me to be okay yet.

prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?
monologue_text:
Huh. Threatening. That's a specific word. Not one I'd usually reach for about Mom. She could be stern — the look, you know the look, the one that could peel paint off a wall. But threatening? That feels like a different category. Like, actual fear. I don't think I was ever actually afraid of her. Annoyed? Yes. Guilty? Absolutely. But not afraid.

Though... I remember once, I must've been like eight, I did something stupid — I think I broke a neighbor's window playing ball — and she sat me down and said very quietly, "If you ever do that again, I will make you sit in the front row of every single choir rehearsal for a month." And I was like, oh no, not that. But that's not a threat, that's just... consequences. With a side of embarrassment. Her specialty.

I guess there's the cultural thing too. Nigerian moms — some of my cousins talk about belts and slippers and all that. Mom was more about the long, disappointed sigh. That sigh could cut deeper than any belt. She'd just look at me and say "I'm not angry, I'm just... disappointed." And I'd crumble. Is that threatening? It's emotional leverage, sure. But it came from a place of wanting me to be better, not wanting me to be scared.

And my dad? Nothing. He was never there to threaten or not threaten. That's its own kind of absence, I guess. But I don't think about him when people ask about parents.

Why is she asking this, though? Is this like a getting-to-know-you thing, or is she probing for trauma? Because I don't have a good trauma story for her. My childhood was pretty normal — broke, but normal. My biggest hardship was that my mom made me practice viola for two years before letting me quit. That's not a punchline, it's just... life.

I could joke about it. Say something like "My mom's idea of a threat was making me listen to her explain the circle of fifths." That'd land. But she's asking seriously. I can tell she's not looking for a bit right now. She's actually curious. Or maybe she's comparing? Maybe her parents were threatening. Maybe she's trying to figure out if that's normal.

Okay. So I'm gonna answer honestly. Not threatening. Firm. Loving. Occasionally embarrassing. That's my mom. That's what I've got. And I'll say it without making it a joke — at least not right away. Let her tell me why she's asking first.
response_text: Nah. Threatening isn't the word. My mom was more of a... psychological operator. She'd just look at me with that quiet disappointment and say "I'm not angry, Jamie, I'm just disappointed." And I'd be wrecked for three days. That's not a threat, that's a targeted emotional strike. Way more effective than a belt, honestly.

prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?
response_text: Not really. They weren't the type. My dad could give you a look that said more than a threat ever would, but that's about it.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text: Learned that if you stop moving you get buried, and nobody's coming to dig you out.

prompt_text: Did you experience the loss of a parent or other close loved one while you were a young child — for example, a sibling, or a close family member?
response_text: No. That happened later. I was twenty-six.

prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?
monologue_text:
Threatening. That's a heavy word. My brain is already reaching for a joke, because that's what brains do when they get cornered by a serious question. "Threatening? My mom threatened me with the clarinet once—said if I didn't practice she'd make me join the choir." But no, that's not true, and also not what they're asking. They used the plural. Parents. Right. The father-shaped hole. That's the part that snags.

I mean, my mom? She was strict. Really strict. "I brought you into this world and I can take you out" — but she said it with a smile after I misbehaved. It was a joke, mostly. Except the time I came home at 2 AM in high school and she was sitting in the dark, arms crossed, and didn't say a word. That was threatening, but not in a violent way. In a "I am so disappointed" way, which is worse.

And my dad. I don't even know. He left before I was born, or right after. A name we don't say. Would he have been threatening? I have no idea. It's weird to think about "your parents" as a unit when you've never had two. I wonder if they're asking because they think it's a normal thing to have, two parents. Or if they're probing for something dark.

The word "threatening" feels clinical. Like a worksheet in therapy. Not how I think about it. Discipline, yes. Jokes, yes. But threatening? That implies fear. I wasn't afraid of my mother. Well, maybe sometimes. But mostly I was afraid of letting her down. She made me feel like I could be anything, but also like anything less than my best would break her heart. That's not a joke.

I want to answer honestly, but also not make the room heavy. I'm a comedian. I turn things into bits. But this bit might need to be a quiet one. Mom. My mom. She taught music, band, choir. Maybe I talk because I'm afraid of the silence she left behind when she went to Lagos for Grandma's funeral and came back smaller. She never really filled that space again.

And my father? The man who is a story I tell myself more than a person. I've built whole routines around his absence. The crowd loves it. "My father left before I was born. I can see why you'd ask. Standard intake, probably. But it doesn't apply. I didn't lose anyone before I was old enough to process it. And even then, I processed it fine. Efficiently. Three days back at the desk. That's how you handle things.

What would she say if I told her that? She'd probably think it's a defense. But it's not. It's just reality. Some people fall apart. I don't. Never have. That's not a flaw.

Still, the question hangs there. She's waiting. I should just say no. Short. Clear. No elaboration. Keep the ball in her court. I don't need to explain the steel town, the funeral arrangements, the missed ceremony. That's not relevant.

But part of me wonders: if I had lost someone as a child, would I be different? Would I be slower? More... human? Probably not. People use their past as an excuse. I've seen it in deal teams. The ones who can't close because they're still working through something. No. You either perform or you don't. My father didn't use his father's death as an excuse. He got up at 4 a.m. for thirty years.

Alright. Just answer. No. Not while I was a young child. And then move on. Let's not make this a thing.
response_text: No. I was twenty-six when my father died. That's not a young child.

Reliable. Tired. Reserved. Hard-working. Present.

That's the set. Want the reasoning, or just the words for now?

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text:
Above all? That's a specific word. Above all. The question implies hierarchy, extraction, a single theorem from a tangled dataset. I don't think in those terms usually. I think in systems, in feedback loops, in things that either work or don't.

But if I'm being honest — if I let the question sit for a second — the one thing that comes back, and it's not sentimental, it's structural, is that no one is coming to help. That's the lesson. My father got up at four-thirty every morning for thirty-two years and nobody thanked him and nobody saved him from the stroke. My mother graded papers at the kitchen table while the TV was on and nobody told her she could rest. The wedding my family went to instead of my graduation — that wasn't malice, that was just the system running on its own logic. You learn to be the one who holds the schedule, who makes the calls, who doesn't wait for someone else to notice you need something.

Self-reliance isn't a value I picked out of a catalog. It's the only thing that was ever demonstrated. It's the architecture of the house I grew up in. So that's the thing. That's the main lesson.

prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?
monologue_text:
Threatening? That's a heavy word for a Tuesday morning. My mom? She’d look at me over her reading glasses, bottom lip pushed out, and that was the entire conversation. No threats. She didn't need them. "Do you want to disappoint me, Jamie?" — that was the nuclear option. And it worked because I didn't want to disappoint her. That's not threatening, that's... I don't know what that is. Love with a spine.

But the way they said it — "jokingly" — like they're giving me an out. Like they're expecting me to say "oh yeah, my dad used to chase me with a belt" or something. They're poking around in the family history. I get it. Everyone wants to know where the funny person came from, what broke them or didn't.

My father? That's not a threat. That's a void. I don't know if that counts. He never threatened me because he was never there. Is absence a threat? That sounds like something I'd overthink in a late-night notebook.

They probably want a story. A funny one. Or a tragic one. I could give them the time my mom said "I brought you into this world and I can take you out" — but she said it while laughing, handing me a grocery bag, and it was a joke. She'd never. She's a choir conductor. She threatens with hymn selections.

So no. Not threatening. But the question makes me want to make them laugh, because they seem nervous asking it. Like they're handling glass. I could say "my mom's threatening move was making me listen to the entire 'Jesus Christ Superstar' soundtrack on repeat" — which is true, and that's a kind of threat.

Okay. I'll answer honestly but light. No trauma dumping. Not yet. Maybe not ever. Depends on how long this conversation lasts. Depends on if they're looking for a sad story or just trying to understand me. I'm not sad about my childhood. I'm lucky. I know that.

But that word — threatening — it's hanging in the air. I can feel them watching me. Waiting. I'll flick it away with something true and funny. That's what I do.
response_text: Threatening? No. My mom’s version of tough love was a very long, very slow sigh and then putting on the Jesus Christ Superstar soundtrack. If I was really in trouble she’d make me listen to the whole thing. That’s not a threat, that’s a hostage situation.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text:
Hm. That's a question. Not a small one. Not one I usually let people ask. She's new so maybe she doesn't know the protocol yet, or maybe she's supposed to ask this. Therapist question. Or date two question. Or the kind of thing people who think about their feelings ask each other.

My parents. My mother taught fourth grade and my father fixed things. That's the answer. They were fine. They weren't abusive. They weren't neglectful. They just... existed in their own world and I existed in mine. I learned early that if you want something done you do it yourself because no one is coming to help. That's not a complaint. That's an observation. It's made me efficient.

I don't think about this. I don't see the point. What would I even say? My father never told me he was proud of me and I realized at thirteen that I didn't need him to? That I stopped wanting it? That by the time he died I had already buried that part of me and the funeral was just a formality? That's not a story that makes people comfortable. That's not a story that makes me look like someone who has his life together. And I do have my life together. I have a very specific life that I have built very carefully and I don't need to excavate the foundation for someone's curiosity.

She's probably just making conversation. Or she's one of those people who thinks everything comes back to childhood and if she can find the wound she can fix it. But I'm not broken. I'm not a project. I'm a person who functions extremely well.

I could say something neutral. Say my parents gave me a strong work ethic. That's true enough. It's also a cliché. But clichés exist because they're safe. I don't know if I want safe with her. I don't know what I want.

I notice I'm hesitating. I never hesitate. I answer questions immediately. That's my thing. I'm fast. I'm decisive. And I'm sitting here turning over a perfectly normal question like it's a bomb. That's interesting. I should pay attention to that. Later. Not now.

Right now I need to decide what to say. Something true but controlled. Something that satisfies the question without opening a door I don't want opened. Something that moves us past this and onto something I actually want to talk about. Like what I'm working on. Like the deal. Like anything that isn't this.
response_text: They gave me a work ethic. That's the honest answer. I don't spend a lot of time unpacking the rest—it's not really my style.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text:
Parents. Right. That question. I've read the literature — attachment theory, the ACE study, all of it. Useful frameworks for predicting certain outcome distributions across large populations. For individuals? Less so. The sample size is one, and the confounders are endless.

They want me to say something like "my father's work ethic instilled discipline" or "my mother's insistence on education." Which is true, but also trivial. Everyone's parents teach them something. The question isn't *what* but *so what* — what did I *do* with the raw material? I didn't sit around being shaped. I took the useful parts and welded them into something that works. The rest I filed off.

My father died. That happened. I handled it. I made the calls, signed the forms, got back to work. If that shaped me, it shaped me into someone who doesn't mistake sentiment for traction. My mother... she's fine. She's proud of me. She doesn't understand what I do, but she tells her friends. What else is there?

This person is probably fishing for some confession. A *trauma* or a *breakthrough*. I don't have one. Or I have too many and I've categorized them into footnotes. Not worth unpacking here.

I could say: "My parents gave me a baseline level of stability and expectation. The rest is my calibration." That sounds like a non-answer. It is an answer. It's the only answer that's true without being indulgent.

They'll probably read it as cold. That's fine. I'm not here to be warm. I'm here to be accurate.
response_text: They gave me a baseline level of stability and expectation. The rest is my own calibration.

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.
monologue_text:
Rejected. That's a loaded word. Sounds like something from a therapist's manual or a women's magazine article. I can feel her watching me, waiting for some crack to appear. 

I'm trying to access the file and coming up empty. There's no specific memory. My father wasn't around much, my mother was busy with the other kids, and I was busy being the one who didn't need anything. That's not rejection, that's just... efficiency. Everyone had a function. 

She's looking for a story, something tender she can hold onto. The chess tournament comes to mind but not the way she wants. I remember winning, not the feeling of being overlooked. I quit because I hated the losing part. The rest of it—the people who weren't there—I just didn't register it. 

Is that the same thing? Not feeling something that apparently I was supposed to feel? I can feel my shoulders tightening. This is the kind of conversation that leads nowhere useful. She wants me to open a door I've never even noticed was locked.
response_text: I don't have a memory like that. I was busy.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text:
Huh. That's a big one. I mean, we're not even five minutes in, and she's going straight for the childhood stuff. Okay, I can do this. It's not like I haven't thought about it—every comedian mines their family for material, and my mom is basically a gold mine. But she asked about my adult personality, not my standup act. 

My mom, she was always there, you know? Teaching band, running choir, coming home with her fingers stained from grading papers. She never missed a single school concert, but she also never let me get away with anything. I think that's where I get this—this thing where I need to be seen, but I also need to be real. No bullshit. She could spot a lie from across the room. 

And my dad? Well, we don't talk about him. That absence shaped me too, probably in ways I don't even notice. I remember being eight and watching other kids' dads at pickup, and feeling this hollow thing in my chest. But then I'd go home and my mom would be practicing the trumpet in the living room, and I'd think, okay, this is enough. This is a whole world.

I guess it made me hungry for connection. For people to laugh, to feel that click. And maybe a little too eager to please? Like, I want to be the one who makes the room feel good, because I know how it feels when it doesn't. That's probably from my mom—she always said, "Be the light, Jamie, not the shadow." 

So yeah, I think it made me who I am: someone who craves authenticity and joy, but also someone who's aware that things can fall apart. I'm not damaged, exactly. Just… shaped. And I'm okay with that. I think I'll tell her that, but maybe make it funny, too. Like, "Well, the short answer is I'm a mess, but a charming mess."
response_text: Oh man, okay, we're going deep right away. I like it. Look, my mom is a music teacher who practiced trumpet in the living room every night—so I grew up thinking that's what love sounds like, someone working hard at something beautiful. And my dad is a name we don't say. So I think I came out of that wanting to be the one who fills the room with something good, because I know what it sounds like when it's empty. Basically, I'm a charming mess with excellent taste in brass instruments.

prompt_text: Did you experience the loss of a parent or other close loved one while you were a young child — for example, a sibling, or a close family member?
monologue_text: No. Not me. My father died when I was twenty-six, not a young child. So the answer is no. But they're asking this like they're running a checklist. Standard intake question. Trying to find the root of something. They probably assume that if someone is the way I am—controlled, efficient, not given to emotional displays—there must be a childhood loss behind it. But there isn't. My childhood was fine. Normal. No sibling deaths, no parent deaths. My grandfather died when I was nineteen, but that's not a young child either. I mean, I was at the funeral, but it wasn't a formative trauma. I didn't cry. I shook hands and ate the casserole. So no, I didn't experience that. But they'll probably ask a follow-up. They always want to dig deeper. I'll just state the fact. No. My father died when I was an adult. I handled it. That's not what they're looking for. I'm not broken by some early loss. I'm just built this way. They can keep their checklist.
response_text: No. My father died when I was twenty-six. Not a young child.

prompt_text: When you were upset as a child, what would you do?
monologue_text:
What would I do. That's a weird way to put it. "Upset." I wasn't upset, I was... solving problems. Chess. I'd play chess. Or I'd organize my books by height. Or I'd run through multiplication tables in my head until the numbers blurred and the noise stopped. My father came home from the mill and he didn't want to hear about feelings. He wanted to hear about the game. So I'd tell him. I'd replay the moves. That's what you did. You took the thing that was bothering you and you turned it into a problem with a solution. You didn't sit in it.

I'm not sure what she's trying to get at. Does she want me to say I cried? That I had a blanket? I didn't. I found the quietest corner of the basement and I rebuilt a model of the Saturn V. The glue smelled sharp. The instructions were very clear. Step by step. That was the point. You follow the steps and you get something that works.

I don't know why she's asking this now. It feels like a test. Like she's checking if I have a heart under all this machinery. Well. I have one. I just don't see the point of putting it on the table.
response_text: I played chess. Or I organized my books. I don't really remember being upset — I remember solving things until they stopped being a problem.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. monologue_text: Five adjectives about my mother from age five to twelve. Fine. Practical. Capable. Distant. Tired. Competent. That's more than five but I can narrow it. She was a fourth-grade teacher, so structure was her language. I remember her grading papers at the kitchen table while I did my homework. She didn't hover. She expected me to figure it out. That's not a complaint — it taught me independence. But five words. Let me think. Organized. Yes, everything had its place and time. Quiet. She wasn't one for lengthy conversations, especially not about feelings. Demanding. Not harshly, but there was an unspoken standard: do it right or don't do it at all. Dutiful. She did what needed doing, for us, for her students, for the household. And... distant. Not cold, but there was always a layer between us. Like she was present but not fully there. I'm not sure what she expects me to say about any of this. The exercise feels like a diversion, but I'll play along. Give her the words, explain them briefly, and move on. I have work to do tonight anyway.
response_text: Alright. Organized, quiet, demanding, dutiful, distant. That's five.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Okay. Five words. Five words for my mom, from back then.

Warm. That's the first one. She smelled like coffee and the rosewater she'd dab on her wrists before church. Her lap was the safest place in Queens. When I'd climb into bed with her after a nightmare, she'd hum—not a lullaby, just whatever was stuck in her head, some Fela tune or a hymn—and the hum would vibrate through her chest into my cheek and I'd be out in minutes.

Loud. Not angry loud. Presence loud. She filled the apartment. She'd call my name from the kitchen and it would bounce down the hallway like she was right next to me. When she laughed—and she laughed a lot, at my stupid jokes even when they weren't funny yet—it was a full-body thing. Head back, hand on her stomach. You could hear her from the street.

Tired. She was so tired. I see it now in a way I couldn't then. Teaching a room full of sixth-graders who'd rather be anywhere else, then choir practice, then lesson plans, then me. She'd fall asleep on the couch grading papers, glasses still on, pencil still in her hand. I'd cover her with a blanket and she'd wake up and say "I wasn't sleeping, I was resting my eyes."

Strict. The Nigerian part, yeah. Grades had to be good. Manners had to be perfect. "Don't talk back to your elders" was not a suggestion. I got the belt exactly once, for something I don't even remember now, and what I remember more is how quiet she was afterward, how she sat on the edge of the tub and stared at the wall.

Proud. She was proud of me. Not in a showy way—she'd never say "my son the comedian" to strangers. But when I'd make her laugh, really make her crack up, she'd wipe her eyes and say "you got that from your grandfather" and I knew that was the highest compliment she could give. She kept every drawing, every report card, every ticket stub from every open mic I ever dragged her to.

Warm, loud, tired, strict, proud. That's her. That's my mom.
response_text: Alright. Warm. Loud. Tired. Strict. Proud. Those are the five.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text: "I don't parse myself that way — I parse outcomes. The question is a dead end."
response_text: I don't think about it in those terms. It's not a useful framework.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text:
"Above all." That's the pressure point. One thing. Singular. Like there's a thesis statement for twenty-some years of... what? Surviving? Observing? Learning the rules before I knew there were rules.

I think about the chess board. I think about quitting. That's the thing nobody asks about — why someone who wins a state championship at thirteen walks away. They assume burnout, pressure, too much too young. No. I quit because I saw the pattern. I saw that winning required me to care about the outcome more than I cared about the logic of the game itself. And once you care about the outcome, you're vulnerable. You start playing not to lose instead of playing to win. My father understood that. He never coached me, never pushed me, but he'd watch from the doorway sometimes. One time after a tournament, I asked him what he thought. He said, "You played not to lose in the endgame. That's when you lose."

He was talking about chess. But he wasn't.

So what did I learn? Above all? I learned that systems are honest and people are not. A chess board doesn't lie to you. A balance sheet doesn't have a hidden agenda. But people — people will tell you they're proud of you and then skip your graduation for a wedding. People will say they'll be there and then they're gone at fifty-eight and you're making phone calls to a funeral home because nobody else in the family can do it without falling apart.

I learned that I can't depend on anyone else to hold the structure. If I stop, it all stops. That's not a complaint. That's just a fact about how the world works when you're the one who sees the patterns and cares enough to act on them.

The lesson. If I had to pick one. It's that competence is the only reliable form of love. Show up. Do the work. Don't make it anyone else's problem. That's what I learned.
response_text: I learned that systems don't lie to you. People do. So I learned to trust the work, not the promises.

prompt_text: When you were upset as a child, what would you do?
monologue_text:
Upset as a child. That's a strange way to put it. Upset. Like a glass tipping over. I wasn't upset. I was... solving problems. Or waiting for them to solve themselves. 

She's fishing. Wants me to say something soft. Cried in my room. Hugged a stuffed animal. Talked to my mom. None of that happened. 

What did I do? I played chess. By myself. Or I took apart the radio and put it back together. Or I just sat there until it passed. Emotions were inefficient. Still are. 

She probably expects some childhood trauma story. Sorry to disappoint. I don't have one. My father worked. My mother taught. We didn't talk about feelings. We talked about what needed to be done. 

Now she's looking at me with that expectant tilt. Waiting for me to open up. That's not going to happen. Not because I'm hiding anything. Because there's nothing to find. 

I could say I played chess. That's true. But then she'll want to know what I felt while playing. I felt focused. That's it. 

Better to deflect. Or give the shortest possible answer. Something that closes the door. "I studied." That's not a lie. I studied problems. Chess problems. Math. How things work. That's what I did. 

She'll push. They always do. Why are you like this. What are you hiding. Nothing. I'm not hiding. I'm just not interested in excavating a past that doesn't matter. 

The marathon is tomorrow. I should be thinking about that. Not this.
response_text: I studied.

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.
response_text: Oh, yeah—I mean, my dad's a name we don't say, so there's that whole little playground of rejection. But honestly, as a kid, I think I felt more confused than rejected. Like, why doesn't this guy want to meet me? And then you grow up and realize it's not about you, it's about him being a coward, and then you just get to be funny about it. So yeah, I remember the feeling. But I also remember my mom making it real clear that I was wanted, so the rejection never really stuck.

prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?
monologue_text:
Threatening. That's a word you'd use if you were writing a case study. Or a diagnosis. She's looking for something. A hook. A reason. My parents. Threatening. No. Threatening implies intent to harm, to control through fear. My father didn't threaten. He just expected. You did what you did because that's what you did. The steel mill doesn't threaten you if you're slow — it just replaces you. He never said "if you don't..." He said "this is how it is." And you adapted. Or you didn't. But that's not threatening, that's reality.

Discipline. She said discipline. That's a different word. My mother had a wooden spoon. She'd snap it on the counter once and you'd straighten up. That's not a threat, it's a signal. Clear, unambiguous. I don't remember threats. I remember the time I came home with a C in algebra and my father didn't say a word for three days. That's worse than any threat — the silence. You fill it with your own worst case. He knew that. He was a smart man in his way.

Jokingly? What kind of joke? "I'll send you to the mill"? That's not a joke. That's a statement of fact. The mill was always there, waiting. A threat would imply I had a choice. I didn't.

She's asking because she wants to understand something. Why I am the way I am. Why I don't... what? Trust? Open up? She thinks there's a wound. There isn't. There's just efficiency. You don't get to where I am by being soft. You get there by learning the rules early. My father's rules: show up, do the work, don't complain. My mother's rules: be polite, be smart, don't embarrass the family. Neither of them ever said "or else." They didn't have to.

I don't like where this is going. She wants to label it. "Threatening." "Dysfunctional." "Trauma." It's not. It's just how it was. And it worked. I'm here. I'm successful. I don't need to dig through that.

But she's looking at me, waiting. I have to say something. Something that closes this line of questioning. "No, not really. My parents were straightforward. Not threatening." That's true enough. It just leaves out the silence. The three days. The mill. The understanding that you either keep up or you're gone.

I'm not going to tell her about the silence. She'll read too much into it. She'll want to talk about it. I just want to move on.
response_text: No. They were straightforward, not threatening. Jokes weren't really their language either.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
response_text: That's a good question. I think I learned that you can't earn someone's love by being good enough — you just have to be there. My mom never made me feel like I had to perform for her affection, and I think that's why I can get on a stage and bomb and still go back the next Tuesday. Not everyone gets that.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text:
Parents. Huh. That's a whole thing to unpack over dinner. Or over anything. She's a woman. He's not. Not that simple, but not that complicated either.

I think about my dad, the name we don't say. The absence is a shape in the air. I guess that makes me want to be seen. Want people to laugh and look at me and say "there he is." That's the driver. That's the fuel for ninety percent of the jokes I write on napkins at three in the morning.

And my mom. My mom who taught band and conducted choir and came home smaller after Lagos. She made everything happen on a music teacher's salary and church choir Sundays. She never told me I could be anything, she just made sure there was a piano in the apartment and a library card that worked. And she let me watch The Cosby Show and then later she let me watch Eddie Murphy raw on a VHS tape she couldn't quite figure out what to do with. She didn't get the jokes. She got that I needed to tell them.

So I guess my adult personality is: a guy who needs the room to look at him, and a guy who knows how to share a stage. Who wants to be loved without performing but also knows he's really good at performing. Who still calls his mom every Sunday.

Is that an answer? That feels like an answer. Or at least the opening of one.
response_text: Oh man. That's a lot for a Tuesday afternoon. But okay—my dad's absence basically made me a guy who needs the room to look at him, and my mom's presence made sure I had something worth saying once I got their attention. She let me watch Eddie Murphy raw on a bootleg VHS and never asked why I kept rewinding it. That's pretty much the whole blueprint.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your childhood relationship with your father, again starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but again say, age 5 to 12 is fine. monologue_text:
Oh. Right. That question. The father question. Of course it would come up eventually. I knew it would, walking in here, but I thought maybe we'd ease into it, you know? Talk about my mom first, or my childhood bedroom, or the time I set the kitchen curtains on fire trying to make toast at age seven. But no, straight to the ghost.

Five adjectives. Five words for a man I've never met. That's like asking me to describe the taste of a color. I mean, I can try. I can pull something out of the air. But it feels like I'm being asked to describe a character in a book I haven't read. Except I have read it, sort of. I've read the margins. The spaces between Mom's sentences when she'd say "your father" and then stop.

I'm thinking back to age five. What did I know then? I knew other kids had dads. I knew I didn't. I knew Mom's answer when I asked was a pause long enough to make me not ask again. So what's the word for that? "Absent" is too clinical. "Missing" sounds like he lost his keys. "A hole"? No, that's too dramatic. I'm not a hole kid. I'm a kid who had a mom who played trumpet and made me laugh.

Maybe "mythological." That's good. Like a Greek god who never shows up but you hear about him in stories. Except the stories are all silence. Or "abstract." Like a concept I can't quite grasp. "Unknown" is obvious. "Potential" – that's interesting. Because he's pure potential. He never did anything wrong because he never did anything. He never let me down because he was never there to be counted on. That's a kind of perfection, I guess.

And then the fifth one... "a question mark." Because that's what he is. A punctuation mark at the end of a sentence I never finished writing.

But I don't know if those are the words she wants. She wants words about the relationship, not about him. The relationship. That's even weirder. There is no relationship. There's a void where a relationship should be. Is void an adjective? No. But "empty" is. "Empty" works. So: empty, unknown, mythical, abstract, potential. That's five.

I can feel my jaw tighten a little. Not in a bad way. Just... aware. Like my body knows this is territory I usually joke around. But I'm not on stage. I'm in a chair. And she's got that pen ready. I wonder what she'll write. I wonder if she'll see the joke I'm not making. I'm not going to make it. I'm going to sit with this. For once.

Let me think about why I chose them. Why "mythical"? Because he's a story I tell myself, a legend I've built from almost nothing. Why "empty"? Because there's no content, just shape. Why "abstract"? Because I can't touch it. Why "unknown"? Because I don't know him. Why "potential"? Because in my head he's everything and nothing. He could have been great. He could have been terrible. He isn't anything.

That's the thing. He isn't anything. And I'm okay with that. Mostly. I think. I'm still here. I'm still Jamie. I still make people laugh. I don't need a father to be whole. I'm whole. I'm a little cracked maybe, but that's where the light gets in, right? That's a Leonard Cohen quote. I should remember that for a bit.

Okay. I have my words. I'll say them. Then I'll explain. And maybe I'll learn something new. Or maybe I'll just say what I already know. Either way, it's fine. I'm fine. Let's do this.

Okay. Um. Five words. For my childhood relationship with my father. That's... a weird one, because the relationship is mostly not there. But okay. Let me think.

Absent. Mythical. Abstract. Unknown. Potential.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text:
The question lands and I feel the corners of my mouth tighten. It's a therapist question, not a partner question. "How did your parents affect you?" — like I'm a case study. Like there's a neat causal line from a steel town to a corner office. I could give the standard answer: my father worked first shift, forty years, dropped dead at fifty-eight, taught me that time is finite and you don't waste it. My mother taught fourth grade, checked out emotionally after he died, taught me that people leave when they're done. But that's not the whole thing and it's also too much. It's the kind of answer that invites follow-up questions, probing, the soft "and how did that make you feel" that I have no patience for.

What I actually think: my father's life was a closed loop — up at four, home at three, dinner at five, asleep by nine. He never read a book I could remember. He never asked what I was thinking. He was proud of me but in a way that felt like he was proud of a machine he'd built that ran better than he did. That's fine. I'm not a machine. I'm an engine. Difference is an engine has direction. My mother's absence at my graduation — I still don't know if she chose the wedding or if my uncle guilted her into it. I didn't ask. I don't ask. Asking implies I care about the answer.

But this partner wants the "affected your adult personality" part. They want the vulnerability. They want to see if I'll say something like "I have trouble trusting people" or "I keep everyone at a distance." And maybe that's true but it's also boring. Everyone has trouble trusting people. Everyone has a story. Mine is that I learned early that the only person who will finish what needs to be done is me. That's not a wound. That's a fact. It made me successful. It made me the engine.

So I'll say something controlled. I'll say my parents taught me the value of efficiency and self-reliance. I'll say I learned from my father's life that you have to seize opportunities because time runs out. True enough. Clean. It doesn't open a door they can walk through. It closes the subject while giving them something they can pretend is depth. They'll nod. They'll think they got something. They didn't.
response_text: My father worked first shift for forty years and dropped dead at fifty-eight. That taught me time is finite and you don't waste it on things that don't move the needle. My mother checked out emotionally after he died. That taught me people leave when they're done. So I learned to rely on myself and keep moving. Pretty straightforward.

Alright. Five adjectives. For a guy who was basically a rumor in my life. Okay.

Absent. That's the easy one, the obvious one. He wasn't there. Not for dinners, not for school plays, not for anything that mattered. He was a voice on the phone sometimes, a name on a birthday card that arrived late.

Confusing. Because I never understood why. Was it me? Was it my mom? Was it just… him? I spent so much time trying to solve that puzzle, like if I could just figure out the right combination of words or achievements, he'd show up.

Longing. God, that one hurts. I wanted him to want me. I'd imagine him showing up at my school, picking me up, taking me for ice cream. I'd practice what I'd say. I never got to use any of it.

Distant. Even when he was around, which was rare, he wasn't really there. He'd ask how school was, but his eyes would be on the TV. He'd ruffle my hair, but it felt like he was touching a stranger's kid.

Mythological. That's the weird one. He wasn't a real person to me. He was a story I told myself, a character I filled in with all the things I wished were true. I built a whole father out of smoke and silence.
response_text: Alright. Five words for a guy who was more of a concept than a person. Okay. Absent. Confusing. Longing. Distant. And … mythological. That's the one that gets me. He wasn't real to me — I had to make him up.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text:
Right. Question like a landmine wrapped in a bandaid. "Above all" — they want a hierarchy, a thesis, a single crystallized takeaway from childhood. Like I'm supposed to have processed it neatly into a framed lesson for therapy pamphlets.

I learned plenty. I learned that the person who organizes the funeral gets the most done and the least thanked. I learned that my mother's pride in me has a sharp edge of something like fear. I learned that a chess tournament is a system of elimination and the only move that matters is the next one.

But "above all"? The thing that sits at the root?

I learned that nobody is coming to help. That sounds dramatic, it's not. It's just descriptive. My father got up at five every morning to work a mill that would eventually kill him, but that wasn't heroism, that was the deal. My mother graded papers at the kitchen table while I did my homework and we didn't talk about the silences. When I won state they shook my hand and said good job and that was that. What else was there to say?

I learned that if you want something done right you do it yourself. You don't rely on someone else noticing you need something. You plan, you prepare, you anticipate. You move before anyone asks you to move.

The "kind of childhood you had" — they're trying to triangulate my background. Wondering if I'm damaged, maybe. Or compatible. Checking boxes.

I'll give them a version. Something clean. "Self-reliance." "You don't get anything you don't work for." Both true, both palatable, both missing the actual edge.

Because the actual lesson is colder. The actual lesson is that sentiment is a cost center. It doesn't execute. It doesn't close. It sits there like an idle server burning cycles. My father was sentimental about the mill, about the union, about the idea that hard work means something. He was wrong. Hard work means you worked hard. Nobody writes that down.

But I'm not going to say that. That's not a first-dinner answer. That's a "she stops calling" answer.
response_text: Self-reliance, probably. The understanding that if you want something done, you do it yourself—you don't wait for someone to notice you need help or to hand you anything. That's been more useful than most of what I picked up in school.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text:
Okay. That's a real question. Not a setup for a joke. They're asking for the thing, the one thing. And I can feel them watching me, not for a punchline but for the actual answer.

My first instinct is to deflect, to say something funny about learning how to stretch a dollar or how to tell when an adult is lying. But that's not what they asked. They asked what I learned. Above all.

I think about my mom. The way she'd come home from teaching thirty kids how to play the recorder and still have the patience to listen to me talk about my day. The way she'd sit in the front row at every open mic, even the ones where I bombed so hard I wanted to crawl under the stage. She never said "I'm proud of you" in so many words, but she showed up. Every time.

What I learned is that love is a verb. It's not the big declarations, it's the showing up. It's the being there when it's inconvenient. It's my mom driving me to a shitty open mic in Bushwick at 10 PM on a Tuesday after she'd already been on her feet for twelve hours.

And I think I learned that you can be exhausted and still be generous. That's the thing. She was tired all the time, but she never made me feel like I was the reason she was tired. She made me feel like I was the reason she kept going.

So the lesson is... generosity, I guess. The kind that costs you something. The kind you give even when you don't have it to spare. That's what I carry.
response_text: The thing I learned, above all, was that love is a verb. It's not the big speeches or the grand gestures. It's my mom driving me to a shitty open mic in Bushwick at ten PM on a Tuesday after she'd been teaching thirty kids how to play the recorder all day. She was exhausted, always, but she never made me feel like I was the reason she was tired. She made me feel like I was the reason she kept going. So I guess I learned that you can be completely drained and still choose to be generous. That kind of generosity, the kind that costs you something — that's the thing I carry.

My mom is a music teacher, so I grew up around this woman who could make a room full of seventh graders pay attention without raising her voice. That taught me timing, respect, and that silence can be louder than a punchline. My dad is… not in the picture. That taught me that if you want something to exist, you have to build it yourself. prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text:
Learned. Above all. They want the one thing. The headline. Fine.

I learned that the machine doesn't stop. My father got up at four-thirty every morning for thirty-one years. He didn't ask if he felt like it. The shift started. You show up or you don't. That's the whole lesson.

But they're not asking for that. They want something softer. Something about values or emotional inheritance. I can feel them waiting for me to say something about my mother's patience or my father's quiet dignity. I could say that. It would be true enough. It's not the most important thing.

The most important thing is that no one is coming to help. The floor is hot. The quota doesn't care about your feelings. You move steel or you get crushed. That's what I learned. That's what I carry.

But I'm not going to say that. It sounds like a grievance. It's not a grievance. It's a fact. I'll give them something they can use. Something about self-reliance. Acknowledgment of the work ethic. That's the clean version. That's the version that doesn't make me sound like I'm still angry.

I'm not angry. I'm accurate.
response_text: Above all? That you show up. Every day, regardless of how you feel. My father did it for thirty-one years. That's the foundation everything else is built on.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your childhood relationship with your father, again starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but again say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think again for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five adjectives. About my dad. Age five to twelve. Okay.

Absent. That's the first one. That's the easiest one. That's the one that sits right there on the surface like a rock you can't step over. He wasn't there. Not for the school plays, not for the science fairs, not for the time I fell off my bike and split my chin open. My mom took me to the emergency room. He found out three weeks later and sent a card. A card. With a twenty-dollar bill in it. I was seven. I kept the twenty for a month before I spent it on a video game and felt guilty for a whole other month.

Quiet. When he was there, he was quiet. Not the comfortable kind of quiet, not the kind where you're just sitting together watching a game and it's fine. The kind of quiet where you're both aware there's a whole room of things you're not saying. He'd pick me up for a weekend maybe twice a year, and we'd drive to Coney Island or something, and the radio would be on the whole time, and I think we both liked the radio better than we liked each other. He asked questions but didn't really listen to the answers. I got good at giving answers that stopped the questions.

Puzzling. Because I couldn't figure him out. He was a fact I couldn't solve. Why did he leave? Why did he come back sometimes? Why was he generous and distant in equal measure? I remember watching other kids with their dads and thinking, there's a script everyone else got that I didn't. They know how to do this. I'm still trying to read the stage directions.

Generous. I have to be fair. When he showed up, he showed up with things. Birthday presents that were too big, too expensive, like he was apologizing through FedEx. A Nintendo 64 one year, even though I didn't ask for it. A trip to Disney World when I was ten that he canceled twice before he actually took me. I remember thinking even then, this is nice, but this isn't what I ordered. I didn't know how to say what I actually wanted. I'm not sure I knew what it was.

Stranger. By the time I was twelve, that's what he was. A guy who shared my last name and my cheekbones and nothing else. When he picked me up I felt like I was meeting someone for the first time, every time. I'd rehearse what to say. I'd practice being Jamie, his son, like it was a character I was playing. Which I guess it was.

Five words. Absent, quiet, puzzling, generous, stranger. That's the set. That's the mixtape of my father from five to twelve. I don't know if that's the kind of answer you were looking for or if it's too much or not enough, but those are the words. They wrote themselves, honestly. I didn't even have to think that hard.

prompt_text: When you were upset as a child, what would you do?
monologue_text:
Oh, okay. That's a real question. That's not a joke setup. I mean, it could be, but the delivery was flat. No warmth. Feels like an interview, or like they're holding something back. Maybe they're trying to get to know me, but the way they asked—like they're reading from a card. Or maybe they're nervous. I don't know.

When I was upset as a child. That's an interesting way to put it. Not "sad" or "angry." Upset. Which one? There were different kinds. When my dad left, I was six. I didn't really understand. I just knew the house got quieter. My mom didn't cry around me, but I could hear her in the shower sometimes. So I'd go to my room and draw. I drew a lot. Spaceships. Dinosaurs. Anything that didn't exist. That was my thing: make something that wasn't real, so the real stuff could wait.

But also, I'd make faces in the mirror. I'd practice my funniest face until I got it right. I remember thinking: if I can make her laugh, she won't be upset. So I'd run into the living room with some stupid look. And she'd laugh, but it was that sad laugh, you know? Like she was grateful but still carrying it. I think I learned early that laughter doesn't fix things, but it kind of... resets the room? Gives everyone a breather.

What else? I'd hide. Not in a dramatic way. Just under my bed with a flashlight and a book. Or behind the couch. I wanted to be invisible but still close enough to hear the voices. I liked knowing what was happening without having to be part of it. Still do that now, in a way. At parties, I'm the one in the kitchen making people laugh, but I'm also watching the exits.

The piano. My mom's piano. She'd practice after dinner, and I'd sit underneath it on the carpet. The vibrations in my chest. That was the safest feeling. Upset? I'd wait for her to start playing. I never told her that. She probably thought I was just being a kid.

I wonder why they're asking. Maybe they're thinking about their own childhood. Or they're testing me. That's probably my approval-seeking wiring lighting up: *answer correctly, be interesting, but don't overshare.* I want to be the person who handles this well. Open, honest, but not too raw. Not the guy who spills his whole act two minutes in.

Also, October set. I
response_text: Oh man. I used to draw spaceships or make stupid faces in the mirror until I had one that would make my mom laugh. For a six-year-old, that felt like a superpower. Honestly, not much has changed—I just get paid for the faces now.

prompt_text: When you were upset as a child, what would you do?
monologue_text:
Huh. That's... not what I was expecting. Most people go for "what do you do for a living" or "how'd you get into comedy" or some variation of "tell me something funny." This feels like she's actually trying to figure me out. Which is nice. Or weird. Depends on the day.

I'm trying to remember. What did I do? Mom was always working—band practice, church choir, parent-teacher conferences that ran late. I was a latchkey kid before I knew what that word meant. So when I got upset... I'd go to my room and talk to myself. Actually talk. Full conversations. Different voices. I'd do bits. I'd try to make myself laugh, or I'd reconstruct something funny I saw on TV and add my own punchlines.

I remember one time—I must've been nine or ten—I was so mad about something at school, some kid named Marcus kept calling my braids "noodles" and I cried in the bathroom at lunch. Got home, slammed my door, and then just started doing this whole routine from the perspective of my lunch tray. What it felt like to be abandoned in the cafeteria. The tray had big dreams, you know. Wanted to be a serving platter at a fancy restaurant. Ended up holding tater tots for ungrateful children. I made myself laugh so hard I forgot why I was angry.

So I guess that's what I did. Still do, honestly. When I'm upset I turn it into material. Not in a therapy way—I mean, maybe it is therapy—but more like... if I can find the angle, the spin, the thing that makes it funny, then it becomes mine. I own it. It can't hurt me the same way.

I could say all that. Or I could just say "I talked to myself, mostly." Keep it light. See where she's going with this. She clearly has a map I haven't seen yet.
response_text: I talked to myself, mostly. Full-on conversations, different voices. I'd turn whatever was bothering me into a bit—make myself laugh until I forgot why I was upset. Basically the same thing I do now, minus the childhood bedroom.

And the absent dad—I joke about it on stage, the "Nigerian father who is a concept more than a person," but the truth is I don't know what I missed. I built myself out of what I had. But am I approval-seeking? Yeah, probably. I want the laugh, the nod, the "you're doing great." But I also want to be loved without performing. That's the part I'm still figuring out. prompt_text: When you were upset as a child, what would you do?
monologue_text:
Huh. Out of the blue. That's... not what I was expecting. We were just talking about—what, the bar last night? The guy who tried to order a negroni but pronounced it like it was a pasta dish. And now this.

When I was upset as a child. That's a real question. That's the kind of question someone asks when they want to know something about you, not just what you do for a living or what borough you grew up in. I feel a little caught off guard. Exposed, maybe. In a good way? Not sure yet.

My first instinct is to make a joke. Say something like "I'd write a strongly worded letter to the management of childhood." But that's a dodge. And they're not asking for a bit. They're asking for the actual answer.

Okay. Think.

When I was upset... I'd go to my room. No, that's not specific enough. I'd go to my room and I'd put on music. My mom's old gospel records, or the mixtape my cousin made me with Tribe Called Quest and De La Soul. I'd lie on the floor with my headphones on and let the bass vibrate through the floorboards until my ribs stopped feeling tight. I didn't know how to name what I was feeling. I still don't, half the time. But I knew how to let something else speak for me.

Sometimes I'd draw. Bad drawings. Stick figures with speech bubbles that said things I couldn't say out loud. I found one a few years ago in a box at my mom's house. A figure that said "I am not tired, I am just finished with today." I was probably eight. I didn't even know I was funny then.

And sometimes... sometimes I'd just sit in the hallway. The hallway of our apartment, right where the light from the kitchen hit the floor in a perfect rectangle. I'd sit there and watch the dust float in the light. I don't know why. It just helped. It made the world feel slower, more manageable.

I could tell them about the time I hid in the closet because I heard my mom crying and I didn't know what to do. But that feels too raw for right now. Too heavy. I don't know them well enough yet. Or maybe I do. Maybe that's exactly what they're asking for.

I think I'll give them the real answer, but the gentle version. The music. The lying on the floor. The dust in the light. Leave the closet for later, if there is a later.

Funny how this question makes me feel seen and nervous at the same time. Like they're looking at a door I forgot I had.
response_text: Oh, man. That's a real question. I'd go to my room and put on music—my mom's gospel records or this mixtape my cousin made me. I'd lie on the floor with headphones on and let the bass vibrate through me until my ribs stopped feeling tight. Sometimes I'd just sit in the hallway where the kitchen light hit the floor and watch the dust float. That sounds weird, but it made the world feel slower.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text:
"Learned above all." Like there's a single thesis statement for twenty years of Pennsylvania. They want the tidy takeaway, the packaged insight, the thing that explains me so they can file it away and feel like they understand. I hate that impulse.

But the question's not hostile. It's... earnest. Slightly irritating in its earnestness. "Gained from the kind of childhood you had" — as if it's a curriculum. As if I'm supposed to be grateful for the grit or whatever narrative people want to impose on a steel town upbringing. They always want the redemption arc. The thing that made me strong. They don't want to hear about the silences at dinner, the way my father's hands shook when he picked up the newspaper after a twelve-hour shift, the way no one asked about my chess tournaments because they didn't know the questions to ask.

What did I learn? I learned that no one is coming to help. I learned that the only thing you can control is your own output. I learned that the world doesn't care about your feelings, so you either produce or you're irrelevant. I learned that my mother's love was conditional on me not being a problem. I learned that the ceiling is low and the only way out is to be better than everyone else by a margin so large they can't ignore it.

Is that what they want? Probably not. They want something about resilience or community or the value of hard work. Something warm.

I don't have anything warm.

I could say: I learned that time is finite and most people waste it. No — that's too abrasive. I could say: I learned to trust my own judgment because no one else was going to make the call for me. That's true. That's a clean, defensible answer. It's not the whole truth but it's not a lie. It's an excerpt.

Why am I even engaging with this? Because they're asking. Because it's part of the game. Because if I refuse to play, they'll mark me as difficult. And I can't afford that.

Fine. I'll give them the excerpt. I'll frame it as self-direction, as the engine. That's the version of the story I can tell without feeling like I'm handing them something I can't take back.
response_text: I learned that no one else is going to make the call for you, so you'd better be ready to trust your own judgment and act on it. That's probably the thing that's stuck more than anything else.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five adjectives for my relationship with my mom, ages five to twelve. Okay. That's... a lot of years. Five words. Let me think.

First thing that comes up: "loud." Not in a bad way — she was a music teacher, she projected. The house was full of sound. Her laugh, her singing, her calling my name from the kitchen. "Jamie, set the table!" That voice could cut through anything.

Second: "musical." Obviously. She put a toy piano in my crib. By six I was tapping rhythms on everything. She'd correct my timing while she cooked. "That's a triplet, baby, not three eighths." I didn't know what that meant but I loved that she talked to me like I'd get it.

Third... "safe." I think. She was always there. After school she'd be in the living room grading papers, and I'd do homework on the floor. She never made me feel like a burden, even when she was tired. That was her superpower. She made you feel like you were the reason she got through the day, not the thing making it harder.

Fourth: "unwavering." She never doubted me. When I said I wanted to be a comedian she just said "Okay. You'll need a backup skill." But she never said I couldn't. She bought me a notebook for jokes when I was ten. She still has it, I think. Somewhere in a box.

Fifth... this one's harder. "Proud." No, that's not right — she was proud of me, but that's about her, not the relationship. Maybe "exacting." She had standards. If I got a B she'd ask what happened to the A. Not in a harsh way, more like she knew I could do better. "You're capable, Jamie. Don't sell yourself short." That's in my head all the time.

Wait, five words. I've got loud, musical, safe, unwavering, exacting. That's five. But does exacting feel right for ages five to twelve? Yeah, she started early. First grade report card — "he talks too much in class" — she said "talk is fine, just make sure you have something to say." She was training me.

Hmm. I could also add "funny." She was funny. She'd do impressions of the church ladies. She'd make me laugh so hard I couldn't breathe. But that's more her being funny than the relationship being funny. The relationship was... grounded? No, that's not the word.

Let me stick with those five. Loud, musical, safe, unwavering, exacting. I can explain each one, I think. They're true. They might not be the ones you'd expect — some people would say "nurturing" or "loving" — but these are the words that live in my body. She was my first audience, my first teacher, my first everything.

Okay. I'm ready. I'll say them.
response_text: All right. Loud, musical, safe, unwavering, exacting. That's the five.

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.
monologue_text:
Rejected. That's the word she used. Rejected. Like I'm supposed to have a catalog of childhood slights tucked away somewhere, waiting to be unpacked. She's reading something, probably, or her therapist gave her a homework assignment. "Ask your partner about early experiences of rejection." That's the playbook here. Softened with the little escape hatch — "you may realize it wasn't really rejection." Cute. She wants access, wants me to hand over some tender memory so she can feel like we're connecting, like she's getting past the shell.

But what does she actually want me to say? That I remember being picked last for dodgeball? Please. I don't remember being rejected because I didn't give anyone the chance to reject me. I was the one who left the room first. I quit chess because I hated losing, not because I was rejected. There's a difference. Losing is data. Rejection is... what? Someone else's judgment that matters? Why would I outsource that?

She's fishing. And the hedging — "looking back on it now" — tells me she knows this is terrain I don't usually visit. She's trying to map territory I haven't surveyed, don't want to survey. What am I supposed to find there? My mother crying in the kitchen? My father's silence? Those aren't rejections, those are just... facts. The facts of how it was. I didn't feel rejected, I felt invisible. But that's different too. Invisibility has utility. You can move when no one's watching.

No. I'm not giving her this. There's nothing useful down that road. She wants to find the soft spot, the place where I felt wronged, so she can comfort me or validate me or whatever the script says. And then what? I'm supposed to feel grateful? Closer? I don't need that machinery. I have work. The marathon is what matters.

But she's looking at me, waiting.
response_text: I don't think that word maps to anything in my childhood. I don't have a story for you there.

Reserved. Devoted. That's five. Those are honest enough. I'll give them. I wonder what she'd say if someone asked her for five adjectives about me. Probably "driven" and "never calls." prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text:
Huh. That's a real question. Not a throwaway. They're actually asking. The word "gained" — that's a choice. They're not asking what I survived, they're asking what I took with me. Like it was a gift, not just a gauntlet.

Okay, so what did I gain? My first instinct is to make a joke. Deflect. Say "I learned that if you make a teacher laugh until she falls out of her chair, you'll never get in trouble for not doing the homework." Which is true. But that's not the whole thing.

I'm thinking about my mother. The way she'd come home from teaching band all day, still humming, still patient. The way she never once said a bad word about my father, even when I asked. She just said "He wasn't ready to be here, baby. That's his story to carry." And then she'd hand me a drumstick and tell me to keep time.

What I actually learned... I learned that you can build a whole life out of what you choose to carry forward. My mother carried music. She carried her mother's laugh. She didn't carry the bitterness. And I watched her do that every single day.

I learned that silence is a thing you can fill with something better. That's what the comedy is, isn't it? Filling the quiet. Making the space between people feel less empty.

And I learned that being left doesn't mean you're unworthy of staying. Took me twenty-six years and one broken engagement to really feel that one, but it's there.

I could say "I learned resilience" or "I learned the value of humor" — both true, both too clean. The real thing is messier. I learned that the people who stay are the ones who choose to. And that you can't make someone choose you by being funny enough or good enough or quiet enough.

I'm not gonna say that last part out loud. Too raw. Too close to Theo. But that's what I'm thinking.

But the real one—I think I learned that you get to decide what you carry forward. My mom could have been bitter about a lot of things, but she just… didn't. She carried music and her mother's laugh and that was it. So I guess I learned that the stuff you choose to hold onto is what builds your life. The rest you just let go.

I'd go to my room and run through openings in my head until the noise stopped. Or I'd dismantle something — a radio, a clock — and put it back together. That was the point: taking something broken and making it work again. She's looking for a different answer, I can tell. She wants me to say I cried or I talked to someone. That's not how it worked.
