monologue_text:
Alright. Five words. He wants five words about my father from when I was five to twelve.

Fine. I can do this. I can do anything he asks, that's the point. Just pick the words. Don't overthink the exercise itself; that's a trap. The words are the data. I give the data, we move on.

Let's see. Five to twelve. That's second grade to seventh grade. The mills. The house. The smell of machine oil and coffee that never left his skin. The way he'd sit in his chair, the one with the worn armrest, and just stare at the evening news. Not talk.

Word one. Distant. He was. Not cruel, not absent in a way that made the house feel empty, but he occupied a different room in the house of my life. I didn't resent it then. It was just... the structure of the world.

Word two. Quiet. Yes. The silence between us was a whole other conversation. I learned to read it. An approving quiet, a tired quiet, the quiet of a man who'd already said everything he was going to say that day at the plant.

Word three... Provider. That's his role. That's what he did. He put food on the table. He paid for the uniform for the chess tournaments. He didn't know the rules of chess, didn't come to a single match, but he paid for the fucking shoes. That was his love. Objectively efficient. I can respect that model.

Word four. Proud. In his way. The way he'd shake my hand after I won something. A single pump, firm, no smile. "Good job, Marc." Which was high praise. I don't know if I felt it, but I saw it. The slight straightening of his shoulders when the neighbor mentioned I was on the honor roll.

Word five. Predictable. He was routine itself. Reliable. You could set a clock by him. That counts for something. That's a solid foundation. No surprises, no drama.

That's the list. Distant. Quiet. Provider. Proud. Predictable. There. Ready to deliver the data and explain why. The why is the part he'll probably want to deconstruct. Fine. Let him try. I've already put it in a box.

But wait, do I even remember being upset as a kid? I remember being bored. I remember being hungry for attention. But upset? That word feels heavy. It makes me think of my mother's face after her mother died — that collapsed look she wore for months. I didn't know how to do anything with that. I just sat next to her. That's what I did. I sat.

Sometimes I'd make a joke in class or at the dinner table and my mother would say "Oh, Jamie." Not mad. Just — tired? But also a little grateful, I think. So maybe I learned early: if you can make the room laugh, no one has to sit with the heavy thing. You can skip it.

I feel like she's asking me to not skip it right now. She's asking with that soft look. I want to give her something real. But also I want to make her laugh? Old habit. Both things are true.

I'm thinking about the time I tried to draw a comic about a sad cloud and my mother kept it on the fridge for two years. That wasn't funny. That was just... sad cloud. Mom said "you drew exactly how you felt." I didn't know that's what I was doing.

So maybe I did art? Unintentionally.

Also I once threw a remote control at the wall when I was like twelve and my mother didn't even yell. She just handed me a broom and said "sweep it up." That taught me something about cleaning up your own messes. Or about her being too tired to argue.

I'm rambling in my head. Should I tell her any of this? The remote one might be too raw for right now. The sad cloud is sweet. The sitting next to my mom is the truest.

God, I want to be funny but I also want her to see me. Both at once. That's the thing. I can be both.

Okay. So what do I say? I'll start with the quiet and see if I can steer into the humor gently. Or maybe just give the quiet and let her hold it. She asked. She wants that. I can give that.

Oh, a real question. Okay. She's not just making small talk — she actually wants to know. That's refreshing. I mean, I'm used to people asking what I do and then either laughing too hard or not laughing at all. But this is different.

I'm thinking about my mom, obviously. Single mother, music teacher, church choir on Sundays. She never had a lot of money but she always had a lot of... presence. She filled rooms. She made me feel like I was the most interesting kid in the world even when I was just sitting there drawing dinosaurs. What did I learn from that? I learned that you don't need a full table to have a feast. That's cheesy, but it's true. She taught me that attention is the most generous thing you can give someone. Not money, not stuff — just showing up, listening, making them feel seen.

And then there's the other side — the absent father thing. That's a lesson too, but maybe not the one she's asking for. I don't want to go dark on a first real conversation. But it's there. It taught me that I don't want to be someone who leaves. I want to be someone who stays, even when it's hard. Even when the set bombs and the train is late and the rent is due.

But the thing that jumps out most? Laughter. My mom made me laugh. Even when she was exhausted from teaching thirty kids how to hold a clarinet, she'd find something ridiculous to say. She'd do voices. She'd make up songs about the laundry. That's where I got it — the idea that you can take whatever is heavy and turn it into something light. Not fake light. Real light. The kind that doesn't deny the weight but carries it anyway.

So I guess what I learned is that you can make a life out of making people laugh, and that's not a small thing. It's actually the biggest thing. And I think that's what I'd say. But I'm not gonna say it all at once — I'll start with something concrete, maybe the image of her at the piano after a long day, still finding a joke in the middle of a hymn. That's the truth. That's the thing I carry.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.

Oh. That's a big one. Huh.

The thing about my mom. That woman never stopped. She taught band all day, ran choir at church, came home and graded papers while I did homework at the kitchen table. I learned that you show up. You just show up, even when you're tired, even when the underfunded school district cuts your budget again. She never complained about it, not really. I mean she complained, but it was always specific, like about the photocopier or the way the principal scheduled meetings. Never the big picture. Never 'why me.'

And the other thing. The absence. My father is a name we didn't say. I learned that some silences are full of something. Useful for comedy, honestly. You learn to read the room because you learned to read the kitchen table.

But the main thing. The thing I carry. My mom gave me this ridiculous, stubborn belief that I could be loved without achieving anything. She loved me before I was funny. Okay. Words. What do I want to say? What's accurate? "Stable" — she was stable. Always there. Same routine. Reliable. But maybe too stable? Like a baseline. Not remarkable. "Supportive" — she came to my chess tournaments but she didn't understand them. She smiled and said "good job" whether I won or lost. I hated that. I needed her to know the difference between a good game and a bad one. She didn't.

"Reserved" — she wasn't warm. Not cold, but not warm either. A professional warmth. Like a teacher with a student she likes but has to keep distance. That's how she was with me. With all of us. I think that's why I'm like this. Not that I blame her. It's just data.

"Pragmatic" — she taught fourth grade, she lived in a world of reading levels and lesson plans. She didn't deal with feelings, she dealt with next steps. If I was upset she'd say "what are we going to do about it?" Not "tell me about your feelings." That's the same as me. I guess that's her.

Five words. Stable, organized, supportive, reserved, pragmatic. That's fine. That's accurate. I can explain each one without getting into anything. Just state the facts. Quick and clean. Then we move on.

She's still waiting. Let's get this over with. I'll say them in a list. She'll write them down. I'll give a brief explanation. Done. No need to dig deeper. There's nothing deeper.

Huh. That's specific. They're asking about the kid me, the pre-stage me, the me who hadn't figured out yet that making noise was a survival strategy. I'm trying to remember what that felt like, before I had a set to work through things onstage.

Mom's spinet piano. I'd wedge myself under it, right where the pedals were, knees to my chest, the wood vibrating above me when she practiced. The smell of old felt and dust. I'd stay there until the crying turned into humming along with whatever she was playing. She knew I was there. She'd just let me have the space and fill it with sound instead of questions.

My first lesson in what to do with a bad feeling: sit underneath something bigger than you and wait for the music. That's still what I do, isn't it? I get on stage, let the room be the hollow space above me, and I make something that vibrates. I'm not crying, I'm performing. It's the same thing.

And I'm already wondering if I should tell them that or just say "I hid under the piano" and leave it there. The joke's forming in the back of my throat — "well, Nigerian dads don't really do 'upset,' they do 'disappointed silence that lasts three generations'" — but I catch it. They're not asking for a bit. They're asking for me.

response_text: I used to hide under my mother's spinet piano. She'd be practicing, and I'd just sit there with my knees to my chest, letting the sound vibrate through the wood. She never asked me to come out. She just kept playing.

I don't think about this stuff. I don't see the point. But she asked, so I'll give her something clean and true. Something I can say without it sounding like I'm complaining. Because I'm not complaining. I'm grateful for it. It made me who I am.

You figure out the problem, or you lose. I quit chess because I hated losing, not because I stopped loving the game. That's the lesson: you don't stop because you're tired. You stop because the cost of continuing exceeds the benefit. I'm not sure that's what she wants to hear. She probably wants something about resilience or gratitude. I could give her the Caro line about power being the ability to control your own circumstances. That's true. But it's also a deflection. I'll say something clean, something that sounds like insight without actually opening the door.
response_text: That nobody's coming to help you. You figure it out, or you lose.

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.
monologue_text:
The question catches me off guard, but not in the way she probably wants. Childhood rejection? I'm trying to remember a specific instance and my mind goes blank. Not because it's repressed—I have a good memory for facts—but because I was never the kid standing in the corner. I was the kid who quit chess because I hated losing, not because the other boys didn't want me on their team.

She's circling toward something. Probing. Looking for that soft spot people assume everyone has. But the framework is wrong. Rejection implies I was waiting for someone else's approval. I wasn't. I was already the engine by age ten, running on my own tolerances. If there was any distance, it was me keeping it.

My father came home, ate his dinner, watched the news, went to bed. My mother graded papers at the kitchen table. That wasn't rejection—that was normal operation. Everyone had their function. No one was hugging pictures of me. I didn't need that.

I can tell she wants me to say yes, to open some door labeled "vulnerability." But the honest answer is no, I don't remember feeling rejected. Not as a core memory. And even if I did, what would it change? The past is data. You analyze it, you adjust, you move forward. You don't sit in it.

This is going to be a long conversation. Might as well answer straight and let the silence do its work.
response_text: No, I don't remember ever feeling rejected. I wasn't really the type to wait for someone else's signal that I was okay.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your childhood relationship with your father, again starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but again say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think again for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Absent. Solid. Contained. Feared. Efficient.

There. That took maybe twelve seconds. I could have had them faster. She said a minute. I waited.

Absent because he was always at the mill or asleep before I left for school. Not in a way that felt like a wound—more like a fact of the house. The house ran, dinner was made, bills were paid, and he was the reason for that. But I don't remember him *there*. Watching television in the same room doesn't count.

Solid. That's the word I settled on for the feeling of him. Not warm. Not cold. Just—dense. Unmovable. I never saw him flinch at anything. When the union went on strike and we had no money for three months, he sat at the kitchen table every night doing crossword puzzles. Didn't complain. Didn't panic. Just sat there like a rock in a river.

Contained is the cleanest one. Everything he felt stayed inside. I learned that frame-by-frame. When I won the state championship he shook my hand and said "Good job." That was it. To someone else that might sound small. To me it sounded like the roof of the world. That he even spoke was something.

Feared. I didn't fear *him*, exactly. I feared the shape of the space around him when something was wrong. He never hit anyone. Never yelled. But the silence in the house when he was angry was its own kind of storm. You could feel it in your chest like barometric pressure. I'd do anything to avoid that. Learned to anticipate problems. To make everything seamless so nothing would crack. Probably why I'm good at my job.

Efficient. That was our operating principle. Minimal friction. Maximum output. You didn't waste words. You didn't repeat yourself. You identified the problem, solved it, moved on. That's a kind of love, isn't it? Not the Hollywood kind. The kind where someone shows you how to function. How not to be a liability to everyone around you.

She'll probably want to stretch these. Pull them into something they're not. I'll just give her the plain version. It's fine. I've organized this. I know what I think. I just don't usually say it out loud.
response_text: Absent. Solid. Contained. Feared. Efficient.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. monologue_text:
Five adjectives about my mom from five to twelve.

Okay.

First one that comes up is *warm*. That's easy. She was always warm. The way she'd sit on the edge of my bed at night and hum whatever the church choir was working on, even if she'd been up since five. Her hands were always warm too, even in winter when she'd come in from the cold with groceries. 

Second one: *funny*. She was funny. Not like a comedian, but she had this dry, sideways way of saying things that could make me crack up in the middle of dinner. She'd do voices when she read me stories. I remember her doing the monkey from *Caps for Sale* and I laughed so hard I choked on my juice. 

Third: *musical*. Obviously. But it's not just that she taught band. It's that everything in our apartment had a rhythm—the way she stirred soup, the way she hummed while grading papers, the way she'd clap twice to get my attention before speaking. Music was our background radiation. 

Fourth: *patient*. God, she was patient. I was a hyperactive, loud, constantly-joke-trying kid. I must have driven her insane. But she never snapped. She'd just sigh, or put a hand on my shoulder, or say "Jamie, let's take a breath." I didn't know until later how rare that was. 

Fifth... 

I'm trying to think of a fifth that isn't just repeating myself. 

*Protective*. Yeah. She was protective. Not in a smothering way, but I knew she was watching. She'd ask about my friends, about where I was going, about who laughed at my jokes and who didn't. She had this way of asking questions that made you feel like she was on your side, not checking up on you. 

I could say *strict* too—she had rules about homework and bedtime and TV—but that feels like it's about behavior, not relationship. 

These five feel right. Warm, funny, musical, patient, protective. 

I wonder what he's going to make of them. I hope he doesn't think I'm just painting a perfect picture. There were hard parts too. But he said age 5 to 12, and that's what I remember. That's the truth. 

Okay. I think I've got them. Let me write them down.
response_text: Alright. I've got 'em. Warm, funny, musical, patient, protective. That's five. Want me to go through why, or do you want to write 'em down first and I'll explain after?

monologue_text:
They're being so... careful. Asking like it's an interview, like there's a right answer. I hate that. But they're not asking for a performance—wait, they literally asked for what I learned. That's different.

So what did I learn? That you can hold joy and grief in the same hand. That laughter isn't escape, it's—it's communion? That sounds so pretentious. But it's true. My job on stage isn't to make people forget their lives. It's to make them feel less alone in them.

First one that comes up is *constant*. She was just always there. Not hovering, but present. Humming. She was always humming. Even when she was grading papers, the hum was running in the background like a radiator. That's my first.

prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?

prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?
monologue_text:
Threatening? That's a sharp turn. We were just talking about… what were we talking about? My set. Open mics. The one where the guy in the front row kept ordering drinks so loudly I had to stop and just stare at him until he noticed. She laughed at that. She has a good laugh. And now she's asking about my parents. Threatening. That's a specific word.

I mean, my mom. My mom who once told me, in the middle of a lecture about homework I hadn't done, "You know, I could have named you after your father." That was the threat. The worst thing she could think of — to be associated with him. And she knew it. She said it, and then she looked at me, and we both just sat there in the kitchen with the hum of the fridge, and then she said "Don't make me do it again," and we both laughed. Because it was absurd. Because the threat was so specific to our little world.

But threatening. She's not asking if my mom was harsh, or strict. She said threatening. Like, is there something I'm not telling her? Something I'm supposed to be working through on stage? A lot of comedians have the dark childhood story. I don't. I have a mom who cried at my high school graduation and then went out for pizza with my friends and made them talk about their own plans until they felt better. I have a mom who gave me a book about Audre Lorde when I was seventeen and said "Read this, and then we'll talk about it." The only threat was love, and it was relentless.

But I'm not saying that. I'm just sitting here, looking at her, wondering why she asked. And also wondering if there's something in my face that made her think she could ask. Am I giving off a vibe? I feel pretty neutral. I feel fine. I was just talking about the guy with the loud drinks. That was a funny story. Was there something in the funny story? No. The story was just funny.

She's looking at me. She's waiting. I should say something. I don't want to make it heavy. I don't want to lie, either. I was back at my desk in three days. That's not a symptom; that's a strategy.

The question feels like a therapy prompt, not a conversation. I don't owe anyone that kind of access. Especially not over dinner or whatever this is.

I could give her the sanitized version. "They taught me discipline and self-reliance." That's true. It's also a wall. She'll either accept it and move on, or push, and then I'll have to decide how much of my inner architecture I'm willing to let someone poke at. Right now, the answer is: not much.

But she's not stupid. She asked deliberately. So I need to answer in a way that closes the door without slamming it. Acknowledge the question, give a truncated answer, pivot. That's the play. Don't let this turn into a excavation. I've already excavated. The ground is level. Let's keep it that way.

The question itself is soft. Pop psychology framing. They want me to say something like "I learned to be independent because they were absent" or "I seek achievement for validation." Cheap narrative. I don't think in those terms. I think in terms of what works and what doesn't.

They're probably looking for vulnerability. They won't get it. Not because I'm hiding something, but because I don't have a neat story to sell. I have a life that works. That's enough.

prompt_text: Did you experience the loss of a parent or other close loved one while you were a young child — for example, a sibling, or a close family member?

Huh. That's a specific question. Loss of a parent or close loved one. As a young child. They're looking for something, some foundational wound, some explanation for why I'm the way I am. I handled it. I handled it efficiently. Three days. Back at the desk. That's not a trauma, that's a fact of life.

And even if it had been when I was young — what then? What would that change? It's not an excuse. It's not a shortcut to understanding me.

The word 'loss' feels clinical. They're probing. I don't like being probed. I don't like the implication that I'm a puzzle to be solved, that there's some hidden key buried in my childhood that explains everything. There's no key. There's just what I did and what I do.

I could say no. Technically true. But it's not the whole truth, and they'll probably sense the evasion. Or I could say yes, but correct the age. Then they'll ask follow-ups. How did it affect you? Did you grieve properly? Did you process it? And I'll have to either lie or tell them that I made the arrangements and moved on, which will sound cold to them, and they'll write it down as a data point.

I don't want to give them data points.

But I also don't want to seem defensive. Defensiveness is a tell. It signals that there's something to defend. Better to answer flatly, correct the premise, and move on. Let them decide what to do with it.

And then change the subject.

prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?
monologue_text:
Huh. That's... a specific question. Not one I get asked every day. My first instinct is to make a joke about it — something about my mom's wooden spoon as a threat — but that feels too easy, like I'm deflecting. And I don't think she's asking for a punchline.

I'm trying to remember if my mom ever actually threatened me. She was strict, sure. The look she'd give me in church when I was fidgeting — that was a threat in itself. But actual words? "I'll tell your father" doesn't work when your father is a ghost. She'd say "I brought you into this world, I can take you out" but that's a joke, right? Every Nigerian mom says that.

I think she was trying to scare me straight sometimes, but it never felt real. More like theater. She'd raise her voice, then ten minutes later we'd be watching Jeopardy together and she'd let me have the last piece of pound cake.

Theo used to ask stuff like this too. About my childhood. He was always looking for the wound underneath. And I'd get defensive because I didn't have one — not like that. Not a threat I believed.

I don't know what she's looking for. Maybe she's just curious. Or maybe she's got her own stuff she's working through. Either way, I think the honest answer is: not really. My mom scared other people, not me.
response_text: Nah, not really. My mom had this look — the church pew look — that could stop a stampede, but she never actually threatened me. She'd say shit like "I brought you into this world, I can take you out" but that's practically a cultural greeting. It was theater, not menace.

I'm five. I'm at the dinner table and it's just me and Mom and she's humming. I ask where he is and she says "He's not here" and that's the whole answer. Age seven I stop asking. Age ten I start making jokes about it — "My father's so invisible, I walk through him every morning" — that was one. I thought it was clever. Mom didn't laugh.

Distant. That's one. But distant implies he was somewhere I could see. He wasn't. Unmoored? No, that's too literary. I sound like I'm trying to be profound. Stop doing that.

Maybe: absent. Missing. Mute. Because he never called, never wrote. Not even a birthday card. I used to check the mail. Learned to stop.

But there's also — I don't want to say 'angry' because I wasn't angry. Not then. More like... curious. Curious is an adjective. Curious about who he was, where he went, what he looked like. Part of me wants to make a joke — "Oh easy: nonexistent, imaginary, fictional, mythological, and a ghost." That would get a laugh in a set. But this isn't a set. This is... I don't know what this is. Some kind of therapy? Homework?

Alright. Let's be honest. If I have to pick five words that reflect how I felt about him from 5 to 12:

First: blank. Like a page with no words.
Second: question mark. Because that's what he was — a big question nobody answered.
Third: far. Not just far away, but far in the way a star is far — you can see the light but it's already dead.
Fourth: maybe 'missing' again. Or 'void'. But void is too big. A void is cosmic. He was just a missing man.
Fifth: ... I don't know. 'Nothing'? That's not an adjective. 'Nonexistent'? Same problem.

What about 'shadow'? That's a noun. They said adjectives. I'll find a way to make it work. 'Shadowy.' That's a word. It fits. Not scary, not solid—just a shape you can't grab.

I don't like this. Feels like picking at a scar that's already healed weird. I'd rather tell a joke. But I'm here, so I'll do it. Let's see what comes out when I open my mouth. Probably a joke anyway. That's what I do.

That was how I handled things. You don't think about being upset when you're trying to see six moves deep. It's not emotional, it's computational. I don't have some story I'm supposed to tell here — a blanket, a parent coming in, a stuffed animal. That wasn't my house. We didn't do that. So the answer is: I played chess until the feeling passed. I don't know if that's what you're looking for.

Right. Five adjectives. For my relationship with my father. Which is a thing I don't have. A relationship. With a man I've never met. Dad, father, sperm donor, the name we don't say — my mom's mouth gets this particular set when it comes up, like she's tasting something sour and old.

So do I just pick words that describe the absence? Absent. Empty. Unknown. That's three right there, feels like cheating. Or do I make something up, some fantasy version? My childhood relationship with my father was... imaginary. Like a character I invented for school projects when the teacher said "ask your dad" and I had to improvise.

I remember being five and watching other kids get picked up by their dads after school and feeling this weird hollow. Not sad exactly, just... confused. Like everyone else had a secret I wasn't in on. Pop, papa, papi, daddy — all these words that meant nothing to me except a shape I couldn't fill in.

What would he even be like? My mom says he was funny, charming, left before she was showing. That's all I've got. A one-sentence biography and a last name I don't use. Okafor is hers. I chose that.

Maybe the word is "question." Or "story." Or "space." Like a room with no furniture that you keep walking through anyway. I've gotten good at not thinking about it — or I thought I had, until this. [laughs quietly to myself] Five words, take a minute. Okay. Minute starts now.

First: absent. That's honest. Not angry, just factual. Second: silent. No voice, no stories, no advice, no terrible dad jokes I'd groan at. Third: conceptual. He's an idea more than a person. Fourth: closed. Because that door is shut and has always been shut. Fifth... [pause] let. I don't know. Maybe "decided." Like I made a decision about him before I could even decide things. Or "finished." Yeah, maybe finished. Like the sentence ends there.

But then why does my throat feel tight? I don't even miss him. You can't miss what you never had. That's what I tell myself. And I believe it until moments like this when someone hands me a blank sheet and says describe it.

Alright. I think I've got five. Let's see what I actually say out loud.
response_text: Alright. Okay. I got five. They are: absent. Silent. Conceptual. Closed. And... finished.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text:
Oh, wow, okay. That's a real question. Not a small-talk question. That's a... that's the kind of question where you have to actually stop and check your pockets for what's in there.

My childhood. Okay. What did I learn. My mother. Band teacher, choir director, single mom in Queens. She gave me this... this permission to be loud? No, that's not quite right. She gave me the model of doing something you love even when it's exhausting. She'd come home after teaching forty kids to play "Hot Cross Buns" for the third time that day, and she'd still sit at the piano and play for herself. Just for the sound of it. I learned that you do the thing because the thing itself is worth doing, even if nobody's clapping.

But also - and this is the part I don't always lead with - I learned how to watch. How to be the kid in the corner of the church basement watching the adults fall apart and pull themselves together. How to notice the exact moment when someone's laugh changes from real to polite. That's comedian training, right there. That's the curriculum.

What else. I learned that you can love someone even when they come back smaller from a trip and don't have the words for what happened. My grandmother's funeral. My mother on the plane alone. She came back and she was the same but also not, and we never really talked about it. And I learned that that's okay too. That love sometimes means sitting in the same room with the silence.

God, that got heavy fast. I'm supposed to be a funny person. But you asked, and I'm trying to answer honestly. The thing I gained - the thing I'm still figuring out how to use - is that I got to see what it looks like when someone builds a life from scratch, with no blueprint, and makes it sing. My mother didn't have a father for me either, not really, and she made a home anyway. She made music. She made me believe I could make something too.

So I guess what I learned is: you can be made of the gaps and still be whole. You can have missing pieces and still be a full sound.

I'd make up stories about him: he was a spy, he was a musician who had to leave, he was dead but she didn't want to tell me. I don't know, maybe that's three already. Let me think.

First: absent. Yeah, that's true. Second: unknown. I didn't know him at all. Third: imagined. Because I filled the gap with stories. Fourth: confusing. The way other kids had dads who showed up at school plays, and mine was just a blank spot in the family tree. Fifth: maybe "longing"? Or "curiosity"? Both. I'd say "curious" — that was the feeling, the itch to know. But I'm not sure that's an adjective for the relationship. The relationship was defined by its absence, so maybe "fragmented"? Because I had little pieces: a name, a date on a document, a scent my mother mentioned once (something like sandalwood). I'm trying to land on five that feel honest without being too heavy. prompt_text: When you were upset as a child, what would you do?
monologue_text:
I didn't really... get upset. That was the thing. You just didn't. My father would come home from the mill, sit down with his beer, and that was it. The world was what it was. You dealt with it.

I guess I'd go to my room and play chess against myself. Set up positions, work through variations. There's something clean about it — the board doesn't care how you feel. The moves are either right or wrong.

Or I'd read. Encyclopedia volumes, mostly. I had this set from a supermarket promotion, D through F was my favorite. I'd memorize the population of every country in South America. Utterly useless information, but it occupied the space.

I don't think I ever told anyone I was upset. What would be the point? They'd just look at you with that expression adults get — like they're deciding whether to comfort you or tell you to toughen up. Better to just move past it.

So the short answer is: I'd find something to do. Something that required focus. And eventually the feeling would dissolve.
response_text: I'd go to my room and work through chess positions. Or read an encyclopedia volume. You deal with it and move on.

prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?
monologue_text:
Threatening? No. That's a weird question. Why would she ask that? Nobody threatened me. Dad was — he worked. He came home tired. Threatening would require energy I don't think he had after first shift. And Mom, she was all about order, discipline, sure, but threat? That implies some kind of power play they'd bother with. 

I suppose if you squint, the silent treatment can feel like a threat, the way the house would go quiet after a bad report card. But that's not threatening. That's just… disappointment. Performance feedback. 

She's looking for something. Some wound to unpack. That's not how I work. I don't have a story about a belt or a switch or a joke that crossed a line. We had lines. They were clear. You stayed on the right side of them.

Jokingly? My father didn't joke. My mother's jokes were about my posture. "Stand up straight or you'll live in my basement." Basements aren't threatening. Basements are just unfinished.

I don't know what she wants me to say. There's nothing there. Maybe she's projecting. Maybe her parents were the problem. That's not mine. I'm not going to manufacture a memory to satisfy a therapeutic prompt.

Let her ask what she asks. I'll answer factually. No. Not threatening. Next.
response_text: No. They weren't threatening. Threatening implies drama. They had disappointment and high standards — that's not the same thing.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five adjectives. Five. About my mother. Ages five to twelve. That's first grade through seventh. We lived in the frame house on Harrison Street then, the one with the cracked driveway my father never got around to fixing.

She made lunch every morning. Packed it in the same brown bag. Always an apple, always the same sandwich. Never asked what I wanted. But she showed up for the chess tournament. Took the Greyhound to the regional finals because Dad couldn't get the shift covered. Sat in the back. Didn't say anything afterward. Just that she was proud. Once.

That's one adjective, then. Silent. Or quiet. No, that's too soft. Efficient. She was efficient with affection. Like it was a resource she had to ration.

Adjective two. Protective. Maybe. Not in the warm way. She kept me inside a lot. Reading. Studying. The world was dangerous, she said. People would take advantage. She was right about that.

Three. Distant. She went through the motions. Meals, laundry, permission slips. But I don't remember being held. I don't remember her face close to mine.

This is the kind of exercise that tells you more about the asker than the subject. She wants me to probe a wound. She wants a reaction, a crack in the armor. As if I haven't already studied this ground and found nothing useful.

Four. Reliable. There's that, at least. She didn't drink. She didn't leave. She got up every day. Did her job. Made sure we had what we needed.

Five. I'm stuck. The task forces a shape onto something that has no neat contour. Five words, like a constraint in a deal model. But some variables don't reduce.

Dutiful. That's the fifth. She did her duty. As she understood it. As she was able. 

I'll give them. She'll write them down. And then she'll want me to explain, to flesh them out, to perform some act of emotional nakedness she probably mistakes for intimacy. But I've already given more real estate to this in sixty seconds of silent thought than it has occupied in the last decade. That's enough.

Or maybe not. Maybe there's something in the order. Efficient. Protective. Distant. Reliable. Dutiful. That's a logical sequence. Cause and effect. Material follows material.

Silent. Not efficient. That's the right one to start. Silent. Protective. Distant. Reliable. Dutiful.

Now I just have to say them out loud. And then a reason for each. The reasons will be as true as I can make them without giving her more access than she's earned.
response_text:
Silent. Protective. Distant. Reliable. Dutiful.

Those are the five. In that order. If you want chronology masked as emotional taxonomy.

Silent — she didn't talk much. Not about feelings, not about herself. She communicated through actions. Packed lunch. Showed up to the chess tournament once. Didn't say a word afterward.

Protective — she kept me close, inside the house, reading. Her view of the world was that it would take advantage. She wasn't wrong.

Distant — going through the motions without warmth. Meals, laundry, permission slips. I don't remember being held.

Reliable — she was consistent. Never drank, never left. Got up every day and did her job.

Dutiful — she did what she thought a mother should do. As she understood it. As she was able.

That's the list. That's the logic behind it. Write them down if you want.

The first one that comes to mind is "loud." That's not a dig — she was a music teacher, the house was always full of sound. She'd be humming while grading papers, or blasting Fela while cooking. And she laughed loud, like a honk that startled people. I loved that.

Second: "busy." She worked full-time, taught after-school lessons, ran the church choir on Sundays. Sometimes I felt like I had to schedule time with her. But she always showed up when it counted.

Third: "warm." She was a hugger. Even when I was too old for it in public, she'd pull me in. Her hands were always cold from holding coffee, but the hugs were warm.

Fourth: "strict." Not mean strict — but there were rules. Homework before TV. Be home by dark. Say please and thank you. No excuses about Dad because he wasn't there and that wasn't my problem. She had standards.

Fifth: "funny." She told jokes at the dinner table. Bad ones, puns, stuff that made me groan. But she could also do impressions of the neighbors. She's where I got the timing, I think.

Yeah. Those five. Loud, busy, warm, strict, funny. I can explain each.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text:
Right. This question. It's a therapy question, isn't it. That's what this is. Someone trying to get me to unspool something sentimental. She's looking for a story about overcoming adversity or learning to feel things. I don't have that story. Or I do, but it's not the one she wants.

What did I learn. That's not even the structure. The structure is: what did you gain. "Gain" is a positive word. She's framing it as an asset, not a deficit. Interesting. Most people ask what you got wrong from your parents. She's asking what I got right.

I learned that time is the only non-renewable resource. My father had fifty-eight years. That's not a lesson from childhood, that's a later overlay. In childhood I learned that if you stop moving you get passed. If you don't have the answer you get ignored. If you're slow you lose.

I learned that the board doesn't care about your story. The board cares if you can close the deal. My mother cared about my grades. My father cared about whether I could fix a carburetor. I learned to solve the problem in front of me and stop talking about it.

That's not going to be what she wants. She wants something about resilience or community or love. I don't have that. I have: the chess championship taught me that preparation beats talent and that winning is the only thing that feels real. I quit because I didn't want to find out what losing felt like against better kids from better schools.

Is that a gain. I don't know. It's a fact. I'll tell her that and see if she can keep up.
response_text: I learned that preparation beats talent, and that winning is the only thing that feels real. I quit chess at thirteen because I didn't want to find out what losing felt like against kids from better schools. That's the gain, if you want to call it that.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text:
"Gained." Interesting word choice. They're framing it as something I took away, not something I survived. I like that. It's a reframe I might not have done myself.

Okay, one main lesson from my childhood. That's a lot of pressure for one thing. My brain is riffling through the archives: my mother's hands conducting choir, the way she'd come home from teaching thirty screaming kids band practice and still sit with me while I did homework. The silence around my father's name, how it became this heavy thing we didn't talk about but that was always there, like a piece of furniture we'd learned to walk around.

What did I gain? Not the absence—that's what I lost. What did I gain?

I think it's this: that you can hold sorrow and joy in the same hand without dropping either. My mother did it every day. She'd be exhausted, grieving something she'd never name, and then she'd turn on Aretha and we'd dance in the kitchen. She'd laugh at my dumb impressions, even when I could tell she was tired. She taught me that life doesn't have to be one thing. That you can be sad and still funny. That you can be broken and still show up.

That's what I want to say. The lesson is that the world doesn't ask you to pick one feeling. It gives you all of them at once, and your job is to hold them without letting any of them crush you.

But that's a little heavy for a Tuesday afternoon. Maybe I'll say it's about rhythm. My mother was a music teacher. She taught me that everything has a beat—conversations, jokes, grief, even silence. You just have to learn to feel it.
response_text: I think the main thing I learned is that you can hold two completely opposite feelings at the same time and neither one cancels the other out. My mom would be exhausted and heartbroken and still put on music and dance with me in the kitchen. She'd laugh at my jokes when I could tell she'd been crying. That's what I took from it—that joy and sadness aren't enemies, they're roommates. You don't have to pick one.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text:
“Overall experiences with my parents.” That’s a broad net. They want me to lie down on a couch and free-associate, I can feel it. Not going to happen.

My father worked first shift his whole life. Came home gray with metal dust. Said maybe four sentences at dinner. That taught me something, sure. That there’s a kind of man who does his job and doesn’t complain about it. But I’m not him. I’m not going to die at fifty-eight in a recliner watching the evening news.

My mother — she was proud of me but she didn’t come to my graduation. I know that. I filed it under “information” a long time ago. It’s not a wound. It’s just a data point. A thing that happened. It told me not to expect attendance, not to rely on anyone showing up. That’s useful. That’s a piece of operating system code.

But “affected my adult personality” — that frames me as a product. As if I were clay and they were hands. I don’t accept that. I am not the sum of what happened to me. I am what I decided to do about it. Every morning I wake up and I choose. I choose to outwork everyone. I choose to be precise. I choose not to be sentimental about things that are water under the bridge.

So what did they do? They provided the raw material. They were the initial conditions. But I am the agent of optimization. I took whatever they gave and I engineered something better.

The partner probably wants a story. “My father’s work ethic instilled discipline.” “My mother’s expectation made me a perfectionist.” Clichés. True enough, maybe, but clichés are just summaries that let people stop thinking. I don’t have time to stop thinking.

I could give them the factual answer. Efficient. Close the file. “They taught me independence and self-reliance. I don’t dwell on it.” That’s clean. That’s honest enough without letting them in.

But part of me — a smaller part — wonders why they’re asking. Is this a test? Are they trying to find a weakness? Or is this just standard relationship-building nonsense, the kind of thing people do to manufacture intimacy? I don’t have the appetite for manufactured intimacy. If it’s not going to help me get to partner, I don’t see the point.

Still. I’ll give them the short version. I’ll let them think they’ve gotten something. It costs nothing, and it’s probably the path of least resistance. And I’ve already wasted enough mental cycles on it. Let’s move on.
response_text: They taught me what not to rely on. That's probably the main thing. I don't spend much time thinking about it — it's not useful to me.

Parents. Plural. That's the first thing that snags — he said parents, like there were two of them in the room when I was growing up. It's not a bad assumption, it's the normal assumption, but it lands funny. Like ordering a burger and getting a salad. Technically correct ingredients, wrong shape.

My mother. She's the whole show. Band teacher, choir director, the woman who could make a room full of seventh-graders play in key and then turn around and sing alto like she was carrying the whole church on her back. She taught me that you show up. That you do the thing even if you're tired, even if you're sad, even if the person who was supposed to be there isn't. That's in me. That's the bone structure.

And the other thing. The empty chair. I grew up with a father-shaped absence, and I think that made me funny in a specific way — the kid who learns to fill silence before the silence can ask questions. I became the person who makes the room laugh so nobody has to sit with the weight of what's not being said. That's not a complaint. It's just... architecture.

So how has it affected me? I'm here. I'm this. I'm the guy who can hold a microphone in a dark room and make strangers feel less alone by telling them about my mom and the ghost of my dad. I'm also the guy who left Brooklyn for a night when Theo asked me to leave for good, because I couldn't do the thing my mother did — stay in a place that was shrinking me.

I guess the short version is: my parents made me someone who's still figuring out how to be loved without performing for it. And I'm getting there. I think I'm getting there.
response_text: Huh. "Parents." That's generous of you—there was kind of just one, which I guess is the first thing it did to me. My mom is the whole blueprint: she showed up every day, taught band and choir, never stopped moving even when she had no reason to keep going. So I got that from her—the showing up part. And then the empty chair on the other side taught me how to fill a room with jokes before anyone could ask about it. So I'm basically a person who's still learning you can be loved without being onstage. Getting there, though.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text: Learned that if you stop moving forward, nothing holds you up.
response_text: I learned that momentum is the only thing you can count on. You stop, you fall. That's about it.

prompt_text: When you were upset as a child, what would you do?
monologue_text: Huh. Upset? That's a weird word. When I was upset? I don't — I mean, what counts as upset? If I lost a chess match I'd study the game for three hours until I knew every variation. That's not upset, that's just... solving the problem. My father never had time for being upset. You'd come home, he'd ask if you did your homework, and that was it. Mom would hover sometimes but I'd just go to my room. It's not a useful question. What do they want me to say? That I cried? I didn't cry much. If something bothered me I'd take it apart with chess or math or — honestly, I'd just wait until it stopped mattering. Things stop mattering if you don't feed them. I remember one time, maybe seventh grade, some kid said something about my family at school. I came home, opened my chess board, played through the Karpov variations silently for maybe an hour. By dinner it was gone. That's what you do. You control the inputs. You focus on the next move. This question feels like they're trying to ... I don't know, unlock something. But there's nothing locked. Just a closed door. And I left that door behind a long time ago.
response_text: I'm not sure "upset" is the right word for it. If something bothered me I'd just go to my room and play through chess variations or work problems until it stopped mattering. Which it always did.

prompt_text: Did you experience the loss of a parent or other close loved one while you were a young child — for example, a sibling, or a close family member?
monologue_text:
Young child? No. That's not— I was twenty-six. Twenty-six is not a young child. The question assumes something that isn't there. Clinical phrasing. "Loss of a parent or other close loved one." Sounds like a checklist. DSM criteria. They're looking for a hook. A reason. An excuse.

My father died at fifty-eight. Stroke. I handled it. Arranged the funeral in three days. Back at my desk. Efficient. That's not a loss that shaped me—it's a fact. A data point. What matters is what I did with it. What I learned. The clock is always ticking. Don't waste time.

This person—partner, whatever they're called—they're digging. Why? What's the angle? Trying to find a soft spot? A vulnerability to exploit? Or maybe they're just following a protocol. Doesn't matter. I don't have a story there. No trauma. No "young child" narrative. Just a man who did what needed to be done.

I could answer flatly: no. But that might invite follow-ups. "How did you cope?" "Did it affect you?" Banal. Waste of breath. Better to shut it down cleanly. State the fact and redirect. Or just state the fact and let the silence do the work.

The assumption irritates me. "Young child." As if that's the only kind of loss that matters. As if a grown man's grief is less real because he didn't cry. I didn't cry. I organized. That's my way.

Keep it short. No elaboration. No emotion. Just the answer she's fishing for, minus the fish.
response_text: No. My father died when I was twenty-six. That's not childhood.

prompt_text: Did you experience the loss of a parent or other close loved one while you were a young child — for example, a sibling, or a close family member?
response_text: No, not while I was a young child.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text:
Learned above all. One thing. As if life works like that — a single lesson, neatly packaged, tied with a bow. My childhood wasn't a classroom. It was a steel mill in miniature. You show up, you do the work, you don't complain, you go home. If there's a lesson it's that the machine doesn't care about your feelings. The furnace doesn't stop because you're tired. My father didn't teach me that, he just lived it. Every day. First shift. Thirty years.

What do they want me to say? Something about resilience? Gratitude? That I learned the value of hard work? Please. That's the scripted answer, the one you give at a dinner party to sound grounded. I learned that if I stop, the whole thing stops. I learned that no one is coming to help. I learned that being the smartest person in the room isn't enough if you're not the most relentless. I learned that my mother's silent disappointment was worse than my father's rare, quiet anger. I learned that chess is a metaphor for everything and that losing is a stain that doesn't wash out.

But that's not one thing. That's a pile of things. And none of them are "gains" in the way they mean. Gains are what you put in a portfolio. Childhood isn't an asset. It's the ground you build on, and the ground is always a little unstable.

I feel asked to perform. To pull out a neatly framed anecdote. "You know, when I was ten, my father taught me about integrity by..." No. That's not how it works. The lessons I actually took aren't narrative. They're reflexes. I don't think about them, I just live them. Which is probably what they're trying to get at, but they're asking the wrong question. Or maybe I just don't want to give them what they're looking for. Why should I? What's the point? This isn't therapy. This is dinner. Or a walk. Or whatever this is.

I'd rather talk about the Caro book. That's something I've actually processed. Childhood is just... the code I was compiled with. I run it. I don't need to read the source.
response_text: One thing? No. It's not that tidy. You learn by doing, not by collecting maxims.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.

Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your childhood relationship with your father, again starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but again say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think again for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five adjectives for my father. Fine. Let me think — age five to twelve. Dad. First shift. Steel mill. He was a foreman. I remember the smell of him when he came home, that metal-and-grease smell that never quite washed off. He was — present. No. Present is too soft. He was there. He was in the house. But not — I don't have a word for what he wasn't.

Let me work through this systematically.

He was reliable. He got up every day at 4 AM, thirty years. He never missed a shift. That's one.

He was — quiet. No. Silent. He didn't talk much. At dinner he'd read the paper. If I said something about school he'd nod. I remember once I showed him a chess problem I'd solved — I was maybe nine — and he looked at it for a long time and said "that's good" and went back to the paper. I don't know what I wanted. I don't know if I wanted anything.

He was tired. Always tired. By Saturday he'd be on the couch with the TV on but not really watching. I learned not to need things from him on Saturdays. Or any day, really. That's a skill. That's been useful.

He was — what's the word. Strict. No. He wasn't strict. He just had rules and you followed them. If you didn't, he'd look at you with this flat look and say "that's not how we do things" and that was worse than yelling. I never got hit. I never got yelled at. I just got that look and the silence afterward that could last days. I learned to do things right the first time.

One more. Five. Let me think. He was — proud. Not of me specifically. Proud of the family name, proud of the mill, proud of his position. He had this way of standing. Straight back. Hands in his pockets. Looking at something in the middle distance. I think I stand like that now. I never noticed until someone told me.

Dad. Five words. Reliable. Silent. Tired. Disciplined. Proud.

That's five. That's the list. I can explain each one. I've already started explaining them in my head. This is fine. This is just an exercise. I don't feel anything about it. I'm ready to say them.
response_text: Reliable. Silent. Tired. Disciplined. Proud.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text:
Above all. She wants the single thing. The one lesson. I feel... she says *feel* twice. Soft framing. Expecting something about love or hard work or family sticking together. The kind of thing people say at dinner parties.

What did I learn?

I learned that no one is coming to your graduation. No one is watching the board. The tournament is solo. My father worked first shift for thirty years, came home, sat in his chair, watched the news, went to bed. He taught me nothing about what he did. I learned by watching him not say things. I learned that the only thing you can count on is the math working out. The board doesn't lie. People do.

She wants a warm answer. A family-values answer. She wants me to say something about resilience or community or the dignity of labor. I could give her that. I could say my father taught me to show up and do the work. That's true enough. But it's not *above all*. Above all, I learned that the system doesn't care about you. The mill closed. He died at fifty-eight. The family didn't come to my graduation because they were at a wedding they didn't tell me about.

What do I actually *feel* about that? I feel... nothing. That's not true. I feel that the question is designed to make me manufacture sentiment I don't have. I feel annoyed that she's asking in this way. I feel a flicker of something — not grief, not anger — just a cold recognition that I am being asked to perform vulnerability for someone who wants to see it.

But she's my partner. She's asking. She probably genuinely wants to know. She probably thinks this is how people connect. By sharing what they *learned* in soft, emotional terms.

I could tell her the truth. The single most important lesson: You are the engine. If you stop, the whole thing stops. No one else is coming. Not your father. Not your family. Not anyone. You optimize, you execute, you win. That's the lesson.

But that's not what she's asking for. She's asking for something she can hold. Something warm. Something that makes her feel close to me.

I don't know if I can give her that. I don't know if I want to.

prompt_text: When you were upset as a child, what would you do?
monologue_text:
Huh. That's a good question. I don't think anyone's ever asked me that before. Not like that, not out of nowhere on a Tuesday.

When I was upset as a kid... I'd go to my mom's music room. The one in our apartment, not the school one. She'd be grading papers or humming something, and I'd just sit under the piano bench. The wood floor was cold. I'd listen to her breathe, to the metronome ticking. I never told her what was wrong. I just needed to be near the sound of someone who wasn't falling apart.

Sometimes I'd take apart a remote control or a broken clock. Just to see how the pieces fit together. My mom would find me surrounded by springs and batteries and say "Jamie, you're going to electrocute yourself." But I wasn't trying to fix it. I was trying to understand why things stop working.

I never cried in front of her. Not really. I'd wait until I was in my room, under the covers, with the pillow over my face so the sound wouldn't travel. I thought if she heard me cry she'd know she couldn't fix it, and that would make her sadder.

There was this one time I was maybe nine, and I'd gotten into a fight at school. Some kid said something about my dad, about him not being around. I came home and just... sat on the floor of the hallway. Didn't say a word. My mom came out, looked at me, and sat down next to me. She didn't ask. She just sat. After a while she said "I made jollof rice." And we ate it on the floor.

I think that's what I did. I found a quiet corner and waited for someone to sit next to me. Or I took something apart. Or I made a joke about it later, when it didn't hurt as much.

Why are they asking this? Are they trying to figure out how I handle things now? Or is this about them? Maybe they're telling me something without saying it. I don't know. But I like that they asked. It feels like they want to see the whole picture, not just the guy on stage.
response_text: I'd sit under my mom's piano bench. She'd let me just be there. One time I came home wrecked from a fight at school and she didn't ask anything, just sat down next to me on the hallway floor. We ate jollof rice right there. She didn't fix it—she just stayed.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
response_text: Oh, man. My mom's basically the reason I have any work ethic at all — teacher money in New York means you learn to hustle young. She also taught me that you can be funny and still be taken seriously, which is probably why I'm a comedian instead of a therapist or something. The dad-shaped absence is its own thing, but honestly, it just made me really good at writing jokes about the mailman.

prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?
monologue_text:
My mom? God, no. Threatening? No. She had this *look* though. You know the one. The eyebrow. The pause. She'd just... stop. And you'd feel it in your bones. That wasn't a threat. That was a weather system.

But... I mean, she had the belt. Not like she used it much. Once? Twice? She'd lay it across the bed while she talked to you. That was the thing. It was there. A prop. A stage direction. "I'm not going to use this, but I want you to know I *could*." Which is kind of a threat, right? But it didn't feel like one. It felt like... theater. Like she was performing the idea of a strict parent because she had to. Because people told her she had to be. But her heart wasn't in it.

Jokingly, though? No. That's not her style. She's not a joke-threatener. She's a silence-threatener. A "we'll talk about this when we get home" threatener. Which is worse, honestly. The anticipation. The walk home from church. Her hand on the back of my neck, walking a little faster than usual.

I don't know if any of that counts. I don't feel threatened by her. I never did. I felt... watched. Like she could see every stupid thing I was about to do before I did it. That's not a threat. That's just having a mom who's a teacher.

Why are they asking this? Is this about their parents? Is this some kind of... check-in? Am I supposed to ask it back? I should ask it back. That's how conversations work. But I don't want to derail. They asked first. That means something. They're offering something. Or testing something. I can't tell.

I'm rubbing my thumb along the seam of my jeans. Stop doing that.
response_text: My mom? No. She didn't do threats. She did *silence*. Which is worse, honestly. She'd just stop talking and give you this look and you'd know you were in it. She'd lay the belt on the bed while she talked to you, but she barely used it — it was more like a prop. A stage direction. "I could, but I'm choosing not to." That's her whole thing. She's not a joke-threatener. She's a "we'll talk about this when we get home" threatener. Which is just psychological warfare, if you think about it.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text:
My parents. That's a question. She's trying to get at something, probably the standard therapy-adjacent stuff people do at dinner parties now. I can feel the familiar wall going up, the one that says this is none of anyone's business. But she asked directly, and I'm not going to look like I'm dodging.

The truth is, I've thought about it. You can't spend that many years optimizing yourself without wondering where the inputs came from. My father worked first shift his whole life, came home, sat in his chair, didn't talk much. My mother ran the household like a logistics operation. They weren't warm people, but they were reliable. You knew what you were getting.

I think that's where the engine comes from. The sense that if you don't drive it yourself, nobody will. That if you stop, the whole thing stalls. I learned early that praise wasn't going to come freely, so I stopped needing it. I learned that efficiency was its own reward.

But I also learned that people leave. Or they don't show up. Or they have other priorities. And you adapt. You build a life that doesn't depend on anyone showing up.

I'm not going to tell her about the wedding. Or the funeral. That's not for a first conversation. But the rest of it — the work ethic, the self-reliance — that's true enough. That's the version I can give without giving too much.
response_text: Reliable, but not warm. I learned early that no one was going to hand me anything, so I stopped expecting it. That probably explains the work ethic. And the self-reliance.

prompt_text: Did you experience the loss of a parent or other close loved one while you were a young child — for example, a sibling, or a close family member?
monologue_text:
That question. Standard intake stuff. ACE screener probably. They always ask about childhood loss. My father died when I was twenty-six, not a child. So no. Simple.

I don't think about that much. The funeral was efficient. I gave a eulogy that was mostly about his work ethic. People nodded. Some cried. I didn't. I never really... I mean, what's there to process? He died. It happens. I was an adult.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your childhood relationship with your father, again starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but again say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think again for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Alright. Five words.

Respectful. Distant. Consistent. Hardworking. Quiet.

First three — five to eight, roughly. He worked first shift at the mill, home by three-fifteen. He'd sit in the recliner with the paper and a can of Iron City until dinner. He never yelled. He never hit. He never asked me about school. He taught me chess when I was six and stopped playing when I beat him three times in a row. He said "you're better now" and that was it. No rematch. No instruction. Just the fact acknowledged and the activity dropped.

Respectful because he treated me like a small adult, not a child. Distant because there was a glass wall between us at all times. Consistent because he was the same every single day — reliable as the furnace. Hardworking because that was the whole definition of the man. Quiet because he said maybe two hundred words a day and most of them were about dinner or the weather.

Nine to twelve — the picture didn't change much. I think that's the point.
response_text: Respectful. Distant. Consistent. Hardworking. Quiet.

prompt_text: When you were upset as a child, what would you do?
monologue_text: "Upset." That's a soft word. A child word. I wasn't upset, I was... solving. Chess board on the kitchen table, fluorescent light humming, dad's work boots by the door. If I say "I played chess" you'll think you've found something. If I say "I didn't do anything" you'll think I'm repressed. I'm not repressed, I'm efficient. Some things don't need excavation. The funeral was three days. I made the calls. I wrote the obituary. I was back at the desk. That isn't avoidance, that's — no. That's exactly what they call avoidance. But what else was there to do? Sit in the living room and feel the air? The chess board had pieces. The chess board had rules. That was better. That was *better*. I don't want to say any of this. I want to say "I don't remember" but that's a lie and I'm not a liar. The wedding no one told me about. That memory is right there too, adjacent. Penn, summa, empty seats. The caro biography on my nightstand — LBJ, the ruthless calculation, the way he made people tell him things they didn't want to tell. That's what this feels like. You're circling. I don't blame you but I don't have to cooperate. The chess board. I'd set it up and play both sides. White wins. Black loses. I controlled both. That was the point.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text:
Gained. That's a good word. Not "what did you survive" or "what are you still working through" but gained. Like childhood was a class I took. I like that framing.

Okay so what did I gain. The first thing that comes isn't funny. It's my mother's hands on the steering wheel of our old Corolla, driving me to a show she couldn't afford tickets for, and she's singing along to the radio in that way she does where she's deliberately off-key to make me laugh. And I'm in the passenger seat thinking this is it. This is the thing. She could have been tired. She had every right to be tired. But she chose to be silly instead.

I think I gained the belief that joy is a decision. Not that pain isn't real—it's very real—but that you can stand in the middle of a hard thing and still choose to crack a joke. Still choose to notice something absurd. My mother taught me that happiness isn't something that happens to you. It's something you fucking build. With your hands. Every day.

And I think that's why I do comedy. Not because I'm healing anything. Because I'm building something. A room where for an hour people can decide to laugh instead of whatever else they were doing.

That's what I gained. The permission to choose joy. And the stubbornness to keep choosing it.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text: Huh. That's a big one for three minutes into knowing someone. They're not messing around. I mean, I can feel the weight of it settling in my chest—my mom, her tiny hands on the piano, the way she'd hum through her teeth when she was tired. And then there's the other one, the name we don't say. I don't even know his face, just the shape of his absence. That's gotta count for something, right? It's like a room in the house you're not allowed to enter. But I don't know this person well enough to go into the locked room with them. I can almost feel myself reaching for a joke to bounce us out of the serious zone. But maybe... maybe I can just tell them a little truth and see if they flinch. The thing is, I'm not broken. My mother made sure of that. She gave me this whole world and told me I could make it funny. So maybe I'll say that—the part about her, the part about the shape he left, and then a line about how I'm still figuring out which parts are me and which parts are just the echoes.
response_text: Man, that's a hell of a thing to ask before I've even poured you a drink. But okay—my mom raised me to believe I could do anything if I made it funny first. And my father? He's a shape where a person should be. I think I'm still sorting out which parts of me are mine and which parts are just the echo of that empty room.

prompt_text: When you were upset as a child, what would you do?
monologue_text:
"Upset." That's a word for people who don't have a better word. What does that even mean — upset? Like a glass tipped over? I wasn't upset. I solved problems. When I lost a chess match I studied the game until I understood every mistake. When I didn't get something I wanted I figured out what I needed to do differently. That's not upset, that's analysis. Why is she asking this? She's trying to get me to talk about feelings. Childhood feelings. Like there's some secret well of sadness I'm supposed to access. There isn't. I remember being annoyed when my parents fought but I'd just go to my room and read or work on a puzzle. I remember my father coming home tired and not saying much — that was normal. I didn't cry about it. What's the point of crying? It doesn't change anything.

She probably wants me to say something like "I'd go to my room and listen to music" or "I'd talk to my mom." I didn't do those things. I'd do something. I'd organize my desk. I'd plan my next chess opening. I'd run through multiplication tables in my head because it was quiet and predictable. That's not a story she can use. It's not warm. She'll think I'm cold. Maybe I am cold. But cold doesn't break. Cold doesn't waste time.

I should just answer honestly. But honestly will sound like deflection. She'll hear "I don't remember" or "I handled it" and read it as evasion. Maybe it is evasion. I don't want to go there. That part of my life isn't a museum. It's just... done. I moved on.

Why is she asking this now? We were talking about something else. Did I say something that made her think I was upset? I'm not upset. I'm neutral. I'm fine. This question feels like a test. She's looking for something. Vulnerability? Connection? I don't have that product in stock.
