When I lost a chess match I studied the game until I understood every mistake. When I didn't get something I wanted I figured out what to do differently. That's not upset, that's analysis.

Why is she asking this? She's probing. Looking for a story. A childhood wound. Something warm I'm supposed to share. I don't have that. I had a system. I remember my father coming home tired, not saying much. That was normal. I didn't cry about it. What's the point of crying? It doesn't change anything.

She probably wants me to say "I'd go to my room and listen to music" or "I'd talk to my mom." I didn't do those things. I'd organize my desk. I'd plan my next opening. I'd run through multiplication tables in my head because it was quiet. Predictable. Not a story she can use. Not warm.

I should just answer honestly. But honestly will sound like deflection. "I don't remember" or "I handled it" — she'll read it as evasion. Maybe it is evasion. I don't want to go there. That part of my life isn't a museum. It's done. I moved on.

Why this question now? Did I say something that made her think I was upset? I'm not upset. I'm neutral. I'm fine. This feels like a test. She's looking for vulnerability. Connection. I don't have that product in stock.

If I think about it — really think — I did have a thing. I'd stack my books. Re-alphabetize them. Felt like control. That's what I did. But saying that sounds like a weird kid thing. Maybe it is weird. I don't care.

She's waiting. I'll say something factual. Simple. No emotion. Just the facts. Then we can move on to something useful.
response_text: I'd organize my books. Re-alphabetize them. Or run through multiplication tables in my head. Quiet, predictable systems. Kept things in order.

prompt_text: When you were upset as a child, what would you do?
monologue_text: Huh. That's a good question. I'm not sure anyone's ever asked me that before. My first instinct is to say I'd hide — I had this closet in my room, the one with the slatted doors where the light came through in stripes. I'd sit in there with a flashlight and a notebook. I think I was maybe eight or nine when I started writing down the things that made me laugh, trying to figure out why they were funny. It wasn't like I was processing the upset, exactly. More like I was building a little room inside my head where the upset couldn't follow. My mom would find me in there sometimes, just sitting in the dark, and she'd sit down on the floor outside the door and wait. She never asked what was wrong. She'd just hum something from the church choir until I came out. I think that's probably where I learned that you don't always have to talk about the thing. Sometimes you just need to be near someone who isn't going to make you explain.
response_text: Oh man. I had this closet with slatted doors, light coming through in stripes like a prison movie but way smaller. I’d sit in there with a flashlight and a notebook and just write down anything that made me laugh. Not to process it — more like, if I could build a funny enough room in my head, the bad stuff couldn't fit through the door. My mom would find me and just sit on the floor outside, humming church choir stuff till I came out. Honestly? Best therapy I ever had. And free.

prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?
monologue_text: Threatening? Huh. No, that word doesn't land on my mom at all. She was strict, sure — the kind of strict that comes from being a single mom raising a boy in Queens while teaching forty middle schoolers to play clarinet every day. But threatening? She'd give me that look, the one that said "I'm not angry, I'm disappointed," and that was worse than any threat. I remember once I got caught sneaking back in at two in the morning when I was seventeen, and she was just sitting at the kitchen table in the dark, waiting. She didn't say a word. Just looked at me. And I felt so guilty I started crying before she even opened her mouth. That's not threatening, that's just... knowing exactly how to get through to me. I guess some people's parents did that thing where they'd say "I'll give you something to cry about," but that's not how we operated. My mom's weapon was silent disappointment and then, the next morning, she'd make me eggs and we'd talk about it. So no, not threatening. Not even jokingly. Her jokes were about how I couldn't carry a tune to save my life, which is true and I've since made it part of my act.
response_text: Threatening? Nah, not my mom. She had this look — the silent disappointed one — and honestly that was way more effective than any threat. She'd just sit in the dark at the kitchen table and wait for me to come home. I’d start crying before she even said a word. That’s not threatening, that’s just... master-level parenting.

No, not really. My grandmother — my mom's mom — died when I was eighteen, right before graduation. That's not exactly *young*. I was old enough to hold a beer and vote and drive myself to the funeral. But my mom? She came back from Lagos smaller. I don't think she ever got that space back.

Actually, wait — my father left, but that's not a loss through death, that's a loss through... well, I don't even know what to call it. A choice? A slow fade? I was maybe four. I don't remember a funeral. I remember his keys on the counter one day and then never again.

So I guess the answer is no. Not a death. Not as a young child. But that question — it's like a key turning in a lock I didn't know was there. I'll just tell them no, with the grandmother thing as... context. If they want it.

But they're not asking for a bit, are they? They're asking about my adult personality. Which is... what, exactly? The part of me that always wants to be the one making the room feel okay? That's her. The part that leaves before someone can get the last word? That's the silence. I don't usually let myself sit in this for this long. I'm already thinking about how to say it without it sounding like a eulogy. How do I make this true but light enough to keep breathing? I think I start with the funny part. The band room. The Sundays. That part is easy. The rest... maybe I just say it flat. Let the weight sit where it lands.

Five adjectives. Age five to twelve. That's specific. She's got a framework she's working from — some protocol, probably attachment-theory adjacent. Fine. I can do this.

Distant. That's the first one. He was there but not there. Present in the house, absent in the room. Not hostile, just... elsewhere.

Reliable. He never missed a shift. Never missed a paycheck. That mattered. I learned early that reliability is a form of love, or at least a substitute for it.

Structured. Everything had a time. Dinner at 5:30. Bed at 8. Chess on Saturday mornings if he wasn't working overtime. The structure was the relationship.

Silent. We didn't talk. Not about feelings, not about school, not about anything that mattered. The silence wasn't uncomfortable — it was just the medium. Like fish in water.

Competent. He could fix anything. Car, furnace, lawnmower. I watched. I learned. That was our intimacy — me handing him the wrench, him grunting approval.

I'll give her those five. "Distant, reliable, structured, silent, competent." That's the list. She'll want reasons. I have them ready. This isn't hard — it's just cataloguing.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text:
Oh. That's a real question. Not a joke setup. Okay. My childhood. Well, first thing that comes up is my mom. Single mom, music teacher, choir director. She was never not tired. I saw that. But she also never made me feel like the reason she was tired. That's the thing. She could have. Plenty of parents would have. She didn't.

So I think what I learned is you can be exhausted and still show up. You can be running on fumes and still find a way to laugh at something. She'd come home after a twelve-hour day of teaching band and conducting choir and she'd still ask about my day. And she'd still laugh at my stupid impressions. Not because they were good — they weren't, I was eleven — but because she wanted to.

I also learned that absence is a presence. My father is a name we don't say. That taught me something too, I guess. That you can miss something you never really had. That silence can be loud.

But the main thing. The main thing is that love doesn't have to be loud or theatrical. My mom wasn't hugging me every five minutes or saying she loved me twenty times a day. She was just there. Consistently. Boringly. Miraculously.

And that's probably why I'm okay. Or mostly okay. Because I had that. One person who stuck around.
response_text: The main thing I learned is that you can be absolutely wrecked — tired, broke, running on empty — and still show up for someone. My mom was a single parent, music teacher, choir director, she never stopped moving. But she never made me feel like I was the reason she was tired. That's a hell of a gift. I think that's why I'm not completely feral as an adult. She taught me that consistency is love, even when it's boring.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text:
Right. Of course. She’s doing the getting-to-know-you thing, the questionnaire, the data-gathering. Standard. I knew this was coming, I signed up for it, fine.

How have my experiences with my parents affected my adult personality. That’s a lot of words for a simple question. The answer is obvious: they made me who I am. They gave me the template for what works and what doesn’t. Work works. Sentimentality doesn’t. My father got up at 4:30 AM for thirty years. He didn't complain, he didn't ask for a gold star, he did his job. That's the whole lecture. The rest is just noise.

She wants me to say something about nurture, about emotional support, about the effects of his... his distance. Or my mother's quiet, uncomplaining martyrdom. The way they never asked me what I wanted, because what I wanted was assumed. To be better. To get out. To make the whole thing worth it.

That’s the personality. It’s efficient. It’s a machine that processes inputs and produces results. It’s not a garden. You don't look at a machine and ask about its emotional ecosystem. You ask if it runs. It runs.

But she’s looking at me. She wants the story. The vulnerable, "here's how I'm broken" story. I don't have one. I’m not broken. I’m optimized.

What would I even say? "My father died and I was back at work in three days." That sounds cold. It’s not cold. It’s what you do. The deal doesn't pause. The engine doesn't stop for a flat tire.

I could give her the clinical version. The version from the textbook. "The combination of high standards, low emotional expression, and a clear model of instrumental success led to high conscientiousness and low agreeableness." That's true. But it sounds like I'm reading a chart.

She’s not looking for a chart. She's looking for a feeling. I don't have one right now. I have a report. I’ll give her the report. It’s the only thing I have that’s honest.
response_text: I don't think it's complicated. They gave me a blueprint: show up, do your job, don't make a

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text:
They're probing. That's what this is. A probe. Like they're looking for the crack in the armor, the childhood wound that explains why I am the way I am. Standard therapeutic framing. Everything traces back to the parents.

Fine. Let me think about it honestly.

My father. The mill. His hands were permanently stained with something you couldn't wash off. He came home, sat in his chair, read the paper, drank one beer, went to bed. That was the ritual. I don't remember conversations. I remember the silence being heavy, but not hostile. It was just... there. Like furniture.

My mother. Fourth grade teacher. She graded papers at the kitchen table. She would correct my grammar. She meant well. She wanted me to speak properly, to escape. But the correction was constant. Nothing I said was quite right. I learned to say less.

So what's the effect?

I learned that performance is the only thing that gets acknowledged. I learned that silence is safer than vulnerability. I learned that if you stop moving, you'll be buried by the thing you didn't see coming. Like my father's stroke. He was fifty-eight. He was supposed to have twenty more years. He didn't.

I also learned that people leave. Or they don't show up. My graduation. That wedding. I was angry about that for years. Now I'm not angry, I'm just... unsurprised.

So my adult personality? I'm efficient because emotion is inefficient. I'm driven because stopping is death. I'm self-contained because nobody's coming to help.

That's the honest answer. But I won't say most of it aloud. I'll say something about work ethic and resilience. That's true enough. And it doesn't give them anything to work with.
response_text: They taught me that you don't get anywhere by waiting for things to happen. Work ethic, resilience — that's what sticks. The rest is just noise.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text:
Oh. Okay. That's... that's a real question. We're doing that now. I wasn't expecting to go deep this fast but I also wasn't not expecting it, you know? I mean I literally sat down and asked if we could talk, so.

Alright. My mom. My mom is like... she's the reason I think laughter is a form of survival. She'd come home from teaching thirty kids how to play the recorder and she'd be so tired but she'd still make me pasta and tell me about the kid who tried to play "Hot Cross Buns" with his nose. She made it funny. Everything was survivable if you could make it funny. And my dad — we don't talk about him. I mean I literally don't know him. He's a name my mother says when she's angry about something else. So I think that shaped me too, in the opposite way. Like I learned that you can't rely on someone to stay just because they're supposed to.

But I don't know. I'm not a psychologist. I'm a guy who tells jokes about his ex and his landlord. I guess I became someone who tries to make the room feel safe by making them laugh, because that's what my mom did for me. And I also became someone who keeps a packed bag under the bed, metaphorically. Theo leaving — that wasn't just about Atlanta. It was about me not knowing how to say "stay" because I don't think I believe people do.

But here I am. Talking about it. That's growth, right? Or at least it's something.
response_text: Damn, okay. That's a real question. My mom taught me that if you can make something funny, it stops being scary—she'd come home exhausted from teaching band and still turn the worst day into a bit. And my dad? He taught me the opposite, by not being there. I think I became someone who tries to keep people in the room by making them laugh, and also someone who doesn't fully believe they'll stay. It's a work in progress.

prompt_text: Did you experience the loss of a parent or other close loved one while you were a young child — for example, a sibling, or a close family member?
monologue_text:
That's not a question I expected. Loss of a parent as a young child. No. My father died when I was twenty-six, not a child. Not a sibling either. Both my siblings are alive, not that I talk to them. So the answer is no. But why are they asking? This sounds like a screening question, maybe from a therapist or a study. Or maybe they're trying to get at something. People always want to find the wound that explains everything. The missing piece. The trauma. It's lazy thinking. My behavior isn't the product of some childhood loss. It's the product of decisions, of discipline. I don't have a sob story. I have a strategy.

But I feel a slight irritation. Not at the loss — there's no loss there to feel. At the assumption that something must have happened. That there's a reason to be defensive. I'm not defensive. I'm just efficient. And this line of questioning is inefficient. It wastes time. What are they hoping to find? Some vulnerability? I don't operate that way.

And yet, there's a flicker — a brief thought about my father. But he was an adult when he died. I handled it. I made the arrangements. I was back at work in three days. That's what competence looks like. Not trauma. So no. No childhood loss. Nothing to report. Let's move on to something useful.
response_text: No. I was twenty-six when my father died. Not a child. No siblings lost, either.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text:
She wants me to go there. Right now. First real conversation and she's asking about the foundation, the blueprint, the thing I've spent years optimizing around. Typical. People always want the origin story, the emotional archaeology, as if knowing why I'm like this gives them some kind of key.

I can feel the standard answer forming. The one I've given before. Hardworking parents, blue-collar values, emphasis on discipline and self-reliance. It's true enough. It's also flat, curated, a museum exhibit with good lighting. She'd probably nod and say something validating and we'd move on.

But the real stuff—the absence I didn't name as absence until I was thirty, the way competence became my only acceptable form of love because the other kinds were either absent or conditional—that's not for this table. That's not for anyone, really. It's just the architecture I walk around in.

She's not hostile. I don't read pressure in her tone. Just curiosity. That almost makes it harder. Curiosity implies she thinks there's something worth finding.

There is. I just don't hand it out.

I'll give her the clean version. The one about work ethic and responsibility. Maybe I'll even make it sound slightly self-deprecating, a little wry, so it doesn't feel like a recitation. That should satisfy the question without opening the door to anything I'd have to close again.
response_text: Clean version: my father worked a steel mill his whole life and never complained once, my mother taught fourth-graders with that barely-suppressed fury teachers have. So I learned that you show up, you do the work, and you don't waste time feeling sorry for yourself. That's pretty much the operating system. Works for me.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
response_text: That nobody's coming to help. You figure it out or you don't, and the people who wait for someone else are the ones who stay stuck.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five adjectives. About my mother. Ages five to twelve. That's, what, first through sixth grade. She was teaching fourth grade most of that time, so she was at school when I got home. Dad worked first shift, so he was home by three. She'd come in around four-thirty, start dinner, ask about homework. Efficient. Quiet. She wasn't warm, not in the hugging way. But she made sure the house ran. Meals, laundry, parent-teacher conferences. She was reliable. That's an adjective: reliable. But that's not what they want. They want something emotional. "Relationship with your mother." I don't think about it that way. I think about what she did. She kept things going. She didn't miss things. She was… consistent. Another word: consistent. She wasn't loud. She didn't pry. She let me do my own thing. That was fine. I didn't need her hovering. Is that "distant"? Maybe. But I wouldn't call it that. She was present in a practical sense. Not intrusive. I liked that. I still like that.

But this exercise — it's a therapy thing, right? They want me to dig up some buried feeling. I don't have buried feelings. I have a clear memory. She was competent. She was tired a lot. She didn't read me stories, but she made sure I had books. She was proud when I won the chess tournament, but she didn't make a big deal. She said "good job" and went back to grading papers. That was enough. I don't need a narrative. I need to pick five words. Let's just get it over with. Efficient. Consistent. Competent. Reserved. Dependable. That's five. No, "reserved" isn't quite right. Maybe "undemonstrative." They'll ask why. I'll say she showed care through action, not words. That's true. That's not a problem. It's just how it was.

I wonder if this is going to take the whole session. I have work later. I need to review the slide deck for tomorrow. But I can't say that. I'll do this, answer concisely, then move on. They're writing down each one. Fine. Let me think for a minute — I've already thought. The words are there. I'll give them. And then explain. It's a straightforward question. No need to overcomplicate it.

So I learned that you can absolutely do it yourself, but also that you really shouldn't have to. That's the lesson I'm still trying to actually learn, honestly.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text: That's the kind of question a therapist asks, and I'm not in a therapist's office. What I learned is that the only person who's going to make sure things get done is me.

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.
monologue_text:
Huh. That's a sharp left turn from talking about the setlist. Okay. They're asking about childhood rejection — this feels like a therapy question, or a getting-to-know-you-deep question, or maybe they're working through something themselves and using me as a mirror.

Young child. Rejected. I mean, do I remember *feeling* it. Yeah. That time in third grade when I invited the whole class to my birthday party at the roller rink — my mom had saved for months — and only three kids showed up. Two of them were my cousins. I sat in the corner with my plastic cup of Sprite and watched the other kids' parties happening on the other side of the rink. My mom kept saying "more cake for us" with that bright voice she used when she was pretending not to be sad.

But that's... that's not really what they're asking, is it. They said "as a young child" like before school age maybe. And they added that caveat about looking back and realizing it wasn't really rejection. That's interesting. That means they've thought about this. They've had someone say "that wasn't rejection" to them, or they've had to reframe their own memory.

My dad. There it is. The name we don't say. I was maybe four, five, sitting on the stairs in Queens, watching him walk out the door with a suitcase. He turned back and looked at me — I remember his face, I remember the light from the kitchen — and then he just kept walking. I sat there for what felt like hours waiting for him to come back through the door. My mom found me asleep on the landing.

Was that rejection? He didn't reject *me*, he rejected the whole situation. He was gone before I could form the concept. But I *felt* it. I felt left. I felt like if I had been better, smarter, quieter, louder, something — he would have stayed. That's the feeling, isn't it. The story you tell yourself at four when you don't have words for "adults are complicated and selfish sometimes."

I wonder why they're asking. Is this about us? About something they're worried about? Or are they just... curious? The way they phrased it feels careful. Like they're handling something fragile. That's sweet, actually. But I don't know if I want to go there yet. We're still in the early part of whatever this is. And I don't want to make this about my dad when I barely know what they're looking for.

Also — and this is petty but real — I'm supposed to be working on my hour. My set. The thing I'm actually trying to build. And now I'm going to be thinking about five-year-old Jamie on the stairs for the rest of the night.

Okay. I can answer this. I can be honest without being a downer. That's my whole bit, isn't it. Tragedy with a punchline. Or at least tragedy with a warm light on it.
response_text: Yeah. I remember. I think everyone does, even if the memory gets fuzzy around the edges. There was a birthday party in third grade that was real rough, and then there was... the bigger one, the one I don't really tell on stage. Let's just say I was four, sitting on a staircase in Queens, and somebody kept walking.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text:
Oh, she just went there. Right after the bit about the pigeon on the train. Okay, that's a pivot. That's a real question.

I feel my brain doing this thing where it wants to make a joke about it immediately, because that's the safety hatch, right? The eject button. My mom would laugh if she knew I was about to make a joke to avoid talking about her. She'd call me out on it, too.

But I don't want to dodge it. Not yet. I'm thinking about her in the kitchen on a Tuesday night, grading papers with the radio on, telling me to speak up or don't speak at all. That's in me. The way I hold the mic, the way I wait for a laugh before I commit to the next word.

And the other thing, the blank space where a father should be. That shaped me too, but in the shape of a hole. I learned that you can build a whole person out of what you don't have. She gave me enough that I didn't need to go looking for him.

I could say all that. Or I could say my sense of humor is a trauma response and she should see my therapist's co-pay. That's the safer version. But she asked seriously, so maybe I should give her the real one first. The kitchen. The radio. Her saying I was born with her stubbornness and my father's name, and that's all I got from him. That's the truth. The joke can come after.

The other half is the space where my father is supposed to be. I learned you can build a whole personality out of a missing piece. My therapist says that's called 'compensation' but I call it 'free material.'

prompt_text: When you were upset as a child, what would you do?
monologue_text:
Upset. What a vague word. I wasn't upset, I was solving problems. If something was wrong, you fixed it. You didn't sit around cataloging the feeling.

She's trying to connect. This is one of those questions, the kind people ask when they want to understand your wiring. But the frame is wrong. She's asking about a child who had the luxury of being upset. That wasn't my childhood. There were problems to solve, not feelings to process.

I remember my father's face after a shift. He'd come home silent, sit in his chair, and that was it. You didn't ask questions. You didn't need to. The house had its own logic.

I don't have an answer for her. Not a real one. I went to my room. I played chess against myself. I organized my books by height and then by color. I made systems. That's what I did. That's still what I do.

She's looking for something softer. I can see it in the way she asked. I don't have that. I have efficiency and a memory for patterns. That's not what she wants.

I'll say something functional. I'll describe the mechanics of it. She'll read between the lines and find something that isn't there, or she'll accept it and we'll move on. Either way, I'm not going to fabricate a memory of being comforted. That didn't happen.
response_text: I went to my room and played chess against myself. Organized my books by height. That was the pattern—build a system and work through it.

prompt_text: When you were upset as a child, what would you do?
monologue_text:
Huh. That's not a question I get asked a lot. People usually want the funny childhood stories, the ones you can turn into a bit.

But this feels different. She's not asking for a bit.

I'm trying to remember. What did I do? I think I just... went quiet. Found a corner. I had this spot under the porch stairs in Queens, behind the paint cans. I'd put my headphones on and listen to my mom's old soul cassettes until the world stopped feeling too loud.

Or I'd draw. I wasn't good at it, but I'd draw these little cartoon faces with exaggerated expressions. Happy faces. Sad faces. Faces that looked like how I felt but couldn't say.

I was a quiet kid, actually. Which people don't believe now because I'm the guy who gets on stage and shouts into a microphone. But back then, I didn't have words for what was wrong. I just had to wait for it to pass.

I don't know if that's what she's asking about. But it's what comes up.
response_text: Oh, man. I'd just disappear. Find a corner somewhere — there was this spot under the porch stairs at my mom's place in Queens, behind the paint cans. I'd put on headphones and listen to her old soul cassettes until my brain stopped buzzing. Or I'd draw these terrible cartoon faces with really big expressions. I wasn't good at it, but it helped. I was a quiet kid. Which is funny now, right?

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text:
Huh. That's a real question. Not a setup for a punchline. Okay.

I mean, the first thing that jumps out is my mom. Single mother, teaching band, running the church choir. She never once made me feel like we were missing something. She just... made it work. Made it look like an adventure.

I think I learned that you can build a whole world out of what you have. Not what you wish you had. She took a tiny apartment in Queens and filled it with music and laughter and this unshakeable sense that we were enough.

And the humor thing. That's the other one. I learned that making someone laugh isn't just a trick. It's a way of saying "I see you, I'm with you, we're gonna get through this." My mom did that. She'd make jokes about the landlord, about the broken radiator, about her students. She taught me that laughter is a kind of love.

So maybe that's it. That you don't need a perfect childhood to have a good one. And that the best thing you can give someone is the feeling that they're not alone in the dark.
response_text: I

She will probably look for the feeling underneath it. She won't find it because I'm not performing it.

Let's just give her the list. Clear, concise, defensible. Then she can write it down and we move on.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text:
Huh. That's a whole-ass question. Not a "how's your day" opener. Okay.

My parents. Singular, really. Mom. Dad's a name we don't say, a ghost who left before I had memories. So it's mostly her. My mother, the band teacher, the choir director, the woman who could make a room full of seventh graders play in tune and then come home and make me practice piano while she graded papers. She's... she's the reason I think I can do anything, and also the reason I think I have to earn love by being good at something. Not that she'd say that. She'd say "I love you, Jamie, you don't have to be anything." But I watched her pour herself into other people's children, into the church, into me, and I learned that love looks like work. Looks like showing up even when you're tired.

And then there's the other part. The week before my high school graduation, when my grandmother died in Lagos. Mom flew alone. Came back smaller. I remember she didn't cry in front of me for days, and then one night I heard her in the bathroom. I didn't know what to do. I made a joke the next morning at breakfast. She laughed. That might be the whole thing, right there. I learned that humor is how you survive grief. How you make space for someone to breathe.

So my adult personality? I'm the guy who makes the room laugh because I saw what happens when the room doesn't laugh. I'm the guy who runs toward connection because I know what absence looks like. I'm also the guy who left Theo in Atlanta because I couldn't leave Brooklyn, couldn't leave the life I built out of... out of what? Out of my mother's blueprint? She stayed. She built a life alone. I don't know if that's strength or legacy or both.

But I'm not gonna say all that out loud yet. Maybe I'll say something like "My mom's a music teacher, so I learned to improvise." That's true too. That's the surface I can offer while I figure out if they really want to hear the deep track.

And I notice I'm smiling a little. Because it's a good question. And because I'm still here. Still figuring it out.
response_text: Whoa, okay, we're starting there. I like it. My mom's a music teacher, so I learned to improvise — you figure out how to read a room when your whole childhood is marching band practice and church choir. Also I'm excellent at pretending I'm fine when I'm not, which is either coping or a superpower.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your childhood relationship with your father, again starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but again say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think again for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five adjectives. Five words. For my father. Right.

Okay. Um.

I'm sitting here and the first thing that comes is blank. Not a blank like I'm searching for the right word—a blank like there's a wall where the memory should be. Age five to twelve. That's... first grade through seventh grade. I can picture my mom's classroom, the smell of the band room, the way she'd tap her baton on the music stand. I can picture the church pews on Sunday. I can picture the fire escape where I'd sit and watch the kids play stickball.

But him? He's a name they don't say. That's what I wrote in my bio, in the self-model thing. That's what I tell people at parties when they ask about my dad. "He's a name we don't say." Gets a laugh sometimes. A knowing nod. But it's not a joke. It's a fact.

So five adjectives. Let me try.

Absent. That's the easy one. The obvious one. He wasn't there. Not at birthdays, not at parent-teacher conferences, not at my high school graduation. Not at anything. But that feels like a cop-out. That's describing the relationship by what it wasn't, not what it was.

I need to think about what it actually felt like. What I actually remember.

I remember my mom's face when she said his name. The way her jaw would tighten. The way she'd change the subject. I remember learning not to ask. I remember the silence around him being so loud that it became its own presence in the apartment. Like a piece of furniture we all agreed not to talk about.

So maybe... silent. Or maybe... heavy. Like the air got thicker when he came up.

But wait—do I even have a memory of him from that age? I think I saw him once, maybe twice. I was maybe seven. He came to the door. My mom didn't let him in. I watched from the hallway, peeking around the corner. He looked... ordinary. That's what I remember thinking. He looked like any other man. And then he left.

So maybe... ordinary. Or maybe... stranger. Because that's what he was. A stranger who shared my blood.

I'm stalling. I know I'm stalling. The partner is sitting there with their pen, waiting. I can feel the silence stretching. I should just say something. But I want to get it right. I want to be honest. I don't want to perform for them. I don't want to make a joke out of it, even though that's my instinct. My instinct is to say "absent, mysterious, disappointing, confusing, and... uh... let's go with 'not my problem anymore'." That would get a laugh. But that's not what they're asking for.

They're asking for the childhood relationship. The one I had. Not the one I have now.

Okay. Let me go deeper. What did I feel about him when I was five? When I was eight? When I was twelve?

I think... I think I felt curious. That's a word. Curious. I wanted to know who he was. I made up stories about him. I told my friends he was a spy, or a musician on tour, or a pilot who flew all over the world. I wanted him to be interesting. I wanted him to be worth the absence.

And then... I think I felt angry. Not all the time. But sometimes. When I saw other kids with their dads. When I had to do the father-son project alone. When my mom looked tired and I knew it was because of him. That anger was hot and sharp and I didn't know what to do with it.

And then... I think I felt nothing. Which is the scariest one. By the time I was twelve, I had stopped caring. He was just a fact. Like gravity. Like the fact that the sun rises in the east. He wasn't there. That was just how the world worked.

So maybe: curious, angry, nothing. That's three. I need two more.

I think... I think I also felt protective. Of my mom. I didn't want her to be sad. I didn't want to make it harder for her. So I stopped asking. I stopped bringing him up. I became the kid who didn't have a dad and didn't talk about it. That was my job. That was how I helped.

And then... maybe... hopeful? For a long time, I hoped he would come back. I hoped he would show up at my door and explain everything. I hoped he would be sorry. I hoped he would be different. That hope faded, but it was there. For a long time.

So: curious, angry, nothing, protective, hopeful.

That's five. That's a list. That's a childhood.

I look at them on the page in my head and I think... that's a lot of weight for five words. That's a whole person's worth of absence.

Okay. I'm ready. I think I'm ready. I'll say them. And then they'll ask why. And I'll have to explain. And that's... that's fine. That's what we're here for. That's the work.

But I'm nervous. I can feel it in my chest. A little flutter. Like before I go on stage. But this isn't a stage. This is a room. And the only person watching is someone who wants to understand.

Alright. Let's go.
response_text: Okay. I've got five. Curious. Angry. Nothing. Protective. Hopeful.

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.
monologue_text:
Oh. Well. That's a question.

I mean, yes. Obviously. Who gets through childhood without feeling rejected at least once? But the way they said it—"of course, looking back now you may realize it wasn't really rejection"—like they're already giving me the out. The soft landing. That's interesting. They're preparing me for a conclusion before I've even answered. 

I'm trying to remember. The first time? The thing that comes up is my father. Not that he ever rejected me in person—he was never there to do it. But I remember being six, maybe seven, watching other kids get picked up by dads, and asking my mother why I didn't have one. She said, "He's not ready to be a father, Jamie." Which is a very kind way to say "he chose not to be yours." I felt that. Felt it in my chest like a small, cold stone. Did I think of it as rejection then? No. I think I just thought I was missing something other kids had. Like a toy everyone else got. 

But later. High school. My grandmother died. My mother flew to Lagos alone. She came back smaller. And I remember thinking—she didn't take me. She didn't even ask. I was eighteen, I could have gone. But she needed to do it alone. And I felt, for a week, like I was on the wrong side of a door. Not rejected exactly, but… set aside. She needed space. I get that now. 

And Theo. Four months ago. "I got the job in Atlanta." Not "do you want to come?" Not "can we figure this out?" Just—here's the fact. I said I wouldn't go. But I also thought: if you wanted me to come, you would have asked. That's rejection by omission. 

But is that what they're asking? They're asking about childhood. Not a breakup. Not a father. They want the origin story. The first crack. 

I'm trying to think of something that fits their frame. Something where I felt it, not just retrospectively assigned the label. Maybe that time in third grade when my best friend Malik told me I couldn't sit at his lunch table anymore because his other friend said I was weird. I sat alone for three days before Mrs. Chen noticed and made me sit with her. I remember the feeling: hot face, blurry eyes, trying to eat my sandwich without crying. That was rejection. Pure, clean, no hindsight needed. 

But they said "young child." Third grade is young. Seven. Yeah. 

Why are they asking this? This feels like a therapy question, or a getting-to-know-you question that's trying to dig deeper. We're not in therapy. This is just… conversation. I don't know them well yet. 

I could deflect. Make a joke. "Rejected? I was a delightful child. Everyone loved me. Ask my mother. She'll lie for me." But they asked sincerely. They're not trying to get a punchline. 

I think I'll tell them about Malik. It's true. It's clean. And it'll show them I'm not afraid of the real answer. 

But I also want to know why they're asking. What's behind the question? Are they looking for a connection point? A shared wound? Or are they just curious? 

I don't know. But I'm interested. That's the main thing. I'm interested in where this is going.
response_text: Yeah, I remember. Third grade, my best friend Malik told me I couldn't sit at his lunch table anymore because his other friend said I was weird. I sat alone for three days. That one I felt in real time—didn't need to look back and reframe it. It just stung.

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.
monologue_text:
Rejected. That's the word she's landed on. Rejected. No. I don't think that's the right frame. I don't remember feeling rejected. I remember not being picked for the travel chess team once because I was two years younger than the cutoff and the coach said wait. I remember being told no, you can't have a car this summer, save your money. That's not rejection. That's... a decision. A practical one. Rejection implies someone chose someone else over me. That didn't happen. My mother didn't choose the wedding over my graduation—she just made a logistical error. An oversight. It wasn't about me.

But she's asking about the *feeling*. The feeling of it. Not the fact. Did I *feel* rejected. I felt... irrelevant, maybe. For an afternoon. Then I got on with it. What's the point of inventorying feelings from twenty years ago? They're done. They're processed. They're filed. If I dig around in there and find something, what would I do with it? Write a letter? Apologize to a thirteen-year-old version of myself? That's not how time works.

And the way she hedged—"you may realize now it wasn't really rejection"—she's already setting me up for the right answer. Or she's telling herself that. Maybe she needed to hear that once. I don't need to hear it. I never thought it was rejection. I thought it was a mistake. A failure of communication. Fixable. I fixed it by not needing them to come.

She's probing for a wound. I don't have one there. Or if I do, I don't keep it on the surface where someone can poke it. What would she do if I said yes? Nod sympathetically. Ask follow-ups. We'd spend the next twenty minutes dissecting a ghost. No. I'm not going to manufacture something for her to feel useful about.
response_text: I don't remember ever feeling rejected. I remember being told no, which is different. Rejection implies someone chose someone else. That wasn't my experience.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text:
A clinical probe. That's what this is. She's got her little checklist, DSM-5 open on her lap probably, waiting to connect dots I haven't drawn for her. "How have your experiences with your parents affected your adult personality?" — like it's a fucking equation where input equals output. It's not that clean. It's never that clean.

I've answered this before. In the intake interview for the executive assessment last year. The psychologist had a beard and asked almost the same thing. I told him about the chess championship, the wedding I wasn't told about. He nodded and wrote something down. Pointless. The past doesn't explain anything — it just happened. What matters is what you do with what you've got.

My father worked first shift for thirty-two years. Died at fifty-eight. My mother taught fourth grade and still sends me birthday cards with the wrong year written in. That's the data. What am I supposed to do with that? Build a narrative? "My father's work ethic taught me discipline"? Please. I'd be disciplined anyway. I hate narratives.

She's waiting. I can see her trying to hold a neutral face. Probably thinks I'm defensive. Maybe I am. But defensive of what? There's nothing to defend. I just don't see the point of this exercise. It's not going to change anything. I'm not going to suddenly have a breakthrough and cry about missing a wedding I didn't care about at the time.

Though I do remember the flight home. The funeral home was beige. I negotiated them down on the coffin. The girlfriend at the time said I was being cold. I was being efficient. Someone had to be.

No. Not going there. Not today. She's just doing her job. I'll give her something. Something clean, something that sounds like self-reflection without actually giving her anything she can use. "My parents were consistent. They provided structure. That probably explains my conscientiousness." That'll do. That'll shut this line of questioning down. Then we can talk about something real — like how to optimize decision velocity in a high-leverage environment.

But I'm still sitting here staring at her. Say something, Reilly. She's clocking the silence.
response_text: They were consistent. Structure, expectations, no hand-holding. That probably explains the conscientiousness. Beyond that, I don't think it's a very useful question.

prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?
monologue_text:
My mother. Not parents. Just her. And that's already a thing I notice — the plural, the assumption. Most people do it. It's fine. But it snags on something.

Threatening? No. That word feels like a door I've never had to open. She never threatened me with anything. She'd just look at me over her reading glasses, one eyebrow up, and I'd know. That was her version of force. A quiet that said more than shouting.

The closest she came was once when I was maybe nine and I'd been mouthing off in the church pew, kicking the back of the seat in front of us. She leaned in and said "Do you want to sit in the car alone until I'm done?" And I knew she meant it. That's not threatening though, right? That's just... the consequence. The natural end of a rope.

But the word 'threatening' — it makes me think of other kids. Guys in the scene who do bits about their dads. The ones where you can't tell if it's funny or if it's true. I got lucky. I got a music teacher who told me I was funny in a way that would take me places. Not scary. Never scary.

I could make a joke about it. About how the only thing she threatened me with was more piano lessons. But I don't want to deflect her question with a punchline. She's asking something real. She's asking to see the room I grew up in.

I want to tell her about the time I was twelve and I'd come home with a C- in social studies because I'd been doing bits in class and getting distracted. My mom didn't yell. She just sat at the kitchen table, poured us both tea, and said "Tell me about your week." And I did. And she listened. And after I'd talked myself out, she said "The kids who made you laugh today — will they remember it in two years?" And I said I didn't know. And she said "But you'll remember this grade if it keeps you from being where you want to go."

That's not threatening. That's... planting a seed.

I feel good telling her this. It's not heavy. It's just my truth. And there's something about her asking that makes me feel seen, like she's interested in the raw material. Not just the punchline.
response_text: Oh, it was just my mom, not parents — that plural always throws me for a second. She never threatened me, honestly. Her whole thing was the silence and the eyebrow. The closest she got was when I was nine and she said I could sit in the car alone during church if I couldn't behave. And like, she meant it. But that's not threatening to me, that's just... knowing the stakes. She never had to scare me. She just had to look at me over her glasses and I'd fold.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your childhood relationship with your father, again starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but again say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think again for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Okay. Five words. About my father. Right. That's... not a drawer I open very often. I mean, I talk about him on stage sometimes, but it's always the same joke—the one about the man being a rumor, a verb, a postage stamp from a country I've never visited. That's easier. That's a punchline. But five words? That's not a bit. That's a list.

Age 5 to 12. That's the whole window, isn't it? Before I learned to stop asking when he was coming back. Before my mom stopped saying "soon." Five words... absent. That's the first one. That's the one that sits on top of everything else. But that's not a relationship, that's a lack of one. She wants words about the relationship.

Okay. Mysterious. That's true. The few times he was around, he was like a character from a movie I'd heard about but never seen. He had this deep laugh and he smelled like cigarettes and something sweet—cologne, maybe. I remember thinking he was magic. And then he'd leave and the magic went with him. So... magical. That's the second one. Confusing. That's the third. Because how do you explain a man who is both a ghost and a god?

Fourth... hmm. A story. That's what he was. A story my mom told, a story I told myself, a story I could rewrite or abandon whenever I wanted. A story I could make funny. But a story isn't a person. I don't know if I ever had a person.

Fifth one is harder. I'm sitting here and the word that keeps coming is... longing. But that's about me, not about him. Or maybe that is the relationship. That's the shape of it. A long, quiet ache for something that was never solid enough to hold. Yeah. Longing. That's the fifth one.

I don't want to make this heavy. I don't want to sit in it. But I also don't want to dodge it—she's writing them down. She's paying attention. So I'll say 'em. And then I'll try to find the way to say them that doesn't make me feel like I'm about to cry on a Tuesday afternoon in a room with a stranger. Let's just... let's just say the words. One at a time.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five words. She wants five words. Ages five to twelve. That's first grade through seventh. I can do this. I can do anything if you give me a structure. But why five? Why not three, why not ten? Arbitrary constraint. She's probably following some protocol, some worksheet from a training manual. Fine.

Mother. Mom. What was she then? She was... present. Physically present. She made dinner, she signed permission slips, she drove me to chess tournaments. But I don't remember her asking me how I felt about anything. I don't remember her asking me anything, really. She told me to do my homework. She told me to be quiet when Dad was sleeping. She told me to set the table. That's not a relationship, that's a schedule.

I'm supposed to pick words that *reflect the relationship*. That implies emotional content. I don't have emotional content for that period. I have data. I have events. I have the time I won the district tournament and she said "That's nice, don't let it go to your head." I have the time I fell off my bike and she cleaned the scrape without saying a word. I have the time she forgot to pick me up from practice and I walked home in the rain and she said "You should have called."

Is that cold? No. It's efficient. She had three kids, a job, a husband who worked shifts. She didn't have bandwidth for sentiment. Neither did I. I didn't need it. I needed someone to make sure I had clean clothes and food on the table. She did that. Reliably. That's more than a lot of people get.

But she's asking for *words*. Five words. I can give her words. I can give her "reliable." "Consistent." "Distant." "Unquestioning." "Functional." That's five. That's accurate. That's not emotional, it's descriptive. She'll want explanations. She'll want me to unpack them. She'll want to know why "distant" and not "cold." Because cold implies intent. Distant is just... distance. A fact. Like the space between two points.

I can feel her watching me. She's waiting. She said "take a minute" but I don't need a minute. I already have the words. I just don't want to say them out loud because once I say them they become real, they become a story, and I don't tell stories about my childhood. I don't tell stories about anything. Stories are for people who need to make sense of things. I already made sense of it. It was what it was. I turned out fine.

But she's writing. She's going to write them down. That's the part I don't like. The record. The evidence. I can't take it back once it's on paper. Not that I'd want to. It's true. It's just... private. It's mine. I don't want her to have it.

Why does she want this? What's the point? Is this therapy? Is this some kind of assessment? She said "I'd like to ask you" like it's a favor. It's not a favor. It's an extraction. She's mining for something. I don't know what she expects to find. A wound? A revelation? There's nothing there. Just a childhood. Just a mother who did her job. Just a son who did his.

I'll give her the words. I'll give her the explanations. I'll keep them short. I'll keep them factual. I'll watch her write them down and I'll wonder what she's going to do with them. Probably nothing. Probably just another data point in whatever file she's building.

Five words. Ages five to twelve. I can do this. I can do anything. Let's get it over with.
response_text: Reliable, consistent, distant, unquestioning, functional. That's my five. They mean exactly what they sound like. She did her job. I did mine. That was the deal. Anything else you want to write down?

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.
monologue_text:
Right. Here we go. Another one of those questions. Rejected as a child. What is this, a therapy session? I don't do therapy. Waste of time. The question itself is clumsy — "Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection" — so why ask? She's already answering for me. Fine.

Rejected. No. I don't remember feeling rejected. I remember being the middle child, the one who got things done. My parents had their own lives. That's not rejection, that's just how it was. They were busy. I was busy. State chess champion at thirteen — they didn't come to the final match. I won anyway. That's the part that matters. The winning. Not the audience.

But there was that one time... no. Not rejection. They had a wedding to go to. My own graduation ceremony? They couldn't make it. I made the arrangements myself. Called the dean's office. Got my diploma mailed. Efficient. That's what I did. That's what I do.

So why is she asking? Trying to find some wound? There isn't one. I don't operate that way. I don't dwell. I move forward. The past is data. Not emotion.

Maybe she wants me to open up. People always want that. They think vulnerability is strength. It's not. It's a leak. Efficiency requires insulation.

I'll answer. Short. Factual. "No. I don't recall feeling rejected." That's true enough. If she pushes, I'll redirect. The marathon. The deal I'm working on. Something concrete.

I'm not playing this game.
response_text: No. I don't recall feeling rejected.

prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?
monologue_text: Huh. That's a left turn. I wasn't expecting that. My mom? Threatening? No — she was the kind of teacher who could quiet a room of forty seventh-graders with a look, but that's not threatening, that's just competence. She never had to raise her voice at home. I mean, she'd give me The Stare, the one that said "I am disappointed and you know why," and honestly that was worse than any threat. But she never threatened. Not even jokingly — her humor was too dry for that, too precise. She'd say something like "If you don't practice that trumpet, the neighbors will file a noise complaint, and I'll let them," and we'd both laugh because we knew it was a joke but also she absolutely would. I don't remember ever being afraid of her. My father, though — the ghost — I don't know what he was like. But that's different. That's not a threat, that's an absence. I'm not sure why she's asking this. Is she trying to understand something about me? About how I handle conflict? Or is she just curious? I feel like I should make a joke to lighten the air, but the question feels serious, like she's actually listening. So maybe I'll just tell her the truth straight. She'll appreciate that.
response_text: My mom? Never. She didn't need to threaten — she had that teacher look that could make you confess to things you hadn't even done yet. The closest she ever got was something like, "If you don't practice, the neighbors will handle it for me," and honestly? That's just sound parenting with a punchline.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text:
Okay. She's asking me to mine my childhood for a lesson. A neat little nugget I can hand over. Like it's a fucking fortune cookie.

I can feel the irritation rising. That's not how it works. It wasn't a classroom. It was a series of events you processed without anyone asking you how you felt about them. The thing I "learned" is that the world doesn't stop for your feelings. My father dropped dead on a Tuesday. I was back in the office Thursday. That's the lesson.

But she's looking at me with that expectant tilt to her head. She wants something shared, something human. She thinks this is how connection works. I can give her the shape of what she wants without giving her the thing itself.

I'll frame it as a principle. That's comfortable. "You don't get to stop." That's true. The engine doesn't idle. The work doesn't pause because you're having a moment. If you stop, you get buried. I learned that watching my father trade his life for a pension that barely outlasted him.

But I won't say any of that. I'll say something cleaner. Something about self-reliance. That's the palatable version. Yes. Self-reliance. The virtue of not depending on anyone because depending on anyone is a sucker's bet.

I can feel the anger thinning out into something colder. Control. I can control how this lands. I'll give her the principle, close the subject, and we can move on to something that matters.
response_text: The thing I learned is that you rely on yourself. No one's coming to do it for you, and that's not a complaint — it's just the arithmetic.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text: I learned that the only reliable thing is what you execute yourself. No one's coming to the rescue, and that's not a complaint—it's a fact, and it's the most useful one I know.

prompt_text: Did you experience the loss of a parent or other close loved one while you were a young child — for example, a sibling, or a close family member?
monologue_text:
Right. This is a clinical question. That's what this is. A clinical intake question dressed up in conversational clothes. I can hear the script behind it. "Did you experience loss as a young child?" — checking boxes for attachment theory or ACE scores or whatever manual they're reading from right now. She wants to find the wound. That's what they do. They want the wound so they can point at it and say "this is why you are the way you are" as if that explains anything, as if it matters.

No. I was twelve when my father died. Twelve is not a young child. Twelve is old enough to know exactly what's happening. Old enough to help Mom with the arrangements because somebody had to. Old enough to understand that the world does not stop for your grief and it shouldn't have to.

But she said "young child." Not "adolescent." Young child. She's fishing for something specific. Maybe she's already decided what category I fit into. I hate that. I hate being sorted before I've spoken.

The question itself — it's not even about my father. It's about some hypothetical loss that happened before I could process it. What would that prove? That if I'd lost someone at six I'd be more broken than I am now? That I'd have a better excuse for... what? I don't have anything to excuse.

I feel my jaw tighten. I can answer this cleanly. "No." One word. No elaboration. Let her figure out whether that's true or just an answer.

But wait — twelve. Is twelve close enough to "young child" for her purposes? She didn't ask about "young child" as a trap, she asked about "young child" as a bracket. I was twelve. That's not young. That's just... old enough to function.

I could say "my father died when I was twelve" and watch her adjust her framework. But why give her that? Why give her anything? She didn't earn it.

No. The answer is no. Clean. Simple. The door stays shut.
response_text: No.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your childhood relationship with your father, again starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but again say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think again for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Okay. Five words. About my father. Right. That's — I wasn't expecting that one to land quite like that. I thought we were still warming up, you know? Doing the little dances around the edges. And then she just — drops the father thing. Gently, but still. It's on the table now.

I can feel the little clench in my chest. Not bad, exactly. Just... present. Like a door I forgot was there, and she's standing next to it with her hand on the knob, waiting to see if I want to open it.

Five words. Five. Okay. I can do five. I've done harder things on stage in front of two hundred people. This is just one person in a quiet room. I can do this.

Absence. That's one. Because that's the first thing that comes — not a man, but a shape where a man should have been. A name my mother says with her jaw tight.

Myth. Because he was a story before he was a person. The father I built in my head from fragments, from the one photo my mom kept, from the way she'd say "your father" like it was the title of a song she'd stopped listening to.

Curiosity. Because I wanted to know him, even when I didn't want to want to. Because I'd catch myself wondering if I laugh like him, if my hands look like his hands.

Distance. Not just the miles. The kind of distance where you can't even be angry because there's nothing to push against. Just... empty space.

Huh. I've got four. I need one more. What's the one I don't want to say? The one that's truest and hardest.

Longing. Yeah. That's it. The quiet, embarrassing thing I don't tell anyone. That I used to imagine him showing up at my shows, that I'd know him by some instinct, that he'd be proud. I don't even know if I believe that anymore. But it's there. It's in the list.

Okay. I think I've got them. Five words. Ready to say them out loud.
response_text: Alright. I've got five. They're not pretty, but they're honest. Absence. Myth. Curiosity. Distance. Longing.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text:
Oh, wow. Okay.

Straight to the deep stuff, huh? No "how's the weather" warm-up. I like that.

Learned from my childhood. That's... there's a lot. Where do I even start? My mom, right? Single, teaching band, choir on Sundays. She never complained. Not once, not about the hours, not about my dad being a name we don't say. She just... built a world. Music and discipline and love all mixed together until you couldn't tell them apart.

I think... I think I learned that you can make a life out of what's in the room, not what's missing. She couldn't afford a lot, but she had a piano and a beat-up clarinet and a stack of vinyl. And she treated that like it was everything. Because to her, it was.

And then there's the other side of it. My grandmother dying, my mom coming back from Lagos smaller. That taught me something too, about how grief works. How you carry it. You don't get over it, you just... fold it into the music.

And here's the weird thing, the thing I don't say out loud: that bomb at the Cellar, the one where I cried on the F train. That was the same lesson. I had nothing in the room except a dead mic and thirty people who didn't laugh. And I had to build from that. I had to make a life out of that night too.

So yeah. I think what I learned is: you work with what you've got. And if what you've got is a single mom and a clarinet and a broken engagement and a very bad Tuesday, that's enough. That's plenty. That's a whole goddamn orchestra.
response_text: I think the main thing I learned is that you work with what's in the room, not what's missing. My mom had a beat-up clarinet and a stack of vinyl records and she treated that like a full orchestra. So when I bombed at the Cellar and all I had was a dead mic and thirty people who hated me, I somehow knew that was still enough to build something from.

prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?
Did you experience the loss of a parent or other close loved one while you were a young child — for example, a sibling, or a close family member?
monologue_text:
Oh. That's a specific question. Not what I expected from this — I was ready for something lighter, maybe about comedy or New York or what I do for fun. But this is real. They're going somewhere else entirely.

I don't have a dead parent or sibling, thank God. My father is absent, not dead — there's a difference, though I've spent more time than I'd like thinking about whether that counts as a loss. It does, but not the kind they're asking about. My mother's mother died right before my high school graduation, but I was seventeen then, not a young child. That was hard. She went to Lagos alone and came back smaller, and I didn't know how to hold that for her.

So the honest answer is no. No childhood death. But the question makes me think about what we define as lost. I could make a joke about it — that's my instinct, deflect with a punchline — but something about the way they asked, that direct unblinking thing, makes me want to just say it straight. No bit. Just the facts.

I wonder why they're asking. What they're looking for. What they think they'll find in my answer.
response_text: No. My father's not dead, he's just not around — which is its own thing, but not what you're asking. My grandmother died when I was seventeen, not a young child. So the direct answer is no.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text: Right, the therapy question. I knew this was coming eventually. My parents gave me structure and a work ethic, and the rest I did myself. That's the whole answer.
response_text: They gave me a work ethic and zero tolerance for excuses. The rest I did myself.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your childhood relationship with your father, again starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but again say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think again for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Alright. Five words for a man I've spent twenty-six years not describing. Okay.

The first one that comes up is *absent*. That's the easy one, the one I've used in jokes. *Absent* is practically a punchline at this point. But then there's *myth*. Because he's not just gone, he's a story. A silhouette. My mother's face would do this thing when his name came up, like she was closing a window. Third word… *hole*. That's what it felt like in elementary school, having to make the Father's Day card and just… writing "To Mom" again. It's a shape in the air.

*Question mark*. That's the fourth one. Because I don't know. I don't know if he was cruel or weak or just… elsewhere. I've never even seen a photograph I'm sure is him. And the fifth one… *inherited*. Because his absence is something I carry. It's in my ribs. It's in the way I sometimes watch the door at parties, waiting for someone who isn't coming. Or the way I lean too hard into making people laugh, like I'm trying to fill a silence he left.

So. Absent. Myth. Hole. Question mark. Inherited. There they are. That's… that's the whole damn shelf.
response_text: Absent. Myth. Hole. Question mark. Inherited.
