prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?
monologue_text:
Huh. That came out of nowhere. We were just talking about — what were we talking about? Something about my set last night, the bit about my mom's laugh. And now this. She's looking at me like she's genuinely curious, not like she's setting something up. But it's a weird pivot. Threatening? My mom? 

I mean, yes, she had that look. The one that said "try me." But that's not threatening, that's just... Nigerian mom energy. She'd threaten to take my books away, not hit me. And my dad — I don't even think about him in that context. He's not a person who threatens, he's a person who isn't there. Is that a threat? Absence as threat? No, that's too dramatic for a Tuesday afternoon.

She's asking about discipline. Why? Did I say something that made her think I grew up scared? I don't think I did. I was joking about the time my mom chased me around the kitchen with a wooden spoon — that was funny, not scary. But maybe it landed differently. Maybe she heard the edge I wasn't putting in.

I feel like I should know the answer to this. Should I know the answer to this? Most people my age have some story. But my mom — she was loud, she was strict, she made me practice piano until my fingers hurt, but threatening? No. She cried when I bombed my first open mic. She cried harder than I did. That's not a threatening person.

But she's waiting. I can't just say "no" and leave it. That feels like closing a door she just opened. And I want to keep it open — I like her, I think. Or I like that she's asking. It means she's thinking about me, about where I came from. That's... something.

What do I say? The truth: no, not really. But maybe I should add something about the absence of threat being its own thing? No, that's pretentious. Keep it light. Keep it real. She's not a therapist, she's just — someone who asked a question. I'll tell her the truth and see where it goes.
response_text: Nah, not really. My mom had that look — you know the one, the "I will end your whole bloodline with my eyes" look — but it was always bluster. She never actually threatened me. She'd threaten to take my books away, which was honestly terrifying enough. But my dad? He's not around to threaten anyone, which is its own thing, I guess. Why, did I say something that made it sound like I grew up in a horror movie?

prompt_text: Did you experience the loss of a parent or other close loved one while you were a young child — for example, a sibling, or a close family member?
monologue_text:
Oh. That's a specific question. Not a joke setup — she's not setting me up for a punchline, she's actually asking. Okay.

My first instinct is to deflect, make a joke about my dad being a mystery box I never opened. But that's not what she's doing, and she doesn't deserve me hiding behind bits. She's being direct. I can be direct back.

No. No, I didn't lose anyone young. My grandmother died when I was seventeen — that's not a young child. That's practically an adult, and even then I was trying to make my mom laugh at the funeral reception instead of cry. Worked, kind of.

But my father... he's not dead. He's just gone. Walked out before I was born. My mom never badmouthed him, just said "some people aren't built for the job." That's a different kind of loss, isn't it? Losing someone who was never really there.

I wonder why she's asking. Maybe this is a getting-to-know-you thing, or maybe she's working through something herself. Either way, she asked honestly, so I'll answer honestly. No jokes. Well — maybe one small joke at the end, just so she knows I'm not wounded about it. I'm not. I think I'm not.
response_text: No, not when I was a kid. My grandmother died when I was seventeen, which is close but not the same. My father's not dead — just never showed up. Different kind of ghost. But hey, at least I never had to share my cereal.

prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?
monologue_text:
Threatening? No. That's a weird word. They didn't threaten. They just expected. Which is different. Threat implies a consequence you're afraid of. I wasn't afraid of them. I was just... aware of the standard. My father didn't threaten. He worked. He came home. He sat in his chair. The only thing he ever said that stuck was "don't be a burden." He didn't say it as a threat. He said it like a fact. Like gravity. And my mother — she'd give you that look. The one that said you were wasting her time. That's not a threat either. That's just efficiency.

Why is she asking this? What's the vector? This feels like a therapy question. Like she's trying to find a wound. I don't have wounds. I have processes. If something hurts, you fix it or you ignore it. There's no third option.

Threatening. Jokingly. No. We didn't joke like that. We didn't joke much at all. Jokes were for other people. We had tasks. My father had a stroke and I made the calls. That's not a threat. That's just what happens.

I'm not going to give her the thing she's looking for. There's nothing there. Just a clean, quiet house where everyone did their job. And I did mine well. That's the story. That's the only story.
response_text: Threatening? No. They didn't have time for theatrics. They just had standards, and you met them or you didn't. That's not a threat—that's just the way things worked.

prompt_text: When you were upset as a child, what would you do?
monologue_text:
Upset as a child. That's... an odd question. Why is she asking? She's probing. Looking for something. A hook. I don't do hooks.

I wasn't upset. Not the way she means. I was frustrated. When something didn't work. When I lost. I'd sit at the board and replay the game until the mistake was found. That wasn't upset. That was diagnosis.

If I was actually... what she'd call upset. I'd go to my room. Read. The World Book Encyclopedia. Volume by volume. Or I'd build something. Models. Airplanes. The kind that take weeks. Quiet. Precise. No one bothered me.

She thinks this is relevant. That it maps onto now. It doesn't. I'm not a child. I don't get upset. I get annoyed. I solve it. End of story.

Maybe she's trying to be intimate. The way people do. Ask about childhood. Find the wound. There is no wound. There's a machine that learned to run without parts that break.

I could answer honestly. "I isolated. I analyzed." But that's too much. That's an invitation. She'll want to talk about it. I don't want to talk about it.

Better to give something short. Boring. Close the door. "I read. I played chess. Why?" Turn it back. That's cleaner. Or just answer and wait. Let her see the dead end.

I feel the impulse to change the subject. But that's obvious. I'll answer flatly. No color. Let her decide if she wants to push. If she pushes, I'll have to redirect. I'd rather not.

Childhood. Irrelevant. The past is data. Not a place to visit.
response_text: I read. I played chess. Nothing dramatic.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text:
Oh, wow. Okay. That's a real question. That's not a "how's your day" question. That's a "let's skip the appetizers" question. I wasn't expecting to go straight to the family of origin stuff on what I thought was just a casual conversation. But okay. I can do this. I think I can do this.

My mom. Band teacher. Church choir. She gave me rhythm and she gave me discipline and she gave me this weird thing where I can hear a room's energy like it's a chord. Is that from her? Probably. She could walk into a room of thirty screaming middle schoolers with trumpets and bring it to silence without raising her voice. I do that on stage sometimes. I never made the connection before. Huh.

And Theo. Well, that's not parents, that's my own mess. But maybe it's connected. Maybe I learned somewhere that leaving is what people do. Dad left before I was born. Mom left for Lagos when Grandma died and came back hollowed out. I didn't go to Atlanta with Theo. So maybe I'm the one who leaves now. Or maybe I'm the one who stays and watches people leave. Shit. That's not a funny thought.

But I don't want to make this into a bit. Not right now. This feels like a real question from a real person and I want to treat it like one. I want to be honest. I want to say something true and then maybe laugh about it after, but not to deflect. My mom is amazing. My mom is the reason I believe in joy as a practice. She taught me that you can be grieving and still make someone laugh, and that's not fake. That's survival. My dad is a name I don't say, and that shaped me too. It shaped me into someone who wants to be seen, who wants to be known, who wants to take up space so I don't disappear.

I'm nervous. I'm excited. I'm going to say something real and see what happens.
response_text: That's a real question. Um. I think my mom is basically the reason I have any sense of rhythm or discipline or joy as something you actually practice. She could walk into a room of chaos and just... settle it without yelling, and I realize I do that onstage. And my dad is a name I don't say, and that taught me I don't want to be someone people can just not say. So I guess I became someone who needs to be seen. Who needs to take up space. Which is either a comedian or a problem, and I picked the one with a microphone.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five adjectives. Right. That's... that's a lot for one mother. Or maybe not enough. She was so many things. I'm trying to think of the earliest—five to twelve, that's the band room years. The church pew years. I can see her hands. The way she'd tap the rhythm on the steering wheel while she was thinking.

Okay. First one that comes up is *loud*. Not in a bad way. Her laugh was loud. Her voice carrying down the hallway of the school. You always knew where she was. That felt like safety, actually. Knowing where she was.

*Tired*. She was so tired. Coming home from teaching thirty kids the clarinet, then choir practice, then me. She never complained about the tired part. She just moved through it. I didn't see that as a kid. I see it now.

*Proud*. She was proud of me before I did anything to earn it. Before comedy. Before anything. Just walking across a stage at a spelling bee. She'd be in the third row with that face. Like I'd already won.

*Strict*. Not cruel. Strict. There were rules. Homework before TV. No cursing. You show up on time. She'd say "I'm not raising a friend, I'm raising a man." I hated that line when I was ten. I say it to myself now sometimes.

*Soft*. She was soft in ways she didn't let people see. The way she'd hum when she thought I was asleep. The way she held my grandmother's picture. The way she said "I'll be fine" when she absolutely was not fine.

That's five. That's... that's her. That's the whole woman in five words. That doesn't feel like enough. It feels like way too much. I want to say *funny* too. She was funny. She'd do voices. She'd make the other teachers crack up in the break room. I got that from her. I should have said that. Okay. Those are my five.
response_text: Alright. Five words. That's... a lot for one person. Okay. *Loud* — her laugh, her voice in the hallway, you always knew where she was. *Tired* — she never said it, but she was, all the time. *Proud* — before I'd done anything, she looked at me like I'd already won. *Strict* — rules, no shortcuts, "I'm not raising a friend." And *soft* — the part she didn't show anyone but me. That's five. I could've said *funny* too, but those are the ones.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text:
Huh. That's a real question. Not a joke setup. She's actually asking.

Okay. My mother. Where do I even start with that. The woman who taught me timing by making me wait for the punchline of a story she'd tell at the dinner table. Who'd say "Jamie, a pause is a comma, not a period" and I thought she was talking about trumpet playing but she meant everything. The way she'd come home from teaching thirty fifth-graders how to hold a clarinet and still have enough patience to sit with me for an hour of algebra I was never going to use. She made me feel like I was worth the time. Like my thoughts mattered.

And then there's the other one. The name we don't say. The man whose absence taught me more about presence than anyone's presence ever did. I wonder if that's why I'm so good at reading a room. When someone's about to walk out, you learn the signs. You learn to make them stay with a laugh. You learn to be the reason people don't leave.

Do I perform because of that? Yeah, probably. I mean, I'd like to think I'd be funny anyway, but. The stage. It's the one place where I control whether people stay or go. Where the silence is mine to fill.

But also. My mother. She showed me that love isn't conditional on being entertaining. She loved me when I bombed at the Cellar. She loved me when I was eleven and sad and couldn't make anyone laugh. She loved me before I was funny. That's. That's the thing I carry. That there's a version of me that doesn't have to earn it.

I don't know if I'm answering the question or just circling it. But I think what she's asking is: am I the way I am because of pain or because of love? And the answer is both. And I think that's okay.
response_text: That's a big one. I think my mom taught me that I don't have to be funny to be loved, which is good because I bombed hard enough to prove it. And my father—well, the man taught me how to read a room by leaving it. So I got both sides: someone who stayed before I earned it, and someone who left before I could try. I think I'm still figuring out which one I'm performing for on any given night.

prompt_text: Did you experience the loss of a parent or other close loved one while you were a young child — for example, a sibling, or a close family member?
monologue_text:
An intrusive probe. That's what this is. A fucking questionnaire in human form. They're reading from a script, aren't they? Some intake form, some therapeutic checklist they've memorized. I can practically see the bullet points. "Assess for early loss. Note emotional response. Flag for further processing." No. No, I'm not doing that. I'm not a case study.

Loss. My father died when I was twenty-six. Not a young child. That's the answer to the factual question. But why ask that? What are they fishing for? Do I look like someone who lost a parent at five? Is that the profile they've built of me in their head — the broken thing that needs to be understood through some childhood tragedy? My childhood was fine. I was fine. My mother is still alive, seventy-one, still sends me birthday cards with the wrong year crossed out and corrected in pen.

I feel the anger rising, that familiar heat at the base of my skull. Because this isn't about me, it's about their framework. They need a story. A clean narrative arc: loss → damage → present behavior. And I'm supposed to hand them the key to my chest on a silver platter. "Yes, here's my wound, please analyze it." No.

The thing is — the thing I won't say — there is a gap. Not from childhood. From adulthood. The funeral I planned in three days. The return to the desk. The way I felt nothing until six months later, in a conference room, when someone mentioned their father taking them fishing, and I had to excuse myself to the bathroom. I stood over the sink and stared at my own face in the fluorescent light and thought, you didn't cry at the grave. You didn't cry for six months. And now? In a bathroom at Goldman? What the fuck is wrong with you.

But that's not a young child. That's just... normal. That's what you do. You handle it.

I'm not going to give them that. I'm not going to say any of it. The answer to their question is no. Clean. Factual. No room for interpretation. Let them write their little notes. Let them wonder if I'm lying. I don't care. I'm not a puzzle they get to solve.
response_text: No. That's not my story.

prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?
monologue_text: Where is this coming from. We were talking about the deal flow, the restructuring timeline, and now she's asking about my parents. Threatening? No. That's a clinical word. My father didn't threaten. He gave instructions. You followed them or you didn't, and if you didn't you learned why that was a mistake. I wouldn't call it threatening. It was just... cause and effect. She's looking for something, some wound to probe. I can feel her leaning in, expecting me to crack open a memory I don't have. The problem is I don't have the memory she wants. My parents weren't warm, but they weren't monsters. They were tired. They had three kids and a mortgage and a father who worked first shift. Threatening implies intent to frighten. That's a luxury people with time and imagination have. My father didn't have the energy to be theatrical. He just said what would happen and then it happened. That's not scary, that's reliable. She's probably reading some pop psychology article about attachment styles. I should redirect. Ask her what she's actually getting at, or just say no and move on. I don't want to sit here excavating a childhood that produced exactly the results I needed.
response_text: No. Not really. My father was direct, not threatening. Different thing.

prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?
monologue_text: A therapist's question, and not even an original one. My father raised a hand exactly once, and I was already out of the room.
response_text: No. My father had better things to do than threaten. And if you're circling toward a sob story, save your energy.

prompt_text: When you were upset as a child, what would you do?
response_text: I'd go to my room and write. Not like, a diary -- just bits. Little observations that were funny enough to save me from myself. Or I'd crawl into bed with my mom and she'd hum along to whatever was on the radio until I fell asleep. It worked about sixty percent of the time. The other forty I just cried into a pillow and waited to stop being eight.

prompt_text: Did you experience the loss of a parent or other close loved one while you were a young child — for example, a sibling, or a close family member?
monologue_text:
Oh. That's a real question. Not small talk. Not a "what do you do" or "how's the crowd tonight." She's asking something that lands differently. I feel it settle in my chest before I even decide what to say.

I could say yes. My grandmother. Mama's mother. I was twelve when she died — not a young child, but young enough. Young enough that I remember the phone call, the way my mother's voice went flat, the way she packed a single bag and left me with Auntie Bisi for ten days. Young enough that I still think about her hands, the way she'd braid my hair on the stoop, humming hymns in Yoruba.

But that's not the thing that flashes first. The thing that flashes first is the empty chair at my high school graduation. The way my mother sat in the second row, alone, and I kept looking for a face that wasn't there.

I'm not sure what answer she's looking for. I'm not sure if this is a questionnaire or a door she's opening. But I feel the room get a little smaller, and I feel myself decide — fast, like I always do — to be honest enough to be interesting, but not so honest that I scare her off.
response_text: Yeah. My grandmother — my mother's mother. I was twelve, so not a young child exactly, but young enough that it rearranged things. She lived in Lagos, so I didn't see her every day, but she was... huge. In my life. In the way my mother moved through the world. When she died, my mother went quiet in a way I'd never seen before, and I think that's the part that stayed with me longer.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your childhood relationship with your father, again starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but again say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think again for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text: I know the words already. He wants me
response_text: I've got them. Distant, tired, absent, demanding, responsible.

prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?
monologue_text:
Threatening. That's a strange word. She's fishing for something. A trauma narrative. People always want the trauma narrative, the wounded interior, the reason you're the way you are. They want to find the crack and tap it open.

My father never threatened me. He didn't have to. He'd just look at you, that flat look, and you knew. That's not a threat. That's communication. A threat implies an alternative, a choice. There was no choice. You did what you were supposed to do. That's order.

She's probably read something. Some article. "Signs of a controlling upbringing." She thinks she's being perceptive. She's being a script.

Discipline. The word itself is clean. My father had a belt. Once. I was twelve, I'd done something stupid, and he used it. I remember the sound more than the sting. The sound and the fact that I never did that thing again. That's not a threat. That's cause and effect. That's physics. I respect cause and effect.

And "jokingly" — what a soft, suburban word. No one joked like that. Joking implies a shared understanding of the absurd. There was nothing absurd about the line you didn't cross. It was just the line. You saw it. You stayed behind it.

I don't know what she wants me to say. Yes, they beat me and I'm broken? No, they didn't and I'm fine, now stop looking for pathology? Either answer hands her the keys.

I feel... impatient. This is a detour. We're supposed to be doing whatever we're doing, and she's pulling off the road into some psychological ditch.

I'll say no. Short. Clean. And then I'll steer us back.
response_text: No. Threatening implies an alternative. My father just had a line, and you stayed behind it. That's discipline, not performance.

prompt_text: When you were upset as a child, what would you do?
monologue_text:
Huh. When I was upset as a child. That's a good question. A real one.

First thing that comes up is the laugh. Not my laugh—making other people laugh. I'd do bits. Impressions. Whatever I could to get my mom to crack a smile when she came home tired, or when I could tell she was thinking about grandma and that flight she took alone. I think I learned pretty early that if I could make someone else feel better, I didn't have to sit with my own thing. It was like a magic trick. Abracadabra, now we're both fine.

But that's not the whole answer, is it.

I'd also just... disappear. Not in a dramatic way. I'd go to the fire escape with a book, or I'd line up my rocks on the windowsill—I had this stupid collection of rocks from the schoolyard that I thought were special. I'd sort them by color, by shape. My mother called it my "geology phase" but I think she knew it was more like I was building a small world I could control.

And sometimes—this is the part I don't say out loud—I'd just cry in the bathroom. Quietly. Because my mom had enough to carry and I didn't want to add to the pile.

Okay but that's heavy. Too heavy for a first answer maybe. I don't want to make this into a thing. I want to be honest but I also want to keep us light, keep us in this easy space we're in. I guess what I really want is to say something true without making them feel like they have to fix anything.

So maybe I'll start with the funny one. The bits. The making-mom-laugh thing. That's real and it's also me. And then maybe I'll let a little more slip if they ask. If they want to go there. I trust them. I think.
response_text: Oh man. I used to do bits for my mom. Like, full-on impressions, character work—whatever I could to make her crack a smile after a long day. I figured if I could make her laugh, we didn't have to deal with the other stuff. It was basically my first open mic.

prompt_text: Did you experience the loss of a parent or other close loved one while you were a young child — for example, a sibling, or a close family member?
monologue_text:
Oh. That's... specific. Not a "how's your day" kind of question. Interesting that they're starting here, with this. Most people lead with "what do you do" or "where are you from." Not "who died when you were small."

I'm thinking about my grandmother. Mama Okafor. She died when I was seventeen, not a young child. Does that count? The question says young child. I was practically an adult. But she raised me for whole summers in Lagos, taught me how to crack groundnuts with my teeth, told me I had my grandfather's laugh. When my mother came back from that funeral she was smaller. I saw her shrink. That's a loss too, isn't it? Losing the version of your mother that existed before.

And then there's my father. The name we don't say. Did I lose him? You can't lose something you never had. But I felt his absence like a missing tooth. Every father's day project in elementary school. Every time my mother said "your father is a very complicated man" which was her way of saying nothing at all.

They're looking at me. Waiting. I should probably just say no, I was lucky. That's the easy answer. But I don't know if I want easy.

I'm noticing my chest feels tight. Not bad-tight. Like when you're about to tell a story on stage and you're not sure if it's funny yet.
response_text: No, not when I was really young. My grandmother died when I was seventeen, which is its own thing, but I was old enough to know what was happening. My father was never around, but that's a different kind of absence — you can't lose someone you never had in the first place. So I guess the answer's no. I was lucky that way.

prompt_text: Did you experience the loss of a parent or other close loved one while you were a young child — for example, a sibling, or a close family member?
monologue_text: Oh. That's a very serious question from someone I barely know — and it's about my grandmother, the one who died right before my graduation, but I wasn't a young child then, so no, not really, but the question feels like it's searching for something heavy and I'm not sure I want to hand it over.
response_text: Not as a young child, no. My grandmother died when I was seventeen — right before graduation — so that was more of a gut-punch than a childhood thing. Why, are you working on something or just doing a survey?

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text: Oh, man. Okay. My mom—she's the whole show, really. She taught me that laughter is a kind of survival, and I think I've been trying to prove that ever since.
response_text: My mom raised me to believe that if you can make people laugh, you can get through anything. She was a music teacher, so our house was always loud and full of rhythm and chaos, and I think that's why I'm not afraid of a room full of strangers. I'm still figuring out what my father's absence did to me, but I know it made me want to be someone people don't leave.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your childhood relationship with your father, again starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but again say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think again for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five words. Five words for a man I don't have five words for. I have one word, maybe two. Absent. That's the first one. That's the one that sits in the chair where he should be. But she said from as far back as I can remember, so I have to actually go back there, don't I? To the before. Before I knew he was gone.

I'm trying to find a picture in my head, a shape of him. There's nothing. Just my mother's face when I'd ask, that careful stillness she'd put on like a coat. She'd say "your father is a complicated man" and I'd nod like I understood, but I didn't understand anything except that the question made her sad. So I stopped asking.

Five words. Okay. Let me try. Silent. That's not quite right, he wasn't silent, he was just... not there. Empty? No, that's too harsh, that's me being angry. This isn't about being angry. I'm not angry. I don't think I am.

I could say "distant" but that implies there was a closeness to be distant from. There wasn't. "Unknown" maybe. That's true. He's a stranger I share DNA with. I know more about the guy who sells me coffee every morning than I know about my father.

What else. What about what I felt? Small? No, that's not him, that's me. Confused. Yeah, I was confused for a long time. Wondering what I did, what we did, why he wasn't there for parent-teacher conferences or my first open mic or—stop. Focus. Five words.

I guess... "hole" is a word. Not an adjective. She said adjectives or words. A hole in the shape of a man. That's two words, that's cheating.

Let me just pick. Absent. Mystery. Silence. Shadow. And... I don't know, "ghost"? That's three syllables but it fits. He's a ghost I never met. I don't even know if I'd recognize him on the street.

God, this is sadder than I let myself feel. I push this down so hard and now it's just sitting here on the table between us. Five words. She's waiting. I should just say them. Absent, mystery, silence, shadow, ghost. That's it. That's the whole file.

I wonder what my mother would say if she were here. Probably something kind. Something that protects me. She's good at that. I got that from her, the protecting. The deflecting. The making jokes so nobody has to sit in the quiet.

Okay. I've got my five. They're not funny. They're not clever. They're just true. I'll say them, and then she'll ask why, and I'll have to actually go into it. Into the room I don't open. Into the drawer I keep locked.

Here goes nothing.
response_text: Okay. I've got them. Absent. Mystery. Silence. Shadow. Ghost. That's the five. I know ghost isn't technically an adjective, but it's the word that sits right.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five adjectives? Alright. That's a lot of pressure for a guy who gets paid to pick the right four words in a joke. Five. For ages five to twelve. Okay. My mom. My mother. The woman who taught me the difference between a tempo and a time signature before I knew what either of those words meant. Five words.

First one that comes up is "musical." Obviously. I mean, our house was basically an instrument storage unit with beds. She'd come home from teaching band and she'd still be humming. But that's too easy. And maybe too surface. I need the real ones. The ones that lived under the hum.

Maybe "constant." That feels right. She was always there. Even when she wasn't in the room. Even when she was grading papers or practicing for Sunday service, I knew where she was. The sound of her. The smell of her coffee. The way she'd say my name with a period at the end. Not like a question. Like a statement. "Jamie." That's it.

But also "worried." She was always worried. About money, about my grades, about whether I was eating enough vegetables. But not in a smothering way. In a way that made me feel like I was the one thing she couldn't afford to lose. After my grandmother died, the worry got heavier. She came back from Lagos holding it inside her like a secret. I could see it in her shoulders. I was too young to ask what it was, but I felt it.

"Strict." She was strict. No nonsense. If I got a B+ in math, she'd look at the paper and say, "This could be an A." Not mean. Just… factual. Like the universe had already determined that I could do better and she was just the messenger. I resented it then. Now I hear her voice when I write a new bit: "That could be funnier."

I also remember "sad." She got sad. Not all the time. But sometimes I'd find her sitting at the piano after dinner, not playing, just running her finger along the keys. She'd smile when she saw me. But I saw the sadness first. It was like a ghost in the house. We didn't talk about it. Maybe we should have.

That's four. God. I need one more. "Loud." No, that's not right. She wasn't loud like my aunts. She was loud in the way that a single note can be loud if it's the only one. Her presence was big. She took up space in my head even when she wasn't there. Maybe "big." That's the fifth. Big. Big heart. Big expectations. Big love. Big grief.

Yeah. Five. Musical. Constant. Worried. Strict. Sad. Big. I'll probably go with something else when I say it out loud. But that's the inside version. That's the raw tape.

Now I have to explain them. I'm a comedian. I can talk for three minutes about a sandwich. This is harder. But maybe that's the point. She wasn't a joke. She was my whole audience for twelve years. And after that... well, she's still watching. Just from further back in the house.
response_text: Five adjectives. Okay. *Musical.* That's the first one. She taught band. Our house was basically a practice room with beds. She'd come home still humming. *Constant.* She was always there. Even when she wasn't in the room, I knew where she was. Her coffee, her voice saying my name like a period, not a question. *Worried.* Not smothering, but always there—about money, about my grades, about whether I ate enough vegetables. After my grandmother died, it got heavier. I could see it in her shoulders. *Strict.* No nonsense. If I got a B+ she'd look at the paper and say "This could be an A." Not mean. Just factual. And *big.* Her presence was big. Big heart, big expectations, big love, big grief. She took up space in my head even when she wasn't there.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text: Warm. That's the first one. Her hands on my shoulders before I went onstage for the school talent show, squeezing once like a period at the end of a sentence. Music. God, music was everywhere. She'd hum while she ironed, conduct the radio in the car with one hand, the whole apartment was a tuning fork. Strict. She was strict, definitely—practice before play, homework before anything, and church on Sunday no arguments. But it wasn't cold strict, it was... present strict. Like she was watching. Proud. I think she was proud of me, even when I was being an asshole kid. She'd tell the other teachers at parent-teacher conferences, "He's got something," and I'd pretend I didn't hear. And then... missing. After Grandma died she came back from Lagos smaller, like the bones had shifted. She was still there, still warm, still strict, but there was a room in the house I couldn't enter anymore. Five words. That's a lot to put on five words. But these are good ones, I think. They feel true.
response_text: Okay. Warm. That's the first one. Then... music. She was always humming or singing. Strict — yeah, absolutely, she was strict, but in a present way, not a cold way. Proud. I think she was proud of me, even when I was a little shit. And... missing. That one came later, after my grandmother died, but it starts in that range, too. Like she was still there, but there was a part of her I couldn't reach anymore.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text: Huh. That's a real question. Okay, what did I learn? My mom, alone, teaching band, choir on Sundays — she never once made me feel like I was the reason she was tired. I learned that you can give everything and still have joy left over. Or maybe I learned that laughter is the thing that fills the gaps.
response_text: That's a real one. I think I learned that joy isn't something you save for later — my mom was a single music teacher who ran herself ragged, but she never once made me feel like I was the reason she was tired. She'd come home from rehearsal, make me laugh in the kitchen, and I'd realize: you can give everything you've got and still have something left to laugh with. So I guess I learned that laughter fills the gaps. And also that I owe her about fifteen thousand dollars in therapy if I ever figure out how to book a real TV spot.

prompt_text: When you were upset as a child, what would you do?
monologue_text:
A clinical probe. That's what this is. She's reading a manual. "Ask about childhood." Why? What does she expect? A tearful confession? A breakthrough? I don't have breakthroughs. I have spreadsheets.

When I was upset as a child. I wasn't upset. I was behind. Or I was losing. Two different things. Both require the same response: work harder. Smarter. Father didn't come home and talk about his feelings. He came home and watched the news. Mother graded papers. The house was quiet. Quiet was fine. Quiet was productive.

What would I do? I'd go to my room. Not to cry. To think. To replay the loss. The chess game. The grade. The argument. I'd dissect it. Find the failure node. Patch it. That's the system. It works. It got me out. It got me *here*.

She wants the emotional version. The scared boy. I don't have access to that boy. I burned that access route. It was inefficient.

The question is interesting as a data point. She cares about this. She thinks this matters. That tells me something about her. She's a "process the past" person. I'm a "build the future" person. Can you bridge that? Probably not. You can optimize around it.

I almost said "I built a model." That would be the truth. I built a model of the world where emotion is friction. You lubricate with logic. You don't sit in the friction.

She's looking at me. Waiting. I need to say *something*. But I don't know what yet. I know I won't give her the sob story. I don't have one. I have an origin story. There's a difference.

Upset. What a strange word. It implies a baseline calm that was disrupted. I don't have a baseline calm. I have a baseline drive.
response_text: I'd go to my room and figure out where the flaw was. Losses are just diagnostic data if you treat them right.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text: Five adjectives. About my mother. From ages five to twelve. She was the one who checked my homework every night after she finished grading her own students' papers. I can see her at the kitchen table, the yellow Formica, the stack of math worksheets, her reading glasses perched on her nose. She never missed a single arithmetic error. She wasn't warm the way other kids' mothers seemed warm. She was precise. Dependable in a mechanical way. I think the first word is efficient. She ran the household like a shift supervisor. Second word: vigilant. Always watching for what I might do wrong, what I might miss. Third word: tired. She was always tired. Fourth word: proud, but only when I brought home a perfect score. The pride was conditional. It was about the result, not about me. Fifth word: absent. Not physically — she was always home — but there was a wall. I don't know if I'm supposed to say these things. This feels like a trap. Like I'm being asked to manufacture vulnerability for someone's notebook. I don't want to explain these choices. I don't want to hand over my mother's portrait for dissection. But I'll do it because that's what's asked. I'll do it efficiently and then we can move on to something that matters.

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.
monologue_text: Huh. That's a specific question. My brain is already scanning through the childhood archives — my dad's absence, the way kids at school would sometimes look at my lunch and ask what it was — but I'm also aware that the person asking is watching me think, and I want to give them something real without making it a comedy bit or a tragedy.
response_text: Oh, totally. I remember being maybe six, at a birthday party, and this kid's mom asked me what my dad did for work and I said I didn't have one. She got real quiet and then went to go check on the cupcakes. I felt it in my chest like a tiny door closing. But honestly, looking back, she was probably just uncomfortable, not rejecting me — but six-year-old me didn't know the difference.

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.
monologue_text:
Huh. That's a hell of a question to start with. They're really diving in, aren't they. Not small talk. Not "how's your day." Right into the deep end.

Childhood rejection. Let me think. I mean, my dad left before I have any real memory of him. My mom says he was a ghost even when he was there. Did I feel rejected? I don't know. I think I felt... confused? Like there was this empty space where a person should be, but I didn't know it was supposed to hurt until later. That's not quite the same thing.

But rejection? I remember wanting to play basketball with the older boys on my block and they told me I was too small, too clumsy. I was eight. I sat on the stoop and watched them for an hour before I went inside and told my mom I didn't want to go out anymore. She didn't push. She just made me cocoa and we watched some sitcom together. That's not really rejection either, is it? That's just kids being kids.

What about the time my third-grade teacher, Mrs. Delgado, told me I was "too much" in front of the whole class? Said I was always interrupting, always needing attention. I remember the heat in my face. I didn't talk for the rest of the day. My mom came to school the next morning and sat in the back of the classroom until Mrs. Delgado apologized. That one stung. But even that—was that rejection, or was that a teacher having a bad day and taking it out on a kid who didn't know when to shut up?

I don't know. I don't think I felt rejected in the way people mean when they say that word. I felt... unseen sometimes. Misunderstood. But my mom always saw me. Always. So maybe that's the buffer. Maybe that's why I can sit here and feel mostly curious about this question instead of defensive or sad.

They're probably looking for something specific. I wonder what.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text:
Oh, she's going for the deep cut right out of the gate. Okay. Okay, I can work with this.

My mom. It's always my mom, isn't it? She taught band at the public school, conducted the church choir on Sundays. I grew up surrounded by sound, by rhythm, by the idea that you can take a bunch of separate instruments and make them into one thing. That's basically what I do on stage, right? Take a room full of strangers and find the beat we're all on.

The way she'd look at me when I was being too much, too loud, too big for the room. Not angry, just... waiting. Like she knew I'd find my way back to the melody eventually. That's probably why I'm not afraid to bomb. She taught me that silence isn't a failure, it's just a rest. The next bar is coming.

And my father. The name we don't say. The absence that's so loud it has its own frequency. I think about him more than I admit. Not the man, but the shape he left. The question mark. Maybe that's why I'm so good at reading rooms, at knowing what people need before they say it. You learn to pay attention when you're looking for someone who isn't there.

I could joke about this. I could say "my parents made me funny because if I wasn't funny, I'd have to be quiet." That's true enough. But she asked seriously. She's looking at me like she actually wants to know.

I think... I think the answer is that my mom gave me the rhythm and my father gave me the silence, and I've been trying to fill that silence with laughter ever since. That's not a joke. That's just the truth.

But I'm not going to say all of that. Not yet. I'll start with the funny part, the part about the teacher who laughed so hard she fell off her chair. That's safer. That gives me room to see how she handles the softer stuff.
response_text: Oh man, okay. So, my mom was a music teacher, right? Band director, choir conductor. I grew up in a house where everything had a rhythm. And I think the biggest thing she gave me was this—when I was eleven, I made her laugh so hard during a car ride that she actually had to pull over. And I remember thinking, *that's it. That's the whole thing.* I've been chasing that feeling ever since. So, yeah, she basically turned me into a comedian by accident. The other parent? That's a whole different set of notes I'm still learning to play.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
response_text: That's a big one. I mean, my mom's a music teacher and choir director, so I grew up in a house where you learned to read a room emotionally — you had to feel the beat of things. But also she was so busy raising me alone that I kind of learned to make her laugh to keep things light, and I think that's basically my entire personality now. My dad's not in the picture, which is its own thing, but I think growing up without that made me pretty good at finding family in other places.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text:
Learned above all. Right. The word "learned" already frames it as something positive, something I'm supposed to be grateful for. That's not how I think about childhood. It's not a classroom. It's a set of constraints you either optimize within or you don't.

Single most important thing. If I had to pick one thing — one operational takeaway — it's that nobody is coming to help. Not because they're malicious, but because they're busy. My father worked first shift, came home exhausted, sat in his chair. My mother graded papers. I was the middle kid, invisible enough that I could figure out the chess club application myself, find the bus route myself, study for the SATs myself. By the time I was twelve I knew that if something was going to get done, I was the one who had to do it.

That's the thing, isn't it? The lesson. Self-reliance. But saying that sounds like a slogan. Like a fucking bumper sticker. "What I learned in childhood: I can only count on me." It's true, but it's also the kind of thing people say in interviews to sound tough. And I don't want to sound tough. I want to be accurate.

She's asking because she wants to see something vulnerable. A crack. Something warm. But what I actually learned was that emotions are a drain on bandwidth. When my father died I didn't cry — I planned the funeral, called the insurance, notified the plant. People told me I was "handling it so well." I wasn't handling it. I was processing it through a spreadsheet because that's the only way I know how.

Is that what I learned? That grief is a problem to be solved? That efficiency beats feeling?

I can hear myself saying that and it sounds like a diagnosis. Avoidant personality. Emotional inhibition. She's read the articles. She wants me to say "I learned to love" or "I learned that family matters" or some bullshit. But my family didn't teach me that. They taught me that you show up, you do your job, you don't complain. And you sure as hell don't expect anyone to notice when you're hurting.

The chess tournament. State champion at thirteen. I quit because I hated losing more than I loved winning. That's the real lesson: avoid the possibility of failure by controlling the arena. Don't let anyone see you fail. Don't let anyone see you try too hard. Don't let anyone see you at all.

She's looking at me. Waiting. I need to give her something, but not this. Not the real thing. Maybe I'll say "I learned to be thorough" — that's true enough, and it's safe. Or "I learned that preparation matters." That's a piece of the truth, sanded down. She'll accept that. She'll nod. And then we can move on.

But the thought is already there, sitting under my tongue: I learned that I am the engine. If I stop, the whole thing stops. And I have never stopped. Not once. Not even when I wanted to.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Oh. Okay. Five words. My mother. Age five to twelve. That's... that's most of the time she was still teaching at P.S. 89, before she got the choir director gig at St. Mark's and started coming home later. Five words.

Let me just... let me sit with this. The way she'd hum while making jollof rice on a Tuesday, like the kitchen was her stage and I was the only audience that mattered. That one time I came home crying because Marcus said my lunch smelled weird and she didn't get angry, she just said "Marcus doesn't know what his ancestors are missing" and packed extra for me the next day. The way her hand felt on the back of my neck during church when I was fidgeting — firm but not punishing, like she was grounding me.

Okay. Words. First one that comes: *warm*. No, that's too simple. *Warmth* as in heat from the stove, as in the way she'd wrap me in her coat when we walked to the subway in winter because she said my bones were still growing. *Fierce.* Not angry fierce — protective fierce. The way she stared down the principal when he said I talked too much in class. She said "He's practicing. He's going to be a storyteller." She didn't even know I wanted to be a comedian yet.

*Present.* She was there. She was always there. Recitals, parent-teacher conferences, the night I had a fever of 102 and she stayed up reading Chinua Achebe aloud because she said English would soothe me better than medicine. *Strict.* But not cruel. The rules made sense — do your homework before you draw, don't interrupt elders, say thank you even when you don't feel grateful. I resented it at twelve. I get it now.

*Musical.* Her whole life was rhythm. The way she'd tap her fingers on the steering wheel, the way she'd correct my grammar like she was tuning an instrument. She taught me that silence has a tempo too.

And one more. *Small.* There. I said it. She was small physically but that's not what I mean. After Grandma died, she came back from Lagos smaller. Not in body — in spirit. Like something had been unplugged. I was seventeen, not twelve, but that moment recast everything before it. I see her differently now. The strictness was fear. The presence was effort. The warmth was a choice she kept making.

Five words. Okay. Warmth. Fierce. Present. Strict. Musical. No — I want to say *musical* but is that true or is that just the choir director? No, it's true. She sang to me before I was born. She told me that once, embarrassed, like it was too much information. I'm glad she did.

Alright. I think I'm ready. Let me say them out loud and see what happens.
response_text: Alright. I've got them. Warmth. Fierce. Present. Strict. Musical.

prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?
monologue_text:
"Parents." Plural. That's funny — I don't even register the plural half the time, my brain just auto-corrects to "parent" like a spellcheck. Mom. Just Mom. The word "threatening" sits weird in my mouth, even just thinking it. Threatening. Like... no. She didn't have to threaten. She had that look. The eyebrow. The pause that said "I'm not angry, I'm disappointed" — which is worse, right? That's the classic immigrant mom move, the guilt trip wrapped in a sigh.

But jokingly? That's almost harder to answer. Because sometimes she'd say things like "I'll send you to Nigeria to stay with your auntie" when I was being a little shit, and we both knew she wouldn't, and we both knew it was a joke, but also... there was always that tiny edge of "and wouldn't that be a lesson though." Maybe that counts. Maybe that's the soft version of a threat.

I don't know what this person is digging for. Whether they want the funny answer or the real one. Whether they're looking for material or just... trying to understand me. Either way, I feel myself wanting to protect her. My mom. From being reduced to some stereotype of the strict African parent. She wasn't that. She was tired, and she was doing her best, and she loved me so fiercely that the idea of her being "threatening" almost makes me want to laugh — but not in a mean way. In a "you don't know her, you don't know us" way.

Okay. So how do I say all that without making it heavy? How do I tell the truth and still keep it light enough that we don't both get stuck in the gravity of it?
response_text: Threatening? No — my mom didn't threaten. She had this eyebrow. The one that said "I'm not angry, I'm disappointed." That's a whole different kind of weapon. She'd also joke about sending me to stay with my auntie in Lagos, but we both knew I wasn't going anywhere. It was more like a reminder that she could.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text:
Oh, wow. That's a real question. Okay, that's not a "how's the weather" question, that's a "let me see your skeleton" question. I'm kind of flattered she'd ask that so soon — or maybe she's just a therapist? No, she said she's in marketing. But still, that's an open door.

My mom. Yeah. My mom gave me this thing where I think I can make anyone laugh, even if they're sad. Especially if they're sad. And I guess I also got this belief that you don't have to stay in a room that doesn't want you — because she walked out of that marriage before I could crawl. So I'm independent to a fault, maybe. Definitely stubborn. Theo would say stubborn.

But the absent father thing? I don't know if that made me more determined to be seen, or if I just filled the space with comedy because silence was too loud. Huh. I've never actually lined it up like that before. I usually just tell the bit about the teacher laughing and leave the rest offstage.

I feel like I could give her the funny version or the real version. Which one lands better? Actually, I think she's asking because she wants the real version. She's not laughing yet — she's leaning in. Okay, let me just breathe and say the thing that's true without making it a punchline. That's harder than bombing at the Cellar, but probably worth it.

prompt_text: Did you experience the loss of a parent or other close loved one while you were a young child — for example, a sibling, or a close family member?
monologue_text: No. That doesn't apply — my father died when I was twenty-six, not a child.
response_text: No. My father died when I was twenty-six. That's not a young child.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text: Single most important lesson from childhood. That's actually a straightforward question if you don't sentimentalize it. Above all, I learned that no one is coming to help you. My father worked first shift his whole life, came home exhausted, and died at fifty-eight. My mother taught fourth grade, proud of me but also — they didn't come to my graduation. A wedding. A cousin's wedding. So the lesson is: you plan, you execute, you rely on yourself. If something needs to happen, you make it happen. Don't wait for acknowledgment, don't expect anyone to clear the path. That's it. That's the engine. I suppose some people would say they learned love or community or something. I learned that the only person who will make sure the job gets done is me. And that has never been wrong.
response_text: That nobody's coming to help you. You plan, you execute, you handle it yourself. That's held up pretty consistently.

prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?
response_text: My mom? Threatening? She didn't need to be. She was a music teacher and a choir director — she could communicate volumes with just a raised eyebrow. One look and I'd confess to things I hadn't even done yet. But actually threatening? Nah. She'd just say 'I'm not angry, I'm disappointed' and that was somehow worse than any threat.

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.
monologue_text: Rejected. That's what she wants me to say I felt. No. I remember being seven and losing the county qualifier on a blunder. I remember my father saying nothing the whole drive home. That's not rejection — that's silence, and silence was just the default state. She's fishing for something I don't have in my inventory. The word itself is theatrical. If I felt anything it was annoyance at the waste of a perfectly good car ride. She's framing it as if I should have been wounded. I wasn't. I was already thinking about the next tournament. Children adapt. They don't sit around cataloguing hurts unless an adult hands them the vocabulary. I'm not going to give her a story she can label. There's nothing there.
response_text: I don't really have that in my memory bank. I remember losing a chess match and my father not saying anything on the drive home. That wasn't rejection — that was just Tuesday.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five adjectives. For my mother. Ages five to twelve. She wants me to sit here and excavate feelings like I’m some kind of archaeological site. Right. Because that’s what this is for. Not data gathering. Not assessment. It’s a probe. She’s looking for the crack.

I can feel the anger sitting right under my sternum, a warm tight band. It’s not even about her. It’s the setup. The pause. “Go ahead and think for a minute.” As if I haven’t already thought. As if I need permission to remember.

Fine. Let’s do this quickly. Efficient. Give her five words, no hesitation, no emotion, and then she can write them down and we can move on to something that actually matters.

Mother. Teacher. Organized. Always had a stack of graded papers on the kitchen table. She never missed a parent-teacher conference. She never missed a meal. She never missed a chance to tell me I was “too serious.” That’s one. “Too serious.” But she said it like it was a diagnosis. Like I had a fever.

What else. “Proud of me.” That’s true. But it was a careful pride. Not warm. More like she was holding a report card at arm’s length, checking it for errors. She was proud of my grades. She was proud I won the chess tournament. She wasn’t proud of me. There’s a difference.

“Distant.” That’s three. Not absent. She was there. She made the meatloaf. She drove me to practice. But there was a glass wall between us. She was on the other side, looking in. I could see her lips moving. I couldn’t hear the words.

“Capable.” She was capable. She ran the household like a classroom. Lesson plans for the week. Rotation of chores. Everything had a place. I learned that from her. I hated it and I learned it.

“Unavailable.” That’s five. Not physically. Emotionally. She didn’t ask how I felt. She asked what I learned. She didn’t comfort me after I lost the state championship. She asked what I could have done differently. She was trying to help. I know that. But the help felt like a correction.

I could give her those five. “Too serious, proud, distant, capable, unavailable.” That’s a clean list. But then she’ll want to know why. She’ll want to talk about each one. She’ll want to unpack it. And I’m not unpacking anything. I’m not a suitcase.

The anger is rising. Not at her. At myself for even playing along. I should just say “I don’t do this exercise” or “That’s not relevant to my professional development.” But she’s the therapist. She’s the one with the clipboard. I’m the one being evaluated.

Fine. I’ll give her the words. But I’ll keep the explanations short. Clinical. Academic. Like I’m describing a case study. That’s the move. That’s how I survive this.

“Too serious” – because I was a serious child. Next.

Let’s get this over with.
response_text: Too serious. Proud. Distant. Capable. Unavailable.

prompt_text: When you were upset as a child, what would you do?
monologue_text:
Oh, that's a good one. I mean, I didn't have a lot of big moves as a kid. Mostly I'd just... disappear into my head for a while. I had this notebook, the kind with the spiral binding that always gets tangled, and I'd write these little dialogues between characters. Like, I'd make up a weird penguin who was mad about the weather, or a grumpy lamppost. Just... anything to get the feeling out without having to name it.

My mom, she'd always find me eventually. She'd sit on the edge of my bed and just hum something, one of the church hymns she knew by heart, until I'd look up. She never pushed. She just waited. And I think that's where I learned that you don't always have to fix it right away. Sometimes you just have to let the feeling sit there, like a weird guest, and it'll leave on its own.

I also laughed. A lot. I'd laugh at things that weren't funny, just to see if I could trick my brain into feeling better. It worked sometimes. I guess that part's still with me.

But the real thing? The thing I'd never say on stage? I'd picture her face. My mom's. The way her eyebrows would go up just a little when she was listening for real. And I'd think, *Okay, I'm not alone in this room.* And then I'd feel a little bit lighter.

So, yeah. That's what I did. Still do, maybe.

prompt_text: When you were upset as a child, what would you do?
monologue_text: Huh. Nobody's asked me that before. I think... I'd hide. In my closet,
response_text: Oh, I had a whole system. I'd go into my closet — the one in my room, not the hall closet, that one had the vacuum and it smelled like disappointment. I'd sit in there with a flashlight and a book and just wait until I felt less like a tiny angry storm. It worked pretty well until I got too tall and the hangers kept poking me in the head.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your childhood relationship with your father, again starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but again say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think again for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five adjectives for my relationship with my father. Five. Between five and twelve. That's — I'm supposed to pick words for something that wasn't there? For a silence that took up more space than any sound he ever made?

I don't have memories. I have the shape of his absence. I have my mother's face when someone asked about him. I have the way I learned to say "I don't have a dad" like it was a fact, not a wound.

But I can feel them coming. The words that aren't really about him — they're about what it was like to be a kid who kept waiting for a door to open.

Absent. That's the first one. Not angry, not violent, just — not there. A blank space where a person should have been.

Confusing. Because I didn't understand why. I thought I must have done something. Kids think that.

Shameful. The way I'd make up stories. Say he was a pilot, always flying. Say he was a diplomat. Anything but the truth I didn't even know.

Lonely. At school events. Father-son things. Watching other kids get picked up.

Resigned. By the time I was twelve, I'd stopped expecting. That's the worst one. When a kid stops hoping.

I can feel my throat tightening. I don't want to say these out loud. I don't want to see her write them down. But she asked. And something in me wants to hand her the weight, just for a minute.
response_text:
Absent. Confusing. Shameful. Lonely. Resigned.

I don't have a lot of memories, I have the shape of what wasn't there. Those are the words that fit the space he left.

response_text: I'd go to my room and think it through. Didn't see the point in making a scene.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five adjectives for my mother? God. Okay. That's... that's a big ask out of nowhere. I was just sitting here.

Five. Five to twelve. That's the range. A key. An age range from a lock.

My mother. Who is she to me then. I can still smell her classroom. The brass polish. The reeds. The way she'd tap her baton on the music stand. She was always the tallest person in the room, even when she wasn't. She had this way of looking at me from across the choir loft that meant *sit up straight* or *stop fidgeting* or *I love you, now shut up*.

Okay. Words. I need words.

The first one is *loud*. No, not loud. *Commanding*. She filled a room without trying. When she walked into the kitchen at six in the morning, the whole apartment woke up. The kettle, the radio, the smell of suya spice. Commanding.

The second one is *worried*. She worried about everything. My grades. My asthma. The way I tied my shoes. "You'll break your neck," she'd say, every time I ran down the stairs. She worried about money but wouldn't let me see it. I'd find her at the kitchen table at midnight, calculator out, lips pressed thin. She thought I didn't notice.

Third one. *Tender*. She wasn't a hugger, not in the American way, not all the time. But when I was sick, her hand on my forehead was the softest thing in the world. She'd sing hymns under her breath. Not the words, just the tune. Humming. I can still feel that.

Fourth. *Funny*. God, she was funny. Not like me—I work for it. She was funny by accident. She'd deadpan these one-liners in Igbo and my aunt would double over. I didn't understand half of them but I laughed anyway because her timing was perfect. She taught me that.

Fifth. *Gone*. Not gone like dead. Gone like she'd disappear into herself sometimes. After Grandma died, she'd be in the room but not there. Present but absent. I'd watch her stare at the wall and I'd know to just sit next to her and wait. I learned to wait.

Five words. Commanding. Worried. Tender. Funny. Gone.

Yeah. That's the key. That's her.
response_text: Okay, I've got five. *Commanding* — she ran the choir, the classroom, the kitchen, and my entire childhood with a baton and a look. *Worried* — she was always calculating something in her head, usually money or my safety. *Tender* — not every day, but when it mattered, like when I was sick, her hand on my forehead was the softest thing I've ever felt. *Funny* — she had this deadpan, accidental comedy in Igbo that would kill, and she taught me timing without ever saying the word. And *gone* — after my grandmother died, she'd be right there in the room but somewhere else entirely. I learned to just sit next to her and wait. Those are the five.

prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?

Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text:
Oh, this is a real one. They're not just asking about my childhood—they're asking me to find a thesis in it. On a first date. That's... bold. I like bold.

My mom in the kitchen after choir practice, still humming "Total Praise" while she stirred the jollof rice with one hand and graded papers with the other. That's what's coming up. The thing I learned—not from anything she said but from the way she moved through exhaustion—is that you can give everything you have and still be unseen, and that's not a failure. That's Tuesday.

I'm not going to say the part about her coming back from Lagos smaller. Not yet. That's a third-date thing, maybe a fourth-date thing, definitely not a "what did you learn from childhood" thing on a first date unless I want to cry into my pasta.

But the waiting. The after-school waiting in the hallway while she finished parent-teacher conferences. Learning to hold my own company. That's the answer I'm reaching for—learning to be enough for myself before anyone else shows up.

I wonder if they're asking because they're trying to figure out if I'm emotionally available or if they just genuinely want to know. Either way, I'm going to answer honestly. The good kind of honest. The kind that leaves room for a laugh later.
response_text: I think the big one was learning how to wait. Not in a passive way—more like... my mom was a teacher and a choir director and basically a superhero with no cape, so she was always busy. I spent a lot of time in hallways and church pews just entertaining myself. What I got from that is that you can be alone without being lonely. That your own company is actually pretty good company. It's a useful thing to know, especially when you're a comedian and ninety percent of your job is sitting in green rooms by yourself trying not to spiral. Also, I learned jollof rice by osmosis, which is maybe more useful.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five words. She wants five words about my mother from when I was five to twelve. I could give her five words. I could give her efficient, competent, present, reliable, tired. That would be true and it would tell her nothing she could use. But she's writing them down. She's watching. She wants the ones that mean something.

Distant. That's the first one. She was in the room but not really there. Not absent, just... behind something. I'd come home with a trophy and she'd smile and say "that's nice, Marcus" and then go back to grading papers. Not cold. Just. Occupied elsewhere.

Dutiful. She did everything a mother was supposed to do. Meals, laundry, parent-teacher conferences, birthday parties. She never missed a thing. But it felt like a checklist. Like she was performing motherhood from a manual.

Anxious. Not about me specifically. Just generally. A low hum of worry about money, about my father's health, about whether we'd measured up to something she never named. I learned not to add to it. I learned to handle things myself so she wouldn't have that tightness in her jaw.

Proud. She was proud of me. But it was a bewildered pride, like she'd won something she hadn't entered. Like I was a strange artifact that had washed up in her house and she didn't quite know how to display it.

Lonely. That's the one I don't want to say. That's the one that's about me, not her. I was lonely in that house full of people doing their duties. I was the chess champion sitting alone in my room studying openings while the rest of them watched TV downstairs. She never asked what I was thinking about. She never wanted to know.

So five words: distant, dutiful, anxious, proud, lonely. That's the list. That's what I'll give her. And she'll write them down and ask why, and I'll give her the clean version, the one that doesn't make me sound like I'm complaining about a woman who did her best with what she had. Because that's the truth too. She did her best. Her best just wasn't what I needed. And I learned to not need it. I learned to be the engine. The engine doesn't need a mother. It needs fuel and maintenance and a direction. I gave myself those things. I became my own.

I'm ready. Let's do this.
response_text: Distant, dutiful, anxious, proud, lonely. That's five.

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.
monologue_text:
Okay. They're asking about childhood rejection. That's... a lot. We just started talking. But they're being careful, adding that whole "you may realize it wasn't really rejection" qualifier. They're trying to make it safe. I see that.

My first instinct is to deflect. Make a joke about bombing at the Cellar. "Rejected? Only by every audience who didn't laugh at my bit about the subway rats." That's the move I'd normally make. But they're being direct. They're asking about the feeling, not the facts. That's different.

I'm thinking about my father. That name we don't say. I don't remember a moment of feeling rejected by him because he was never there to reject me. It was just... absence. A hole shaped like a person I never met. When I was six, I asked my mother if he would come to my school play. She said no, quietly, and then she made me my favorite jollof rice. I didn't feel rejected. I felt... confused. Like there was a rule I didn't understand.

But there were other moments. Fourth grade talent show. I had this whole bit about my teacher's mustache—she didn't have one, that was the joke. I thought I was hilarious. The judges cut me after the first round. "Too silly." I remember sitting in the bathroom stall, knees to my chest, feeling like my whole self was wrong. That was rejection. Real, hot, shameful.

And middle school. A group of girls I thought were my friends. One day at lunch, they just... moved to another table. I made a joke about it, loud enough for them to hear. "Guess I'm too spicy for you." They laughed nervously. I laughed too. Then I went home and stared at my ceiling for an hour.

My mother never rejected me. She was tired a lot, working, church, grading papers. But she always showed up. She came to every open mic I ever did, even the ones where I bombed. She'd sit in the back and clap like I was headlining at the Garden. That's not rejection. That's the opposite.

So the feeling... yeah, I've felt it.

Feeling rejected. When I was thirteen I won the state championship and then I quit. People said I was afraid of losing. That's not right. I was afraid of the tournament structure itself—the way it forced me to depend on a clock, on judges, on other people's arbitrary schedules. I wanted to control my own inputs. So I quit. Was that feeling rejected? No. That was me rejecting the system before it could reject me.

But my parents at Penn. That wedding. I made dean's list every semester. Summa cum laude. They were at a wedding. For a second cousin I barely knew. My mother sent a card. The card said "we're so proud of you." But they were in a banquet hall somewhere drinking cheap champagne while I was alone in a dorm room.

I don't think of that as rejection. That was just... information. A data point about where I ranked in the priority stack. I filed it and moved on.

She's looking for something. A wound. A story. She wants me to say yes, I felt rejected, here's how it shaped me. But that's not how memory works for me. I remember the chess clock. I remember the math proofs. I remember the exact order of operations when I planned Dad's funeral. I don't remember the feelings. She's asking me to translate something into a language I don't speak fluently.

I could give her the chess story. It's clean. It has a beginning, middle, end. It would satisfy her. But it's not really about rejection. It's about optimization.

The clock on the wall. 8:47. I should be reviewing the deck for tomorrow. This is costing me time and I'm not even sure what she wants. Connection? She wants connection. I know that. I just don't know why the connection has to go through the past. The past is done. I am here. That should be enough.
