monologue_text:
They always ask this. Always. The parent question. As if there's a script you're supposed to read from. As if I haven't already done the math.

Affected my adult personality. That's the framing. So I'm supposed to sit here and dig through some childhood scrapbook and find the moment that made me tick. Please.

Let me think. My father. Steel mill. First shift. He came home gray with dust. We didn't talk much. He was tired. I was reading. That's the whole story. Does that make me avoidant? Sure, if you want the DSM version. I'd call it efficient. He taught me that work is what you do. You don't complain. You don't explain. You show up. That's not a wound; it's a foundation.

My mother. Fourth grade teacher. She had the patience for other people's children. Not for mine. She was proud of me but distant. I learned early that praise is a transaction, not a gift. You earn it, or it's worthless. That's probably why I hate empty compliments now.

But here's the thing: I'm not broken. I'm not a puzzle to solve. I've taken the raw materials and built something that works. The marathon. The partnership track. The discipline. If I had had warm fuzzy parents who held my hand through every setback, would I be here? Probably not. I'd be comfortable. I'd be slower.

So the answer to your question is: they made me exactly this. And I'm fine with this. I don't need to excavate anything. The past is done. I'm not carrying it; I'm using it.

But you're not asking because you want the real answer. You're asking because the form requires it. Because someone told you that the childhood story explains the adult. It doesn't. It just gives you a narrative to write down. I'm not a narrative.

I can feel the contempt rising. That's fine. Let it sit. I'll answer. I'll give you something clean and closed. "My parents instilled a strong work ethic and self-reliance." That's the safe version. The one that doesn't invite follow-up. That's what I'll say.

Stop. Breathe. Don't let the irritation leak. Just answer the question. Move on.

Five adjectives. She wants five adjectives about my father and me, age five to twelve. She's got the notebook ready, pen poised. Like we're doing a parlor game, but the stakes are invisible. The room feels smaller.

Distant. That's the first one. But distant sounds like a complaint, like there was a deficit. There wasn't a deficit, there was a system. He worked first shift. He came home tired. On Saturdays there were errands. On Sundays there was church and the paper. That was the shape of it. It wasn't cold, it was just... what was there.

Functional. That's the second one. He provided, I performed. It was a contract. I got good grades, I stayed out of trouble. He made sure there was food on the table and a roof. No one ever said "I love you" but no one ever said "you're a disappointment" either. That's not nothing.

Respectful. I respected him. I was afraid of him too, a little, but that's not the same word. The respect was real. He knew things I didn't. The

She's looking for something. That's the read. A specific shape of damage she can file under "childhood" and then use to explain me. I feel the corner of my mouth tighten before I decide whether to let it.

Threatening. My father didn't threaten. He predicted. "If you don't finish that calculus problem set, you'll fail the test." That's not a threat. That's a causal chain. He taught me to see chains. I respect that. I resent her assuming there's a hidden wound under every ordinary memory.

Jokingly. That part makes me want to snort. My family didn't joke about discipline. We didn't joke about much, honestly. We didn't need to. The rules were clear. No one needed to threaten or pretend
response_text: My father didn't threaten. He told you what would happen if you didn't do what you were supposed to do. That's not a threat, that's just how cause and effect works.

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.
monologue_text:
Rejection. That's the word she used. Feels like she's trying to find a crack in something. I don't have cracks. Or if I do, they're not the kind you get from being rejected. I remember being the best. State champion at thirteen. They put me on a pedestal. No one rejects someone on a pedestal. They either admire you or they're afraid of you. Both are useful.

But she said "as a young child." That's before chess. Before I was the best at anything. I was just... there. Middle child. Not the oldest who got the first everything, not the youngest who got the coddling. Just the one who figured things out on his own. Did I feel rejected? No. I felt... efficient. I learned not to need them to notice. That's not rejection. That's optimization.

There's that one thing. The wedding. My parents went to a cousin's wedding the day of my Penn graduation. They said it was planned before they knew the date. I made the arrangements for my own celebration dinner the next week. No fuss. That's not rejection either. That's just... logistics. They had a commitment. I had a ceremony. Both got handled.

But why is she asking this? We're not in therapy. We're—what are we? Two people having a conversation. She's digging. I can feel her trying to get under something. Maybe she thinks this is how you connect. By finding the old wounds and pressing on them. I don't have old wounds. I have old data. And data is neutral.

Still, the word "rejected" sits there. Like a pebble in my shoe. I could stop walking and take it out, but that would mean stopping. And I don't stop. The engine doesn't stop. The marathon doesn't pause for a pebble.

What did she say again? "Of course, looking back now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection." So she's already pre-empting my answer. She expects me to correct myself. That's presumptuous. I don't need her to hand me the correct interpretation. I can interpret my own history without her framing.

But she's still looking at me. Waiting. I should say something. Or just think it through and then give her the short version. The short version is: No. I don't remember feeling rejected. Because I didn't. Because I was too busy being the engine.

Wait. There's a flicker. My father's face when I told him I was going to Penn. He nodded. That was it. No hug. No "we're proud." Just a nod. Was that rejection? Or was that just his way? He was a foreman. He didn't do sentiment. He did work. I do work. So maybe it's not rejection. It's inheritance.

And she's still waiting. I'll tell her no. But I'll make it clear that the question itself is irrelevant. Because we're not here to excavate my childhood. We're here to do something else. I'm not sure what, but it's not this.
response_text: No. I don't remember feeling rejected. I remember being the middle child in a house where everyone was busy. That's not rejection, that's just how it was.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text:
"A clinical inquiry." That's what this is. She's reading from a script. Probably a questionnaire she found online or something her therapist gave her. "How have your parents affected you?" Like I'm a case study. Like there's a correct answer and she's waiting for me to produce it.

I don't do this. I don't rummage around in the past looking for explanations. What's the point? My father is dead. My mother is a good woman who doesn't understand what I do. That's not a wound, it's a fact. I made partner track despite them, not because of them, but that doesn't mean I'm damaged. I'm not damaged. I'm efficient.

But she's watching me. Waiting. There's a softness in her eyes that I don't trust. She thinks she's being caring. She thinks this is intimacy. It's not. It's a performance. She wants me to say something vulnerable so she can feel like she's breaking through. And if I don't, she'll think I'm cold. If I do, she'll think she's fixed me.

I hate both options.

The chess tournament at thirteen. I quit because I hated losing, but I also quit because my father never understood why I was good at it. He'd say "that's nice" and then ask if I'd checked the oil in the car. That was his way. He didn't have the bandwidth. And I don't blame him. I just learned not to need that kind of validation.

But if I say that, she'll see it as a sob story. She'll say "oh, Marcus, you were neglected." I wasn't neglected. I was raised. I was fed and clothed and taught to work. That's more than most people get.

So what do I think? I think the question is irrelevant. My personality is a tool I built. It works. Whatever raw materials were there are gone now, reshaped. I don't look back.

But she's still looking at me. I need to say something. Something that satisfies her without giving her anything real. Maybe I deflect with a dry observation. "I learned to be self-reliant early, I suppose." That's true enough. And it's sterile. She can't do much with that.

Or I could ask her why she's asking. Turn it back. That's a power move. She'll know I'm deflecting, but she'll also know I'm not playing her game.

Discipline. That's what she said. Discipline. We didn't have "discipline." We had expectations. You did what you were supposed to do. If you didn't, you felt it in the air. That's not threatening. And "jokingly." Jesus. My old man never joked. Not once. That wasn't his job. My mother maybe, sometimes, but it was always about something practical. "If you don't finish that book, you'll have to read it twice." That's not a threat, that's a fact. She was a teacher. Teachers state facts.

I already pushed myself harder than they ever could. The only person who threatens me is me. And I don't do it jokingly.

Let her chew on that. Or not. I don't care. Just move this along. I have a model to run after this.

Let me think. Early childhood. I'm in Queens, she's teaching band at the school down the street, coming home with that smell of brass and reeds and chalk. She'd sit me at the kitchen table with homework while she graded papers, humming. Always humming something. So one word is... musical. No, that's too easy. That's like saying water is wet. But it's true. She was music. Even when she was tired, even when she was yelling at me to pick up my shoes, there was a rhythm to it.

What else? She was... strict. Not mean strict, but like, "you will do your homework before you watch TV" strict. "You will say please and thank you" strict. I remember once I talked back to her, and she gave me that look—the one that said, "I am not your friend right now, I am your mother." And I shut up. But strict doesn't capture the softness underneath. She'd be strict about the piano practice, but then she'd sit next to me and play the duet part, and we'd laugh when I messed up.

Warm. That's another one. She was warm. Her hugs smelled like lavender soap. She'd let me crawl into her bed after a nightmare. She'd make jollof rice on Sundays and the whole apartment would smell like home. But warm feels... generic. Everyone's mom is warm, right? No, not everyone. I know that. I'm lucky.

Patient. God, she was patient. I was a loud kid. I was always doing bits, making jokes, pestering her while she was trying to read the newspaper. She'd put the paper down and say, "Okay, show me." She never told me to be quiet. She watched my little skits like I was the funniest person alive. That's why I'm here. She made the room safe for me to be ridiculous.

And... proud. She was proud of me. Not in a pushy way, but in a quiet way. She'd brag to her choir friends about my report card, but she'd never tell me to my face. I'd overhear her on the phone: "He's doing great, he's so smart, he's so funny." And I'd pretend I didn't hear. That pride—it's like a foundation. I know she's proud of me now, even when I bomb. She says, "You'll get it next time, Jamie."

Five. That's five. Musical, strict, warm, patient, proud. But I'm missing something. The thing that's harder to name. The thing about her going to Lagos alone after Grandma died. That wasn't in the 5-12 window, but it colors everything. She came back smaller, like I said. But when I was a kid, she was big. She filled every room. She was my whole world.

Okay, I think I have them. Let me go through them again before I say them out loud. Musical. Strict. Warm. Patient. Proud. That feels right. That feels like her.

That's my baseline for love: someone making something, someone filling a room with sound. She taught me that you can be full of joy even when you're tired, even when money's tight. That's in my bones, that's the thing I carry on stage with me. The need to fill the silence, to make people feel something.

And then the other side. The week before graduation. She came back from Lagos smaller, like someone had let the air out of her. I didn't know what to do with that. I still don't, really. I learned that grief is quiet, that it can live in a person and you just have to let it be there. That's probably why I'm so bad at staying still, why I need to be moving, making jokes, keeping things light.

But also the father thing. The name we don't say. That taught me something too—about stories you choose not to tell, about the shape of an absence. I don't know if I'm more open or more closed because of it. I talk about everything on stage except that. Maybe that's the answer, right there.

So yeah. My mother made me brave and soft and loud. The silence around my father made me... curious, I guess. Always looking for the joke nobody's telling. Always wondering what's underneath.

She made me feel like being funny was a talent worth having, not just a thing that got me in trouble in class. That's probably the biggest thing—she never made me feel like I had to dim myself to be loved. I could be loud, I could be ridiculous, I could bomb a set and come home and she'd say "Did you learn something? That's the tuition."

And then there's the other side. The name we don't say. My father is an absence that I've learned to make jokes about because that's easier than admitting it still does something to me. I think growing up without him taught me that I have to be enough on my own. Which is good and bad. It makes me self-sufficient. It also makes me scared to ask for help sometimes, or to let someone in too close without waiting for them to leave first. That's probably why I almost said yes to Theo, and why I didn't.

So I guess... my mother gave me the permission to take up space, and the lack of my father gave me the drive to fill it myself. They're both in every set I write, even when I'm not talking about them. Which is weird to say out loud to someone I just met. But you asked.

prompt_text: When you were upset as a child, what would you do?

Oh. That's a question. That's a question I wasn't expecting right now. It lands somewhere soft and unexpected in my chest.

When I was upset as a child. I'm trying to grab that memory, to see what surfaces first. I'd sit on the floor and make up silly voices to myself, talk to the instruments, try to make myself laugh. It was like I needed to find a way to change the air in the room.

My mom would find me there sometimes. She never asked what was wrong. She'd just sit down and start playing something, anything, and I'd listen until the knot in my stomach loosened. That was her way. She didn't pry, she just made space.

I think I learned that. That you don't have to fix it immediately, you just have to let it breathe. Sometimes I'd just lie on the floor and stare at the ceiling and wait for the feeling to pass. I didn't have a lot of words for it back then, but I knew how to wait.

Now when I'm upset, I still do something similar. I go quiet. I find a corner. I make jokes in my head until one of them sticks and I can breathe again. I didn't realize until she asked that the shape of it hasn't changed that much.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your childhood relationship with your father, again starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but again say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I feel like a child being asked to draw a picture of something I've never seen. Five adjectives for a man who was essentially a rumor in my house. We're supposed to talk about the age 5 to 12 range, which is almost the entire span of my childhood, and the word that keeps floating up is absent, but that feels like a cheat — it's a description of a space, not a person.

My mother used to say "your father" the way other people say "the weather" — as something that just happens to you, beyond anyone's control. I don't even know if I'm angry or just tired of explaining the shape of an absence to people who have dads who show up for parent-teacher conferences.

Five adjectives. I could say mysterious, but he wasn't mysterious — he just wasn't there. I could say imagined, because I built a version of him out of the few stories my grandmother let slip before she stopped talking about him entirely. I could say finished, because by the time I was old enough to ask questions, the conversation had already been closed.

I wonder what it says about me that my first instinct is to make a joke about it. But I know she's not going to laugh at this one. She's writing things down. She's going to look at me with that careful expression and I'm going to feel like a specimen.

Okay. Absence. Distance. Silence. Mythology. And maybe — just maybe — forgiveness, though I'm not sure I believe that one yet.

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.
monologue_text:
Rejected. That's a loaded word. I don't think I—well, maybe when I was what, ten? I wanted to play on the town baseball team but Dad said I was too small, better to focus on school. I remember being angry, not rejected. There's a difference. That's just practical. He was right, I did focus on school. State champion at thirteen. That wasn't about being rejected, that was about winning. When I quit chess, people thought I was upset about losing? No, I just didn't like the unpredictability. The format. Too many variables. Not efficient.

She's asking like it's a given that everyone has this feeling. "Looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection." That's her framing, not mine. I don't need to reinterpret anything. I remember exactly what happened. I don't carry baggage like that. Or maybe she thinks I do? That's annoying. I don't need to be analyzed like some case study.

I think of that time in college when my parents didn't come to my graduation. I was—actually, I was relieved. Saved me the trouble of seating arrangements, dinner reservations. They'd have been awkward in that crowd anyway. That's not rejection, that's just knowing your audience. I made my own way. I don't need external validation.

But why is she asking this now? What's the angle? This feels like a therapy prompt, a relationship check. I don't do those. I'm not broken. I have a marathon to train for, deals to structure. The Caro biography is sitting on my nightstand—I'd rather think about that than dig through childhood incidents that don't matter.

I feel a slight irritation. She's expecting me to produce some emotional nugget. I won't. There's nothing there. Or maybe there is something? No, I'm not going there. That's a rabbit hole. I'll just say "no" or give a one-word answer. Keep it moving. I have no interest in this.
response_text: No. I don't remember feeling rejected. I remember being told no, and I moved on. That's not the same thing.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your childhood relationship with your father, again starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but again say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think again for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Oh. That's... a specific ask. Five adjectives about a childhood relationship with a man who wasn't there. That's like asking me to describe the flavor of a glass of water that's been sitting out for three days. There's nothing in it. There never was.

I have to find words for an absence. That's the trick here, isn't it? She wants me to excavate something that requires dirt to dig in, and I'm standing on concrete. Quiet. Most people say that when they mean peaceful. I mean quiet like a room with no echoes. Invisible is probably the honest one — not because he was hiding but because I stopped looking.

She's got her pen ready. I can feel the pause stretching, and the performer in me wants to fill it with something sharp, a punchline about how the first adjective is "who?" to buy time. But she's not my audience. She's trying to see me, and that deserves the truth, even if the truth is just an empty inventory of a ghost who never haunted me properly.

Fragile? No. That's about me, not him. Let me just... name what was there. The shape where a person should have been. That's what she's asking for.
response_text:
Alright. You want five words for a relationship that didn't really happen. That's the thing — I've got five words for a shape where a person was supposed to be. 

"Absent" is the obvious one. That's the whole deal, he wasn't there. Not in a dramatic way, not in a 'he left in a storm' way, just... a no-show. 

"Invisible" — because I stopped looking for him pretty early on, and eventually he just wasn't part of the picture I painted of my life. 

"Quiet" — not the peaceful kind of quiet. The kind where you notice the silence because someone should be making noise and they're not. 

"Unreal" — he felt like a concept more than a person. Like Santa Claus, but with less goodwill and no presents. 

And "fine" — because that's what I told myself. I was fine without him. And I was. Mostly. That's the word I used so my mom wouldn't worry.

monologue_text: Right, another one of these. She's trying to go deep, find the wound. I'm supposed to produce a memory I never organized that way. Rejection. That's a psychologist's word, not mine. I remember the chess state championship, the trophy on the mantelpiece, quitting because losing was inefficient, not because I felt rejected. I remember the family at the wedding. They had a choice. They made it. That's not rejection, that's resource allocation. You can't feel rejected by a spreadsheet. And the father's funeral—I was efficient. That's what you do. You don't sit around feeling rejected by a corpse. So no. I don't have that in my catalog. She wants me to search for something I never filed. I could say "no, I don't remember that" and move on, but then she'll push. She's the type to push. Maybe I'll give her a data point she can't use: I remember being annoyed when I lost at chess. That's not rejection. That's competition. Let her make of that what she wills. But the way she phrased it—"you may realize it wasn't really rejection"—as if she's already editing my memory for me. That's condescending. I'm not a patient. I'm a person who plans. And this line of questioning doesn't fit the plan. I'll tell her I'd rather talk about something else. Or I'll deflect. Or I'll just say no and wait. Let her do the work. I'm not playing the broken-record game.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
response_text: Hmm. Okay. Give me a second — this is gonna be a little specific. All right: loud. Warm. Tired. Proud. Unshakeable.

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.
monologue_text:
Rejected. That's an imprecise word. A seven-year-old feels rejected when he's not picked for a team. A twelve-year-old feels rejected when he doesn't make the chess club. I was state champion at thirteen — I was the one doing the picking. No. That's not the question. The question is about feeling it. Feeling rejected. I remember the day I lost the state qualifier my sophomore year. I didn't feel rejected by the game. I felt stupid. And then I solved the problem of feeling stupid by never losing again. That's not rejection. That's a data point.

But she's asking about childhood. Young child. Before data points? Before I knew how to optimize around outcomes? I remember my mother saying my father had to work late and couldn't come to the science fair. That wasn't rejection. That was a schedule conflict. She's asking about the feeling, not the fact. The feeling is inefficient to analyze. It has no clear variable to adjust.

Maybe the question is about my family not coming to my graduation. I didn't attend my own ceremony — I was working. It's symmetrical. It's a feature, not a bug. I don't feel rejected by people who are slower than me. I feel — what? Disappointed in their capacity? That's not the same.

She's probably fishing for something. That line "you may realize it wasn't really rejection" — she's already telling me the answer she wants. She wants me to say yes, I felt rejected, and then she wants to reframe it. I don't have time for that. I'm twenty moves ahead of this conversation.

No. I don't feel rejected. I feel irritated that this is the topic when there are actual problems to solve. But I won't say that. I'll give her a clean, efficient, non-committal answer. "I don't recall feeling rejected." That's technically true. The recall isn't there because I don't catalogue feelings. I catalogue outcomes.

But she'll push. She'll ask about the wedding. The wedding I wasn't told about. That could be framed as rejection. I remember the call — my sister said "we didn't want to bother you with it." I said "good." That was true. It would have been a bother. So if I felt anything, it was relief that the system was working efficiently. But maybe that's after the fact. Maybe the initial microsecond before the cognitive override had something else. I'm not going to find it by digging. There's nothing there.

Just answer. Simple. Dismiss. Move on. This is a probe I don't need to engage.
response_text: I don't recall feeling rejected. I remember events, not the emotional color around them.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five adjectives. About my mother. Early childhood. Right.

She was... there. That's one. Present. Efficient. She ran the household like a shift schedule. Breakfast at 6:45, laundry Tuesday, church Sunday. No surprises. Predictable. That's probably two already.

Three: distant. Not cold, exactly. Just... busy. She had thirty fourth-graders all day and then three of us at night. By the time I was eight I knew not to bring her problems unless there was blood. Problems were for solving, not sharing. I learned that early.

Four: proud. She was proud of me. The chess trophy stayed on the mantel for years. She told the neighbors. But she never came to a match. Too many parent-teacher conferences to grade. The wedding they skipped — that was her side's cousin, my mother's sister's daughter. Family obligation. I understand the logic. I still remember staring at the empty row during the ceremony that wasn't mine. But that was later. Age twelve, she was proud from a distance.

Five: what's the fifth? Competent. My mother was competent. She handled my father's drinking without drama, managed the money, kept the house clean, made sure we had shoes that fit. She didn't complain. I respected that. I still respect that. Competence is the highest compliment I can give anyone.

So there. Present, predictable, distant, proud, competent. That's the list. Now you want why. Fine. Each one has a reason. Each one is true. But this isn't a therapy session. This is a task. Let's get it over with.
response_text: I don't need a minute. Present, predictable, distant, proud, competent. That's five.

prompt_text: Did you experience the loss of a parent or other close loved one while you were a young child — for example, a sibling, or a close family member?
monologue_text:
Oh. That's a real question. That's the kind of question that tells me you were actually listening when I mentioned my grandmother, which is... yeah, that's nice. That's more than most people do on a first date that's technically a walk that became coffee that became dinner.

But the phrasing is interesting — "while you were a young child." I was seventeen. That's not young child territory. Seventeen is old enough to know exactly what's happening and still feel completely helpless about it. My grandmother was in Lagos, I never even got to say goodbye properly, my mother went alone and came back like someone had turned down the volume on her.

I don't think I've ever told that story on stage. Not the real version. I've done bits about Nigerian funerals being three days long and somehow involving both deep grief and an argument about catering, but that's the joke version. The real version is my mother sitting at the kitchen table for three weeks not playing any music.

But that's not a young child loss. That's a teenager loss. And also — my father left when I was six. Does that count? He's not dead, just disappeared. Theo-style, I guess, except with less notice and no text messages. Loss is loss is loss, but the question is specifically about death, so. No. No parent, no sibling, no close family member died when I was a young child. Just some people who were supposed to stay and didn't.

I'm not sad about it right now. I'm just... noticing that I said the word Lagos out loud tonight and didn't make it into a joke. That's new.
response_text: Hmm. No. Not a death, anyway. My grandmother died when I was seventeen — that's not really young child territory, that's old enough to know exactly what's happening and still feel useless about it. My father left when I was six, but he's not dead, just... gone. So no, nobody died on me when I was little. Just some people who were supposed to stay and didn't. Different kind of loss, I guess.

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.
monologue_text: Huh. That's... specific. My brain's already scanning through childhood memories, looking for something that fits, but mostly I'm just aware of how carefully they're wording this.
response_text: Oh, yeah. I mean, I think every kid who grew up as one of the only Black kids in a mostly white school has a whole little highlight reel of those moments. There was this birthday party in third grade — whole class got invited except me. My mom said it was probably just a space issue. I knew it wasn't a space issue. But you know what? I showed up to school the next day and told everyone I'd had a better weekend anyway, and I think that was the first time I realized I could just… make the story work for me.

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.
monologue_text: Rejected? No. I don't remember feeling rejected; I remember noticing that other people were slow and that my family had other things to do. This feels like a therapy question, and I don't have a therapy answer for it.
response_text: No. That's not a category I've ever used on myself. I grew up understanding that the world didn't revolve around me — that's not rejection, that's just how things are.

prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?
monologue_text:
Huh. That's a weird way to put it. "Threatening." Like I'm supposed to have a story here. I don't. My mom never threatened me. She'd just look at me over her glasses and I'd know. That look was enough. No threats needed.

But they said "parents" — plural. It's always plural with people who don't know. They don't think to ask about my dad, they just assume two. I could correct them, but then it becomes a thing. A heavy thing. I don't want that right now.

And "jokingly"? That's a strange add-on. Like they're trying to give me an out if the answer is uncomfortable. Or maybe they're trying to make it not sound like an accusation. But it still feels like a probe. Like they're looking for a specific kind of damage. I don't have that damage. I have the damage of being loved too hard by one person and abandoned by another before I was born. That's different.

I could make a joke. "My mom's threats were all passive-aggressive: 'I'm not angry, I'm just disappointed.' The horror." But that's too glib. They're asking something real. I should give them something real. But what? That my mom never threatened because she didn't need to? That sounds like I'm bragging. Or like I'm deflecting.

They're watching me. I can feel it. They want to know if I have scars. I have scars, just not from threats. From silence. From a father-shaped hole. From watching my mom come back from Lagos smaller. But that's not what they're asking.

I'm surprised they went here. We were having a light night. Now I'm in my head about my childhood. I want to pull back to something funny, something that lets us both breathe. But I also don't want to shut them down. They're trying to know me. That's good. That's what I want.

Okay. I'll answer honestly. No joke. Just the truth. "My mom didn't threaten. She had this look. You just knew." That's true. That's enough.

But I'm still stuck on "jokingly." That word. Why did they add that? Maybe they're thinking of their own parents. Maybe they're trying to normalize the question. Or maybe they think I'm the kind of person whose parents joked about threats. Which is weird. I'm not sure what kind of person that is.

I'm overthinking. I do that when I'm surprised. Let me just answer. Let me not make it a bit. Let me be here.
response_text: My mom didn't threaten. She'd just take her glasses off and give me this look over the top of them. That was it. You just knew.

prompt_text: Did you experience the loss of a parent or other close loved one while you were a young child — for example, a sibling, or a close family member?
monologue_text:
Oh. That's... specific. That's a very specific question. Not "have you ever lost anyone" — "while you were a young child." Like there's a checkbox they're mentally filling. A form. A diagnostic criteria list. I feel the edges of my mouth twitch — is this a joke setup? No, the tone is wrong. No punchline coming.

My grandmother. But I was seventeen. That's not a young child. Is seventeen still a child? That week my mother came back smaller — I remember watching her unpack, the way she folded her mother's headscarf and put it on her own dresser, and I didn't know what to say. I made her tea. I didn't make any jokes. I didn't know how.

They're watching me. Waiting. I feel the pause stretching, and I hate silence onstage but this isn't a stage.

Do I say "no" because technically no parent or sibling died when I was young, and I know they're probably looking for a specific kind of answer, the kind that would explain something. Or do I say "my grandmother" and watch them decide if that counts. I don't want someone I don't know to decide if my grief counts.

Why does this feel like a test I didn't study for.

I want to deflect. I want to ask why they're asking. I want to make a joke about how my dad was never around but he's not dead, just absent, and is that a loss? But that's too much. That's sharp. That's the thing I don't say to strangers, the thing I barely say to myself at 3 AM.

Okay. Breathe. They asked a question. I can answer the question. I can answer the question I want to answer. I don't have to give them the whole file.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your childhood relationship with your father, again starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but again say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think again for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five adjectives. Right. Five words for my father. I can do this faster than a minute, I already know the list. Distant. That's the first one. He was home but he wasn't *there* — not in the way that matters for a kid. Reliable. He showed up for work, he showed up for dinner, he showed up for the paycheck. That's reliability. That's what I inherited. Silent. We didn't talk. Not about feelings, not about school, not about anything that wasn't the Steelers or the car needing an oil change. I don't think I heard his voice raise more than five times in my life. He didn't need to raise it. The quiet was louder. Hard. His hands. The calluses. The way he'd come in from the mill and just... sit. Not tired. Hardened. Like the steel itself. The man had no soft edges. I admired that. I admire that. It's efficient.

And then... what's the fifth one. What's the honest fifth one. Proud? No, that's not right. He was proud of me, I know he was. He told my mother. He never told me. So maybe... distant already covers that. Okay. Absent. Not physically. He was always there. But the part of him that could have been a father to a son who needed something other than a roof and three meals — that part was absent. He didn't know how. I don't think he knew how. His father was the same way. It's a pattern. It's a goddamn transmission line.

Five words. Distant. Reliable. Silent. Hard. Absent.

That's the list. I could give it now. I don't need to think for a minute. I've been thinking about this for thirty years.
response_text: Distant. Reliable. Silent. Hard. Absent.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your childhood relationship with your father, again starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but again say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think again for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five adjectives for my father. Right. Because that's what this is going to solve. I can already feel her pen hovering, waiting to write down something like "strong" or "distant" or whatever box she wants to check.

Let me think. If I'm honest — and I'm never sure why honesty is the goal here — the first word that surfaces is "tired." He was always tired. First shift, up at four, home by two, asleep on the couch by seven. That's not an adjective though. That's a sentence.

Second: "reliable." He showed up. He paid the bills. He didn't drink, didn't hit us, didn't leave. That's the bar, apparently. Low bar.

Third: "silent." We didn't talk. Not about feelings, not about chess, not about my scholarships. The only time I remember him looking proud was when I beat my uncle at poker. That's not father-son stuff, that's competition. He understood competition.

Fourth: "fast." He died fast. Stroke at fifty-eight, three days in the hospital, then nothing. I made the arrangements before my mother could fall apart. That's what he taught me: handle it, don't feel it, move on.

Fifth... I don't know. Maybe "unavoidable." Even now, when I'm running a model or closing a deal, I feel him watching. Not warmly. Just... watching. Like he's waiting for me to slip.

She's going to ask why I chose each one. I'll give her the clean version. The one that doesn't make me sound like a case study. "Tired" because he worked hard. "Reliable" because he was there. "Silent" because he was private. "Fast" because he died young. "Unavoidable" because... I'll say because he shaped my work ethic. That's true enough.

Let's get this over with.
response_text: Tired. Reliable. Silent. Fast. Unavoidable. That's five. Write them down.

response_text: Oh, man. I think the biggest thing is that my mom never once told me to have a backup plan. She was a public school music teacher who somehow kept a church choir, a band program, and a whole kid

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.
monologue_text: Feeling rejected? No, I remember being right about things other people couldn't see coming—that's not rejection, that's being faster.

monologue_text: No. That's not what this is. I don't do post-mortems on a perfectly functional childhood.

prompt_text: Did you experience the loss of a parent or other close loved one while you were a young child — for example, a sibling, or a close family member?
monologue_text: They're fishing. This is a clinical probe dressed up as concern. "Young child" — like I'm supposed to have some foundational wound that explains everything. My father died when I was twenty-six. That's not a young child. I was already at Penn by then, already a year into the job. I handled it. Three days off, funeral arrangements, back at the desk. Efficient. Clean. They want a trauma narrative, some story about loss that shaped me. I don't have that. I didn't lose anyone as a kid. No siblings, no grandparents, no close family. So what do they want me to say? That I'm broken because I don't fit their template? The question feels like a test — reveal something vulnerable or seem closed off. Either way, they're sorting me into a category. I feel the contempt rising, that familiar curl in my chest. But I also notice a small flicker of something else — the memory of the funeral home, the efficiency of it, the way I made the calls and checked the boxes. And then I shut that down. Not relevant. They asked about childhood. The answer is no. Simple. But they'll read into that too, won't they? A "no" without elaboration will be a data point. Fine. Let them have it.
response_text: Not as a child. My father passed when I was twenty-six — well past the developmental window you're probing.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text:
Childhood. I mean, what is this, a therapy intake? I don't do sentimentality. The question itself feels like a probe—some kind of emotional evaluation. I can feel the contempt rising, a thin wire of irritation. Why does she want to know this? What's the angle? Is this about assessing my "fit" or my "emotional intelligence"? Please.

Fine. I learned that the only thing that reliably works is performance. You show up, you do the work, you don't make excuses, and you don't expect anyone to hand you anything. My father got up at four in the morning for thirty years and never complained once. That's the lesson: you endure, you execute, and you don't waste time on self-pity. I'm not going to say any of that to her, of course. She'd probably try to frame it as a "coping mechanism" or some other therapeutic vocabulary. I just need to give her a clean, short answer that satisfies the form of the question without actually giving anything away.

Something about personal responsibility, maybe. The utility of expectations. That sounds neutral enough. I'll say I learned that the world doesn't owe you anything and that the only reliable source of security is your own output. She can make of that what she wants. It's true, in its way. Just not the part where I also learned that no one is going to show up for your chess tournament or your graduation. That part stays inside.
response_text: I learned that the world doesn't owe you anything, and the only reliable source of security is your own output.

prompt_text: When you were upset as a child, what would you do?
monologue_text:
Oh, that's specific. That's a good question. First thing that comes to mind: I climbed trees. There was this one tree in the park by my apartment, a big sycamore with a branch that curved like an armchair. I'd sit up there and watch people walk their dogs, just, like, disappear. I never told anyone that, not even my mom.

Actually, wait. What did I do? What do I even remember being upset about? Or was I one of those kids who didn't get upset much? No, that's not true. I was mad sometimes. Stupid things. A kid at school said my mom's choir was "weird old people singing noises" and I punched him in the arm and then I felt terrible and I ran to the library. I think I just read a lot. Books where the main character was alone and exploring without permission.

And sometimes when I was really small, I'd find my mom's metronome and set it going and just watch the arm swing back and forth, tick tick tick. That's not something I've ever thought about. That's probably not what she means. She's asking like, what did you *do*? What was your strategy? I don't know if I had a strategy. I just waited until it passed.

But now I want to tell her something funny. Something that makes it sound like I was always a little comedian, even then. Like, "I'd write angry haikus about the unfairness of snack time." That's not true, but it's a good bit. And I can already feel myself wanting to *perform* the answer instead of, you know, actually answering.

Okay, but she didn't ask for a joke. She asked seriously. And I think she actually wants to know. That's nice. It's a little weird, maybe — we're not exactly at the "what was your childhood upset ritual" stage of intimacy yet? Or maybe we are? She asked it gently. I like that. I want to be honest.

So, what did I do? I escaped. Physically or mentally. Climbed a tree, read a book, listened to the metronome. I didn't go to anyone. I handled it alone. And that's kind of still true, I guess. When I'm losing my mind about something — Theo leaving, bombing on stage, money — I still go to my corner and wait it out. I don't know if that's the answer she's looking for. But it's the one that's there.

I think I'll tell her the tree part. Maybe not the metronome. The metronome feels too young, too quiet. Too true. Save that for later. If there's a later.
response_text: Oh, man. I climbed trees. There was this sycamore in the park near my apartment with a branch that fit me like a throne. I'd just sit up there and watch people. Completely disappeared. I never told anyone that — feels weird saying it now, actually.

My father died at fifty-eight, on a Tuesday, during the second shift. I made the calls. I wrote the obituary. I was back at my desk seventy-two hours later because that's what you do. The question assumes a vulnerability I don't carry. Or maybe it's trying to manufacture one. Either way it's a waste of time — theirs and mine. prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text:
Huh. That's a real question. Not the kind you get at a bar at 2 AM. She's asking about childhood like it's a source, not just a story I tell on stage. 

I could make a joke. I could say I learned that if you make enough noise, nobody notices you're hungry. That's true, actually. But it's not the whole truth. 

The thing that comes up first, the thing I can feel sitting in my chest, is my mother. The way she'd come home from teaching thirty kids how to hold a clarinet and still sit at the kitchen table and ask me about my day like I was the only person in the world. She was tired. Bone-tired. And she still showed up. 

I think I learned that love is attention. Not the big gestures, not the applause. Just showing up when you're exhausted and asking someone how they are. 

I learned that you can be broke and disappointed and still choose to be kind. My mother never once made me feel like a burden. That's a skill. That's something she *decided* to do, every day. 

And I learned that laughter is a kind of honesty. My grandfather—my mother's father, the one I barely knew—he could make a room full of strangers feel like family. I think I'm chasing that. Not the laugh itself, but the thing that happens in the room when people remember they're alive together. 

I want to say all of that. But it's a lot for a first real conversation. She asked, though. She's not looking for a bit.
response_text: That's a real question. I think the main thing I learned — and I'm still learning it, honestly — is that love is attention. My mom would come home after teaching band all day, absolutely wiped, and she'd still sit down at the kitchen table and look at me like I was the only thing in the world worth seeing. She was broke, she was tired, and she never made me feel like a burden. That's a choice. That's a skill. I think I walked away knowing that showing up for someone when you have nothing left to give is the whole thing.

prompt_text: Did you experience the loss of a parent or other close loved one while you were a young child — for example, a sibling, or a close family member?
monologue_text:
Oh. That's a specific question. Not what I expected but okay.

Grandma. My mother's mother, in Lagos. But I wasn't a young child - I was seventeen, about to graduate high school. Does that count? The question says "young child" so probably not. But it's the closest thing I've got to that kind of loss.

My father isn't dead, just absent. Which is its own thing but not the same. People always want to know if I'm fucked up about it and honestly... I don't know? I made jokes about it. I still make jokes about it. That's how I process.

But Grandma dying - that was the first time I saw my mother break. She flew to Lagos alone and came back smaller, like someone had taken a shaver to the edges of her. I didn't know what to do with that. I was supposed to be graduating and she was supposed to be proud and instead we were both just... quieter.

I'm not sure if this is what they're asking for. The question feels clinical, like a form. But I can answer honestly - no parent loss in early childhood. Grandmother loss in late adolescence. And I'm still figuring out what that meant.
response_text: Nah, not as a young child. My grandmother died when I was seventeen, right before my high school graduation. And my father's alive, just not around. So I missed the early childhood loss quota, but I got the existential confusion discount.

monologue_text:
Okay. Five adjectives. From five to twelve. That's... that's a specific window. I'm back in Queens, the small apartment near the elevated train line. The rumble shaking the pictures on the wall. Mom's classroom voice at home, that teacher tone that could make you feel two inches tall or ten feet tall depending on the day.

Fierce. That's the first one. She was fierce in a way that didn't need volume. A look could stop me mid-sentence. But also fierce in how she defended me from a schoolyard bully in third grade—she showed up the next day and had a word with the principal. She didn't yell. She just... made things right.

Tired. God, she was tired. Teaching band all day, choir on Sundays, grading papers at the kitchen table with her reading glasses slipping down her nose. I'd wake up for water and see her there, still in her work clothes, a half-cold cup of tea beside her. I learned early not to ask for things she couldn't give.

Musical. Our house was never quiet. She'd hum while cooking. She'd put on Fela or Nina Simone and make me listen to the textures. She taught me the difference between a verse and a chorus before I could tie my shoes. That's her gift to me, really—the rhythm of everything.

Private. She didn't talk about the hard stuff. Not my father, not Nigeria, not her mother's death until years later. I learned to read her silences. To know when a closed door meant she needed to be alone. I think that's where my own tendency to joke instead of confess comes from. We were close but there was always a room in her heart I wasn't allowed into.

Present. Even when she was exhausted, she showed up. School plays, parent-teacher nights, the one open mic I did at fourteen that I didn't tell anyone about—she somehow found out and sat in the back row. She didn't say much after. Just smiled and said, "You made them laugh, J. That's a skill."

I think I'll start with *fierce*. That feels like the root of everything else.
response_text: Alright. First one: fierce. She didn't need to yell—she had this look that could pin you to the wall. But also fierce in the way she showed up for me, like when I got bullied in third grade and she went to the principal and just... handled it. No drama, just results.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text: Huh. Where's this coming from? We're at dinner. I wasn't prepared for a therapy session. She's looking at me like she expects some kind of revelation. The thing is, I've thought about this before — not in a navel-gazing way, but in the way you check the odometer on a used car. You want to know what mileage the chassis has been through. My father worked a furnace line for thirty-eight years. My mother graded spelling tests at the kitchen table. They did their jobs. I did mine. That's the whole architecture right there. I don't think it's more complicated than that — you watch someone punch a clock for decades and you either decide that's the life for you or you don't. I decided I wanted a different kind of clock. The chess thing, maybe. That was me building a system where losing had clear causes and winning had clear causes. My father never understood why I quit. He said winners don't quit. But he didn't understand that I quit because the tournament structure was inefficient — I'd learned everything the local circuit could teach me. That's the kind of calculation I make. She wants me to say they made me cold, or driven, or afraid of failure. But they didn't make anything. They provided the raw materials. I built the structure.
response_text: They gave me a work ethic and a sense that the world doesn't care about your feelings. Everything else is my own construction. I quit competitive chess at thirteen because I'd squeezed out everything worth learning from it — that's the kind of decision they taught me to make, even if they didn't agree with it. So the short answer is I watched them do their jobs and I built a better one for myself. That's about it.

Oh, that's — that's a big one. They're not just asking what happened to me, they're asking what I took from it. What I *gained*. That word. Gained. Like it's a gift. I don't know if I'd call it a gift, but I know what they're after.

Okay. Think. My mother. The way she'd come home from teaching thirty kids how to play "Hot Cross Buns" on the recorder and still find the energy to make me laugh. The way she'd say "we don't have money for that" without making it sound like a tragedy, just a fact, and then she'd find a way anyway. The way she never once made me feel like I was the reason she was tired.

I learned that you can be exhausted and still be kind. That you can be broke and still be generous. That the thing you give away — time, attention, a joke, a song — that's the thing that multiplies.

I learned that my mother's laughter sounded like a door opening.

And I learned that my father's absence was not about me. That took longer. But I learned it.

I think what I *gained* — God, this is making me feel like I'm about to get emotional on a first date or whatever this is — I gained the conviction that you can build a life out of what you have, not what you're missing. That the empty space doesn't have to be a hole. It can be a room you choose not to furnish.

And I gained the ability to make people laugh when they're tired. That's the direct transfer. That's my inheritance.

I don't know if that's what they're looking for. But it's true. And it's mine.
response_text: I think I learned that you can be exhausted and still be kind. My mom came home from teaching thirty kids the recorder and still found a way to make me laugh. That's the thing I took—that you build a life out of

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.
monologue_text:
Oh, huh. That's... a specific question. Not what I expected from a first real conversation, but okay, I can roll with it.

I'm already scanning. My dad. Obviously my dad. The name we don't say. But that's so obvious it feels like a trick answer, like the first thing anyone would reach for. Did I *feel* rejected? I don't know if I felt it or if I just learned the shape of his absence and filled it in later with the word "rejected." I was a baby. Babies don't have concepts, they just have... empty space where a person should be.

But then there's the other stuff. The small stuff that's harder to name. Being the only Black kid in a mostly white class until middle school, and the way I learned to be funny before I learned to be anything else, because funny was safe. Funny made people like me. Was that rejection? Or was that just... strategy? Survival.

And my mother, who loved me so fiercely it sometimes felt like a room with no exits. She'd look at me with this intensity, like I was the only good thing she'd ever made, and I'd think — what if I fail her? What if the thing I am isn't enough to justify all that love? That's not rejection from her, that's me rejecting myself preemptively, isn't it?

I remember being eight and my best friend Marcus told me I couldn't play at his house anymore because his mom said I was "too much." Too loud, too much energy, too much. I went home and I didn't tell my mom. I just sat in my room and tried to be smaller. That was rejection. That was real.

But also — I'm a comedian. I turn pain into punchlines. So part of me is already trying to figure out how to say this in a way that makes them laugh, or at least makes it easier for them to hear. But they're not asking for a joke. They're asking for the truth. And I can tell they're paying attention, they're not just filling space.

God, I feel like I'm about to say something I haven't said out loud before. That's terrifying. But also... interesting. That's why I'm here, right? To be seen?
response_text: Yeah, I do. I think the one that lives in my bones is when I was eight and my best friend's mom said I was "too much" and I couldn't come over anymore. I went home and sat in my room and tried to be smaller. That was real. That wasn't me reading into anything.

prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?
monologue_text:
Huh. Out of nowhere. I mean we were just talking about… what was it? The guy on the train who was selling peppermints? And now this. Threatening. That word lands weird. Feels like a loaded question — like she's already got a theory about me and she's checking boxes. Or maybe she's just curious. People ask about childhood when they want to know if you're broken or not. I'm not broken. My mother never threatened me — she'd give me a look, that long silent stare, which was worse than any yelling. But that's not threatening, that's… I don't know, it's her being a single mom who had to command a room full of seventh-graders with trumpets. She didn't need to threaten. She had presence.

But the word "threatening" makes me think of something else — of men. Of fathers. Of the name we don't say. She's not asking about him, she's asking about my mom. My mom who flew alone to Lagos and came back smaller. She never threatened me. She held me too tight after that trip, like she was checking that I was still solid. That's not threat, that's… grief. I don't know if I should say that. It's too much for a first date? Is this a date? We're in my apartment, she's sitting on my couch, my copy of *Sister Outsider* is on the coffee table face-down. She probably saw it. That might have prompted this — women who read Audre Lorde ask questions like this. They want to know if you've done the work. I haven't done the work, I just started the book. But I'm thinking now about that time my mom said "If you don't clean your room, I will donate everything you own to the church rummage sale." That's a threat. But it was funny. She was joking. Mostly joking. I did clean my room.

Why am I trying to find a threatening memory? There isn't one. That's the answer. But now I feel like I have to say something interesting, like "oh yeah my mom was a real taskmaster" so I don't seem boring or too well-adjusted. Because well-adjusted people are boring, right? No, that's a comedian's reflex. I can just say no. She'll either believe me or she won't. I'm not going to perform trauma for approval. But I am performing something — I'm sitting here, eyebrows raised, trying to look thoughtful when really I'm just surprised she asked that. I wonder what she's looking for. If I say no too quickly, does that sound defensive? If I say yes and invent something, that's lying. I'll just say no, my mom was strict but never threatening.
response_text: Nah, my mom wasn't threatening. She had this stare, though — the one she used on seventh-graders with trumpets. She'd just look at me and I'd fold immediately. That's not a threat, that's a superpower.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five adjectives. Five words for me and my mom when I was a little kid. That's... okay, that's a specific ask. I like that they're giving me time, not rushing me. They're writing them down — that feels careful, like these words matter.

I'm sitting here and my mind is going to her hands. She had these hands that could play piano and also chop vegetables so fast. She was always doing something. Teaching band, running choir, grading papers at the kitchen table with a glass of red wine. I'd be on the floor with my LEGOs or drawing and she'd hum while she worked. That humming. That's a sound I forgot until just now.

Five adjectives. 

First: *constant.* She was just there. Not hovering, but present. Like gravity. I knew where she was in the house. I could feel her.

Second: *funny.* She had this dry, cutting wit she'd use on the phone with her friends. But with me she was goofy. She'd do voices when she read to me. She'd make up songs about putting on shoes. She taught me that laughter is a thing you give people.

Third: *tired.* She was always tired. I didn't understand it then — I just thought that's what adults were. Now I know she was working two jobs and raising a kid alone and probably not sleeping enough. But she never let the tiredness make her mean. That's... that's a lot.

Fourth: *proud.* God, she was proud of me. Embarrassingly proud. She'd tell strangers at the grocery store about my school play. She'd record my open mics on her phone even when the audio was garbage. She kept every drawing I ever made. I think she still has them.

Fifth: *teacher.* Not just because she was a teacher. She taught me how to read music, how to ride a bike, how to apologize. She taught me that you can be soft and strong at the same time. She taught me that leaving someone doesn't mean you stop loving them — I think about that one a lot now.

I could say *strict* or *loud* or *church lady* but those feel like adjectives for her, not for us. The relationship was... we were a team. It was us against the world, even when we didn't say it out loud.

Okay. I have five. I'm ready to say them out loud. I hope they don't think I'm being too sentimental. But that's the truth. That's what it was.

prompt_text: Did you experience the loss of a parent or other close loved one while you were a young child — for example, a sibling, or a close family member?
monologue_text:
No. That's not... I was twenty-six. Twenty-six is not a young child. Why would she ask that. She's following something. A script. Some therapist's flowchart. "Did you experience loss as a young child." Like she's checking boxes. I can see her mentally ticking. No. Next.

But why that question. What's she looking for. If I say yes then what. If I say no then what. She's trying to find the crack. The thing that made me this way. Everyone always wants to find the thing that made me this way. As if there's a single thing. As if I'm broken and need a origin story for the break.

The old man died at fifty-eight. I was at my desk. I flew home. I did what needed doing. That's not a young child's loss. That's an adult's problem. That's Tuesday.

She's probably read something. Some pop psychology about attachment and early trauma. She's probing. I can feel the probe. It's not warm. It's clinical. She's not asking because she cares about my father. She's asking because she wants to label me.

I don't want to be labeled. I don't want to be understood that way. Understood as a set of deficits.

The hospital. The smell of it. The way his hand felt when I held it. That's not for this room. That's not for her checklist.

I'm annoyed now. I can feel it in my jaw. The question is irrelevant. It changes nothing. My father died when I was an adult. I handled it. I always handle it.

What does she expect me to say. What does she want.

I should just say no. Clean. Factual. No elaboration. That closes the door. That keeps the probe out. She can't work with nothing.

But she'll ask something else. There's always something else. People like her always have another question. Another layer. They think they're peeling an onion. They don't realize some things are just... stone. No layers. Just core.

I feel cold. Not angry. Cold. The right word is contempt. She's trying to be delicate with me and it's transparent. I can see the gears.

No. The answer is no. I didn't lose anyone as a young child. And even if I had, what would that prove. What would that explain. What would that fix.

Nothing. It would fix nothing.

I'm done thinking about this. Let me just say no and move on. Don't give her anything to work with. Don't let her see the hospital room. Don't let her see the chess championship empty chair. Don't let her see any of it.

No. Clean. Next.
response_text: No. I was an adult.
