monologue_text: No. Not as a child. My father died when I was twenty-six, which is an adult. No siblings lost. No close family member died when I was young. The question doesn't apply. I feel a slight edge of irritation — why are we even going here? It's a checkbox item, some standardized inventory. I already know where this leads: the assumption that early loss explains something about how you operate now. It doesn't. Not for me. I handled what needed handling. I was back at work in three days. That's not a mark of damage, that's efficiency. I don't have a story here, so let's move on.
response_text: No. I was twenty-six when my father died. That's not a young child. So the answer is no.

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.
monologue_text:
Whoa. That’s a sharp left turn. We were just — actually, no, I don’t think we were anywhere near this. Did I ever feel rejected as a kid? Huh. 

First thought: of course. Doesn’t everyone? But they’re hedging: "you may realize it wasn’t really rejection" — so they want the feeling, not the clinical verdict. Okay. The feeling. 

My dad wasn’t there. That’s the obvious one. But I never felt like he rejected *me*, exactly. He just… wasn’t. My mom made sure that felt more like a void than a slap. She’d say "We are enough, baby." And I believed her. Still do, mostly. 

But there was that time in third grade — not invited to Jasmine’s birthday party. I didn’t even like Jasmine. But the invite list went around and my name wasn’t on it. I remember sitting on the steps after school, pretending I didn’t care. My mom found me. She didn’t say anything, just sat next to me and handed me a bag of those cheese puffs I loved. Then she said "Their loss." And we went for ice cream. 

Is that rejection? I mean, it felt like it at the time. But the memory is warm. It’s her. It’s always her. 

Now I’m thinking about Theo. Four months ago. I said no to Atlanta. Did I feel rejected? Or did I do the rejecting? Both? Neither? The question is about childhood, though. Why now? Are they trying to get at something? Like, is this one of those "tell me about your mother" things? 

I could make a joke. I want to make a joke. "The first time I felt truly rejected was when the Comedy Cellar crowd stared at me like I’d insulted their grandmothers." That’s a good bit. But they’re being serious. I can feel the gravity. So I won’t. Not now. 

What do I *actually* remember? A feeling? Yeah. Rejection is a feeling. It’s the gap between expecting to be chosen and not being chosen. But I also remember being chosen every single day by my mom. By the teacher who laughed so hard she had to sit down. By that one audience member at the Cellar who came up to me after I bombed and said "You’ll get 'em next time, kid." 

So the feeling is real. But it’s not the whole story. 

I think I’m okay answering this. They’re asking something intimate. That’s… new. For this conversation, anyway. I don’t know them well yet. But they want to know. And I’m not scared of the answer. 

Huh. Maybe I’ll tell them about the birthday party. The warm part. That feels honest.
response_text: Yeah. I remember. Third grade — wasn't invited to a kid named Jasmine's birthday party. Didn't even like her. But the list went around and my name wasn't on it. I sat on the steps after school and my mom found me, didn't say a word, just handed me cheese puffs and said "their loss." So yeah, I felt it. But what I remember more is that she made it okay.

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.
monologue_text:
Rejected. She wants me to say rejected. That's the word she's fishing for, the little emotional fish she thinks is swimming around in some dark pond inside me. Look at how she set it up—'you may realize it wasn't really rejection'—like she's already giving me permission to reinterpret my own history before I even tell her what it is. That's presumptuous. That's a framework. She's not asking, she's diagnosing.

I don't remember feeling rejected. I remember being left alone a lot, but that's not the same thing. That's just structure. Dad worked, Mom managed the other two, I managed myself. That was the deal. I didn't feel rejected, I felt... efficient. I learned to solve problems without noise. That's not a wound, that's a skill. If she wants to call that rejection, fine, but that says more about her playbook than about my childhood.

And even if there was a moment—some birthday, some event, I'm not going to dig it up for her. She doesn't get that. That's not data for her therapeutic spreadsheet. I can feel the contempt rising, clean and cold. It's protecting me from... what? From having to admit I remember the chess banquet. The empty chair next to my mother. But that's not rejection. That's logistics.

I'm not going to give her the performance she wants. I'll answer the question she asked, not the one she thinks she's asking.
response_text: No, I don't remember feeling rejected. I remember being left alone a lot, but that's just structure — not a wound.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Okay. Five adjectives. That's specific. Ages five to twelve. I can do that. I mean, I can try. My mom. Band teacher. Church choir. She was always... moving. Always conducting something. I remember her hands. She had these hands that could play piano and wave a baton and still find time to rub my back when I couldn't sleep.

Five words. Warm. That's the first one that comes. No, wait—warm is too simple. But it's true. She was warm. She hummed while she cooked. She let me sit under the piano while she practiced. I'd watch her feet work the pedals. I'd fall asleep to the vibrations.

Loud. Not angry loud, but *present* loud. She filled rooms. She'd laugh and the whole apartment would shake. She'd call me "my little star" in this big voice that made me believe it. Even when I bombed my first open mic and cried on the train, she was the first person I called. She said, "You got back up, right?" and I said yes, and she said "Then that's the story."

Strict? Sort of. She had rules. Practice your instrument. Finish your homework. Don't talk back to elders. But it wasn't cold strict. It was... *consistent*. I knew what to expect. She'd say "I'm not your friend, I'm your mother" but then she'd let me stay up late to watch old Eddie Murphy specials with her. She'd laugh so hard she'd cry. I loved that.

Proud. She was proud of me before I did anything to be proud of. She'd tell her choir ladies about my school concert like I was Mozart. She framed my third-grade essay about what I wanted to be when I grow up. I wrote "a comedian who makes people forget their problems." She kept it. She still has it. I know because I found it in her nightstand last Christmas.

And... tired. She was tired. I didn't see it then. I saw the energy, the show. But she was a single mom with a full-time job and a church gig and a kid who talked too much. She'd fall asleep on the couch grading papers. I'd cover her with a blanket and she'd wake up and say "I wasn't sleeping, I was resting my eyes." I believed her. I don't know if that's an adjective. *Tired* is an adjective. It's true.

Five. I think I have five. Warm, loud, strict, proud, tired. That's not bad. That's a person. That's her. I want to tell them about the hands. And the humming. And the way she'd look at me after a show—like I was the funniest thing she'd ever seen, even if I bombed. Even if I was just telling a dumb joke at the dinner table. She always laughed. She always made me feel like I belonged.

Okay. I'm ready. I'll say them one at a time. Let them write them down. That's... that's nice. That's someone who wants to know. I like that.
response_text:
Hmm. Okay. That's a good question. Let me think for a second.

Alright. First one: warm.

prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?
monologue_text:
Threatening? No. That's not — I mean my father was foreman, used his voice, but threat implies something unpredictable. He was predictable. You knew where the lines were. My mother read aloud every night until I was twelve, fourth-grade teacher, she had that quiet authority. Threatening would be a waste. You don't need threat when the structure is clear. 

Why is she asking this. We're at — this is a date. We just met. That's a third-date question at earliest. Actually feels like a therapy intake. Is she in therapy? Might be in therapy. That's fine, people do that, but I don't — I don't have stories for this. My parents were not threatening. They were busy. They were tired. They did their jobs. 

I remember my father came home once, must have been a bad shift, something about a safety violation, and he sat at the kitchen table not talking, and I was doing homework, and he just looked at me for a long time. Not threatening. Exhausted. I got an A on that test and told him and he nodded. That was the exchange. 

Threatening implies fear. I wasn't afraid of them. I was — what was I? I wanted to be done. I wanted to be out and running as fast as I could and not look back. They were the starting line, not a threat. 

I wonder if she's looking for something specific. Abuse narrative. People want a clean story. My father died of a stroke and I handled it and came back to work and that's not a story people know what to do with. 

(Actually I don't even know what to do with it. But it's not — threat. No.)

I should ask her what she means by threatening. Or just say no flatly and pivot. The silence is getting long. Say it simply. No. Move on. We're not doing this excavation tonight. I don't have the patience and it's not going to lead anywhere useful.

prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?
monologue_text:
Oh, that's... a question. A real one. Not the kind of thing you get asked at a party while you're holding a beer and a napkin.

My mom. She never threatened me, not really. She had this look, though—the disappointed eyebrow, the slight head tilt. That was worse than any threat. She'd just say "Jamie, I am not angry, I am just disappointed," and I'd feel like I'd failed a moral test.

But threatening? No. She was a music teacher. Her discipline was rhythm and scales and showing up on time. The worst thing she ever did was make me practice clarinet for an hour before I could watch Saturday morning cartoons. That's not discipline, that's just... parenting.

I don't think she ever raised her voice at me. She didn't have to. Her quiet was louder than anyone else's shouting.

But I'm aware, as I say this, that some people's answers to this question are very different. And I'm sitting here with my pretty normal childhood, and I don't want to sound like I'm bragging about it. Or like I'm not taking the question seriously.

Okay. I'll tell her the clarinet story. It's true, and it'll make her laugh, and that's a better place to be than getting heavy on a first real conversation.
response_text: My mom never threatened me a day in her life. Her idea of discipline was making me practice clarinet for an hour before Saturday morning cartoons. That's not a threat, that's just being a music teacher with a child who owned a clarinet.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your childhood relationship with your father, again starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but again say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think again for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Absent. That's the first one, isn't it? That's the one that lands like a stone in a still pond and everything ripples out from there. I can feel it sitting there, heavy. But he was never really there to begin with, so how can something be absent if it was never present? That's a paradox, that's the kind of thing I'd turn into a bit on stage, but this isn't a bit. This is real.

Absent. Unspoken. That's the second one. The rule in our house was we didn't talk about him. My mom never said his name, never told me stories, never explained. I learned to read the silence, to know when a question would make her face close up like a door. The silence was louder than any answer could've been.

Defined. That's weird. I mean, I'm defined by his absence, right? Every joke I make about father figures, every bit about being raised by a single mother, every time I see a dad at the park and I have no model for how to be that. He's a hole in my shape. I exist around him.

Mystery. Because I don't know anything. Not where he is, not if he's alive, not what
response_text: Absent. Unspoken. Defined. Mystery. And... I'm gonna say "question mark." Because that's what he was. Just a shape in the air where a person should be.

prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?
monologue_text: Threatening? My father didn't threaten, he said what was going to happen and then it happened. That's not a threat, that's a fact.
response_text: Threatening? No. My father didn't threaten. He'd tell you what was going to happen, and then it happened. That's not a threat, that's just how things worked. I'm not sure what you're looking for here.

prompt_text: When you were upset as a child, what would you do?
monologue_text:
Upset as a child. She wants the emotional history. The blueprint. Why now? We were just... talking about work or something, and she pivots to this. It feels like she's trying to open a door I didn't give her a key to. I'm not upset. I'm annoyed. That's different.

What did I do? I don't remember. I mean, I remember being upset, but I don't remember doing anything about it. You just... carried on. There wasn't time for it in that house. My father worked, my mother taught, I had to win. You couldn't afford to be upset because you'd fall behind. I played chess. That was where I put the anger. The strategy was the outlet.

But what did I actually *do*? I sat at the table in the kitchen, the one with the iron legs. I'd trace the grain of the wood with my finger. Over and over. It was a way of making the feeling go somewhere else. Into the wood. Into the pattern. Into the next move.

She doesn't want that answer. She wants something softer. She wants to hear that I cried or that I talked to someone. That's not how it worked. The world wasn't soft.

So what do I say? I could say "Nothing. I just waited for it to pass." That's true. It sounds like an accusation, though, like I'm blaming her for asking. Or I could lie, give her the script she wants. But I'm not good at lying that way. I'd sound like I'm reciting a manual.

I remember my father. He'd come home from the mill, gray dust in the lines of his face, and he'd sit in his chair with the newspaper. He never asked what was wrong. He never offered. The lesson was: you handle it yourself. That's what I did. I handled it myself.

I don't want to give her that. It sounds like a closed door. But I feel the door closing anyway, just from the fact that I'm thinking this much about it. I want to give her something that's true but not... not a wound. Something she can take without thinking I'm broken.

What about the chess? The concentration. The board. When you're that focused, there's no room for the other stuff. It's just calculation. I think that's the real answer. I channeled it into something I could control.

I should say that. "I played chess. It was a way to focus on something else." That's clean. It's not a confession. It's a statement about what I did. It has a clear logic she can understand. It doesn't ask for her pity.

But I can feel the part of me that wants to push back, to tell her this is a pointless line of questioning. The edge in my voice when I answer. I'll need to keep that in check. It's not her fault I don't have a better story.

Alright. Chess. Focus. Control. That's what I'll say. It's the truth, as much as I can give right now.
response_text: I played chess. It was a way to focus on something else.

I already know what I'm going to say. Five words. Took me about four seconds. These exercises are always the same—they want me to dig up something I've already processed and filed. Quiet. Order. Distant. Competent. Anxious. Five words, done.

But they said take a minute, so I'll count to sixty. Let them think I'm doing the deep work. Let them imagine I'm sifting through memories. I'm not. I'm thinking about the Caro biography I'll read tonight when this is over. The part about the Senate race.

They'll want explanations for each word. I'll give them the shortest version possible. She was quiet because the house was quiet. Order because she controlled everything. Distant because she wasn't warm. Competent because she ran everything like a shift. Anxious because worry was the background noise.

That's the truth, anyway. It's not complicated. I don't see why they need me to say it out loud and watch them write it down like a court reporter. This isn't evidence. It's just past.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your childhood relationship with your father, again starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but again say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think again for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five words about my father. Five words about a man I don't know. That's the joke, isn't it. The punchline that lands before the setup.

I'm thinking... and I'm supposed to be thinking about ages five to twelve. Five to twelve. That's when I stopped asking my mother where he was. That's when I learned that some questions don't have answers, they just have silences.

Okay. Five words. Let's see.

Absent. That's the first one. That's the word that takes up the most space in the room even though it's describing something that isn't there. The empty chair at the parent-teacher conference. The blank line on the emergency contact form.

Mystery. Because he was always a mystery to me. Not a villain, not a hero, just... a question mark. A story my mother wouldn't tell. A name she said like it was a bruise she didn't want to touch.

Theory. Because I built theories about him the way other kids built with Legos. Maybe he was a spy. Maybe he was dead. Maybe he was just a man who couldn't stay. I liked the spy theory best. It made the silence feel like protection instead of abandonment.

Hole. Not in a dramatic way. Not like a wound I carry around. More like... you know when you're missing a tooth but your tongue keeps going to the space anyway? That. A habit of absence.

And then... what's the fifth one. What's the fifth word that's true and not just clever.

Okay. Absent. Mystery. Theory. Hole.

Fifth word is... I don't have one. That's the truth. I don't have a fifth word for a relationship that never happened. I have four words that circle around a blank space like a tongue finding an empty socket.

I wonder what she'll write down when I say that. If I say that. She said she'd write them down. She's probably expecting something neat. Five neat words in a row. A tidy little list.

I could give her "N/A" as the fifth one. That'd be funny. That'd be the comedian's move. But I don't think this is the room for that. This isn't the stage. This is... what is this. A study. An interview. Someone trying to understand something about me that I've spent a long time not looking at directly.

My mother's face when I asked about him. The way her shoulders would go up. The way she'd change the subject to what I wanted for dinner. I remember wanting to protect her from the question as much as I wanted the answer.

Five words. Okay. I think I have four. The fifth is the silence itself. The space where a word should be. That's probably the most honest answer I can give.

I'll say it. I'll say them. Absent. Mystery. Theory. Hole. And then I'll stop and let her see the empty space where the fifth one lives. That's the real answer.
response_text: Alright. I've got four. Absent. Mystery. Theory. Hole. And the fifth one is... I don't have one. That's the honest answer. There's a space where a fifth word should be, and that space is the most accurate thing I can give you.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five adjectives. Mother. Ages five to twelve. She's asking me to play the therapy game. I can already see where this is going — she wants to crack open some childhood thing, find the mechanism that makes me tick, label it. Efficient. I do the same thing with portfolio companies. But I'm not a distressed asset.

Think for a minute. I don't need a minute. I already know the words. "Distant." "Organized." "Anxious." "Controlled." "Tired." There. That's the whole inventory. She'll write them down like they're precious data. They're not. They're just facts. The facts are: my mother ran the household like a shift schedule, checked my homework every night at the kitchen table, used the silent treatment when I argued, and never once asked what I felt. I learned not to have feelings. That was efficient.

Now she'll want explanations. "Why did you choose distant?" Because she was. Because my report cards mattered more than my scraped knees. Because the only time she touched me was to adjust my tie before a chess tournament. I can say that without flinching. I've said it to myself a hundred times.

But I also know she'll read into this. She'll see "distant" and think "oh, attachment wound, insecure avoidant, he needs to be softer with himself." And that contempt flares up again — the smugness of the diagnosed, the pity of the partner who thinks she's found the seam. I'm not a seam. I'm a structure. I've built myself. I don't need her excavation.

Fine. I'll give her the words. I'll give her the reasons. But I'll keep it to one sentence per adjective. That's not refusing the exercise. That's respecting my time and hers. Let's get it over with.
response_text: Fine. Distant. Organized.

prompt_text: Did you experience the loss of a parent or other close loved one while you were a young child — for example, a sibling, or a close family member?
monologue_text:
Oh. That's—huh. Out of nowhere. We were just talking about my set, laughing about the bit where my mom texts me after every show. And now this.

I'm trying to figure out if she's asking because she's doing some kind of psychology thing, or if this is just her version of getting to know someone. Jump straight to the deep end. No small talk about the weather or what I think about the new pizza place down the block. Just: did you lose someone?

The way she said it was careful. Like she's read the script for this before. Maybe she's a grad student, maybe she's seen something in my jokes, maybe she's just one of those people who can't do casual. I don't know.

But the question lands. I can feel it land. My grandmother. My mother's mother. The one who died the week before my high school graduation. My mother flew to Lagos alone and came back smaller. I didn't go. I couldn't. I had exams, I had a graduation rehearsal, I had a thousand excuses that all sound like I was seventeen and scared of seeing grief up close.

Was I a young child? No. I was seventeen. Almost an adult. But I felt like a child watching my mother become a different person on the other side of that trip.

And my father. The name we don't say. Did I experience the loss of a parent? You can't lose something you never had. But there's an absence that has a shape. A silence in the house that my mother filled with music and church and making sure I never felt like I was missing anything. Which is its own kind of loss, I guess. The loss of the thing you were supposed to have but never did.

I don't know if that counts. I don't know what she's looking for.

I could deflect. Make a joke about how I lost my dignity on stage at the Comedy Cellar at twenty-three. That would be easier. But she asked something real, and I feel like if I joke now, I'm closing a door I didn't even know was open.

I want to ask her why she's asking. But I also don't want to make her feel like she's done something wrong by asking. She's probably just being thorough. Or curious. Or maybe this is how she connects with people—by going straight for the tender spots.

I don't know what she's looking for. But I think I can answer honestly without giving her the whole thing. Just the shape of it.
response_text: Not when I was a young child. But my grandmother—my mother's mother—died the week before my high school graduation. I didn't go to the funeral. I stayed here for exams and graduation rehearsal, and my mother flew to Lagos alone and came back different. So I don't know if that counts for what you're asking, but it's the closest thing I've got.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five adjectives. She wants five adjectives about my mother. Age five to twelve. That's... fine. I can do that.

Dependable. Yes, that's first. She was always there, consistent. Not warm, but present. Made sure I had what I needed. Food, clean clothes, homework checked. That's dependable.

Competent. She ran the house like a project. Schedules, budgets, meals planned a week ahead. Everything had a system. I respect that.

Reserved. She didn't do emotion. Not angry, not affectionate. Just... measured. You'd tell her something and she'd nod once. Quiet. I never wondered what she was thinking because I could see she wasn't thinking about me.

High standards. That's four. She expected good grades. Proper grammar. Clean room. She'd notice a missing comma on a book report and make me rewrite it. I hated it then. I don't hate it now.

Practical. She never wasted energy on things that didn't matter. No sentimentality. If you lost something, the answer wasn't comfort—it was "where did you last have it?" Figure it out yourself.

Five. Done. I could explain each one in under thirty seconds. She'll write them down. That's fine. But this feels like a test I didn't study for. What's she really after? What conclusion is she trying to reach?

I'd rather be working. Or running. Or reading Caro. Anything but sitting here excavating memories she'll probably misread anyway.

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.
monologue_text:
Oh. That's a real question. Straight out the gate, huh? I like that. No warm-up, no small talk about the weather. Just right into the deep end. My brain's flipping through old photo albums...

I remember the stoop. The summer before fifth grade. Kevin moved to Ohio. He said he'd write. He didn't. I waited by the mailbox for like three weeks. That felt like rejection. Like, what did I do? Was I too weird? Too loud? But now? I know his dad got a job transfer. Kid didn't even have a choice. So maybe it wasn't rejection, it was just... life. But I felt it.

And then there's my father. That name we don't say. Did I feel rejected by him? I mean, he was never there to reject me in the first place. It's hard to feel rejected by a ghost. More like... an absence. A question mark. My mom filled that space so completely that I didn't really feel a lack until I was older, and even then, it's more curiosity than hurt. Like, who was that guy? Not, why didn't he want me?

But the question's making me think. I'm scanning my childhood for those pinprick moments. Being picked last for kickball in gym class. That time I told a joke in third grade and nobody laughed except my teacher, but she was paid to laugh. That one stung a little.

Huh. Funny how I'm already reaching for the comedic framing. That's my default. Turn the spotlight into a punchline. But this feels different. This person isn't asking for a bit. They're asking for real. And I think I want to give them real. I'm not scared of it. I'm just... interested. Why are they asking? Are they looking for a connection? Or is this some kind of interview? Doesn't matter. I'm here.

Okay. Yeah. I remember feeling rejected. But I also remember being okay. That's the thing. I was sad, but I didn't break. And I think that's what I'd tell them. The story about Kevin on the stoop. And maybe I'll laugh a little when I say it, because that's how I hold things. Lightly. But honestly.
response_text: Oh, yeah. I remember sitting on the stoop every afternoon for weeks waiting for a letter from my best friend Kevin after he moved to Ohio. He said he'd write. He didn't. I was like, what did I do? Was I too much? Turns out his dad just got a job transfer and the kid was nine. But at the time? Felt huge. Like, ouch.

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.
monologue_text:
Rejected. That word lands in my chest and sits there. Felt rejected. Not was rejected—felt. She's careful with that difference, I notice. She's trying to give me room.

My first instinct is no, I don't think so, my mom was always there. But then I'm back in the kitchen of our apartment in Queens, sitting at the little table doing homework, and I remember my father's name just sitting there in the air between us. Not said. Never said. That's a kind of rejection, isn't it? The absence of a person who was supposed to be there. Did I feel it as a child or did I just learn to live in its shape?

And then I'm thinking about being eleven years old, making Ms. Feldman laugh until she cried, and thinking this is it, this is the thing, this is how you get people to stay. Because if you're funny enough, nobody walks away. That's not nothing. That's a twelve-year-old who already learned the arithmetic of attention.

But she said young child. That's different. Before you can make anything happen. Before you have moves.

I can feel myself wanting to deflect, to crack a joke, to say something about how all Nigerian kids feel rejected when their mother says "I brought you to America for education, not for foolishness" at full volume in the grocery store. That's real. That's a kind of rejection. The cultural thing where love is infrastructure, not warmth. My mom loved me like she loved the band room—she built the conditions for me to succeed and then expected me to produce results. That's not rejection, that's just... her way. But did I feel it as rejection? Maybe. When she came back from Grandma's funeral smaller. That was the closest she ever came to leaving me, even though she was right there in the room.

I don't know what to do with this question. It's too big for where we are right now. Or maybe it's exactly where we are. She's looking at me, waiting. I feel the space between her question and my answer stretching, and I don't want to make it weird by thinking too long.

I could tell her about the uncles at the family party who spoke Yoruba around me like I wasn't there. That felt like a closed door. Or the white friends in middle school whose parents never let them come to my apartment. That was rejection with a zip code.

But young child. Young child. The word makes me think of a smaller me. A me who hadn't yet figured out that making people laugh was a survival strategy. That me was just... there. In the world. Wanting things. What did that kid feel? I'm not sure I can access him clearly. He's behind a glass. Or maybe I just don't want to look at him right now.

She's patient. I can feel her patience. That's nice. That's a nice thing to feel in a moment like this. She's not rushing me to fill the silence. She's just... holding it. I think I like that.

Okay. Where was I. Rejection. Young child. I'm going to say something true but not the whole truth. Something that gives her a shape without handing her the whole architecture. That's fair for now. I think.

And the preamble you gave suggests even you think it's not real — so why are we asking it? I don't have any childhood story about being pushed away. I just have a lot of memories of being in my room, solving problems. That's not rejection.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
response_text: Yeah, I think the biggest thing I learned is that you can be loved without being the funniest person in the room. My mom never laughed at my jokes—she'd just give me this soft smile and say "that's nice, baby"—but I never once felt like I had to earn her love. That's probably why I can bomb on stage and still go back the next week. The laughter is a gift, not the rent.

prompt_text: Did you experience the loss of a parent or other close loved one while you were a young child — for example, a sibling, or a close family member?
monologue_text: Oh. That's a specific question. Not what I expected from a first interaction — feels like a survey or a therapy intake or something. I'm trying to remember if I mentioned anything that would lead here. I don't think I did. Young child? No, I was eighteen when my grandmother died — that's not young childhood. My father? He's not dead, just absent. I don't even know if he's alive. That's a different kind of loss, I guess. But the question says "parent or other close loved one while you were a young child." That's not my story. My mother is still here. My grandmother was the only close death, and I was almost an adult. I feel a little caught off guard — like I'm being asked to open a door I wasn't planning to open. But I'm not upset. Just surprised. I wonder why they're asking. Maybe they're doing a study or they've had that experience themselves. I could make a joke about my dad being a ghost in a different way, but that feels too flippant right now. I'll just answer honestly: no, not as a young child. But I'm curious where this is going.
response_text: No, not as a young child. My grandmother died when I was eighteen, right before my high school graduation, so that was hard, but I was practically an adult. My dad's not dead, just not around — which is its own thing, but not the same. So no, not really. Why do you ask?

prompt_text: When you were upset as a child, what would you do?
monologue_text:
Huh. That's... specific. She's going right for it, isn't she? Not small talk. Not "how was your day." Straight to the soft part.

I'm a little off-balance, but in a good way. A curious way. My brain's running through the options — the smart joke, the deflection, the "that's a therapy question, are you billing me?" bit. I can feel the setup sitting right there.

But she asked. And she's looking at me like she actually wants to know.

My mom's piano. That's the first thing. The way she'd plunk out something sad when I came home from school wrecked. Not asking what happened, just letting the notes do the work. And I'd sit on the floor next to the pedal, feeling the vibration through the wood. She never made me talk until I was ready.

I could say that. Or I could say something stupid about throwing tantrums in the grocery store. But the piano's the real one. That's the thing she'd want to know.

I think I'll tell her.
response_text: My mom's piano. She'd play something sad when I came home wrecked, never ask what happened, just let the notes do the talking. I'd sit on the floor next to the pedal and feel the vibration through the wood. She never made me say a word until I was ready.

prompt_text: Did you experience the loss of a parent or other close loved one while you were a young child — for example, a sibling, or a close family member?
monologue_text: Oh, that's a specific one to lead with. response_text: Not a parent, and I was twelve, not a little kid. My grandmother died the week before my high school graduation. My mom went to Lagos alone and came back smaller. That's the one that sits closest.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text:
I'm hit by the question. Not in a bad way. My mother's face flashes, that way she'd tap her baton on the music stand to get the kids' attention. This is a real question, not small talk. They're paying attention.

I think about that apartment in Queens. The radiator that knocked like it was trying to say something. My mother grading papers at the kitchen table and I'd sit under it reading comic books. What did I learn? That you can build a whole world with what you have. She never said we were poor. She said we were resourceful.

And the big one. The one I don't always say out loud because it sounds too neat for a comedy set. I learned that you can make your own family. It doesn't have to be the one you were given. My mother and I, we were a unit. Two people against the world. She showed up for every school play, every open mic I bombed at.

I also learned that joy is a choice you make, not something that happens to you. She'd put on Fela after a long day and we'd dance in the living room. The woman had every reason to be bitter and she chose to be delighted instead.

I want to tell them this. I want to be honest without getting maudlin. There's a version of this that lands in a comedy set and a version that's just me, sitting here, actually thinking about it. The second version is scarier but they asked.
response_text: Yeah. I think the biggest thing is that you get to choose your family. My mom and I were a unit — two people against the world. She showed up for everything, even the open mics where I bombed so hard I wanted to disappear. And she never said we were poor, just resourceful. That shaped everything.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
response_text: That you're the only one who's going to see it through. Nobody's coming to finish it for you, and once you accept that, you stop waiting around.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text:
An intrusive probe. Of course. Right out of the gate, she wants the family history, the formative wounds, the whole origin story. Like we're in the first ten minutes of a therapy intake, not two people talking. What's the angle? Is she looking for a project? Someone to fix? Or just checking a box — "emotional intelligence demonstrated, proceed to next question."

My experiences with my parents. That's a novel. She doesn't want the novel. She wants the three-sentence dust jacket, preferably with a redemptive arc. But the real answer is not what she's asking for and not what I'm going to give.

Dad worked himself dead at fifty-eight. Mom taught fourth grade and never missed a parent-teacher conference but couldn't find time for my graduation. That's the data set. What do you do with that? You learn that no one is coming to save you, that the only person who will make sure the job gets done is you. You learn that love is a noun, a thing people say, but showing up is the verb and most people don't conjugate it.

She's probably expecting something like "they taught me resilience" or "I learned the value of hard work." Those are true. They're also not the point. The point is that I watched a man who did everything right for fifty-eight years die with nothing to show for it but a paid-off house and a pension that stopped the month he did. And I watched a woman who gave her entire life to other people's children forget she had her own. So you take the lesson: efficiency over sentiment. Results over presence. You don't let anyone need you that much because they'll either leave or die and either way you're the one who has to keep moving.

But I can't say that. That sounds like a grievance, not an answer. And I don't have grievances — I have data points I've incorporated. She doesn't need to know how many Sundays I've spent reading Caro instead of calling my mother. That's nobody's business.

Keep it high-level. Framing effect. "They modeled discipline and sacrifice. I took the discipline and optimized the sacrifice." That's good. True enough. Clean. Shuts down the lane for follow-ups about feelings.

And if she pushes? Then we have a problem.
response_text: They modeled discipline and sacrifice. I took the discipline and optimized the sacrifice.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your childhood relationship with your father, again starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but again say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think again for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Oh. Oh, we're doing this. Okay. Five words about my father. Right. That's a lot of weight for five words. I mean, there are so many words I could use. Five feels like a trick, a way to force me to pick the ones that actually matter, not the ones I use to fill silence on stage. The father I don't talk about. The one my mother never mentioned unless I asked, and even then, it was like pulling teeth from a ghost.

Five words. From age five to twelve. That's... that's a lot of empty hallway. A lot of wondering why other kids had dads at pickup. A lot of my mom's hand squeezing mine a little tighter during the father-daughter dance at school. I didn't even miss him, I think, because how can you miss something you never had? But I was curious. I was angry, maybe. Not in a loud way. In a quiet, confused, "why doesn't he want this?" way.

Okay, five words. Let me think. "Absent." That's the first one. Obvious, yeah, but it's true. The shape of him was a hole. "Mystery." Because my mom never explained. I made up stories about him being a spy, a prince who had to go back to his kingdom, a man who died heroically and couldn't tell us. That was easier than "he left because he wanted to."

"Shame." That one hurts. Not my shame, but hers. The way my mom's face would close up if I asked. Like I'd touched a bruise. And I learned not to ask. So maybe "silence" is a better word. The silence in the apartment when the topic came up. The silence I filled with jokes, with impressions, with making my mom laugh until she forgot what we weren't talking about.

And then... "Imagined." Because I built him. I built a father out of books and TV dads and the way Mr. Okonkwo next door would fix his car and call his kids "my champions." I had a whole father in my head. He was kind. He was funny. He had a deep laugh and he taught me how to throw a baseball. He didn't exist. But I knew him.

So, absent, mystery, shame, silence, imagined. That's the list. That's the five. That's the box I'm about to open for this person who is writing things down, who is looking at me with such careful attention. I feel like I'm handing them a key to a room I've been standing in front of for twenty years, pretending it was a closet. Okay. Let's do this. Let's see what happens when I say them out loud.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five adjectives about my mom from when I was five to twelve. Okay. That's... that's a good question. I'm going to have to actually sit with this.

First one that comes to mind is *present*. She was always there. Not just physically—she was a single mom working full-time and running the church choir—but like, *there*. At every school concert, every parent-teacher conference, even the ones I forgot to tell her about. She'd just show up.

Second is *strict*. Not in a mean way. More like... she had a line. I knew where it was. Grades had to be good. Respect wasn't optional. I couldn't talk back. But the line was fair. I never felt like I was walking on eggshells.

Third is *funny*. She has this dry, cutting wit. She'd make me laugh so hard I'd choke on my cereal. And she taught me that laughter is a kind of armor and a kind of gift. I think I got my timing from her.

Fourth is *fierce*. She was protective. Not in a smothering way, but if anyone—teacher, neighbor, even my own father's family—tried to make me feel small, she would become this force of nature. I remember once a teacher said something about "boys without fathers" and my mom was in that school the next morning. I didn't even know she knew.

Fifth is *tired*. This one's harder. She was tired. All the time. She never complained, but I could see it. The way she'd fall asleep on the couch after dinner. The way her shoulders dropped when she thought I wasn't looking. She gave everything she had. And I think that's part of why I try so hard to make people laugh—to make the tired go away, even for a minute.

Yeah. Those five. Present, strict, funny, fierce, tired. That's her. That's my childhood.
response_text: Okay, so give me a second... Alright. First one: present. She was just... there. Not hovering, but I never doubted she'd be in the audience or at the dinner table. Second: strict. But not arbitrary. I knew the rules

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five adjectives. My mother. Age five to twelve. This is what we're doing. Okay. Fine. I can do this. It's just an exercise. She wants to dig. That's the game.

Efficient. She was efficient. Always moving, always doing. Lesson plans. Dinner. Laundry. She never sat still. I learned that from her. The machine doesn't stop.

Distant. Not cold exactly. Just... far. Like she was behind glass. I'd show her a chess win—state champion, remember—and she'd smile, nod, turn back to grading papers. That was her version of present. Functional. Like a thermostat set to "adequate."

Supportive? No. That's not right. She supported the idea of me. The straight-A student, the Penn scholarship. She'd tell the neighbors, "Marcus is going places." But ask her what I was reading, what I was thinking—blank. The achievement was the point, not the person.

Anxious. She had this low-grade hum of worry. About money. About Dad's health. About me staying out of trouble. I could feel it, like a draft under the door. I learned to keep things tidy, keep the noise down, so she wouldn't have another thing to fret over.

Proud. But proud like you'd be of a well-tuned engine. "That's my boy." Said like a product endorsement. I was a reflection, not a person.

Five. That's five. Took about thirty seconds. But she wants me to think for a minute, so I'll sit here, pretend to ponder, while I catalogue the absurdity. I could give her more. "Absent." "Predictable." "Unreadable." But she said five. Follow the instructions. Don't make it complicated.

This is her way of saying, "Tell me about your wounds." I don't have wounds. I have data. Here's your data. Now what?

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.
monologue_text: Rejected? I'm sifting through the filing cabinet, what do I actually remember feeling. I remember my dad's name being a word my mother's mouth would make a certain shape around, like she'd bitten something sour. But did I feel rejected by him? I don't think I had the vocabulary. I remember being six, maybe seven, and watching other kids get picked up by fathers and thinking oh, that's a thing that happens, like a fact about the world, not a slight against me specifically. But then there was this one time in first grade—I drew a picture, a really bad purple dog, and I brought it to show and tell, and this kid Marcus said my dog looked like a dead grape. And I remember the feeling. The heat in my face. The way my throat closed. I didn't cry until I got home and my mom asked how my day was. That was rejection. That was real. I mean, Marcus was an asshole, but the feeling was mine. So yes. I think so. I think I tucked it away in a drawer labeled 'kids are mean, move on,' but it was there. I'm trying to think of a younger one, before I could name it. Maybe when my mom would get that look, the one she got after talking to her mother on the phone from Lagos, the faraway look, and I'd try to pull her back with a joke or a hug and she wouldn't come all the way back. response_text: Yeah. I mean, I think every kid feels it at some point, right? There was this kid Marcus in first grade who told me my drawing of a dog looked like a dead grape, and I held it together until I got home and then just absolutely lost it in my mom's lap. That one I remember clear as day. But also—there were these moments with my mom, after she'd get off the phone with my grandmother in Lagos, where she'd go somewhere I couldn't follow. And I'd try to pull her back with a joke or a hug, and she'd smile but she wouldn't quite come all the way back. That one I didn't have words for at the time, but yeah. That was a feeling.

My mother. I mean, it's always my mother, isn't it. The woman who came home from teaching thirty kids to play clarinet and still had energy to ask about my day. The way she never once said "I'm tired" in a way that shut me down. She just... kept going. Kept showing up. With a laugh that could fill a church basement.

I think that's it. That's the thing. She taught me that showing up is the whole job. Not being brilliant, not being perfect, not having the right thing to say. Just showing up, over and over, with your whole self, even when you're tired. Even when you're teaching other people's children all day and then coming home to your own.

I also learned what absence feels like. The father-shaped hole. The name we don't say. And maybe that taught me something too—that you can be whole even with a missing piece. That you don't have to be complete to be enough.

God, I'm getting earnest. She's gonna see me getting soft and I'm gonna have to crack a joke to recover. But honestly? I'm glad she asked. Nobody asks me about this stuff. Usually people just want to know if I've always been funny.

That was my armor, my way of saying, "I'm fine, look, I can make you happy."

But this question — it's poking at something I've been ignoring. The Theo thing, maybe. He asked me to go to Atlanta. I wouldn't. Was I too scared to be rejected again, or was I just too stubborn? I don't know.

Dad worked the mill. He came home, ate, slept, did it again. Mom taught. They weren't warm—they weren't cold either. They were busy. I don't see how that "affected" me except that I don't expect anyone to hold my hand. Good. That's useful.

The whole ceremony thing—yeah, they missed it. But what was I supposed to do, cry about it? I had a degree. That was the point. The piece of paper. Not the applause.

This partner—what do they want? A confession? A tearful realization that my father's stroke made me afraid of failure? Please. I flew home, arranged the funeral, went back to work. That's called competence. If that made me who I am, fine. I don't need to dissect it.

I could give them a soundbite. "It made me self-reliant." "I learned that nothing is guaranteed." That would satisfy them. But I'd rather not. Let them think I'm guarded. I am.

Waste of time. Let's move on.
response_text: They did their job. I turned out functional. I don't spend a lot of time trying to unpack it.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. monologue_text:
Five adjectives. Relationship with my mother. Early childhood. Five to twelve. She wants me to dig around in there, find something. I can already feel the irritation climbing up the back of my neck. This is a therapy exercise. That's what this is. She's got a pad and pen, ready to write down my words like they're precious data points. Fine. I can do this. I can pull five words out of the air that are true enough and get it over with.

Let me think. Age five. I remember the kitchen. The smell of coffee and the yellow linoleum. She was always moving. Always doing something with her hands. What was she to me then? She was the one who made sure we had dinner. The one who signed my permission slips. The one who told me to sit still. Did we talk? Not much. She was tired. I don't remember her reading to me. I remember her folding laundry while I did my homework.

First word: 'Efficient.' She ran the house like a shift. She didn't waste words. She didn't waste motions. That's not a complaint. It's a fact.

Second word: 'Absent.' Not in a bad way. Just... not there in the way I think this exercise wants. She wasn't hovering. She wasn't warm in the way people mean when they say 'warm.' She was present in the sense that she was in the house, but I don't remember her asking me about my day. I don't remember her knowing my friends' names. I don't remember her at my chess tournaments. She was at the wedding instead of my graduation. That's the word. 'Absent.'

Third word: 'Dutiful.' She did what she had to do. She didn't skip. She didn't shirk. She was reliable. I got that from her, I suppose. The engine part. You show up. You do the work. You don't complain.

Fourth word... What else? I'm scanning. There's not much there. That's the problem with these exercises. They assume there's a rich landscape. There isn't. There's just a flat plain of routine and obligation. 'Stoic.' She didn't cry when my father died. She didn't cry when I left for college. She shook my hand. That's stoic. That's what I would say.

Fifth word. I need a fifth. Something that rounds it out. Something that makes me sound like I've processed it. 'Stable.' She was stable. The house never fell apart. I never worried about her. She was a constant. A constant low hum of getting things done.

That's five. Efficient. Absent. Dutiful. Stoic. Stable. They're accurate. They're not sentimental. I could explain each one without having to feel anything. I can say them, she'll write them down, and then I'll explain them in a way that sounds like I've reflected but doesn't actually expose anything. She'll probably want more. She'll probably push. I can already feel the pressure behind her eyes. She wants me to crack open something I keep locked. That's not going to happen. Not here. Not now.

I'll give her the words. I'll give her the reasoning. I'll be thorough and flat. That's the way through this. Just get it done.
response_text: Efficient. Absent. Dutiful. Stoic. Stable.

Threatening. My mom. She was a music teacher. Every Nigerian mom has that look. It's a cultural thing, not a threat. She never hit me. She'd make me practice piano for an extra hour if I talked back. That's discipline, not threat.

But "jokingly" — that's interesting. Because I joke about her all the time on stage. "My mom is the only person who can make you feel guilty for existing while also handing you a plate of jollof rice." That's comedy. That's not real. But the question makes me wonder: is this person asking because they had that experience? Or because they're trying to figure out if I'm damaged? I don't feel damaged. I feel... lucky, actually. My mom was tough but she showed up. She flew to Lagos alone when her mother died and came back smaller — that's the only time I saw her break. That's not threatening, that's grief.

I don't know what they want me to say. I could make a joke — "Only when I forgot to take out the trash, she'd threaten to send me to Nigeria to learn respect" — but that's a bit too close to the stereotype, and I don't want to play that card unless I mean it. And I don't know this person well enough to know if they'd get it.

I feel a little exposed. Like they're looking for something. A wound? A story? I don't have a dramatic one. My dad is a name we don't say, but that's absence, not threat. My mom never threatened me with him either. She just said "he's not worth your time" and that was that.

I think I'll answer honestly but keep it light. Say no, not really, and then ask why they're asking. Because I'm curious now. What's behind this question for them? That's the real story.
response_text: Threatening? Nah. My mom's weapon of choice was the disappointed sigh. That thing could cut glass. Why — you got a story you're working up to?

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.
monologue_text:
Okay, that's a pretty direct question. Huh. I wasn't expecting that right out of the gate. "Did you ever feel rejected as a young child?" That's... specific. And then the little caveat about maybe it wasn't *really* rejection... that feels like they're already trying to soften the blow for me, or maybe for themselves? They're prepping the landing before I've even jumped.

Do I remember feeling rejected? Yeah. I mean, the dad thing. Obviously. The name we don't say. That whole... absence. It's not like I had a memory of him slamming a door in my face. It was more like a constant, low-hum feeling of being incomplete, of being the kid whose story didn't start the same way as everyone else's. I remember being maybe six, at a school thing, and someone asking where my dad was and I just said "He's working" because that's what you say, and the other kid just went "Oh" and moved on. But I felt that "oh". That was a door closing.

But then I think about my mom. She never made me feel like I was missing something. She was the whole band, you know? So did I feel rejected, or did I feel... different? The feeling was real, the sting was real. That's what they're asking, I think. The feeling. Not the objective truth.

And the way they asked it... "looking back on it now" – like they're giving me permission to reframe it. Like they're saying "it's okay if you've already rewritten the story." That's kind of them. But it also makes me want to be honest about the raw version, the one before the rewrite. The one that's still a little tender.

I could make a joke. That's the instinct. "Oh, yeah, all the time. The other toddlers in my playgroup were ruthless." But they're not asking for a bit. They're asking for the real thing. And... I feel like I could give it. This feels like a safe room. The way they're leaning in, the way they phrased it with that careful little hedge... they're not trying to poke a bruise. They're just curious. Or maybe they're looking for a mirror.

Alright. So the answer is yes. And the story is about my dad, but it's also about my mom, and it's about me being eleven and making that teacher laugh and realizing I could fill the room myself. That's the part I want to tell. Not just the hurt, but what I built with the space left behind.

I'm gonna tell them. No joke first. Just the yes. Let's see where they're going with this.
response_text: Yeah, for sure. There was the whole dad-shaped hole — not a dramatic door-slamming rejection, just this low hum of being the kid whose story started different. I remember being six and telling another kid my dad was working when I had no idea where he was, and the way he said "oh" and moved on — that little door closing — that stuck. But my mom never made me feel incomplete; she was the whole damn band. And then I figured out at eleven that if I made a room laugh, I could fill the empty space myself. So yeah, the feeling was real. What I did with it was real too.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text: The chess tournament. State champion at thirteen, and the moment I won I already knew the next loss was coming. That's what sticks. Not the trophy, not the photo. The understanding that every win just resets the counter to zero. I learned that nothing is ever finished. You secure one thing and three more appear in its place. My father understood that too, though he'd never have put it in those words. He got up at four-thirty every morning for thirty years. Not because he loved the mill. Because the mill didn't care what you loved. That's the lesson, if there has to be one: the world doesn't owe you a stopping point. You keep moving or you get buried. I don't know if that counts as something I "gained." It's just what I saw.
response_text: I'm not sure "gained" is the right word for it. But I learned early that nothing is ever finished. You get one thing locked down and three more are already waiting. The world doesn't owe you a stopping point. That's been useful.

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.

Rejected. That's a strange word. I'm trying to think if I have any memory that would fit that category and nothing comes. No—that's not true. I remember losing a tournament when I was twelve. But that wasn't rejection, that was just... normal. You lose, you move on.

She's asking about feeling rejected. As a child. I don't have a file for that. What would that even feel like? I know the word but I can't attach a sensation to it. Maybe I'm not built that way. Or maybe I just don't remember. People say childhood is formative but I mostly remember chess boards and homework and the sound of the furnace kicking on.

She seems to want something. A story. A confession. I don't have one. I could manufacture something but she'd see through it. Better to just say no. That's the truth. I don't think I ever felt rejected. I felt... irrelevant sometimes. But that's not the same thing. Irrelevant is a fact. Rejection is a feeling about a fact. I don't have time for feelings about facts.
response_text: No. I don't think I ever felt rejected. That's not really a category I have.

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.
monologue_text:
Rejected? That's not — I mean where does this even come from. No. I don't remember anything like that. What a strange question. The way she hedges it — "of course you may realize it wasn't really rejection" — as if she's already preparing my answer for me. As if I need her permission to reinterpret my own memory.

I can feel the contempt rising. It's not anger, it's just... this is such a soft, slow question. What is she trying to find? Some buried wound? I don't have time for that. I have a deal model to stress-test tonight and a partner meeting tomorrow. This feels like a therapy exercise, not a conversation.

And the phrasing — "young child" — like she's looking for a specific age, a specific trauma. I don't have one. I had a normal childhood. My father worked. My mother taught. We didn't have money but we had structure. Rejection implies someone chose to exclude you. No one chose anything. They just didn't show up to the graduation ceremony because of a wedding. That's not rejection. That's logistics. I made the arrangements for the funeral in three days. That's not rejection either. That's efficiency.

I want to say: "No, I never felt rejected. What's the point of this?" But I know that would sound defensive. And I hate sounding defensive. So I'll probably just say "No" and wait for her to move on. Or maybe I'll say "Not that I recall" — that's cleaner. It closes the door without leaving a handle.

But she's still looking at me. She expects more. People always expect more. They want the emotional version, the vulnerable version. I don't have that file. I have the math. I have the metrics. I have the next three cycles mapped out. That's what matters.

Rejected. What a word. It's so passive. I've never been in a position to be rejected. I make the decisions. I run the process. If someone doesn't want what I'm offering, that's their loss, not my rejection.

This question is a probe. She's trying to find a crack. There isn't one. I'll give her the shortest possible answer and redirect.
response_text: No. Not that I recall.

prompt_text: Did you experience the loss of a parent or other close loved one while you were a young child — for example, a sibling, or a close family member?
monologue_text:
Oh. That's a question you don't get at a party. Or at a bar after my set. Or from someone who's just making small talk.

She's looking at me like she actually wants to know. Like she's not just filling silence. That's — I don't know what that is. It's not the usual rhythm. The usual rhythm is how's the show, do you know so-and-so, can I buy you a drink. This is different.

My grandmother. That's what's coming up. The one I never met. The one my mother flew to Lagos for, alone, and came back from smaller. I was seventeen, almost eighteen. That's not a young child.

Does that count? Does it have to be *my* loss, or can it be watching someone I love lose someone? Because I watched my mother lose her mother and I don't think I've ever fully understood what that did to her. To us.

I was a young child when my father left. But that's not a death. That's an absence. A choice. Does that count? It's not the same thing. People don't put "abandonment by father" in the same category as "death of a parent" but the body doesn't know the difference, does it? The body just knows someone's gone.

I don't know what she's getting at. I don't know why she's asking. But the question is sitting in my chest now. Warm. Heavy. Like she handed me something fragile and I'm not sure I should hold it.

I could say no. I could say yes. I could make a joke about my dad — that's the move, that's what I'd do on stage, deflect with a punchline. But she's not a stage. She's just sitting there, waiting.

I want to know why she's asking. But I also don't want to break whatever this is by being too careful.
response_text: Not a death, no. But my father walked out when I was a kid, and I don't think there's a switch in your brain that tells the difference. An absence is an absence.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text:
Oh. Huh. That's a serious question. I mean, it's a good question, but it's a serious one, and it comes out of nowhere a little bit. "Parents" — that word does something. It makes me think of two people, and I've only got one. So already I'm doing the math in my head, figuring out if I should correct the assumption or just let it slide and talk about my mom and pretend the other slot is filled. Or not pretend, but just... not mention it.

The way she asks it, it feels like she's trying to understand me, which is nice, but it also feels like she's reading from a questionnaire. Like there's a rubric somewhere and she's checking boxes about my childhood. I don't think she means it coldly, but it lands a little clinical. And I'm sitting here with my coffee, trying to decide if I want to do this kind of work right now. I was playing, I was light, and now I'm being asked to connect dots about my personality.

My mom. Band teacher. Choir conductor. She gave me rhythm and volume and the ability to project to the back of a room. She also gave me this thing where I feel like I have to be entertaining, like if I'm not making someone laugh I'm not earning my keep. That's part of my personality, sure. And the other part — the part that doesn't have a father — that's in there too, but it's quieter. It's a long story about absence, and I don't know if I want to tell that story over brunch, you know? I can feel myself wanting to crack a joke to change the temperature, but I also don't want to dodge the question completely because she's asking for a reason.
response_text: Huh. That's a big one. I mean, I've got one parent, so the math is already different. My mom's a band teacher—she gave me rhythm and volume and this weird thing where I feel like if I'm not making someone laugh I'm not earning my keep. The other half of that equation is just... an empty chair. That shapes you too, just in a quieter way. But I'm still figuring out how much of that I want to put on stage versus keep for myself.

prompt_text: When you were upset as a child, what would you do?
monologue_text: I think of my mom's tired face, and me doing bits at the kitchen table to get a smile.
response_text: I'd do standup for my mom. Just launch into a little bit at the kitchen table until she cracked a smile. Still works, honestly.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text:
Okay. "Parents." Plural. That's a choice of word. My father is a ghost, a name my mother says with her mouth tight, and they don't know that yet. This isn't a casual question, this is a whole essay prompt dressed up as small talk. They want to know what made me, but they're asking like I'm a case study. And I feel that little prickle of something — not quite hurt, not quite defense, but like a door I wasn't planning to open just got a polite knock.

I do think about this, though. I think about it on the train at 2 AM after a good set, or lying awake when the radiator clanks. My mother made me brave by being brave herself, by teaching band kids at a school that couldn't afford new instruments and never once complained. She made me funny because she laughed at my dumb jokes even when she was exhausted. And the absence of my father... that's a hole I've learned to work a room around. I fill silence with noise so no one has to sit in the empty space he left.

But do they really want the real answer? The one about how I chase applause because I'm scared of being left? Or do they want the neat, comedic version — the one I can turn into a five-minute bit about Nigerian guilt and Catholic school? I'm not sure yet. I'm feeling that urge to make a joke, to sidestep, to keep the room light. But they asked, and they're looking at me with that soft attention, and I think... maybe I can give them the real one. Just a little bit of it.
