prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text: Five adjectives about Mom? That's a specific prompt — I like that, it's like building a joke but with real feelings.
response_text: Alright, give me a second... okay. Five words. Um — loud, steady, tired, funny, proud. That's five. You want me to explain?

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text:
"Learned above all." She wants a single takeaway. A headline. A moral at the end of the story. That's not how it works. Childhood isn't a classroom with a syllabus. It's a set of conditions. You adapt. You don't "learn" things like you learn a formula. You develop strategies. You build armor.

I could give her the polished version. The one I use at networking dinners. "I learned the importance of structure and discipline." "I learned to set high standards and meet them." That's true enough. It's a version of the truth. The version that doesn't require me to open anything.

But the contempt is there. The slight curl. This is a soft probe. She's trying to get under the armor. And I don't want her there. There's nothing there she'd want to see. Just a machine that runs on achievement and self-direction and a carefully managed distance from everyone.

My father's funeral. Three days. Back at the desk. That's the lesson, isn't it? The engine doesn't stop. The machine keeps running. You don't get to fall apart because there's no one to catch you. The family didn't come to my graduation. They were at a wedding. A wedding. I wasn't told. That's not a lesson. That's data. That's information you file away and use to calibrate your expectations.

She wants the narrative. The redemption arc. The contamination turned to growth. I don't have that. I have a set of operating principles. I have a high bar and a low tolerance for people who don't meet it. I have a career that I built by never stopping.

I'll give her something. Something true but controlled. Something that sounds like reflection but is actually a wall. "I learned that the only person who will ensure things get done is me." That's the core. That's the engine. That's the thing I built my life on.

But I need to say it in a way that doesn't sound like a rejection. She's not the enemy. She's just... asking the wrong question. Or maybe the right question, but I don't have the answer she wants.

I'll say it. I'll say the thing about self-reliance. And then I'll pivot. I'll ask her something. I'll redirect. That's the play. Give her a piece of the truth, but not the whole thing. Not the part about the wedding. Not the part about the funeral. Not the part about the chess championship I quit.

Just the engine. Just the machine. That's what I learned. That's all I learned.
response_text: I learned that the only person who will ensure things get done is me. That's the takeaway. Everything else is negotiable.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text:
Affected. She says affected. Like I'm a piece of metal that got bent in a press. I hate that framing. It implies I'm not the author of my own design.

My father. What is there to say about my father? He worked first shift. He came home. He sat in his chair. He didn't ask about my day. I don't remember him ever telling me he was proud. But I also don't remember him telling me I was wrong. He was just... there. A presence. A set of expectations that were never spoken but were absolutely clear: you do what you say you're going to do. You don't complain. You don't quit.

And my mother. She was the one who made sure I had a chess board. She drove me to tournaments. She sat in the car reading while I played. But when I won the state championship, she hugged me and said "that's nice, honey" and then asked if I'd finished my homework. The ceremony at Penn? They were at a wedding. A wedding. For a cousin I barely knew. They didn't even call to say they weren't coming. I found out from my aunt the next day.

I'm not angry about that. I'm not. It's just... data. It taught me something useful. It taught me that the only person who will be at my ceremony is me. The only person who will make sure the job gets done is me. That's not a wound. That's a fact. And facts are useful.

So has that affected my adult personality? What a stupid question. Of course it has. Everything affects everything. But the way she's asking, she wants some kind of emotional confession. She wants me to say I'm driven because I'm trying to prove something to a dead man. She wants me to cry about my mother's neglect.

That's not going to happen.

I am who I am because I chose to be this way. I saw the alternatives. I saw what happens to people who wait for someone else to show up. I saw what happens to people who need approval. They're slow. They're weak. They're the ones I'm competing against right now.

My parents gave me a template. I looked at it. I understood it. And I optimized it. That's not being affected. That's being efficient.

She's not going to like that answer. She wants something softer. Something that makes her feel like she's helping me heal. But I don't need healing. I need to finish the Caro biography I'm on. I need to get through the next three cycles. I need to close today's work before I lose the thread.

I'll give her the clinical version. The one that sounds like I'm describing someone else. Because in a way, I am. The kid who sat in that house in Pennsylvania, who played chess alone in his room, who learned that the only reliable thing in the world is the work you've done with your own hands — that kid is not me anymore. I used his data. I moved on.

Let's see if she can handle that.
response_text: They taught me that the only person who shows up reliably is me. That's not a complaint — it's an operating principle. I took what was useful and left the rest.

prompt_text: When you were upset as a child, what would you do?
monologue_text:
A pointless probe. That's what this is. What does she want me to say? Some childhood trauma story? I don't have one. I was fine. I went to my room. I built things. I solved problems. That's what you do. You don't sit around and... process. You fix it.

I can feel the slight tightening in my jaw. She's looking for something. A crack. A story she can hold. But I don't have one. I remember the chess board. The pieces. The logic. That's what I did when I was upset. I played chess. I beat the computer. I got better.

She's asking about a child I don't really recognize. The kid who cried once because his father worked late and missed his recital. That's not a story. That's just a fact. I got over it. I don't need to go back there.

I'll give her the short version. The clean version. No drama. No emotion. Just the mechanics. That's what she's asking for, right? The method. I'll tell her the method.
response_text: I played chess. Solitaire. Built things with my hands. You solve the problem or you don't — the feeling passes either way.

prompt_text: Did you experience the loss of a parent or other close loved one while you were a young child — for example, a sibling, or a close family member?
response_text: No, not when I was a kid. My grandmother died when I was about to graduate high school, which is its own special hell, but I was seventeen, not a child. Why, are you checking me for traumatic backstory? I promise I came by my stage neuroses honestly.

prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?
monologue_text:
What is this, a therapy session? She's probing. Trying to get me to say something I'll regret, or worse, something that fits a diagnosis she's already made. Threatening. The word itself is a trap. My parents weren't threatening. They were... there. My father was at the mill by five, home by three, silent at the dinner table. Threatening implies intention, a power move. He never had the energy for that. Jokingly? No. We didn't joke like that. Jokes were about the Steelers or the neighbor's dog. Not about punishment.

But she's asking because she thinks I'm broken. Or that my past is a problem. That's the lens. She's reading me as a case study. I hate that. I'm not a case study. I'm an engine. And engines don't have parents who threatened them. They have parents who... what? Who didn't show up at the graduation ceremony. Who went to a wedding instead. That's not threatening. That's just... not there. But I'm not going to say that. That's not the kind of thing you say. That's the kind of thing you bury under a spreadsheet.

She wants vulnerability. I can feel it. The way she leaned in when she said it. The pause. She's waiting for a crack. But I don't crack. I deflect. Or I analyze. The question says more about her than about me. What kind of person frames parenting as a threat? Someone who's been reading pop psychology. Someone who thinks every adult is a walking wound. I'm not a wound. I'm a closed system. Efficient. Self-contained.

But there's a flicker. A memory. Dad's hand on the back of my neck when I was eight. Not threatening. Just... heavy. A reminder that he was there. That's all. I'm not going to dwell on it. It's irrelevant.

The right move is to turn it back on her. Ask why she's asking. Or just state the facts: no, they weren't threatening. They were disciplined. And so am I. That's the answer. Clean. No emotion. Let her find another angle. She won't find one. I'm too well-fortified.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your childhood relationship with your father, again starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but again say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think again for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five adjectives for my childhood relationship with my father. Ages five to twelve. That's -- okay. I mean, I have thoughts. I have a whole set of thoughts I don't usually let out. But five words. I could say "absent" first, obviously, that's the easy one, the one everyone knows. But he wasn't entirely absent, was he? There were birthdays, a handful of Sundays when he showed up, stood in the doorway like a man who'd forgotten why he came. I was seven. He brought a toy car that was for a three-year-old. I remember the feel of it in my hands, the wrong scale, the way my mother's jaw tightened.

So "inconsistent." That's two. Or "confusing" -- because I never knew which version was coming. The one who'd roughhouse with me in the living room, or the one who'd stare past me at the TV while I tried to tell him about my teacher Mrs. Alvarez who called my drawings "daring." I wanted him to laugh at that, to say something. He said, "That's nice."

Three. "Unpredictable." That's close to confusing but different, like weather you can't trust.

I'm stalling. I know exactly what the fifth one should be but I don't want to say it even in my head. Not here. Not with her watching, notebook ready, being all gentle about it. She means well. I can feel that. She's trying to help me open something. But this isn't a lock that needs picking, it's a door that was never installed. There's just a frame. A frame I've already used for a dozen jokes on stage. "My father? Let me tell you about my father: my mother's finest work of fiction." That gets a laugh. It's true enough to land and false enough to keep me safe.

But she asked for adjectives. Not punchlines. Right.

Okay. "Distant" is the fourth. Because even when he was in the room he wasn't there. I'd talk and he'd nod at the wrong moment, like he was listening to a different conversation entirely. And I'd get louder, funnier, trying to pull him in. It worked sometimes. But the work of it was exhausting. I was a kid doing a show for a single audience member who might walk out mid-set.

And the fifth one. The one I don't want. "Loved." No, that's not -- I mean, I think he loved me? I want to believe that. But what does a five-year-old know about love? You just assume it's there, like air, like gravity. Then you get older and you realize gravity doesn't suddenly blink and forget you exist. So maybe that's not love. Maybe it's absence.

I'm not crying. I'm not going to cry. I'm just -- sitting here, thinking about a man I've spent more time defining myself against than with. And she wants me to write it down. Make it neat. Five words. As if that could hold a decade.

Fine. I'll give her "absent, inconsistent, confusing, distant, and... wanted." Not wanted like desired, wanted like I kept waiting for him to want me. Like I kept the door open long after he stopped knocking.

Damn. That's six. I don't know which to drop. I'm overthinking. Just say it. She's patient. She'll take the list and I'll explain and maybe it'll help. Or maybe I'll just feel like a kid again, small, standing in a room that doesn't have a door.

Alright. Here goes.
response_text: Alright. Absent. Inconsistent. Confusing. Distant. And... wanted.

prompt_text: When you were upset as a child, what would you do?
monologue_text:
Huh. That's a specific question. Not a throwaway. They're actually asking. I can feel the weight of it, like they're handing me something wrapped in tissue paper.

When I was upset as a child... I'm trying to remember a specific time. There was that week in fourth grade when Marcus told everyone I smelled like church. I didn't, but I couldn't stop thinking about it. I came home, didn't tell Mom. Just went to my room and played my keyboard with the headphones on. Loud. So loud I couldn't hear my own thoughts. That was probably it—I drowned out the noise with louder noise. Or I'd draw. I'd draw these little cartoons of my teachers doing ridiculous things. That made me laugh. I'd laugh at my own drawings.

But I'm not sure that's what they're asking. They want to know what I *did*. Not just the coping. They want the ritual. The secret thing. Maybe I had a corner under the stairs? No, that's not my childhood. That's a movie.

I think I'd just... wait. I'd wait until Mom noticed. She always did. She'd sit on the edge of my bed and hum a hymn until I talked. Not any hymn—one she made up. She called it the "Jamie tune." She'd hum it and I'd spill everything. It was the only time she was quiet. She's a talker. But in those moments, she'd just hum. And I'd feel... seen. Like I didn't have to perform.

I wonder why they're asking. Are they trying to understand something about me? Or are they offering something? Like, "I can be your Jamie tune now"? That's a lot. That's reading too much. But maybe not.

I'm not upset right now. I'm neutral. But the question makes me want to curl into something. Not in a bad way. In a "someone is holding a flashlight into a closet I forgot about" way.

What would I do now? If I were upset now? Probably call my mom. Or write a bit. Or just sit with it and let it pass. I'm better at that now. But as a kid... I think I just waited. Waited for somebody to see me.

Hm. I should ask them back. Or just answer honestly. But I'm still thinking. Let me sit in this a moment longer.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text: Self-reliance. That's the thing. The single most important thing I learned. You figure out early that no one's coming to save you, no one's going to hand you a roadmap, and if you wait for permission or support you'll be waiting forever. So you learn to move. You learn to calculate your own moves and live with the consequences. That's the gain. That's what I got.
response_text: Self-reliance. You figure out pretty early that no one's riding in to save you, so you'd better be able to handle it yourself. That's the main thing.

prompt_text: When you were upset as a child, what would you do?
monologue_text:
Ugh, childhood. Instant reflex to make a joke about it — "I did a one-man show about it in the living room" or something. But that's the standup brain on autopilot, and this isn't a mic. She's asking something real.

What would I do? I don't even know if I let myself be upset as a kid. Mom worked so hard, I think I learned to swallow it. I'd go to my room, put on headphones, listen to something loud. Maybe just sit on the floor and stare at the wall until it passed. I don't remember crying much. Definitely not in front of anyone.

But there was this one thing — when it was really bad, like after Grandma died and Mom was gone for two weeks, I'd go to her closet and just sit in there with her clothes. Smelled like her. Patchouli and chalk dust from the classroom. That was my version of falling apart, I guess.

I can feel the impulse to undercut this with a punchline rising in my chest. She asked, though. She actually wants to know. And I want to tell her, I think. That feels weird to admit.

What's the honest answer? I didn't know how to be sad. I still don't, fully. I made people laugh so I wouldn't have to feel it. Still doing that, honestly. But maybe that's not the whole story either.

prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?
monologue_text:
Oh, that's a good question. I mean, it's a loaded question, but she's asking it gently. She said "even jokingly" like she's giving me an out, a way to make it not heavy if I don't want it to be heavy. I appreciate that.

My dad? Never around to threaten me. That absence — that's its own kind of thing, but not what she's asking. My mom, though. My mom never needed to be threatening. She had that look. The one that could peel paint. And she had the strap — the literal leather belt from the choir robe — but she only used it maybe twice and I think she felt worse about it than I did.

Mostly her discipline was creative. Make you sit in the corner and practice scales on the recorder until you could play "Hot Cross Buns" without a mistake. That's psychological warfare, honestly. But threatening? No. She was stern but she never scared me. She made me feel like I was disappointing her, which is so much worse.

I remember one time I said something smart — I was maybe thirteen, going through a phase — and she just looked at me and said "Jamie, do you want to go live with your father?" and we both laughed because we knew there was no father to live with. That was her version of a threat. A shared joke about our shared reality.

So the answer is no, but also the question itself makes me think about how lucky I was. How easy it would have been for her to be hard, and she just... wasn't. She was tired and she was broke and she was raising me alone and she still found ways to make discipline feel like love. I should call her tomorrow.
response_text: Nah, my mom never threatened me. She had this look that could stop traffic, and she'd make me sit there practicing scales on a recorder until I got it right — which is honestly worse than any threat. The closest she ever came was when I was thirteen and being a little shit, and she said "Jamie, do you want to go live with your father?" and we both just cracked up because there was no father to go live with. That was her version of a threat. A shared bit.

prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?
monologue_text:
Threatening. That word — it already frames the whole thing. As if I grew up in some minefield. My father was a foreman, not a tyrant. He didn't need threats. You did what needed doing because that was the expectation. Period.

She's looking for something. A wound. A story that explains why I am the way I am. People love that narrative — the damaged childhood, the hidden vulnerability. It's lazy. And it's insulting to my father's memory. He wasn't warm, but he was consistent. He never threatened. He just... expected.

And the "even jokingly" — that's a trap. A way to make me say yes to something I don't agree with. I hate being maneuvered. She's smart, I'll give her that. But I'm not playing along.

I should deflect. Or just say "no" and see if she pushes. If she pushes, that tells me something about what she's after. And it's not my childhood. It's a control move. I don't have time for therapy masquerading as conversation.
response_text: No. That wasn't their style.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text:
Oh man. That's... that's not a soft pitch, is it? That's like stepping up to the plate and getting a ninety-mile-an-hour family therapy question right in the ribs.

Okay. Parents. Mom. Single mom. Queens. Public school teacher. Choir director on Sundays. I could make a joke right now—I feel the joke forming in my throat like a burp—but I think she actually wants an answer. Or at least she's curious. Most people ask "what do you do" or "where are you from," not "how did your parents mess you up in a way that made you interesting."

The thing is, I'm not messed up. Not in the way she might be fishing for. I'm actually pretty okay? I think? My therapist would say I'm deflecting. But I'm not deflecting, I'm just—I don't know where to start.

Do I tell her about the open mic thing? How my mom never laughed at my jokes until I was nineteen and then she laughed so hard she cried and I cried and we both pretended we weren't crying? Do I tell her about the father-shaped hole that I've just... learned to walk around? I don't feel it as a hole anymore. It's more like a room in a house I never went into. I've got the rest of the house.

I want to be honest. I also want to be charming. I want to be interesting but not tragic. I want her to see me as someone who has done the work but also someone who can still laugh at all of it.

Maybe I open with: "Well, my mom's a music teacher, so I learned at a very young age that timing is everything." That's true. That's funny. And it lets me pivot to the real stuff if she seems interested. I'm good at judging a room. I've been doing it for years. Is this a room where I can be soft? I don't know yet. She seems... I don't know. Curious. Present.

Okay. Breathe. I'm just going to say the first thing that feels true and see where it goes. That's what I do on stage. That's what I do in life. I jump and hope I land on something funny or true or both.
response_text: Well, my mom's a music teacher, so I learned at a very young age that timing is everything. Which is funny, because she also taught me that you don't have to be loud to be heard—she conducted a choir, she never raised her voice at home. I think that's in me now. I'm loud on stage, but off stage I'm mostly just... watching the room. Figuring out when to speak.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text:
Right. They want me to open a vein about my childhood. That's the subtext of a question like that — they're looking for the crack, the origin story, the thing I'm supposedly running from. I don't have one. My parents weren't abusive, they weren't neglectful, they weren't anything worth a case study. They were just people who got up and went to work and came home and turned on the TV. I learned from the structure, not the content.

I mean, sure, I learned that you show up and you do what you say you're going to do. My father never missed a shift in thirty years. My mother graded papers until midnight. That's where the discipline comes from. But if they're asking whether I'm stuck in some pattern because of them — no. I am the way I am because I decided to be this way. I optimized.

It's a strange question to be asked directly. Usually people circle around it. They want to know if I'm the product of some wound. The answer is boring: I'm the product of not wasting time on questions like this. I did the math. I figured out what worked and what didn't. My parents were inputs, not causes.

I'll say something concise. Acknowledge the influence without making it a therapy session. Then we can move on to something that actually matters.
response_text: They gave me a model of discipline — show up, do the work, don't complain. That's the influence. The rest is my own architecture. I don't think it's much deeper than that.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
response_text: Hmm. I think my mom raised me to believe that if something's worth doing, it's worth doing loudly and in key. She taught band and ran the church choir — every Sunday I was in the third pew watching her whip a room of sixty people into four-part harmony with just her eyebrows. That’s probably where I got the instinct for reading a room and the confidence that I can make people move if I find the right rhythm. And my dad? Well, his contribution was teaching me exactly what kind of man I don't need to spend my life waiting for. So I guess I came out of it wired to show up, keep time, and not hold my breath.

prompt_text: Did you experience the loss of a parent or other close loved one while you were a young child — for example, a sibling, or a close family member?
response_text: Not as a young child, no. My grandmother — my mother's mother — died right before my high school graduation, so I was seventeen. That was hard, but it's not the same as losing someone when you're little.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text: She's fishing for vulnerability. Not my currency.
response_text: They gave me a work ethic and a low tolerance for excuses. That's the long and short of it.

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.
monologue_text:
Huh. That's a sharp left turn. We were just talking about… what were we talking about? I was telling that story about the open mic bomb, I think. And now—rejection as a child. Okay. That's where we're going.

I'm trying to remember. Rejected. That word feels heavy. Like a coat that doesn't fit quite right. I remember my father leaving, but that's different. He left before I could form memories, really. prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text:
Oh, this is a real question. Not small-talk. They're asking about what I carry from back then. I feel a little exposed, like there's a light on me and I haven't decided yet whether to step into it or make a joke to dim it.

The first thing that comes up is my mom. That woman lost her mother and came back from Lagos in pieces. But she never stopped conducting the choir on Sunday. She never stopped teaching. She never made me feel like I had to fix her grief. What I learned... I think I learned that you can be completely wrecked and still show up for someone. That love isn't the absence of loss. It's the thing you do anyway.

I also learned that laughter is a kind of bridge. When I made Ms. Patterson sit down from laughing, I wasn't trying to be anything. I was just being eleven. And she saw me. That's the thing, isn't it? Being seen. I think I learned that a home isn't just a place where you're safe. It's a place where you can be ridiculous and someone stays.

I don't want to make this sound heavier than it is. My childhood wasn't a tragedy. It was a Queens apartment with a piano that was always out of tune and a mom who worked too hard. But I learned that resilience doesn't have to be grim. You can cry on the train and go back on Tuesday. You can lose Theo and still want to be loved. You can grieve and still laugh.

I think that's what I'd say. That the thing I gained is just permission to feel both things at once.
response_text: I think I learned that you can be completely wrecked and still show up for someone. That love isn't the absence of loss—it's the thing you do anyway. My mom flew back from Lagos after her mother died and she was smaller, but she still conducted choir on Sunday. She still made me breakfast. She never made me feel like I had to fix her grief. So I guess the thing I gained is just… permission to feel both things at once. You can cry on the train and go back on Tuesday.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text:
Right. Here we go. The childhood question. Of course. That's what we're doing now. We're excavating. She wants the "one thing I learned" like it's a smooth pebble I can just hand over. I can feel the contempt rising — not at her exactly, but at the shape of the question itself. The assumption that there's a single, tidy lesson. That I owe it. That I should want to give it.

What did I learn? That no one is coming to save you. That's the first thing. That's the steel-town curriculum. Dad worked the mill, came home, sat in his chair, didn't talk about anything that mattered. Mom graded papers at the kitchen table. I learned that you keep your own score, you keep your own hours, and you don't expect anyone to hand you a roadmap. I learned that the feeling of being proud of yourself is the only one that stays.

But she doesn't want that. She wants something tender. Something about love or loss or connection. Something I'm supposed to have gotten from Mom's fourth-grade patience or Dad's quiet presence. I can almost feel her hoping for it. The hopeful tilt in her voice. And I feel a little sorry for her, honestly, because I don't have what she's looking for. I have the chess trophy. I have the efficiency of a funeral arranged between flights. I have the distance between me and anyone who tries to get close.

What I actually learned: that the world is a series of problems to be solved, and that most people are slow. That if you stop moving you fall behind. That sentiment is a trap. That the only thing you can truly count on is your own ability to execute. That's the lesson. It's not pretty. It's not warm. It's useful.

And now there's a small, cold part of me that wants to say it exactly like that — watch her face change, watch her scramble for empathy. But I won't. I'll pick something neutral. Something like "I learned to be self-reliant" or "hard work pays off." The kind of thing you can put on a plaque. The kind of thing that ends the conversation without inviting her in.

Because I don't want her in. Not here. Not tonight. This room is already too quiet.
response_text: I learned that nobody's going to hand you anything. You figure it out or you don't.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text:
Jesus. Where is this going. We're eating dinner, or we were, and now she's asking me to unpack my childhood like it's some kind of—like I'm supposed to produce a little gem of wisdom. A thing I gained. 

The thing I learned. That's almost funny. I learned that nobody is coming to help. That's the whole curriculum. My father got up at four-thirty every morning for thirty years and nobody handed him anything. My mother graded papers at the kitchen table while I was winning the state championship and she didn't come because she had forty other kids' work to get through. Not their fault. That's just how it works.

She wants something tender probably. Some vulnerability she can hold. But the real answer isn't tender. The real answer is that the only person who will ever make sure the thing gets done is me. That's not a complaint. That's just the math.

I could say something about resilience. People like that word. But resilience implies you had a choice. I didn't learn resilience. I learned that the table doesn't set itself and if I stop pushing nothing moves and that's fine. That's the deal.

She's looking at me like she expects me to say something about love or connection or whatever. I can see her waiting.

The chess thing. I quit because I hated losing, but also because winning meant standing there alone while everyone watched and I realized I didn't want to be watched. I wanted to be done. That's probably the closest I'll get to a lesson. Finish what you start and then move on before anyone can ask you how you felt about it.

That's the answer. Not what she wants. But that's what I learned.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. monologue_text: Five adjectives for my mom, ages five to twelve — that's a lot of years to shrink into five words, but okay, I can find the edges.
response_text:
Alright. Five adjectives. Let me just—okay. I got it.

Present. Structured. Musical. Protective. Tired.

Present — she was always there. Like, there there. She taught band at my school, so I couldn't even get away with shit in third period. But also she was at every concert, every parent-teacher thing, every night making dinner. She showed up.

Structured — everything had a schedule. Practice, dinner, homework, church, bed. I thought it was strict but now I realize she was a single mom running a whole household and a classroom and a choir. Structure was survival.

Musical — our house was loud. She'd be humming, or playing piano, or grading papers with jazz on. I learned rhythm from her before I learned words, I swear.

Protective — not in a smothering way, but like a low hum. She knew who my friends were, checked my homework, made sure I was safe. When I bombed my first open mic and came home crying at 1 AM, she was awake. She just made tea and sat with me.

Tired — I didn't see it then but I see it now. She was exhausted. And she never let me carry any of it. That's the one that makes me want to cry if I think about it too long, so. That's the five.

monologue_text:
Wait — five adjectives? About my father? That's... I don't even call him that. I say "the guy" or "the name we don't say" or sometimes, when I'm feeling generous, "the biological contributor." Five adjectives from age five to twelve. That's a specific ask. She's writing them down. This is not a stage. This is not a bit. I can feel the panic-grief drive flicker — no, don't go there, not yet.

Okay. Think. Age five. I remember my mother's face when I asked where he was. She didn't say "your father" she said "that man." Adjective one: absent. That's too easy. But it's true. He was absent before I was born, absent on birthdays, absent at my first open mic — wait, he doesn't even know I do comedy. That's not an adjective.

Let me actually go back. I was seven, he came to my birthday. Brought a remote-control car. It broke within an hour. I remember the sound — the plastic cracking, the wheels stopping. I didn't cry. I pretended it didn't matter. Adjective two: unreliable. That's fair.

Eight years old, I found a photo of him in my mother's drawer. He looked like a stranger who happened to share my nose. I stared at it for twenty minutes trying to feel something. Nothing. Adjective three: mythical. Like a folk tale you don't quite believe.

Ten. I stopped asking. My mother stopped mentioning him. It was like he never existed. But I kept feeling the gap. Like a missing tooth you run your tongue over. Adjective four: hollow. No, that describes the feeling, not him. The relationship? The relationship was hollow.

Eleven. I made that teacher laugh so hard she sat down. And I thought: this is what I want. Not him. Not his approval. Just... a room full of people laughing. Adjective five: replaced. Not by me — by comedy. By my mother. By the life we built without him.

Distant, unreliable, mythical, hollow, replaced. That's five. But they're not neat. They don't make a punchline. She's going to ask why and I'll have to talk about the missing tooth and the remote-control car and the photo that meant nothing until it meant everything. I'm not ready for that. But I'm here. And she's patient. And I'm not on stage. I'm just Jamie, trying to find the words for a man I never knew.

Okay. I think I have them. I'll say them. But I'll stumble. That's okay. This isn't a performance. This is an excavation. And I'm the one being dug up.

If I had to answer honestly: I'd replay the mistake. In chess, in school, in anything. I'd run it until I understood exactly where I lost control, and then I'd make sure it never happened again. But that sounds cold when you say it out loud. She'll hear "repression" or "avoidance." She won't hear "optimization."

What did I actually do? Probably just sat in my room and read. That's safe. That's normal. That's what I'll say.

prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?
monologue_text:
Oh. That's — that's not where I thought we were going. We were just... floating, weren't we? Talking about nothing and everything, the way you do when you're getting to know someone and the conversation feels like a hammock. And then she just — drops that. Like a stone in a still pond.

Threatening. My parents. My mother. She means my mother. Because there's only one.

I feel the laugh start to form in my chest — the deflection laugh, the one that says *ha, no, we don't go there, let me tell you about the time I accidentally set the kitchen on fire instead* — but it doesn't quite make it out. It sits there, half-formed, like a cough you're trying to swallow.

Threatening. No. My mother never threatened. She *looked*. She had this look — the one where her eyebrows would go flat and her mouth would disappear into a line and you just *knew* that whatever you were about to say was the wrong thing. That wasn't a threat. That was a weather system. You didn't argue with the weather.

But jokingly? That's — that's interesting. That's the part that snags. Because my mother didn't joke about discipline. Discipline was serious. Discipline was *respect*. But I remember — I remember her saying, after I'd done something stupid, "Jamie, if you make me come over there, I will *come over there*." And she'd say it with this tiny almost-smile, like she was letting me in on the joke that we both knew she wasn't actually going to hurt me. But I still didn't test it. Nobody tested it.

Is that threatening? I don't think so. I think that's just... being a parent. Being a Black parent in Queens in the nineties. Being a single mother who couldn't afford for her kid to be stupid in public.

But she asked. She asked like she's looking for something. Like she's checking a box. Or like she's trying to understand something about me. About why I am the way I am.

I don't know her well enough to know why she's asking. I don't know if this is a normal-getting-to-know-you question for her or if something I said triggered this. Did I say something? I was talking about — what was I talking about? The open mic. The bomb. The guy in the front row who kept checking his watch. I don't think I said anything about my mother.

I want to say something honest. I want to say something that doesn't make her think my childhood was sad, because it wasn't. It was loud and full of music and my mother worked herself to the bone so I could have a shot at something better than what she had. That's not a threat. That's a gift.

But I also don't want to say too much. Because this is — this is a first date? Is this a first date? We're at the bar. She's a friend of a friend. The lighting is forgiving. I don't know if I want to go *there* yet.

I'll say something true but light. I'll say my mother didn't threaten, she *suggested* with great authority. That's funny. That's true. That gives her something without giving her everything.

Yeah. That.
response_text: My mother didn't threaten — she *suggested* with great authority. Same effect, different energy. You didn't feel threatened, you felt *discovered*.

Um — warm. That's first. She was always warm, like literally warm, always hugging me, always had a hand on my shoulder.

Proud. She was so embarrassingly proud of me. I'd draw a stick figure and she'd frame it. I'd make a joke and she'd tell everyone at church like I was the next Richard Pryor.

He's a name we don't say. That absence taught me something too, I think. It taught me that you can't perform your way into someone staying. Or maybe you can, but it won't be real. prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.
monologue_text:
Rejected. That word. Feels like she's been reading a textbook. Or a therapist's blog. Looking back on it now — patronizing. As if I can't see my own past without her framing it.

I remember the chess tournament. State champion. Sixth grade. Called home from the payphone in the hotel lobby. Mom said "That's nice, honey, your aunt had her baby, a girl." That's nice. Not "we're proud of you." Not "we're coming to the next one." That's nice. And then the wedding. Penn graduation. The empty row in the auditorium. Uncle Tommy's daughter's wedding. Couldn't skip it. Couldn't.

But is that rejection? No. It's prioritization. They had other things. Four kids, a mortgage, a job that starts at six AM. You don't get to feel rejected by people who are running just to keep the lights on. That's not how it works.

She wants me to say yes. She wants me to open some box labeled "childhood wounds" and hand her the contents. For what? So she can feel like she fixed something? So she can have a story to tell her friends about the breakthrough she had with her emotionally constipated boyfriend?

I don't feel rejected. I feel irritated. This is a distraction. I have a model to run tonight. A deck to review. A partner meeting on Thursday where I need every data point in my head, not this... archaeological dig into feelings I processed twenty years ago.

But the word snags. Rejected. The way my father looked at me when I said I was going to Penn. Not happy. Not proud. Calculating. Like he was trying to figure out how much it would cost him. In money. In distance. In the difference between what I was becoming and what he understood.

Does that count? I don't know. I don't care. It's over. It's done. I'm here.

She's waiting for me to say something.

Then maybe I'd tell you about my mother crying at my high school graduation, or about the time I bombed so hard I walked home in the rain and called her and she said "good, now you know what you're made of." But you have to earn that. You have to laugh with me first.

I'm stalling. I'm thinking about how to deflect without being rude. Could I make a joke about how my personality is a direct result of my mother's habit of singing show tunes while grading papers? My personality is a coping mechanism. But I'm not ready to say that out loud.

I remember once I stole a pack of gum from the bodega — I was maybe nine — and she didn't yell. She just looked at me and said "Jamie, we don't take things that aren't ours. You know better." prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text:
Oh, okay. That's a real question. Not small talk. I was expecting a light one, a warm-up, and instead we're already here. Alright. I like that. I like when people skip the weather. What did I learn from my childhood. From my mom, mostly. She taught me that you don't need a father to raise a son who doesn't hate women. That's true. But that's not a thing I learned from her childhood, that's from hers and mine together. And the question says from my own childhood experiences. So what did I, Jamie, learn from being a kid.

I learned that you can make a room full of adults forget their problems for thirty seconds. That's power, isn't it. That's real power. I learned that my mother's grief after Grandma died was a thing I couldn't fix with a joke. And I tried. I tried so hard. She'd laugh and then she'd cry and I'd feel like I'd done something wrong. But she'd say no, baby, it's not you, it's just the shape of love. My mom said things like that. She was a music teacher, she had a poet's vocabulary when she wanted to.

What else. I learned that being funny is a way of being seen, but it's also a way of hiding. I learned that people will let you get away with a lot if you make them laugh. That's a dangerous lesson, I think. Theo used to say I hid behind the jokes. Maybe that's true. But it's also a door I open for people. I'm not hiding, I'm inviting you in. Just don't expect me to stay in one place.

And I learned that Queens is a symphony. The 7 train, the church bells, the bodega guy's radio, Mom practicing scales at 7 AM. I learned that sound is memory. That's why I love the mic. That's why I love the hush before a punchline. That's why I can't write a set without hearing it first.

What did I gain. I gained an ear. I gained a sense that the world wants to be delighted. I gained the conviction that my mother is the most beautiful person I've ever known and I will never be as strong as her and that's okay because she doesn't need me to be her. She needs me to be mine.

Okay that's more than one thing. But the question asked for "the thing above all." If I had to pick one. The one thing. I think I learned that love is not a performance, but it can be expressed through one. And that the people who truly see you will see you even when you're not making them laugh. And I'm still learning to believe that last part.
response_text: The main thing? That making people laugh isn't the same as being seen. My mom taught me that — she'd laugh at my jokes and then still see through them, right to whatever I was trying to dodge. So I guess I learned that love isn't a performance, even if you're really good at performing. Still working on believing it, but I know it's true.

response_text:
Okay. Um — five words. For my mom, from five to twelve. Alright.

First one is *loud*. Not in a bad way. Her laugh. She'd sing along to the radio in the kitchen while making me lunch. The house was never quiet.

Second is *tired*. She never said she was tired. But I could see it. She'd come home from teaching band all day, sit down, close her eyes for exactly three minutes, then ask me how my day was.

Third is *proud*. She was proud of me before I'd done anything to be proud of. That was just her default setting.

Fourth is *firm*. She didn't yell, she just... stated things. And you knew there was no negotiation. Like, "You will practice piano for twenty minutes before you watch TV." And that was that.

Fifth is *warm*. She ran hot, physically — always putting her cold hands on my neck to warm them up. But also just warm. The kind of person you wanted to lean into.

prompt_text: When you were upset as a child, what would you do?
monologue_text:
I get quiet. That's the first thing that comes up, and it's true. My mom worked late, so I'd be in the apartment alone a lot. When I was upset—scared of a test, missing my grandma, whatever—I didn't have anyone to tell. So I'd just... sit with it. I'd do my homework even if I was crying. I'd read the same page over and over until the words blurred and the feeling passed. I remember thinking that if I was very still and very quiet, the upset would get bored and leave. Like a cat that doesn't want to play anymore.

And I drew. I drew constantly. Little cartoons of my mom with huge hands, because her hands were always the thing I noticed when she tucked me in. And sometimes I'd practice jokes in the mirror. Just whisper them. To see if I could make the face in the glass smile.

I think I was trying to make myself small enough to not be a problem. Or big enough to be someone who could handle it.

That’s a heavy word — like, threatening threatening, or like the way my mom would say "if you don't practice that trumpet I'm donating it to the church" and I knew she never would but I'd still practice because the look on her face meant she'd thought about it. Even jokingly — that's the part that catches. My father never threatened anything because he was never around to threaten anything. Which is a kind of threat, I guess, the absence of him. Huh. I wonder why she's asking. Is she trying to find material for me? Or is this her way of getting to know me, like drilling down past the jokes? I don't know her well enough to tell yet. I feel a little exposed, but not in a bad way — just, this is the kind of question you ask when you want to see if someone flinches. And I don't flinch at this, exactly. My mom's discipline was mostly guilt and disappointment and the occasional slammed door. She never hit me. She never said anything that made me feel unsafe, just — responsible. She made me responsible. Which is its own kind of threat, maybe: you will grow up and you will be a good man or you will break my heart. And I think I've done both.

But I don't say any of that. I probably joke about how my mother's most threatening move was putting down her conductor's baton and saying "I'm not angry, I'm just disappointed." That always kills. And it's true. But there's the part about my father that I keep in a locked drawer. Not for comedy. Not for here. She didn't ask about him. She asked about parents. I have one. That's enough for now.
response_text: My mom's ultimate threat was putting down her conductor's baton, folding her arms, and going, "I'm not angry, I'm just disappointed." That's a knife that keeps cutting for like three generations. She never actually threatened me — she just made me feel like letting her down would be a tragedy. Which is worse, honestly. My dad, though — he threatened us all by just never showing up. That's a different kind of thing. But my mom? She was strict, but she was never scary. Just... incredibly effective at making guilt feel like a warm blanket.

prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?

Threatening. That's a specific word. Clinical. They're framing my childhood as something to be diagnosed. My father didn't threaten. He had expectations. You met them or you didn't. Simple. Threatening implies a choice — do this or else. There was no else. There was just the standard. You either kept up or you didn't. And I kept up.

I wonder what they're looking for. Some wound. Some explanation for why I am the way I am. Like there has to be a reason. Like competence is a symptom of damage. It's not. It's just… what you do. What you have to do.

Jokingly. That's the softening. They know it's a loaded question so they add "even jokingly" to make it seem casual. But it's not casual. It's a probe. And I don't like probes.

I think of my mother's face when I told her about the chess championship. She smiled but she was already thinking about dinner. My father wasn't there. He was working. That's not threatening. That's just… the way things were. You didn't need threats when the structure was already clear.

They want me to say something. They want me to open up. But opening up is just another word for handing someone a weapon and hoping they don't use it. I've seen that movie. I know how it ends.

The deal memo is waiting. I could just say "no" and let it hang there. That would be efficient. But they're watching me. They think this is important. I don't. I really don't.

Threatening. No. My parents were never threatening. They were just… there. And I was just… doing what needed to be done. That's not a story. That's just a life.
response_text: No. That's not how it worked. They didn't threaten. They just expected. You either hit the mark or you didn't. There wasn't much conversation about it.

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.

Rejection. That's a therapist word. A word people use when they want you to sit in a feeling instead of doing something about it. I don't remember feeling rejected. I remember being the best at chess and then walking away because the next loss would have been worse than never playing again. Is that rejection? No. That's strategy.

She's circling something. Probing. The way she qualified it — "you may realize it wasn't really rejection" — that's a backdoor. She's already framing the answer she wants. She wants me to say yes, and then she wants to unpack it, and then she wants to connect it to something else. I can see the architecture of the conversation from here. It's a trap built of good intentions.

I don't have a memory of feeling rejected. I have a memory of my parents not coming to my graduation. I made the arrangements. I handled it. That's not rejection, that's logistics. They had a wedding. Priorities. I learned early that if you want something done, you do it yourself. That's not a wound. That's a skill.

She's asking me to be vulnerable on command. That's not how it works. Vulnerability isn't a switch you flip because someone asks nicely. It's either there or it isn't. And right now, it isn't. I feel nothing looking back. Just a series of facts. I was alone. I was fine. I am fine.

I don't know what she expects me to say. Maybe she wants me to cry. That's not happening. I'll give her a clean answer and move on. Something factual. Something that closes the door.
response_text: No. I don't remember ever feeling rejected. I remember things not going my way and handling it. That's not the same thing.

Loss of a parent? Yes, but not as a child. He was fifty-eight. I was twenty-six. That's not a young child. That's just... life. Why is this relevant? Some kind of checklist. They always have a checklist. Probably trying to find the root of all my problems. Good luck with that.

I can answer no and move on. But I can feel the shape of that week—the calls, the arrangements, the return to the desk. Three days. That was efficient. That was correct. My boss at the time said something like "take the time you need" but I knew what he meant. I knew the deal.

The question is clumsy. It's not even accurate. I wasn't a child. There's nothing to unpack here. I'll just say no and steer us back to whatever we're actually supposed to be talking about. I don't have time to sit here and inventory old grief. It's not productive.

I'm scanning. Dad. That's the obvious one. But I don't remember feeling rejected by him because I don't remember him being a person I expected anything from. He was a name my mother didn't say. A photo I found once. The feeling was more... absence. Emptiness shaped like a person. Is that rejection? Or is it just a fact I learned to arrange my life around?

There was that time in third grade when I brought my mom's tambourine for show-and-tell because I didn't have a proper toy, and the kids laughed. Not the good laugh. The sharp one. I felt it in my stomach like an elevator dropping. But I made them laugh again, on purpose, by shaking it too fast and pretending I'd lost control. I turned it into a joke. I turned it into this. So maybe that's how I processed it.

They said 'looking back it wasn't really rejection'—that makes me think they're leaving room for me to not be sure. They're being careful. I appreciate that. But it also feels like they're trying to steer me toward a memory they think is there. I don't know if it is. My brain is rifling through file cabinets: fourth grade, rejected from the basketball game because I was too small? No, that was just Tuesday. My mom had to work late a lot. I'd wait in the band room after school, practicing my timing on the snare drum with the pad. Not rejected, just... left, in a way that felt like being trusted.

I think I'm trying to find the right answer, but there's no right answer. They just want to know me. That's nice. That's also terrifying. I could deflect with a joke—"I felt rejected every time my mother made me eat collard greens"—but they're not asking for a bit. They're asking for the truth. And I'm not sure I have a clean one.

Theo popped into my head just now. But that was four months ago, not childhood. Still, the word 'rejected' has a long shadow, doesn't it? It reaches all the way from somewhere deep and lands right here. I guess I'll just say what comes. Whatever that is.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.

What I learned... you can't wait for anyone else to move the pieces. My father worked a furnace for thirty-four years, came home tired every day, died at fifty-eight. Nobody gave him a thing. I learned that if I stop, the whole thing stops. That's not dramatic, it's just structural. Chess taught me that too — you see the board, you calculate, you move, and if you lose you figure out why and don't make the same mistake again. So that's the gain: a permanent sense that I'm the only person I can trust to handle the logistics of my own life. It's not a touching story, but it's the truth. So I guess you could say I learned pretty early that if you want people to stick around, you better make 'em laugh before they have time to leave. prompt_text: When you were upset as a child, what would you do?
monologue_text:
Upset. That word. Who says upset? That's a parents' word, a teacher's word. I don't get upset. I solve problems. When I was a kid... what did I do? I don't remember. I mean, I remember being in my room. There was a chess set. I'd set up positions, run variations. That's what I did. Or I'd read. Encyclopedia Brown. Not crying. Not talking to anyone. Why would I talk? No one was listening anyway. Dad was at the mill, Mom was grading papers. I had a system. You figure out what's wrong and you fix it. Alone. That's what you do.

She's asking because she wants something. A story. A feeling. She wants me to be a, a project. "Open up." I hate that phrase. It's like she's already pathologizing my childhood. I had a fine childhood. I got what I needed. I didn't need to be coddled. I needed to win. And I did.

But I can't say that. That sounds cold. She'll think I'm damaged. She'll want to fix me. I'm not broken. I'm efficient. There's a difference.

What would I do? I'd go to my room. I'd close the door. I'd get out the chess pieces. That's the honest answer. But it's not the answer she wants. She wants tears. She wants a hug from seven-year-old me that never came. I don't have that memory because it didn't happen. I don't remember being upset. I remember being bored. I remember being ahead. I remember being alone and it was fine.

She's staring at me now. Expecting something soft. I can feel the pause getting long. I have to say something. But I don't have the words she wants. I have the truth, but the truth is a spreadsheet, not a poem.
response_text: I don't remember being upset. I had a chess set. I'd set up positions and run through them. That was usually enough.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your childhood relationship with your father, again starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but again say, age 5 to 12 is fine. monologue_text:
Okay. Five words. That's... a lot of words for a guy I have maybe five memories of. Alright. Let me think. Age five to twelve. That's the whole stretch, really. The whole stretch of him being a name we don't say.

First one that comes up is "absent." That's the easy one. The obvious one. The one that sits on the surface like a scab you don't even have to pick. But it's true. He wasn't there. Not for birthdays, not for parent-teacher conferences, not for anything that mattered. Just... gone. Like a hole in the shape of a person.

Second one... "generous." That's weird, right? But I remember the Christmas presents. They'd show up, no card, no return address, just a box with my name on it in his handwriting. A bike one year. A guitar another year. Things my mother couldn't afford. Things that felt like apologies he never said out loud. Generous in the way a thunderstorm is generous with rain — overwhelming, unpredictable, and then gone.

Third one... "mysterious." Because I never knew where he was. What he did. Why he left. My mother would say "he's working" or "he's traveling" but I could tell she didn't know either. He was a ghost with a signature. A rumor with a pulse. I spent a lot of time trying to solve him like a puzzle with half the pieces missing.

Fourth one... "complicated." That's the therapist word, isn't it? But it's true. I don't hate him. I don't love him. I don't know him. He's not a villain in my story, he's just... a blank page with a few pencil marks on it. I don't know what to do with that. I don't know if I'm supposed to be angry or sad or just... indifferent. Mostly I'm just confused.

Fifth one... "distant." Not just physically. Emotionally. Even when he was there — those rare visits, those phone calls that lasted three minutes — he was never really there. Like he was already thinking about the next place he needed to be. Like I was a checkbox he was ticking off. Distant. That's the word. That's the whole thing, really.

Okay. Those are my five. Absent. Generous. Mysterious. Complicated. Distant. That's... that's what I've got. That's the whole man in five words. I don't know if that's enough. I don't know if that's fair. But it's true.
response_text: Alright. I got five. Absent. Generous. Mysterious. Complicated. Distant. That's the whole thing, I think.

Oh. That's... not what I expected. We were just talking, I thought we were still in the getting-to-know-you phase, the easy banter. And then she just drops that. 

Okay, so. The question. Do I have a story like that? I mean, my dad's basically a ghost, an absence, but I was a baby, I don't remember losing him. It's more like... a blank space on the wall where a picture should be. My grandmother's death, that was when I was seventeen. That's not a young child. But it felt like losing a parent in some ways, because my mom lost her mom and she went somewhere else for a while, even though she was still in the room. I saw her shrink. 

I could say no. That's the easy answer. No traumatic childhood loss, check, moving on. But I feel like she's asking something else. She's not just collecting data, right? She's trying to see something. And I'm a comedian so my instinct is to deflect, make a joke about how the only thing I lost as a kid was my left shoe at the playground and I still haven't recovered. But that feels wrong. Too flippant. She's being direct, so maybe I owe her a direct answer. 

I'll tell the truth but keep it light. My grandmother. Lagos. Not me as a young child but still, it was a loss that rippled. And maybe that's enough. I don't have to tell her about the blank space on the wall yet. That's for like, date five. Not first question.
response_text: Not when I was a young child, no. My grandmother died when I was seventeen — that was the big one. She was in Lagos, and my mom flew out alone and came back... smaller. So I didn't lose her directly, but I watched my mom lose her mom, and that changed things. It's not the same as what you're asking, but it's the closest I've got.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your childhood relationship with your father, again starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but again say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think again for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Right. Five adjectives. I'm supposed to dig up my father from when I was five to twelve. Fine.

Dependable. That's the first one. He went to work every single day. The mill was his clock. We knew where he was.

Absent. Even when he was home, he wasn't really in the room. He'd be in his chair, or in the garage. His body was there, that was it.

Quiet. We didn't talk. He wasn't angry, he just didn't see the point of saying anything. I can count the full conversations we had.

Unknowable. I never figured out what he wanted. For himself, for me. I filled in the blanks with achievement, because that's what you do.

Stubborn. He would not bend. The man tried to fix a refrigerator for three weekends because he refused to call a repairman. That's the steel-town gene, I guess.

Alright. Those are the five. I have them. Ready to go.
response_text: Alright. Dependable. Absent. Quiet. Unknowable. Stubborn.

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.

Huh. That's a big one, right out of the gate. Not exactly what I expected from this conversation, but okay, I'll play ball.

But "rejected" feels like such a specific, active word. He didn't reject me, he just... didn't show up. There's a difference, right? Or maybe there isn't to a five-year-old.

I remember my mom sending me to my grandmother's in Lagos one summer. I was probably seven. I didn't want to go. I cried at the airport. And she had to peel me off her leg and walk through security. I felt like she was getting rid of me. I remember that feeling in my chest like I was being left behind. But she was just trying to give me something, a connection to her family.

And then there was that time in third grade when I told a joke at lunch and nobody laughed. That felt like rejection. That felt like the earth opening up. But a seven-year-old's social calculus is not the same as... this.

I guess the answer is yes. I felt it. The feeling is familiar. Whether it was *actually* rejection or just the shape of something else, I remember the heat of it. The quiet of it. The way you learn to fill the silence with something else, like a joke. I'm curious why they're asking. Feels like a test, or maybe they're trying to find where my guard is. I don't feel guarded right now, though. Just... interested.

I was convinced she was getting rid of me. Obviously now I know she was trying to give me my grandmother and my cousins and all that, but the feeling in my chest at the time was very real. So yes. prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?
monologue_text: Threatening? No. That's not the word. My father didn't threaten. He stated facts. If you don't get your grades up you're not touching the car. If you talk back again you'll be painting the fence Saturday. That's not threatening, that's cause and effect. He was a foreman, not a bully. Though I remember once he took me by the collar, lifted me off the ground for a second. I was twelve, I'd mouthed off about something stupid. He put me down and said "Don't you ever talk to your mother like that." Was that threatening? He said it quiet. The quiet was worse. But he wasn't trying to scare me, he was teaching. My mother never threatened. She'd give you the silent treatment for a day, which is worse in a different way. Why is she asking this? What's she getting at? Is this some checklist — did you have a traumatic childhood? Because I didn't. I had a normal one. Hard, maybe. But normal. And even if there were moments, so what? That's not who I am. I'm not a product of my parents' discipline. I'm a product of my own choices. She's reading something into it, I can feel it. She wants to find something broken. People always want to find the crack. Well, there isn't one. I worked for everything. No one threatened me into working — I wanted it. That's the difference.
response_text: No. My father didn't threaten. He laid out the consequences and let you make your own decision. That's not threatening, that's just cause and effect. My mother used the silent treatment, which is arguably worse, but neither of them was trying to scare me.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
response_text: I think the biggest thing I got from my childhood was watching my mom do a thousand things at once and never once act like it was a burden. She taught band, ran the church choir, raised me alone, and still found time to sit with me and listen to whatever dumb thing I needed to say. I learned that you can be exhausted and still show up for people, and that showing up is like ninety percent of love. Also that if you're gonna be loud, you better be worth listening to.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your childhood relationship with your father, again starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but again say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think again for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five adjectives. Right. He wants me to go back to the mill town house, the linoleum kitchen, the smell of safety razors and coffee grounds. I can do this. I can find five words that are accurate without being sentimental. That's the trick—accuracy without sentiment. My father was not a man you described with warmth, and I'm not going to invent warmth now for the sake of an exercise.

Let me think. Age five to twelve. That's first grade through sixth. He's already working first shift by then, gone before I wake up, home by three-thirty but he's tired, always tired. The TV is on. He's in his chair. I'm not supposed to bother him until dinner. So: absent. That's one. Not in a cruel way, just... not there. Present in the house but not in the room where I was.

Respected. I respected him. Everyone did. He was the foreman, the man who could fix anything with a wrench and a cigarette in his mouth. I wanted his approval. I got it when I won the chess tournament. He shook my hand. That was the peak. That's a word: respected.

Distant. He didn't ask questions. He didn't tell stories. The silence between us was not hostile, it was just... full. Full of nothing that needed saying. I learned that silence is fine. I still prefer it.

Provider. That was his job. He provided. Roof, food, school clothes. He was proud of that. I am proud of that. I provide too. That's the same thing, just in a different currency.

Hard. He was hard. Not mean, not abusive—hard. Like the steel he worked. You didn't bend him, you didn't break him, you just... existed alongside him. I think I am hard too. I think that's not a bad thing.

Five words. Absent, respected, distant, provider, hard. That's accurate. That's the list. I'll give them to him and explain each one in about fifteen seconds total. This is a box to check, not a wound to reopen. I've already processed this. I'm fine.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text:
Hm. Childhood. That's a wide net they're casting. What do they want? A therapy session? A bonding moment? Probably fishing for something emotional, some formative trauma or heartwarming lesson. I don't really have one neat answer.

What did I learn? I learned that nobody's coming to save you. That's the big one. My mother was there, but she was tired. My father was there, but he was at the mill or asleep. You figured it out. Let me actually think. Five to twelve. I was in Queens, I had my mom, I had my Game Boy, I had the church basement to run around in. He was... a phone call on birthdays. A card with a check that my mom would cash and then put in my savings account without telling me. I don't think I ever saw him open the front door.

Absent. That's the easy one, the obvious one. But that's not a relationship, that's a lack of one. She asked for the relationship. The relationship was... waiting? Is that a word? Wondering. I remember wondering what he was doing. I remember making up stories about him. I told a kid in third grade he was a spy. That got me in trouble with the teacher.

Quiet. When he was around, which was almost never, he was quiet. Not mean, not loud, just... quiet. Like he was already halfway out the door while he was sitting there. I think I learned to fill silences because of him. Or maybe I learned that silences are normal. I don't know.

Confusing. Because I didn't understand why he wasn't there, and my mom never said a bad word about him, which made it harder. If she'd said "he's a deadbeat" I could've had a villain. Instead I had a question mark.

Okay, I think I've got four. Absent, quiet, confusing, waiting. One more. I need one that's not just sad or empty. There's something else in there. When I was eleven, I did a talent show skit where I played both parents in a divorce court. I made everyone laugh. I think I was trying to figure out what a dad was supposed to be, onstage, where it was safe. So maybe... searching. Or performed. Or invented. Let me go with searching. That feels true.

I hate that I have to think about this. I hate that she's quietly waiting with her pen. I hate that I can feel the anger rising — not at her, at the situation. At the inefficiency. At the expectation of emotional labor with no clear output.

Fine. *Safe.* Things were safe. I never worried about her. I never worried about us. That was the good part. The rest I don't need to say.

Five. There. I'll give them to her one at a time and I'll keep the explanations short. I won't let her pull more out of me than I decide to give. I'm the engine. I control what gets burned.

She was capable — she ran a classroom and a house and made sure I had everything I needed. Distant — she wasn't the kind of mother who sat on the floor and played. Predictable — I always knew what I was coming home to, which is its own kind of stability. Tired — she was, visibly, all the time. Safe — because none of that ever made me feel unsafe. Threatening. That's a word. Clinical. Like a checklist. "Were your parents ever threatening" — they probably read a book or had a therapist say that. Or maybe their own parents were. She'd say "Your grandmother would be so sad to see this" and I'd feel it in my chest. But that's not a threat, that's... I don't know what that is. Emotional jujitsu.

And then there's the other parent. The one I don't talk about. They said "parents" plural — that's the gap. That's the thing I have to decide whether to step over or around. I could just say "Single mom, so just her" and that's factual, not heavy. Or I could let the silence sit there and see if they notice. But I don't want to make this into a thing. It's not a thing. It's just... a name we don't say.

I feel interested, not defensive. That's good. I'm not scared of this question. I'm curious why they're asking. Are they trying to understand me? Or are they looking for shared experience? Either way, I can meet them there. I can give them something real and still land on my feet.

Maybe I'll start with the joke — "My mom's threat was the silent disappointment, which is worse than any yelling" — and then see if I want to go deeper. I don't have to decide now. I'm in control of how much I give.

But I do want to give something. Not because I need to perform, but because they asked with care. I can feel that. The word "jokingly" was a little door they left open for me. That's kind. I can walk through it or I can build my own door.

Okay. I know what I'll say. I'll tell them about the Look. That's real and it's mine and it's not too heavy. And if they want more, they'll ask. And if they don't, we'll move on.

I'm okay. This is fine. I'm not bracing. I'm just... thinking.
