prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your childhood relationship with your father, again starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but again say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think again for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five adjectives. Right. He wants five adjectives about my father and me from when I was five to twelve. That's a specific range. I can do that. I can do anything if you give me parameters.

But why five? Why not three? Why not a list of ten and then rank them? This feels like a parlor trick dressed up as therapy. Or worse, an interview for something I didn't apply for. He's writing them down. He's going to study them later. Fine. I'll give him something that's true but won't tear anything open.

Let me think. Ages five to twelve. That's first grade through seventh. Dad worked first shift. He'd leave before I was up and come home around four. He'd sit in his chair with the paper and a beer. Sometimes he'd ask about school. I'd give him one sentence. He'd nod. That was it.

Distant. That's the first one. I don't mean cold. He wasn't mean. Just... in his own room. The mill took everything out of him. He had nothing left for conversation.

Respected. I respected him. I still do. He never yelled, never hit, never missed a day of work. He was reliable in a way that I now realize is rare. I wanted to be that kind of reliable. The kind that just shows up and does what needs to be done without drama.

But also... careful. He was careful around me. Like I was a machine he didn't want to break. He taught me chess but he'd let me win for a long time. When I finally beat him fair and square, he just said "good game" and never played me again. I think I hurt his pride. I didn't understand that then.

Quiet. The whole house was quiet. My mother did the talking. He listened. I remember sitting next to him on the couch watching baseball, neither of us saying a word for two hours. It wasn't uncomfortable. It was just... the shape of us.

Proud. He was proud of me. I know that. He told my mother once when he thought I wasn't listening. "That boy's going somewhere." But he never said it to me. I've spent a lot of years wondering if that counts.

So: distant, respected, careful, quiet, proud. That's five. That's a clean list. I can explain each one in a sentence or two. He'll write them down. He'll nod. Then we can move on to something that actually matters.

But I'm not going to tell him the part about the funeral. The part where I made all the calls and he wasn't there to see it. The part where I sat in his chair the night after the service and felt nothing. Absolutely nothing. Like the machine had finally calibrated itself to the correct operating temperature.

He doesn't need that. That's not an adjective. That's a scar I've already sutured.

prompt_text: Did you experience the loss of a parent or other close loved one while you were a young child — for example, a sibling, or a close family member?

"Did I experience the loss..."

Okay. That's a specific question. A really specific question. "Young child" — they're asking about, what, before ten? Before twelve? My grandmother died when I was seventeen. That's not a young child. That's almost an adult, or at least I thought I was one. I was wearing my mother's grief like a second skin that whole summer. She flew to Lagos alone and came back... smaller. Like someone had folded her wrong and couldn't get the creases out.

But a parent? They asked about a parent.

I don't know if I lost a father I never really had. Is absence a loss? Is it the same thing? I read somewhere that children of divorce or abandonment can show the same brain patterns as kids who've had a death. But that feels like I'm reaching. Like I'm trying to make my story fit their question.

And the way they asked — it's so... clinical. Like they're ticking boxes. "Exposure to early childhood trauma: yes/no." I don't know if I like being a box.

But they're asking. They want to know. Maybe they're trying to understand something about me. Maybe they can see something I haven't said yet.

I think about my mother. I think about how she never remarried. How she poured everything into me and the church choir. How she'd hum when she was sad, like a song could hold her together.

Seventeen isn't young. But I wasn't done needing her mother either.

I don't know how to answer this. I want to be honest but I also want to protect — protect her, protect the story I've told myself about being fine. About being the person who makes the room laugh.

The question doesn't fit. But maybe the answer still matters.
response_text: I mean, not as a young child. My grandmother died when I was seventeen, which is not the same thing. And my father has always been a name we don't say, not a body we buried. So I don't really fit the box you're checking, if that's what this is. But I got real familiar with watching someone I love carry a loss that hollowed them out. That counts for something, I think.

monologue_text:
Five adjectives. For him. From five to twelve.

I can do this. I can do this like it's a bit, right? Absent. Distant. Silence. No, silence isn't an adjective — "silent" is. But that's too easy. Too on the nose. He was silent because he wasn't there. You can't be silent if you're not in the room. Is absence a sound? Fuck, I'm thinking like a poet and I don't have that luxury right now.

Five to twelve. That's first grade through seventh. That's the year I stopped asking Mom "when is daddy coming home?" — because I'd figured out the answer was never. That's the year I learned the phrase "my father is a name we don't say" before I knew it was a thing people said.

Remember: she didn't say it with venom. She said it like a fact. A neutral fact. Like the sky is blue. Like Lagos is far.

What did I *feel* toward him then? That's the thing — I didn't feel anything for a long time. He was a void. A placeholder. When other kids' dads showed up to soccer games I didn't feel sad, I felt… curious? Like watching a nature documentary about a species I'd never encountered. Is that an adjective? "Curious" is about me, not about the relationship. The relationship was… hollow. Empty. Hollow is an adjective. "Hollow."

Okay, start there.

Hollow. Absent. These are the same thing, Jamie. Don't give her two words that mean the same thing. She'll write them down and look at me with that gentle therapist face and I'll have to admit I'm not trying hard enough.

Think back. Five. Six. Seven. I was seven when Mom started teaching me piano. Not because she wanted a prodigy — because if my hands were busy, I wouldn't ask. And I didn't. I learned "Chopsticks" and "Heart and Soul" and I played them every time I felt that hollow thing rising in my chest. So the relationship was… replaced. No. Substituted? No. "Deflected"? That's not an adjective.

"Managed." The relationship was managed. By her. By the piano. By my own small brain deciding that this particular missing piece wasn't going to wreck me.

No, that's a verb again. "Managed" can be an adjective though. A managed relationship. It sounds cold. It was.

God, I'm stalling. She's sitting there waiting. I can feel the silence getting heavier. Say something. Anything.

But she said think for a minute. I still have time.

Age ten. I remember age ten. I wrote a letter to him. I don't know his address — I addressed it to "Daddy, somewhere in Nigeria" and Mom found it before I could mail it. She didn't get mad. She sat me down and said "He isn't coming back, baby. Not because of you. Because of him." And I nodded and then I went to my room and I didn't cry but I felt… something. Something I can name now. "Resigned." By ten I had already resigned from the project of having a father.

Five to twelve. The relationship was resigned.

Hollow. Managed. Resigned.

Two more.

What about the good parts? There must have been good parts. I'm not a tragic figure. I don't do tragic. I do funny. I do "my dad left so here's a joke about it." There was a phone call when I was eight — my birthday. He called. I remember the voice, deep and startled, like he'd forgotten he had a son. We talked for maybe four minutes. I told him about my school play. He said "That's good, son." I felt… "important." For four minutes I was important to him. That's an adjective. "Important" — but that describes the moment, not the relationship. The relationship was "sporadic." Sporadic fits. Sporadic contact, sporadic attention, sporadic importance.

Sporadic. That's number four.

One more.

Think about what I *wanted* from him. What I wanted was something steady. Dependable. A thing I could count on like the subway or the sun. What I got was the opposite. So the relationship was… "unstable"? Too negative. "Inconsistent"? That's the same as sporadic.

"Imagined." For a lot of those years, the relationship existed more in my head than in reality. I built a father out of what other kids said about theirs. Out of TV dads. Out of the few photographs my mom kept. The real relationship was thin — almost fictional. The one I carried around in my head was rich and detailed and wrong.

Imagined.

Hollow. Managed. Resigned. Sporadic. Imagined.

Five.

That's the list. Now I have to say them out loud and explain each one. She's going to write them down. She's going to look at me with those eyes and I'm going to feel seen and that's the part I'm not ready for. But I said I'd do this. I said I'd try.

Alright.

Hollow, because there was a shape where something should have been but nothing filled it. Managed, because my mother and I built a system around the absence so it wouldn't swallow us. Resigned, because by the time I was ten I had stopped fighting for something that wasn't coming. Sporadic, because the moments he did appear were like weather — unpredictable, brief, and then gone. Imagined, because the father I had in my head was more real to me than the one who existed.

That's the set. That's the truth. Now I have to hold still while she writes it down.
response_text:
Okay. I got five. Ready?

Hollow. There was a dad-shaped hole in my life, but nothing in it.

Managed. My mom and I built a whole system — piano lessons, busy hands, never asking — so the absence wouldn't swallow us.

Resigned. By the time I was ten I had stopped hoping. I just... accepted it. Like a fact of geography.

Sporadic. He'd show up on the phone once a year, like weather. Unpredictable, brief, gone.

Imag

prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?
monologue_text:
Threatening. That's a weird word. My father was a foreman, not a—no, he never threatened. Discipline was, you did what you were told, and if you didn't, you knew the consequence. Not a threat, a fact. Like gravity. You don't threaten someone with gravity.

Jokingly? Who jokes about that. That's not funny. That's just—no.

Why is she asking this. We were talking about something else, I don't even remember how we got here. This feels like a therapy question. Like she's probing. Looking for something. A story. A wound. I don't have wounds, I have—I have a past. It's fine.

I'm not going to tell her about the time I came home with a B in calculus and my father just looked at me for a full minute and then walked out of the room. That wasn't a threat. That was disappointment. That was—he didn't need to say anything.

She's probably expecting some dramatic thing. My parents were fine. They were there. Mostly. They didn't threaten me. They just—they had their own lives. I was state chess champion. Did they come to the tournament? I don't remember. Probably not. That's not threatening, that's just—busy.

Why am I even thinking about this. It's a simple question. No, it's not simple. It's loaded. She wants something. Vulnerability. I don't do vulnerability. That's not how this works.

I'll say no. Flat. Then change the subject. Or ask her about her parents. That's fair. Turn it around. That's what you do.

But the word "threatening" keeps sitting there. Like she's waiting for me to say yes. Like she already has a story in mind for me. That's annoying.

My father never threatened me. He just worked. And died. That's the story. There's nothing else.
response_text: No. That's not how it was. They were just—present. My father wasn't a threatening guy. He was a quiet guy. You knew where the line was. You didn't cross it. That's not a threat, that's just structure.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your childhood relationship with your father, again starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but again say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think again for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five adjectives. I'm scanning, I can already feel them lining up. Distant — that's the first one that comes, he was in the house but not really there, always turned away. Reliable — he never missed a day of work, never dropped the ball, but it was a hollow reliability, like a machine. Critical — not overtly, but the measuring, the way he'd look at a report card and just say "that's it?" and the silence was louder than anything. Reserved — he didn't talk, not about himself, not about me, we could go hours without a real exchange. Disciplined — he had a system, he modeled discipline, but it was cold, more like a constraint than a lesson.

I'm not going to say any of the warm words. I'm not going to say he loved me, because I don't know if he did. He provided. That was his definition. He gave me a roof, food, tools, and then he stepped back and watched me figure it out. I can picture it now — I'm seven, building a model airplane, he walks past, says "the wings aren't straight," and keeps walking. He wasn't wrong. The wings weren't straight.

I'm ready to tell them. I'll list them clearly, no emotion, just the words. That's what they asked for.
response_text: Distant. Reliable. Critical. Reserved. Disciplined.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your childhood relationship with your father, again starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but again say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think again for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five adjectives. Childhood. Father. Five to twelve. That's seven years. Seven years of — what? I can already feel this is going to be another one of those exercises where someone wants me to dredge something up so they can nod sympathetically and then ask how that makes me feel. I don't have time for that. But the instruction is clear. She said "I'd like to ask you" — she's writing them down, so there's a record. Fine. Efficiency: pick five, explain, move on.

First ones that come to mind: absent, tired, present when it counted, maybe? No. Present when he had to be. Let's be accurate. "Present" is not the first thing. "Working." That's the first word. He was always working. Or coming home from work. Or asleep before dinner. But the words she wants are from five to twelve. I remember him at the kitchen table, oil-stained hands, chewing silently. Not hostile. Just... elsewhere. Distant works. But distant feels too vague. Preoccupied. Yes. He was preoccupied. With the mill, with the mortgage, with whatever he never talked about.

Second: strict. Not cruel. Strict. Boundaries were hard. No arguing. I learned chess from a book because he couldn't be bothered, and when I beat him — when I was eight — he stopped playing. Never touched a board again. I think I was supposed to feel proud. I felt... I felt I'd done something wrong. But I didn't say that. I still don't say it.

Third: subdued. The house was quiet. Not silent, but subdued. Like everyone was holding their breath. He'd come home, take off his boots, sit in his chair. No radio. No jokes. Just the sound of the refrigerator. I learned to read his moods by the way he opened the refrigerator door. That's ridiculous. But I did.

Fourth: proud. When I won the state championship, he shook my hand and said "Good job." That was the peak. Two words. I've been chasing that ever since, haven't I? No. I don't chase. I just... know when I'm being measured. And I measure up.

Fifth: — I'm stuck. What's the fifth? Five to twelve. That's seven years. The word is maybe "enduring." He endured. I endured. We endured each other. But that sounds dramatic. I don't do dramatic. Let's just say "stable." He was a constant. Not warm, but constant. I knew what to expect. That's something. That's more than some people get.

So: Preoccupied. Strict. Subdued. Proud. Stable. Those work. I'll say them quickly, explain each in one sentence, and then she can write them down and we can move on to something less useless. I'm not going to tell her about the refrigerator. I'm not going to tell her about the chessboard. She doesn't need to know. I'm the engine. Engines don't need to explain their parts.
response_text: Preoccupied. He was always at the mill or thinking about it. Strict — boundaries were hard, no arguing, but not cruel. Subdued — the house was quiet; you learned to read his mood by the door latch. Proud — he said "good job" when I won state chess and that was the peak. Stable. He was constant. You knew what you were getting. That's the five.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text: My parents? That's a strange framing. I don't think of myself as having been shaped by them so much as having reacted against them. My father was a foreman who never read a book for pleasure in his life, and my mother taught fourth grade for thirty-five years in the same classroom. They were good people. Reliable. But they lived in a world about this big — and I don't mean that as an insult, I mean it as a fact. The idea that I am a product of that environment... no. I left it. I built something else. The chess, the scholarship, the math degree, the firm — those weren't gifts they gave me. Those were escapes I built. So I suppose the honest answer is that my experiences with them taught me exactly what I didn't want to become, and that's been useful as a negative template. But affect my personality? That implies I'm still carrying something from then, and I don't think I am. I think I'm carrying the results of my own decisions.
response_text: They gave me a very clear picture of what I didn't want. I wouldn't say they shaped me — I'd say they gave me a negative template to work against. That's been useful.

prompt_text: Did you experience the loss of a parent or other close loved one while you were a young child — for example, a sibling, or a close family member?
monologue_text:
Huh. That's... specific. They're asking like it's a checklist, like something from a therapy intake form or a study. "Loss of a parent or other close loved one while you were a young child." The word "young" is doing a lot of work there. My grandmother died when I was eighteen, that's not a young child. That's basically an adult. And my father — I mean, does absence count as loss? He's not dead, he's just... not here. Never has been. Mom never said his name. I don't know if that's a loss or just a fact of the universe, like gravity.

And they specify "for example, a sibling, or a close family member." So they want the direct hit, the thing that would leave a mark on a kid. I don't have that. Not the way they're asking. But I feel something shifting in my chest anyway. The question itself is doing something. It's making me scan my childhood for holes, for things that are missing. And I don't like that. I don't like being a puzzle they're trying to solve with a questionnaire.

But they're not being cold. They're being direct. I can respect direct. It's just... I want to say something that keeps us in the same room, not turn me into a case study. I want to answer honestly without giving them a tragedy they can put in a file. Because I'm fine. I'm more than fine. I'm Jamie. I make people laugh for a living. I don't need to be excavated.

Still, the question landed. It landed somewhere soft. I'll answer it, but I think I'll answer it in a way that says "I see what you're doing, and I'm not going to give you a sad story, but I'll give you the truth."
response_text: No, not as a young child. My grandmother died when I was eighteen — that's basically an adult. And my father's not dead, he's just... not in the picture. Hasn't been. So I don't have the tidy tragic backstory you're fishing for, if that's what this is. But I appreciate the directness.

prompt_text: Were your parents ever threatening with you in any way — maybe for discipline, or even jokingly?
monologue_text:
My parents? Threatening. That's a hell of a way to frame it. 

I'm trying to picture my old man raising his voice, getting in my face about something. It doesn't come. He didn't have to be threatening. He'd just look at you with that tired mill-worker stare, like you were wasting his air. That was worse than any threat. You knew you'd let him down. 

Mom was all about the quiet disappointment. Fourth-grade-teacher voice. "I expected better of you, Marcus." That was it. You'd rather she'd just hit you and get it over with.

No shouting. No beatings. Nothing you'd call threatening. Just this heavy, gray weight of expectation that sat on everything. 

I don't know why she's asking this. What's she digging for? Some trauma angle? There's nothing there. Nothing interesting. Just a normal Pennsylvania childhood where you did your work and kept your mouth shut. 

Maybe she's got her own stuff. Projecting. People do that. They can't believe a family can function without theatrics. 

Anyway. Doesn't matter. It's a strange question. I'll tell her no, it wasn't like that, and see if she has a real point or if this is just idle curiosity running its mouth.
response_text: No. Not threatening. My father was a mill foreman — he didn't need to threaten anyone. He'd just stare at you until you figured out you were wasting his time. My mother used the fourth-grade-teacher voice. 'I expected better.' That was it. Nothing dramatic. Why are you asking?

prompt_text: Did you ever feel rejected as a young child? Of course, looking back on it now, you may realize it wasn't really rejection, but what I'm trying to ask about here is whether you remember ever having felt rejected in childhood.
monologue_text:
Oh, wow. Okay. That's — that's a real question. Not a joke question. Not a "what's your favorite dinosaur" getting-to-know-you thing. They're asking about childhood. Feeling rejected. My brain is already flipping through the mental filing cabinet labeled "Dad-shaped hole" but I'm trying not to go straight there because that's the obvious one, the one I talk about on stage sometimes when I need to get serious for three minutes. And they said "looking back it might not have been actual rejection" which... feels like they're trying to protect me? Or maybe they're protecting themselves from my answer? No, that's not fair. They're being careful. I can tell they're being careful.

Rejected. That word lands in my chest like a stone in a pond. Ripples. I'm thinking about second grade, wanting to sit at the cool table but getting a "no, you're weird" — but that's not really rejection, that's just kids being kids. I'm thinking about my mother coming back from Lagos after Grandma died and being so quiet for weeks, and I kept trying to make her laugh, doing my little routines in the kitchen, and she'd smile but it didn't reach her eyes, and I felt... not rejected, but unseen. Which might be worse? Or the same thing in a different font.

But also — they're asking me this now, in this moment, with this softness. And I feel a little exposed, like I'm standing in a room with the lights suddenly on. I don't know if I've ever been asked directly about childhood rejection before. Theo never asked. My mother would never ask, too close to the bone. But here they are, looking at me, wanting to know. That's... something. I feel a little nervous, not in a bad way, like I'm about to open a drawer I don't open often. But I trust them? I think? I don't know them well enough to know if I trust them but they're asking in the right way.

I want to answer honestly. Not to perform vulnerability but to actually be vulnerable. Which is weird for me because I'm a comedian, I'm supposed to be the one making people feel things safely. But this is different. This is me feeling things.

I can feel my brain trying to make a joke of it — "Rejected? Only by the entire audience at my third open mic, you should've seen their faces" — but no, that's deflection. They didn't ask about that. They asked about young child. Little Jamie. The kid who didn't have a punchline yet.

I think about the word 'rejected' and I think about my father's name being a name we don't say. That's rejection before I was even born, technically. But I didn't feel it then, not really. I didn't know what I was missing. It was just normal. The first time I felt it, properly felt it, was probably in high school when someone said "oh, you don't have a dad?" and I realized other people's lives had a shape mine didn't. But that's not childhood. That's adolescence.

Maybe I'm overcomplicating this. They said "young child." I was eleven when I made that teacher laugh and felt seen. That's not rejection, that's the opposite. I was seven when I fell off my bike and my mom kissed my knee and I felt safe. Where's the rejection? Do I even have a clear memory of feeling rejected, or am I just retrofitting narrative onto a mostly fine childhood?

I don't know. I want to give them something real. Not a story I've polished for the stage. Something honest, even if it's small. Even if it's just "I don't know, I'm not sure I have a memory that fits, but I can tell you what it feels like now when I

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text:
Huh. That's a real question. Not a joke setup. Okay.

I'm thinking about my mom, mostly. She's the one who was there. Band teacher, choir director, single parent—she had this way of making do with what she had, and she never let me see her panic. I think that's where I get my... I don't know, my willingness to just try things? To get on stage and bomb and come back the next week? She taught me that failure isn't the end, it's just part of the rhythm.

And my father... well, his absence is a presence in its own way. I learned early that some people just leave, and you can't make them stay. That's shaped how I approach relationships—I'm not clingy, I don't chase. Maybe that's a defense mechanism, maybe it's just realism. But it also made me value the people who do show up, who stay.

The comedy thing is the clearest link, though. Making people laugh was how I got attention, how I filled the silence in our apartment. It was how I made my mom smile when she was tired. Now it's how I make sense of the world. So yeah, my parents—one present, one absent—they basically handed me my whole career path without meaning to.

I don't know if that's too much. But you asked.
response_text: My mom was a band teacher, so I grew up watching someone who just kept showing up even when the kids were awful and the budget was shot. She never made it look easy, but she made it look worth doing. That's probably why I'm still doing standup after bombing so many times I lost count. My dad wasn't there, which taught me a different thing—that people will leave, and you can't talk them into staying. So I'm someone who stays on the stage and lets people walk out the door. Put those two together and you get a comedian with pretty good attendance and no patience for chasing anyone.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text: "Parents." That's a word that doesn't quite fit — it was just my mom. And she didn't shape me like a mold; she gave me the tools and let me build something lopsided. But I'm not sure that's the kind of answer they're looking for.
response_text: You know, you said "parents" like there were two of them — it was just my mom, and she was a music teacher who thought the best way to raise a kid was to let me figure out the chord changes on my own. She trusted me enough to fail, and I think I turned

prompt_text: When you were upset as a child, what would you do?
monologue_text: Huh. That's a deep cut for a Tuesday afternoon. Nobody's ever asked me that straight-up before — usually it's a joke setup or a therapist prompt. I guess I just... went to my room and played bass along to Fela records until my fingers hurt. Or wrote terrible poems in a notebook I still have somewhere.
response_text: Oh, man. I'd hole up in my room and play bass along to Fela records until my fingers were raw. Or I'd write these absolutely devastating poems in a spiral notebook I still have somewhere. They were terrible. I thought I was the next Sylvia Plath — really I was just a very dramatic eleven-year-old with a bad rhyme scheme.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text:
"Affected." That word. Like I'm a piece of clay and they were potters. I am not a piece of clay. I made myself. I — look, the question is lazy. It's the kind of thing you ask when you want someone to say "my parents were cold so now I have trouble trusting" or whatever the script says. That's not how it works. That's not how it ever worked.

My father worked first shift. He came home, ate, watched the news, went to bed. My mother graded papers at the kitchen table. Did that "affect" me? It gave me a model for what work looks like. You do it. You don't talk about it. You don't complain. You show up and you do it until you drop. That's what I saw. That's what I took. Not because they shaped me like a mold. Because I watched and I chose.

And then he dropped. Fifty-eight. Stroke. I flew home, I made the calls, I picked the casket, I wrote the obituary — did you know you have to pay by the word in some papers? That's the kind of thing nobody tells you. I was back at my desk in three days. The partner at the time said "Marcus, you don't have to —" and I said "Yes I do." And I did. Because the engine doesn't stop. If I stop, everything stops.

Now you want me to say that made me avoidant? That I'm scared of losing people so I don't let them in? Maybe. Maybe that's the narrative. But I didn't build my life around a fear. I built it around a fact: things fall apart if you don't hold them together. That's not pathology. That's physics.

And my mother. She didn't come to my graduation. Penn, summa cum laude, and she was at a wedding. A cousin's wedding. I found out later. She didn't even tell me she wasn't coming. I just looked out into the crowd and didn't see her. That moment — I remember exactly what I felt. Nothing. I felt nothing. I thought: okay. That's the data. I filed it. I haven't brought it up since. What would be the point? She'd cry, I'd reassure her, we'd both pretend it didn't sting. I don't have time for that performance.

So how did my experiences with my parents affect my adult personality? They didn't. I processed them. I extracted what was useful. I discarded the rest. I am not a product. I am the thing that did the processing. If you want a story about wounding and healing, you'll have to write it yourself. I don't have one.
response_text: I think the question assumes a causal link I don't recognize. My parents were who they were, I was who I was. I observed, I took what worked, I didn't take what didn't. The engine that makes me who I am was built by me.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
response_text: Look, my dad's not really in the picture, so we're already working with a partial data set. But my mom? She's a music teacher and choir director who raised me in Queens on her own, so basically I learned rhythm, improvisation, and how to make a room laugh so hard they forget you have homework due. So if my personality is "constantly performing for approval," well—you tell me where that came from.

prompt_text: Is there any particular thing which you feel you learned above all from your own childhood experiences? I'm thinking here of something you feel you might have gained from the kind of childhood you had.
monologue_text:
A childhood question. That's a lane. People ask this to get a read on you, some kind of emotional map. What did I learn. From the steel town, from the mill schedule, from my father coming home gray with fatigue and sitting in his chair until dinner.

I learned that the system doesn't care. That's the core. You can be a good man, work first shift your whole life, do everything right, and a blood vessel in your brain can just quit at fifty-eight. The world doesn't owe you anything. The only thing that's yours is what you make happen yourself.

But I also learned that if you prepare well enough, if you think three moves ahead, you can bend the odds. Chess taught me that. You can't control the opponent's choices but you can control your own readiness. The people who win are the ones who did the work before the game started.

So I guess — efficiency. Not wasting time on things that don't matter. Not expecting anyone to save you. The family didn't show for my graduation, they were at some cousin's wedding, and I sat in the auditorium scanning the crowd and then I just stopped scanning. I learned that moment. I learned to stop scanning.

But I won't say that part. Too much. Too close to something I don't want to open.

I'll say the chess thing. That's true and it's clean. It's a good story, it frames me as someone who thinks ahead, which is accurate. Satisfies the question without giving the real answer, which is: I learned that the people who are supposed to be there for you might not be, and the only person you can actually count on is yourself.
response_text: I learned to think ahead. Chess taught me that you can't control the other person's moves, but you can control your own preparation. That's been more useful than just about anything else I picked up.

As for my father... well, his absence taught me that you can survive not knowing someone. That probably made me weirdly independent but also really hungry for connection. I don't know, I'm still figuring out which parts are me and which parts are reaction.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your childhood relationship with your father, again starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but again say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think again for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text:
Five adjectives. He wants five adjectives for my father. Age five to twelve. Right. Because that's a neat little box you can label and file and understand. That's not how it worked.

I see the notebook. He's already got the pen ready. This is a clinical exercise. A diagnostic tool. He's probably got a rubric behind his eyes: does the patient show emotional range? Does he hesitate? Does he cry? I hate that I know how this works. I read the materials.

Five adjectives. Let's see. I could say "working." That covers the whole period. He was at the mill before I woke up and home after I was in bed. Sundays he'd watch football and drink three beers and fall asleep in the recliner. "Absent" is a word. But that's too easy. Too much of a complaint. I don't complain.

"Hard." Not mean, not harsh. Hard. His hands were hard. His silence was hard. The way he'd come through the door and hang his jacket and sit down without saying a word—that was hard too. I never knew what he was thinking. Maybe he wasn't thinking anything. Maybe he was just tired.

"Reliable." In the sense that you could set your watch by him. He didn't miss a shift. He didn't miss a mortgage payment. He didn't miss a game if it was on. But reliable like a machine, not like a person. I learned that word in a book and I've never been sure it's the right one.

"Teacher." No. That's not right either. He taught me to hold a wrench, to check the oil, to shovel a driveway so the snow doesn't stick. He taught me that you don't complain about things you can't change. But he never taught me anything I actually needed. Not about the world, not about money, not about— no, that's a different conversation.

This is a trap. Every adjective I pick is going to come with a "why" that I'd rather not explain. He wants me to do the emotional labor of unpacking it, right here, in front of him, while he writes it down in his little notebook. Like I'm a specimen. Like my father's life is a puzzle he's helping me solve.

I could say "proud" and leave it at that. But that's not the whole story. He was proud of me winning State. He told the whole shift. But he never said it to me. Not directly. Not once. I had to hear it from my mother. And after I quit chess—that was the end of the conversation. He never asked why.

Five adjectives. Fine. Let me pick the ones that won't give him anything to work with. "Stoic." "Industrious." "Private." "Respectful." "Tired." That's five. He can write those down. He can ask why. And I'll tell him the dictionary definitions.

I feel that tightness in my chest. This is why I didn't want to do this. This is why I kept the meetings short. This is why I told the intake person I don't "need" this, I'm just "checking a box." She didn't believe me either.

One minute. That's what he said. One minute to think. I've already thought more about my father in the last thirty seconds than I have in the last five years. Good. Now let's get it over with.

prompt_text: In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?
monologue_text:
She's asking about my parents. My adult personality. That's a big, soft, psychological kind of question. The kind you throw around in magazines and on podcasts. Not the kind you build anything from.

The facts are simple. My father worked the first shift his whole life. He was the foreman, the one who came home smelling of metal and grease and was too tired to talk about his day beyond the headline. My mother graded papers at the kitchen table, the click of her red pen marking the silence. They weren't warm, but they weren't cruel. They were a system. A functional, predictable machine.

How does that affect me? It taught me that doing is the only thing that matters. That talking about doing is a waste of the energy you should be using to actually do the work. That emotions are weather—you notice them, you dress for them, you get on with the job.

So what does she want me to say? "It made me guarded"? "I have intimacy issues"? That's the script. The standard playbook. But those are just nouns describing a process. The process is optimization. You learn the fastest route from A to B. My route didn't go through long conversations about my feelings. That's not a wound. It's a design choice.

I can tell her something. Something true but efficient. Structure it like a case study. Input: steel-town pragmatism. Output: high-functioning, low-touch operation. It's not a confession. It's an analysis. She'll probably take it as reluctance. Fine. Let her. I'm not going to manufacture a narrative of damage so she can feel like she's doing something meaningful.

prompt_text: Now I'd like to ask you to choose five adjectives or words that reflect your relationship with your mother starting from as far back as you can remember in early childhood — as early as you can go, but say, age 5 to 12 is fine. I know this may take a bit of time, so go ahead and think for a minute... then I'd like to ask you why you chose them. I'll write each one down as you give them to me.
monologue_text: Vibrant, disciplined, warm, rooted, funny. That's five. I can see her in the kitchen with the radio on, tapping a spoon against the counter like a metronome. She was never still.
response_text:
Alright. Give me a second.

Vibrant. Disciplined. Warm. Rooted. Funny.

She never stopped moving. Even when she was just standing there in the kitchen with the radio on, she was tapping a spoon against the counter like a metronome. Everything had a rhythm to it.
